news_summary: Sasol plans to expand its alumina spheres production capacity in Germany. Because alumina is a major feedstock for aluminium smelters, incremental alumina output can improve availability and potentially reduce input tightness. The article provides limited detail on timing or volumes, so the near-term effect is likely moderate. | Alcoa says aluminum is set to swing into surplus after recording its fourth quarterly loss. The company’s outlook points to easing market tightness and potentially weaker demand/supply balances, which can pressure expectations for near-term aluminum prices. | Copper prices slipped after the U.S.-China Phase 1 trade deal as investors doubted it would materially lift metals demand. LME three-month copper eased 0.1% and ShFE copper 0.2%. LME inventories hit a 10-month low, but spreads widened, suggesting no urgent supply shortage. Aluminium rose slightly; nickel traded mixed across exchanges. | SMM data show China’s social inventories of primary aluminium across eight consumption areas rose by about 20,000 mt in the week ended Jan 16 to 630,000 mt. The increase reflects higher arrivals and broadly weaker consumption, following a small prior uptick after a longer downtrend.

Rio Tinto reiterated its position on the Tiwai Point aluminium smelter, lobbying for cheaper energy as the business struggles with low profitability. The company described challenging global conditions and noted aluminium prices staying weak, reinforcing bearish expectations for the metal in the near term. | China and Indonesia signed a $695m contract to build the Mempawah alumina refinery in West Kalimantan. The project is expected to produce 1m tons of alumina annually and feed Indonesia Asahan Aluminium for ingot production. It aligns with Indonesia’s ore export ban and import replacement goals.

London copper held steady near an eight-month high on firm China industrial output, retail sales and investment data, though volumes were thin before the Lunar New Year holiday. Copper on ShFE eased as trade slowed. LME aluminum and SHFE aluminum fell slightly; nickel declined on SHFE, implying limited net directional pressure. | SMM data show China’s social inventories of primary aluminium across eight consumption areas rose 21,000 mt from Jan 16 to 651,000 mt as of Jan 20. The build was driven by concentrated arrivals and softer consumption ahead of Lunar New Year holidays, suggesting near-term bearish pressure for aluminium. | Indonesian and Chinese firms signed a $695m contract to build the Mempawah alumina refinery in West Kalimantan. The project is expected to produce 1m tons of alumina annually and replace part of Inalum’s alumina imports, supporting domestic refining and eventual aluminium output as export bans push value-add. | London copper rose to an eight-month high after solid China industrial output and retail sales data lifted demand sentiment, though volumes were light ahead of Lunar New Year. LME aluminium inched up, while LME nickel fell slightly. China’s scrap standards add detail but not an immediate shock. | SMM reports SHFE nonferrous metals mostly closed lower Monday as liquidity thinned ahead of the Jan 24 Chinese New Year holiday. Copper fell 0.47% with technical softness, though PBOC reverse repos supported sentiment. Aluminium dropped 0.49% on weakened demand, and nickel declined 1.44% and stayed rangebound. | Doubleview Capital reports a new mineralized zone south of the Lisle deposit at its Hat gold-copper porphyry project. Drill results show sulphide mineralization with chalcopyrite/magnetite intercepts and higher intensity with depth; assays for copper/gold/cobalt are pending. It’s an exploration discovery without production implications.

Group Ten Metals reported 2019 drill results at its Stillwater West PGE-Ni-Cu project, including 399m of continuous nickel-equivalent mineralization with associated copper values. The company says the results expand known Platreef-style PGE-Ni-Cu-Co mineralization, supporting incremental short-term optimism for nickel and copper supply potential. | Global aluminum production fell 1% in 2019, but LME prices stayed weak around a two-and-a-half-year low, reflecting poor demand and expectations that China’s production growth will resume. Analysts forecast renewed oversupply pressures, suggesting the market may need additional supply cuts to prevent further downside. | The Centre agreed to restart the Angul Aluminium Park infrastructure upgrade project in Odisha after bottlenecks were addressed. Union minister Dharmendra Pradhan said the JV between NALCO and IDCO is expected to complete by July 2020, supported by central funding, aiming to boost aluminium industry competitiveness and jobs. | Residents protest plans to mine bauxite in Ghana’s Atewa Forest Reserve and vow to sue the government if the project proceeds. They argue mining would harm water and livelihoods and call on authorities to exclude the area from the government’s Syinihydro deal, amid international criticism of the $2 billion Chinese infrastructure plan. | The Bloc Quebecois plans to block fast-track ratification of the updated NAFTA deal, citing insufficient protections for Quebec’s aluminum industry compared with steel. Ratification could be delayed in Canada while the agreement is studied and debated. The U.S. and Mexico have already advanced their steps, raising near-term aluminum trade uncertainty.

Canada’s Bloc Quebecois says it will not fast-track ratification of the updated NAFTA, citing insufficient protections for Quebec’s aluminum industry compared with steel. The party may stall approval in a minority Parliament while reviewing the agreement. The U.S. and Mexico have already moved forward, leaving Canada as the holdout. | The Union government agreed to restart the infrastructure upgrade project for the Angul Aluminium Park in Odisha, after earlier delays and a decision to drop the project and refund the central grant. The upgrade is expected to enhance aluminium industry competitiveness and is targeted for completion by July 2020. | London copper stayed near a two-week low as LME-approved inventories jumped 38,700 tonnes and coronavirus fears spread beyond China. The benchmark three-month contract fell as much as 0.5% and was near unchanged by early trade. Aluminium fell slightly while nickel rose on the day, reflecting mixed macro impact. | London copper hovered near a two-week low as the Wuhan coronavirus outbreak in China raised travel/consumption concerns and LME-approved copper inventories jumped by 38,700 tonnes to 162,925. LME three-month copper fell up to 0.5% and had a larger prior-session drop, reflecting bearish near-term sentiment. | London copper hovered near a two-week low as LME inventories rose by 38,700 tonnes and coronavirus spread from China to other countries. LME three-month copper fell up to 0.5% and was near unchanged later, with overall metals sentiment weaker, while aluminium and nickel also moved amid risk concerns. | AB InBev CEO Carlos Brito said progress on trade deals supports consumers, but “aluminum tariffs aren’t welcome.” Because beer cans use aluminum, tariffs act as an added tax on consumers and disadvantage domestic U.S. can purchasers. The headline implies weaker aluminum demand sentiment. | Copper fell to a near three-week low as China’s coronavirus death toll rose, renewing concerns about weaker economic growth and base-metals demand. The move was reinforced by rising LME copper inventories and wider signals of plentiful supply (cash discounts). Aluminium and nickel also closed lower amid the same risk sentiment.

Copper prices fell to a six-week low as LME-approved inventories jumped 20% and Shanghai inventories rose, while concern over a spreading flu-like virus in China raised fears of weaker demand growth. Trading activity also weakened ahead of Lunar New Year. The broader base-metals complex fell, including aluminium and nickel. | The Centre agreed to restart infrastructure upgradation work at Odisha’s Angul Aluminium Park, a JV involving NALCO and IDCO. Dharmendra Pradhan said renewed focus on removing bottlenecks should enable completion by July 2020, funded partly by a central grant, and restore support to the aluminium cluster, boosting the sector’s outlook. | Copper prices fell to a six-week low as coronavirus fears in China intensified, with two cities locked down and liquidity disrupted by the Lunar New Year. The drop was reinforced by rising LME warehouse inventories and technical selling. Other industrial metals also declined, including aluminium and nickel, reflecting broad risk-off demand expectations. | Copper prices fell to the lowest in six weeks as coronavirus fears in China intensified, driving concerns about weaker economic activity and metal demand. The move was reinforced by speculators cutting long positions, rising LME inventories, and broader selloffs in industrial commodities, with nickel and aluminium also trading lower.

Copper jumped to an eight-month high after the U.S. and China agreed an initial trade deal, lifting hopes for stronger metals demand. LME copper prices rose about 0.7% with declines in warehouse inventories. The article also reports aluminium and nickel up modestly, reflecting positive broader sentiment. | Copper steadied but was still set for its biggest weekly drop in 19 months as China’s coronavirus controls raised fears of slower growth and weaker metals demand. LME-approved copper stocks rose sharply and China inventories increased. The broader complex also weakened, with aluminium and nickel heading for weekly declines. | The Beer Institute says aluminum costs for U.S. brewers have surged due to 10% aluminum tariffs and the Midwest Premium pricing system. It claims brewers pay a full “tariff-paid” premium despite using recycled or Canada-origin metal. The institute urges leaders to demand more transparent aluminum benchmarking and pricing. | Copper prices posted the biggest weekly drop in five years as coronavirus spread raised fears of weaker Chinese growth and reduced metals demand. China’s lockdown measures and holiday disruptions heightened the outlook risk, while LME-approved and China inventories rose sharply. Nickel and aluminium also fell alongside copper.

SSY Futures Ltd arranged the first options trade tied to a mid-western aluminium delivery premium. The coverage is directly connected to how regional aluminium premiums are priced and hedged, but it does not describe any immediate change in supply, demand, inventories, or production. Net near-term price impact is therefore limited.

Technavio’s press release forecasts growth in the global aluminum market, citing increasing demand for aluminum cans in the beverage industry. It also highlights that high volatility in LME aluminum prices could hinder growth. The piece is descriptive and does not provide concrete short-term supply or policy shocks. | A Reuters poll expects copper prices to rise on improving global growth and less seamless supply return, projecting a 2020 copper deficit. It also expects nickel support from Indonesia’s nickel ore export ban. Conversely, analysts foresee an aluminium surplus in 2020, implying LME prices will decline. | President Trump imposed additional duties on imports of downstream aluminum articles—10% for aluminum products—effective February 8, 2020, under Section 232 national-security findings. Tariffs target derivative goods where aluminum is a major input and import volumes rose after prior tariffs, likely tightening supply and supporting aluminum demand/prices. | A Reuters poll expects copper to rise in 2020 due to improving global growth and less seamless supply returns, with a projected market deficit. It also expects nickel support from Indonesia’s nickel ore export ban, implying tighter supply. In contrast, aluminium is forecast to swing to surplus, weighing on prices. | Coronavirus fears in Wuhan are driving a sharp risk-off move and disrupting China’s production and return-to-work expectations. LME copper falls about 10% from mid-January highs, with reduced Chinese liquidity and sharply unwound speculative longs. Citibank expects very weak Chinese demand for 4–6 weeks, delaying recovery.

London copper rebounded from a four-month low as investors reassessed the coronavirus outbreak’s economic impact in China, where virus containment measures extended holidays and threatened factory output. The report notes factory shutdown fears for copper demand and also shows smaller gains in aluminum and stronger gains in nickel on day-to-day sentiment shifts. | Aluminium Association of India (AAI) urges the Prime Minister to raise customs duties on imported aluminium metal and scrap, arguing imports have increased due to global trade disputes and scrap policy changes. AAI disputes claims by ASMA that such a hike would harm small downstream firms, but the proposed tariff is price-supportive for aluminium.

The article reviews the $1.4bn Emirates Global Aluminium Guinea Alumina Corporation bauxite project, including rail capacity expansion and financing structures. It highlights Guinea’s bauxite as feedstock for alumina and aluminium smelting. While relevant to the aluminium value chain, it provides no clear new short-term operational or policy shift. | Egyptian aluminium producer reported H1 2019/20 net losses of EGP 595.7m versus prior-year profit, citing higher electricity and production costs and a decline in the LME basic aluminium price. Revenues fell sharply, and currency moves and investment exits also weighed on results, underscoring aluminium price weakness. | Reuters poll shows analysts expect nickel and copper to outperform on supply deficits (Indonesia export ban for nickel; copper shortfalls), but the coronavirus outbreak is weakening China demand expectations and funds are unwinding longs, with nickel and copper already down 12% and 9% since early January. Aluminium views are mixed. | Trump issued a Section 232 proclamation imposing duties on imports of certain steel and aluminum derivatives. The Federal Register publication shows the covered product list is extremely limited, and additional annexes may expand coverage later. Near term, the aluminum duties raise import costs and may worsen demand/supply expectations for aluminium.

London copper, though up slightly on Friday, is set for its biggest monthly fall in over four years as the coronavirus outbreak raises fears of weaker Chinese demand. WHO declared a global health emergency, factory activity softened, and prices have fallen ~10% since headlines began. Aluminium and nickel also moved lower/flat with the risk backdrop. | Alcoa’s Australian aluminium smelter reportedly faced a power cut during a heatwave. The outage can force production to slow or halt, reducing short-term aluminium output and tightening immediate supply expectations. Such disruptions usually pressure aluminium prices lower until operations normalize and capacity utilization recovers.

Shanghai copper sank to a more than three-year low as coronavirus concerns drove investors to sell futures. China’s market closure during Lunar New Year meant prices caught up with London declines. ShFE also showed aluminium and nickel falling, reflecting broad risk-off demand worries. | Shanghai base metals fell as China reopened trading after the Lunar New Year break, amid coronavirus fears that could reduce demand. ShFE copper sank 7% to a more-than-three-year low; aluminium and nickel also declined by about 3–4%. The outbreak and market reopening pushed prices lower despite some support from LME copper. | Shanghai copper fell to the lowest level since late 2016 as investors sold futures over escalating coronavirus worries in China. China’s Lunar New Year closure and virus-related uncertainty drove ShFE copper down the daily 7% limit, while ShFE aluminium and nickel also dropped sharply amid softer activity and disrupted trading. | CRU announced its World Aluminium Conference in London (20–21 April 2020), highlighting sessions on aluminium market outlook, supply/demand, premiums, sustainability, EV-driven demand, Chinese sector perspectives, trade impacts, recycled aluminium, and raw material/price discussions. It is an industry meeting announcement without new figures or concrete immediate catalysts.

Glencore reported 2019 output with copper down 6% and cobalt up 10% after boosting production at its Katanga mine and shutting Mutanda early due to falling cobalt prices and rising costs/taxes. The firm also noted slightly lower nickel output and kept 2020 outlook unchanged, driving shares higher. | Emirates Global Aluminium (EGA) secured a US$600mn syndicated revolving credit facility priced at a competitive margin, replacing prior uncommitted lines. The deal is intended to support working capital and liquidity management across its aluminium value chain, including mining and export facilities in Guinea. No direct production or export change is stated. | Braidy Industries ousted CEO Craig Bouchard amid controversy over $2 billion financing for a Kentucky aluminum rolling mill. The project relied on foreign investment from Rusal after U.S. sanctions were lifted, including a 40% stake and a 10-year supply contract. Plans claim no change, but funding uncertainty remains.

SMM reports copper extending its rally after China’s central bank pumped large liquidity and trimmed lending rates to stabilize markets amid the coronavirus outbreak. LME base metals rose broadly as stock markets rebounded. Copper, aluminium, and nickel all finished Tuesday higher, with short-term trading ranges suggesting continued positive momentum but some macro/FX limits. | US CBP issued guidance on Section 232 additional duties for “derivative aluminum” articles under Proclamation 9980, effective for entries on/after Feb 8, 2020. Importers must use specified HTS codes and cannot claim certain duty preferences or drawbacks, increasing landed costs for covered aluminum products. | Aluminium Bahrain (Alba) joined a Bahraini delegation to Italy and signed agreements with Italian firms including Fluorsid, FATA and Techmo Car. The deals relate to supply of aluminium fluoride, EPC work for Alba’s port/Line 6 expansion and related equipment collaboration, suggesting ongoing aluminium production activity but no immediate price shock. | Copper rebounded to a one-week high after China’s central bank stimulus, prompting investors to unwind bearish positions on coronavirus-hit demand. LME three-month copper rose about 2%. Aluminium and nickel also increased, reflecting broader industrial metals gains as market sentiment stabilised despite ongoing virus-related headwinds. | SMM said SHFE nonferrous prices ended mixed, with copper (+1.09%) and nickel (+1.43%) rising and aluminium climbing modestly (+0.29%). The rally was linked to eased risk aversion after China’s central bank injected 400 billion yuan to support the economy and offset coronavirus disruption. | Copper rose to a one-week high after China’s central bank announced stimulus, prompting investors to unwind bearish positions tied to the coronavirus’s demand impact. Copper gained about 1.9% to $5,722/t. LME aluminium and nickel also climbed, reflecting broad support for industrial metals sentiment.

SMM data show China’s social inventories of primary aluminium ingots across eight consumption areas increased 61,000 mt from Feb 3 to 945,000 mt by Feb 6, after a 194,000 mt stock jump during the CNY holiday. Arriving deliveries and logistics bottlenecks deterred warehouse outflows, pointing to softer near-term supply tightness. | SMM says copper is on course for a third day of gains, with LME copper hitting a one-week high before settling higher. Nickel rose 1.4% on SHFE and gained strongly on LME. Aluminium also climbed, supported by Tomago smelter production cut reports and China demand offset steps. | Owners of the former Century Aluminum property in West Virginia deeded the site to the state to prevent foreclosure after default on a loan tied to the former smelter assets. The plant is long shut due to aluminum prices and utility costs, and the state plans to market the site for redevelopment. | Ma’aden CEO Darren Davis told Reuters that coronavirus-related disruption and longer China Lunar holiday will hit aluminium demand, while China is also set to increase supply, reinforcing a surplus outlook. He characterized the 2020 aluminium price outlook as “more negative” than at the start of the year, weighing on prices. | China’s environmental authorities issued another 1,440 mt of aluminium scrap import quotas, bringing 2020 allowances to 284,449 mt, with Q1 likely below 300,000 mt. The outbreak delays secondary aluminium producer restarts, reducing scrap demand, while market supply looks adequate in February, pushing scrap prices to track refined aluminium lower. | SMM reports China’s alumina market is temporarily imbalanced: upstream supply is relatively sufficient but transport curbs and logistics delays restrict shipments of bauxite/caustic soda/lime. Smelters’ feedstock inventories are running short, prompting SMM to expect a two-week alumina price rally, with continuity tied to transport policy changes.

Getec Industrial says its Thermal Management Solutions division increased sales in 2019 and is expanding for 2020 with upgraded CAD workstations and 12 new CNC milling machines. It also added friction stir welding to manufacture large, seamless extruded aluminum heatsinks, potentially supporting incremental near-term aluminum demand. | SMM says copper’s rally is losing momentum as coronavirus concerns linger, with processors delaying operations and February consumption expected to fall. LME and SHFE copper face moderating gains from seasonally rising inventories. Aluminium is marginally firmer, while nickel edges up slightly despite pullbacks. | The Trump administration announced additional Section 232 tariffs on specified downstream steel and aluminum articles effective Feb. 8, 2020. Aluminum derivative products face 10% duties and steel derivatives face 25%, with exemptions for select countries. Commerce will consider product exclusions via its 232 exclusions portal. | A SpendEdge procurement report argues the aluminum market faces a CTP shortage, noting CTP is a key ingredient in carbon anodes and that China’s steel production has increased CTP demand. The report also points to higher oil and gas prices raising aluminum producers’ fuel and electricity costs, supporting higher aluminum prices. | SMM reports SHFE nonferrous metals mostly closed lower as renewed coronavirus concerns raised worries about near-term economic impact. Copper fell 0.54% after investors paused a rally amid outbreak escalation ahead of post-holiday travel. Nickel edged down 0.11%. Aluminium rose 0.48% on short-covering and downstream resumption expectations.

Spot aluminium in east and south China strengthened from last week as traders increased stockpiling ahead of factory reopenings after an extended holiday. Shanghai aluminium futures rose in morning trade, helped by better-than-expected January inflation data. Prices were higher versus Friday, though social inventories of primary aluminium ingots rose to 1.04 million mt. | SMM reported that China’s social inventories of primary aluminium ingots rose to about 1.04 million mt as rail deliveries arrived and shipments out of social warehouses were limited amid the epidemic outbreak. Inventories increased roughly 90,000 mt from Feb 6 to Feb 10 across eight consumption regions.

The update says President Trump expanded Section 232 tariffs to additional aluminum “derivative” products, keeping the 10% ad valorem rate and applying duties from Feb. 8, 2020. It also discusses a new, potentially retroactive exclusion process and added CBP compliance risk, which may affect near-term aluminum import flows and pricing. | Braidy Industries’ new CEO told Kentucky lawmakers the aluminum mill in northeast Kentucky still needs roughly $500 million to start construction, with the build potentially beginning late 2020 and commissioning in late 2022/early 2023. The project depends on funding plans and Rusal-linked supply, though scope appears limited for near-term global prices. | Braidy Industries told Kentucky lawmakers it still needs to raise $500 million to build a planned $1.7 billion aluminum plant/rolling mill in Appalachia. After a leadership shakeup, executives cited $65 million on hand and $200 million financing commitments, projecting jobs and future production if funding is secured by year’s end. | Mexico refused to include aluminum in tougher auto sourcing requirements under the new NAFTA, citing inability to compete in supplying the metal because it lacks aluminum smelting capacity. The pact still raises North American purchase content to 70% for vehicles, supporting demand for regional aluminum supply versus cheaper imports. | Red River Resources reported January production at its Thalanga copper-zinc-lead operation in Northern Queensland. Copper concentrate output rose to a record 860 tonnes versus 655 tonnes in December (+31%). The update also includes higher zinc and lead concentrate production, alongside increased ore mined and processed. | Braidy Industries’ new CEO told Kentucky lawmakers the company still needs about $500 million to start construction of a planned aluminum mill, with potential startup pushed to end-2020 and commissioning not until late 2022 or early 2023. The update highlights financing turmoil and pledged commitments, but timing limits immediate aluminum price impact.

Rio Tinto said coronavirus containment measures have slowed copper concentrate imports crossing the Mongolia–China border, while BHP also delayed shipments as Chinese plants shut. The disruption and heightened uncertainty about demand and supply chains triggered falls in key commodity prices, including copper, nickel, and aluminum. | The news headline says Rio Tinto is reviewing the viability of its ISAL aluminium smelter in Iceland. This kind of review can signal potential changes to operating status or output, which may tighten or loosen perceived aluminium supply expectations and weigh on prices if closure becomes more likely. | Rio Tinto said it may cut operations or shut its Iceland aluminium smelter after it remained unprofitable due to high power costs and tough market conditions. Production has already been reduced to 85% of capacity. Rio will review options with Icelandic partners, while weak demand strains the broader aluminium market. | Rio Tinto plans a strategic review of its ISAL aluminum smelter in Iceland, considering options including reduced production or possible closure. Output is already cut to 85% due to unprofitable operations from uncompetitive energy costs and historically low aluminum prices. Discussions with Iceland’s government aim to restore profitability. | Copper rose modestly on easing coronavirus figures in China, reducing fears for demand and lifting LME stocks. Analysts said the sell-off may have been exaggerated but risks remain for factory activity. Rio Tinto also reported slower copper concentrate shipments and possible aluminum smelter curtailments, while aluminium traded slightly weaker. | Copper rose about 0.6% after China reported its lowest new COVID-19 cases since late January, improving sentiment for demand from the biggest metals consumer. LME aluminum also edged up, while nickel stocks climbed to a multi-year high. Rio Tinto flagged shipment slowdowns and potential aluminum smelter cuts, keeping caution. | SMM outlines COVID-19’s impact on China’s aluminium supply chain, noting demand concerns and significantly higher aluminium ingot social inventories (up 37% vs pre-holiday) that could further rise over the next ten weeks. It reports aluminium futures fell 3.5% on the first trading day after CNY, despite some alumina price increases from logistics constraints. | The Trump administration announced additional U.S. Section 232 tariffs on certain downstream (derivative) aluminum imports (10%) and steel derivatives (25%), effective Feb. 8, 2020, with country exemptions. Commerce cites circumvention of existing duties. The move may pressure aluminum supply-demand dynamics and also affect industrial metals sentiment, including copper.

China’s social inventories of primary aluminium rose sharply in the second week after CNY, increasing to 1.09 million mt (up 148,000 mt WoW). Rail arrivals boosted supply while demand stayed subdued amid the ongoing coronavirus outbreak. The inventory build signals near-term pressure on aluminium prices. | President Trump expanded Section 232 tariffs by adding derivative aluminum articles to the existing aluminum tariff schedule, effective Feb. 8, 2020. The new coverage includes certain aluminum stranded wire/cables/plaited bands and vehicle/tractor bumper-body stampings. A new exclusion application process is authorized, but duties also apply retroactively and to some FTZ entries. | AA Metals and International Dominican Aluminium (IDA) formed a joint venture to extrude AA’s aluminum product range at IDA’s Dominican Republic facility. IDA highlights expansion and increased workforce, aiming for significant sales over the next three years, suggesting higher aluminum product throughput and associated material demand. | SMM estimates China’s secondary aluminium operating rates will fall to a record low of 33% in February. Production resumption is delayed by 7–15 days and only limited firms were operating by Feb 10, with slower worker return and weaker downstream demand after the holiday, driven by a coronavirus outbreak.

London LME copper fell as the coronavirus death toll in China continued rising, weakening the near-term demand outlook. The article also shows declines in LME aluminium and nickel. It cites Hubei’s rising cases and notes potential disruption fears for LME’s Hong Kong event, reflecting risk to industrial activity. | Canyon Resources says it expects to launch exploitation of the Minim-Martap bauxite deposit in Northern Cameroon in 2022. The project involves road and rail logistics to move ore to Douala/Kribi for export, targeting about 3 million tons annually then scaling up, potentially improving future aluminium raw-material supply expectations. | Showa Denko (SDK) developed an AI-based image analysis system with BLUE TAG to inspect and quantify spherical alumina sphericity defects. The system will be deployed at an SDK line in April and targets faster, more consistent quality control by matching skilled operators’ judgments and improving inspection accuracy over time.

The European Commission launched an anti-dumping investigation into Chinese aluminium extrusion exports after complaints from EU producers. The article notes China’s sizable shipment volumes to the EU and that duties may be imposed provisionally within about seven to eight months, potentially reducing Chinese availability and supporting aluminium-focused prices.

Antaike forecasts China’s refined copper imports to fall 12.7% in 2020 and says coronavirus logistics and high sulphuric acid inventories have led smelters to cut output, narrowing but still leaving a market surplus. For aluminium, it projects slightly lower consumption alongside new smelting capacity additions, pressuring balances. | Antaike projects China’s refined copper imports will fall 12.7% in 2020, with coronavirus-related logistics and high sulphuric acid inventories forcing recent smelter production cuts. The market is seen in surplus. Aluminium consumption is expected to dip slightly while capacity additions, including Yunnan, raise supply. | SMM reports Chinese reopening and falling new COVID-19 cases calmed markets, lifting Shanghai nonferrous closes mostly higher. SHFE copper and nickel rose less than 0.1%, while aluminium gained about 0.3%. Lingering worries about the epidemic’s economic impact capped further gains in base metals. | Canyon Resources’ Camalco subsidiary released early results expanding its estimates for the Minim-Martap and Ngaoundal bauxite deposits in Cameroon, identifying more bauxite-bearing plateaux and raising total potential. The new estimate includes significant high-grade bauxite for aluminium production, supporting improved supply expectations and boosting sentiment toward aluminium. | EMX executed an option agreement with Pursuit Minerals over three Norwegian nickel-copper-cobalt projects, with EMX receiving a 3% NSR royalty plus advance and milestone payments. The assets are described as historic Ni-Cu-Co sulfide systems, but no near-term production or supply changes are indicated.

Copper rose modestly as China’s reported new coronavirus cases fell for a second straight day, easing concerns about demand from the world’s top metals consumer. The piece also notes updated LME/SHFE prices for aluminium and nickel, reflecting broader risk sentiment rather than a specific new supply or policy shock. | Hambro Forest Products says the value of scrap aluminum paid for California CRV containers has fallen substantially—about 25% since 2018, with recent deliveries dropping from 41 cents/lb to a processor-reported 27 cents/lb. The lower scrap prices threaten recycling-center profitability, prompting proposals for expanded local buy-back services. | SMM says China’s coronavirus outbreak is expanding alumina refinery capacity cutbacks to 4.6 million mt/year and leaving demand-supply imbalance largely unresolved. Logistics recovery is gradual, while smelters face falling alumina inventories. Some producers raise purchase prices and switch sourcing, supporting firmer near-term aluminium sentiment. | Copper prices rose as hopes for additional Chinese stimulus lifted risk sentiment and as new coronavirus cases declined. The outlook for China’s economy and metal demand improved; firms in Hubei received temporary social-insurance relief. The article also notes gains in LME aluminum and nickel alongside copper. | Copper prices rose on hopes of additional Chinese stimulus and signs the virus outbreak is stabilizing, improving sentiment for the world’s top metals consumer. The piece cites policy relief for firms in affected areas and possible financial support. It also notes small concurrent moves in LME aluminium and nickel and mentions copper production/inflow fundamentals. | SMM expects aluminium sheet, plate and strip producers’ operating rates in February to fall by over 10 percentage points versus January, the lowest level for the same month in three years. COVID-19 shutdowns and delayed approvals, travel restrictions, and slow end-user reopenings are cited. | Egypt Aluminum (EGAL) plans to announce a public tender for engineering consultancy specialized in managing aluminum smelter projects. The move supports its seventh line project aimed at increasing primary aluminum production. While it signals future supply expansion, the tender and project timing are not immediate enough for a clear short-term price effect. | Alcoa announced expansion of its Sustana line with EcoSource, a low-carbon smelter-grade alumina brand with a maximum 0.6 t CO2e/t alumina. The company says it outperforms the industry average and cites lower-refinery emissions, which may support demand for compliant alumina feedstock and bolster aluminium sentiment.

SHANGHAI Feb 20 (SMM): China’s social inventories of primary aluminium ingots rose again, increasing 137,000 mt in the week to Feb 20 to 1.23 million mt. The build is attributed to demand not yet recovering amid the coronavirus outbreak, suggesting weaker near-term aluminium fundamentals. | SMM data in China showed 6063 aluminium billet inventories rose for a second consecutive week, increasing by 22,500 mt from Feb 13 to 193,100 mt as of Feb 20. The build was reported across major consumption areas including Foshan, Wuxi, Changzhou, Huzhou and Nanchang. | China will allow one-year tariff exemptions for 25% duties on US aluminium scrap starting March 2, but US scrap will remain subject to the 25% tariff before entering China. Importers are expected to keep using processing trade to avoid duties. Quotas and import restrictions mean volumes should barely change, limiting aluminium price impact. | SMM says Shanghai base metals closed mostly higher, but gains were capped by a stronger US dollar after FOMC minutes signaled steady US rates. China’s PBOC cut LPR to support firms amid the coronavirus impact, supporting sentiment. Copper edged up, nickel fell slightly, and aluminium dropped ~0.3%. | Century Aluminum reported Q4 and full-year 2019 results, noting higher primary aluminium shipments driven by ongoing Hawesville restart and Sebree returning to full production. Net sales declined mainly due to lower LME prices. Management said improved industrial activity and low inventories supported sentiment, but near-term weakness reflected the virus outbreak.

Shanghai base metals mostly fell as renewed coronavirus concerns in South Korea and other Asian economies revived risk aversion. On SHFE, copper dropped about 0.86% to a one-week low, aluminium fell 0.69% to the lowest close since Feb 4, and nickel slid 1.49% on short-position buildup. | Coronavirus measures are hitting China’s aluminum demand hard just as the market expects a post-holiday recovery. Fabricators are delayed in lockdown zones, while smelters keep running, pushing up ShFE stocks. Supply chain disruptions to bauxite and alumina refining add further instability, reinforcing bearish aluminum price pressure and limiting rebounds. | The piece promotes a report on aluminum die casting for automotive parts, arguing EPA/CAFE pressure for lighter cars and rising EV production should boost aluminum die-cast demand. It also flags aluminum price volatility from sanctions/trade wars as a hindrance. Overall, impacts are mixed and not immediate for prices.

SMM data show China’s social inventories of primary aluminium ingots increased by 84,000 mt from Feb 20 to Feb 24, reaching 1.314 million mt. The build occurred across multiple consumption areas, as demand recovered only slowly amid the ongoing coronavirus outbreak, implying weaker near-term tightness. | China imported 100.66 million mt of bauxite in 2019, up 21.9% year-on-year, with Guinea, Australia and Indonesia supplying over 94%.
