LME three-month aluminum hit a record while nickel and copper rose. | LME aluminum prices in trading rose above $3,500 per tonne, setting a new historical maximum. By 9:55 Moscow time, the contract was around $3,520.5. The prior record had been reached on February 24, indicating renewed strong upside momentum. | Russia-Ukraine tensions escalated, lifting crude oil and worsening Europe’s natural gas/energy crisis. Higher energy costs suppressed European aluminium plant supply and supported LME aluminium, which rose over 3% to $3,470/mt. LME inventories destocked, while domestic production resumed but total output remained below last year. | Velocity Ventures bought the former Aluminum Shapes complex for $32 million at auction and received planning approval to resume aluminum production at a Pennsauken (Delair) plant. The renewed operations are expected to occupy only part of the site, with about 200 jobs and remaining space marketed for warehousing. | The report says aluminium is rallying after Rusal halted shipments at a key Ukrainian plant. By disrupting near-term export supply, the move is expected to tighten physical availability and raise market expectations for aluminium prices over the coming days. | Wood Mackenzie/Reuters reports Rusal has halted alumina shipments from its Nikolaev refinery near Mykolaiv after Russia’s invasion and new sanctions. Nikolaev alumina feeds Rusal smelters in Russia, and diversion or failed counterparties could disrupt overseas alumina supplies, worsening aluminium market tightness amid an expected deficit. | The piece reports that Russian metal exports are sliding as sanctions interfere with commodity financing. Reduced access to trade finance and logistics can limit prompt shipments, tightening near-term supply expectations for industrial metals like copper, nickel, and aluminium and supporting bullish price pressure. | Aluminium prices surged to a record on the LME after Western nations expanded sanctions on Russia, raising supply disruption worries. Additional banking restrictions and Rusal halting alumina shipments from its Nikolaev refinery pressured the market. The piece also flags energy and sanctions spillovers that can affect other industrial metals.

Swisstek Aluminium in Sri Lanka received the GREENSL® eco-friendly certification from the Green Building Council of Sri Lanka, highlighting sustainability across raw-material sourcing to end-of-life recycling. The firm also cited a major solar installation that reduced its carbon footprint. The news is corporate/ESG-focused with no clear near-term supply-demand impact. | Russia-Ukraine escalation is flagged as the main driver of short-term aluminium volatility: Russia is a major producer/exporter, while European energy costs and possible Rusal sanctions could tighten supply. However, China’s production resumes gradually, downstream rates recover, and inventories remain in accumulation, with coal-market reform restraining prices. | Century Aluminum’s Nordural subsidiary signed a letter of intent with Ocean GeoLoop to deploy carbon capture at its Grundartangi aluminum plant in Iceland. The project targets meaningful emissions reductions and supports ambitions for carbon-neutral aluminum using renewable-assisted technology. No immediate capacity changes or market disruptions are specified. | Rusal plans to temporarily suspend operations at its Nikolaev alumina refinery in Ukraine because Black Sea logistics are congested. Alumina output will be curtailed, potentially reducing feedstock for smelters. Any alumina diversion or sanctions-related trading disruption could tighten European supplies and support aluminium prices in coming weeks. | Rusal suspended production at its Nikolaev alumina refinery in Ukraine after unavoidable Black Sea logistics and transport congestion. The outage will severely curtail alumina output in the short term, though other smelters are not immediately impacted. Rusal is expected to face more constrained operations amid Russia-Ukraine conflict, tightening aluminium-linked supply expectations. | BNN Bloomberg’s headline says Alcoa’s CEO reaffirmed a commitment to avoid new aluminium smelter projects. By signaling limited near-term capacity additions, the market may expect slower supply growth for aluminium, which can lift aluminium price expectations in the coming days. | AuKing Mining reported final assay results from the Onedin deposit within the Koongie Park copper-zinc project, finding further high-grade copper intervals (up to ~3.28% Cu) alongside zinc, lead, silver and molybdenum. It remains an exploration and metallurgical testwork stage update, without new near-term supply.

Wisconsin Aluminum Foundry (Manitowoc, WI) is expanding by acquiring castings maker DEE in Crookston, Minnesota. The company says DEE uses similar equipment and will help meet rising customer demand for aluminum casting, expecting production growth and job additions. The acquisition supports incremental aluminum demand in the near term. | Rising US/Western sanctions on Russia raise concerns about disrupted shipments from Rusal and other Russian suppliers. Rusal halted production at its Nikolaev alumina refinery due to Black Sea logistics, and analysts warn this could reduce Russian primary aluminium smelting. Shipping risks also threaten global nickel supply, lifting both metals. | The article headline says aluminium is trading near record highs as Rusal falls on Ukraine-related concerns. A decline in output or operational risk at Rusal, a key aluminium producer, would tighten near-term aluminium supply expectations and support firmer prices in the coming days. | AAI says captive power plants for India’s power-intensive aluminium industry face an acute coal crunch, with depleted stocks of 3–4 days and a backlog of over 1,200 coal rakes. It urges PMO to resume uninterrupted coal and allocate 25–30 rakes daily to prevent shutdowns and output cuts. | Aluminium prices in the LME and SHFE rose sharply amid Russia-Ukraine conflict and escalating sanctions. Russia’s temporary alumina refinery shutdown in Nikolaev and suspended alumina exports tightened supply. The piece also highlights Alcoa’s decision not to build new conventional smelters, implying limited incremental aluminium capacity. | LME aluminum prices surpassed the previous record, reaching above $3,550 per tonne and briefly easing to around $3,538/tonne. The new high was set after the prior peak on Feb. 28, signaling strong immediate upward momentum in the aluminium market. | Sanctions on Russia amid the Ukraine invasion disrupted air and sea transport of Russian commodities, tightening global supplies. The article cites that Russia accounts for 6% of global aluminium production and 10% of nickel supply, and notes aluminium and nickel surging to record/near-record prices on expectations of supply outages. | Russia’s invasion of Ukraine is tightening supplies as LME inventories for aluminium and freely available nickel decline sharply, while European smelters cut output due to surging power prices. Shipping disruptions (container/route changes) and sanctions risk threaten exports from Russia, lifting aluminium to a record and nickel to an 11-year high. | Aluminum set a record and nickel hit an 11-year high as Russia’s Ukraine invasion raises fears of supply disruptions from key producers (Rusal, Norilsk Nickel). LME inventories are falling to critical levels amid tight European power-driven curtailments and shipping container/route disruptions, supporting higher premiums and contract gains.

Nickel surged to an 11-year high, topping $12 a pound, as traders anticipate Russia-driven supply disruptions and point to sharply reduced stockpiles. LME nickel rose 3.3% to about $25,925/t. The piece also says LME aluminium inventories are at critical levels, with European smelters curbing output due to high power prices. | Aluminium hit an all-time high as escalating sanctions on Russia and suspended container shipments raised fears of Black Sea supply disruptions, with Maersk stopping operations for Rusal. Nickel surged to an 11-year peak on similar Russia-related supply worries. Copper also rose, reflecting broader disruption-driven base-metals strength. | Aluminium hit an all-time high and nickel an 11-year peak as Western sanctions and shipping disruptions tied to Russia’s Ukraine invasion intensified concerns over Black Sea supply. Maersk suspended operations for Rusal and container lines reduced Russia cargoes as inventories were low, lifting LME/Shanghai price contracts. | LG, SK and POSCO are expanding EV battery recycling. LG Energy Solutions will take a stake in a North America recycler and receive 20,000 tons of nickel annually, while SK and POSCO develop recycling operations to recover nickel and lithium from battery waste. Nickel prices referenced as having surged, highlighting recycling’s growing role. | A new simplified VAT invoice for aluminium scrap became available March 2 (3% tax), but market participants lack details of the full VAT policy and remain cautious. Downstream buyers now require VAT invoices, yet aluminium scrap trading stays thin due to uncertainty, environmental limits on production, and focus on domestic aluminium price direction and costs. | LME aluminium surged to an all-time high as Russia-related sanctions intensified supply fears. Shipping firms suspended cargoes to/from Russia amid already low inventories, with LME stocks dropping sharply. Russia’s role in aluminium production also supports tightness expectations, with spillover risk for nickel ore supplies. | SMM reports improved China spot aluminium trades and stabilized spot discounts (~70 yuan/mt) alongside gains in SHFE and LME aluminium prices. It cites resumed smelter production in Yunnan/Guangxi/Guizhou, rising primary/secondary aluminium offers, and improving downstream consumption, supporting bullish near-term aluminium sentiment despite macro rate uncertainty. | SMM data on March 3 showed China’s domestic aluminium ingot social inventory at 1.12 million mt, up 21,000 mt from last Thursday. Increases in Wuxi and Nanhai were only partly offset by declines elsewhere. Smelters and traders were willing to ship, but high prices and weak consumption recovery restrained buying. Inventory could rise next week. | SMM says China’s domestic aluminium billet inventory fell to 241,500 mt on Mar 3, down 19,200 mt (7.35%) from the previous week, extending three straight weeks of declines. Demand for billets rose as downstream firms preferred them over ingots amid high aluminium and natural-gas prices, while extruders partially resumed production. | The Reuters report says commodity markets extended their bull run, highlighting aluminium hitting record levels. It attributes the strength to Western sanctions against Russia seen affecting aluminium supplies, alongside rising Brent and WTI and wheat strength tied to Black Sea supply curbs. | A DRC court appointed a state mining administrator (Gécamines) to manage Tenke Fungurume, a major copper and cobalt mine. While production was said to continue, uncertainty over reserves and administration timing could worsen delays in cobalt hydroxide deliveries to China, supporting higher cobalt prices and potentially affecting copper expectations.

SMM reports that on March 3 LME aluminium inventories fell 8,650 mt day-on-day. Half of the decline was in Klang (-4,525 mt), with additional decreases in Gwangyang (-2,150 mt) and Singapore (-1,425 mt). Other warehouses, including Johor and Rotterdam, saw smaller declines. | NIO and CHINALCO signed a strategic partnership to cooperate on R&D, supply and procurement of new materials and auto parts, including aluminum and copper, plus supply-chain digitalization and carbon footprint verification. The deal aims to leverage smart-EV aluminum and improve emissions reductions, but provides no immediate volume commitments. | SMM reports SHFE aluminium (2204) rose modestly but failed to match stronger LME gains. Billet inventories fell for three weeks (supportive), while ingot social inventories increased and spot discounts widened, reflecting weaker near-term demand. Operations improved into peak season and production resumed, but overall effects appear mixed. | SMM reports that PV manufacturers are increasingly replacing part of AC copper cabling with AA8030 aluminium alloy cables to achieve cost reductions and comparable performance under standards. It states aluminium alloy materials are 25%-60% of copper cable cost, implying potential demand shift from copper to aluminium in PV cable supply chains. | SMM reports PV manufacturers increasingly replacing parts of AC copper cable with AA8030 aluminium alloy cable to cut costs and maintain performance and safety. It notes aluminium alloy conductors are materially cheaper than copper for equivalent design targets, with comparable losses and load capacity via larger cross-sections. This implies demand shift toward aluminium. | Commodity markets extended gains as disruptions from Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and subsequent sanctions upended raw material flows. The report says aluminium hit new record levels, while crude oil and wheat also rose to multi-year highs, indicating broad bullish pressure tied to supply constraints. | Aluminium futures surged in London toward the biggest weekly gain on record as further sanctions on Russia raised fears of supply disruptions. The piece notes Russia’s shares of global aluminium, nickel and copper production and gas-driven power inputs, alongside tight nickel and rising copper and nickel prices. | SMM says China’s alumina prices keep falling due to production resumptions and new capacity. The domestic price is now close to 800 yuan/mt below overseas levels, and the alumina import window has been closed since Feb 18 with unlikely reopening soon. The spread could widen further, weighing on sentiment.

Rusal’s alumina operations are affected by the Russia-Ukraine war, including a temporary closure of its 1.77 Mt Nikolaev refinery. With alumina markets shifting toward short supply, overseas alumina prices rose 22% in a month. Any further sanctions could tighten supply further and pressure aluminium costs upward. | Escalation of the Russia-Ukraine war and broader sanctions raised supply-chain disruption and energy-cost fears, pushing LME aluminium to new highs. SHFE aluminium rose but less due to faster China smelter restart and weaker downstream consumption. Production resumption lifts supply while destocking is delayed, yet costs and constrained supply underpin prices. | Safe-haven demand rose after Russia’s invasion and sanctions, while logistics disruptions and potential Russia supply isolation spurred sharp commodity moves. Nickel surged 76% to a 15-year peak on supply-chain concerns, and copper and aluminium also briefly hit record highs before reversing later. | Rusal shares surged after reporting that the Biden administration is delaying direct sanctions, citing risk of disrupting global aluminium supplies amid tightness. Alcoa also discussed how it may manage aluminium sales to Russian firms. LME aluminium rose 4.2% to a record above $4,020/mt, reinforcing bullish sentiment for aluminium. | Copper and aluminium prices hit record highs on Monday, with aluminium surpassing $4,000/t and copper reaching a new peak around $10,845/t. The move is attributed to supply fears tied to Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, alongside broader commodity pressure, higher oil prices, and a sharp nickel jump. | Aluminium and copper prices hit record highs as Russia’s invasion of Ukraine sparks supply fears. Aluminium topped $4,000/tonne for the first time at $4,026.50, while copper reached a record $10,845/tonne. The move aligns with broader commodity stress, including a surge in nickel and higher oil and gold.

SMM reports changes in LME aluminum inventory across major warehouses on March 7, highlighting a reduction of 7,675 tons at the Klang warehouse. Falling visible inventory suggests tighter aluminium availability near term, which can support expectations for firmer aluminium prices in the coming days. | Energy prices in Europe have surged, pushing electricity costs higher and worsening losses for aluminum producers there. LME aluminium jumped about 4% to above $4,000/ton, while China’s electrolytic aluminum smelting profits are rising and aluminum rod inventories have been in storage for three straight weeks. | EN+ said it is reviewing Rusal International’s strategy, with options that could include separating and transferring Rusal’s international aluminium-related assets into a new structure. The report notes Rusal’s Ukrainian alumina refinery has stopped shipments, adding near-term supply uncertainty for alumina used in aluminium production and potentially weighing on aluminium prices. | China’s stainless steel futures hit the 12% daily limit to a record as nickel prices surged more than doubled on the LME to over $100,000/tonne. Russia supplies ~10% of global nickel demand, driving fears of supply disruption/export bans and potential short-term squeezes. Nickel also rose 15% on Shanghai. | Nickel surged to around US$20/lb before retreating to $17.32, driven by investor fears that sanctions on Russia could disrupt nickel shipping and tighten an already tight market. The piece also says copper and aluminium rose to all-time highs in Asia and London amid supply-shock risk tied to Russia’s invasion. | The piece argues electrolytic aluminium profits should rise as China relaxes energy-consumption controls and supports orderly dual-carbon policy. It notes Europe’s aluminium producers have reduced output due to higher energy costs, while China’s domestic production is expected to recover by nearly 2 million tons in Q1. | The announcement states that Inner Mongolia Chuangyuan Metal Co., Ltd. is withdrawing 47,000 tons of electrolytic aluminum production capacity, following China’s guidance on nonferrous metals restructuring and capacity replacement via merger/reorganization. The move reduces primary aluminum supply and is expected to be supportive for near-term aluminum prices. | China is considering buying or increasing stakes in Russian energy and commodities firms, including Rusal, via talks with state-owned companies. The goal is to bolster China’s import security amid surging energy and metal prices during the Ukraine war. Discussions are early and not guaranteed, but could support aluminium supply expectations. | SMM reports Shanghai nonferrous metals closed mixed. Nickel surged ~15% and LME nickel nearly doubled on acute supply concerns and sanctions tied to Russia-Ukraine. Copper fell slightly and aluminium dropped more, linked to weaker downstream demand, higher energy/spot prices, and rising inventories, plus cautious trading amid geopolitical uncertainty. | Carmakers face higher costs as nickel prices surge, doubling to record levels after Russia’s invasion. LME suspended nickel trading amid panic and short-covering, with nickel crucial for EV batteries and partly sourced from Russia. The crisis also boosts aluminium costs used in vehicle bodywork, raising supply-chain uncertainty.

SMM says China produced 2.95m mt of aluminium in Feb and smelters increased output as more capacity resumed, led by Yunnan supported by power and government measures. Yunnan alone added/resumed 740,000 mt capacity. March output is forecast near 3.3m mt, while downstream demand recovers seasonally and inventories may fall. | SMM reports China’s aluminium industry generated ~80% YoY higher instant profits in February as costs from thermal coal, electricity, alumina and prebaked anodes stayed elevated. It expects March full costs to remain high, with profitability supported by overseas alumina tightness after Rusal’s plant closure and strong domestic fundamentals. | Article links US/UK bans on Russian energy with higher oil/chemicals, but for metals it highlights LME nickel trading suspension and domestic nickel limit-ups (nickel up strongly). It simultaneously notes Shanghai aluminium dropping >5% and Shanghai copper down ~1% as global bullish mood cooled and funds flowed out from non-ferrous. | LME aluminium reversed gains amid concerns over worsening eastern Europe conflict and heightened sanctions on Russia. The piece cites a US ban on Russian energy imports, noting Russia supplies key aluminium and nickel output and natural gas for power generation. Aluminium stock levels remain tight, while nickel surged sharply before trading was suspended. | Aguila Copper Corp. signed a contract to run an induced polarization (IP) geophysical survey at its Lida copper-silver project in Nevada to identify drill targets following high-grade copper sampling. The release contains copper grades and planned survey timing (Q1/Q2 2022) but no production impacts, so near-term price effects should be limited. | Alcoa says it has completed an energy infrastructure upgrade at its Deschambault, Canada aluminum smelter. The project is intended to boost aluminum production and improve plant stability. Such operational improvements can affect short-term supply expectations and disruption risk for the aluminium market. | SMM data show LME aluminum inventories fell by 8,175 tons on March 9, led by the Klang warehouse. The lower stock level in key LME locations suggests tighter near-term supply and can improve market sentiment for aluminum prices in the coming days.

Aughinish Alumina, a Russian-owned refinery in Ireland, was suspended from the EU Emissions Trading System after EU sanctions were imposed on Russian entities. The Taoiseach said the firm is not sanctioned and operations continue, but the ETS account suspension may complicate emissions allowance trading and raise cost uncertainty for alumina supply. | Two participants set a quarterly aluminum premium for Japanese buyers at $250/ton for Apr-Jun, with Japan using it as the regional benchmark. Prices recently fell sharply (Shanghai and LME), framed as sentiment disturbance. Overseas supply risk from Russia is mentioned, but high premiums and some support remain, while domestic fundamentals may stabilize demand. | Aluminium prices are falling sharply on both SHFE and LME, with futures and spot weakness persisting. The piece cites rising billet conversion margins, continued declines in aluminium scrap, weak downstream consumption, spot discounts widening, high inventories, and accelerating domestic smelter restart and output expectations, weighing on demand. | China’s social inventory of aluminium ingots rose to 1.14 million mt as of March 10, up 22,000 mt week-on-week, led by Wuxi and Nanhai. Spot discounts narrowed as SHFE aluminium prices fell. Smelters and traders accelerated sales on bearish expectations, while downstream buyers stayed sidelined, waiting for lower prices. Inventory may start declining next week. | Rio Tinto will terminate commercial relationships with Russian firms after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. For Mongolia’s Oyu Tolgoi copper-gold mine, the company is exploring alternative fuels but says it cannot fully stop buying from Russia. The piece also notes Russia’s aluminium output crunch tied to alumina feedstock disruptions involving Rusal. | ICRA says low global aluminium inventories and a tight demand-supply balance will aggravate availability worldwide. It flags potential sanctions on Russian aluminium exports to Europe, and highlights power-price-driven smelting cost pressures in Europe. The agency expects aluminium prices to stay elevated until normal supply conditions return. | The UK imposed full asset freezes and travel bans on several Russian oligarchs, including Oleg Deripaska, part owner of the Aughinish Alumina refinery in Limerick. The sanctions could complicate refinery ownership or financing and raise near-term alumina supply uncertainty, pressuring aluminium-linked expectations. | UK sanctions were imposed on Russian oligarch Oleg Deripaska, a major shareholder in En+ Group that owns Rusal, which controls Aughinish Alumina. Irish officials and the union say there are currently no indications of job or operational impact at the Limerick alumina refinery, limiting near-term aluminium supply concerns. | The news (headline) says Rio will halt shipments to a Russian-owned alumina plant in Ireland. Reduced alumina feedstock availability can constrain aluminium production and inventories, tightening near-term supply expectations and typically supporting aluminium prices over the following days to weeks. | SMM reports Shanghai-area aluminium billet inventory at 225,000 mt on March 10, down 16,500 mt (6.83%) from a week earlier. The decline stems mainly from Foshan, Wuxi and Huzhou. Rising conversion margins and downstream buying—after three days of aluminium price falls—plus extruder restart could further reduce inventories. | After LME and SHFE suspended trading of nickel contracts, Shanghai nonferrous metals closed lower across the board. Copper (down 1.11%) and aluminium (down 0.93%) both fell alongside nickel suspension-related risk aversion, despite copper spot premiums improving and aluminium inventory dynamics showing mixed but not enough support to reverse losses. | Tesla raised prices on Model 3 and Model Y Long Range by $1,000 after a rapid nickel rally linked to the Russia-Ukraine conflict. The spike reportedly hit a maximum near $10,700/ton and triggered an LME trading halt. Long Range uses nickel-cobalt-aluminum cells, so nickel demand/supply expectations tightness is directly implicated. | A UK sanctions list has named the billionaire Russian owner of the Co Limerick aluminium plant. Sanctions against key ownership can disrupt plant operations, financing, or commodity trading and shipping. This increases perceived near-term aluminium supply risk, typically pressuring prices higher over coming days to weeks. | Analysts expect Israel may be forced to more openly align with the West and join sanctions on Russia as the Ukraine war deepens. The piece notes existing partial alignment and a dispute involving El Al. If sanctions broaden, market participants may anticipate tighter Russian-related supply and higher metal risk premia. | Tesla increased the starting prices of its Model 3 Long Range and Model Y Long Range by $1,000, citing a spike in nickel costs. These models use nickel-cobalt-aluminum (NCA) battery cathodes, while lower-range variants use LFP chemistry that avoids nickel and cobalt. | Rio Tinto says it is terminating all commercial relationships with Russian businesses and is evaluating its aluminum joint venture with Rusal. Analysts warn the move could curb Rusal-linked supply from Queensland Alumina, tightening the already tight aluminum market and supporting higher prices near term. | Britain imposed asset-freeze and travel bans on Russian oligarch Oleg Deripaska, part owner of Aughinish Alumina in Ireland. Bloomberg reports Rio Tinto may halt bauxite supply and alumina sourcing from Rusal’s Aughinish refinery. With Aughinish the EU’s top alumina producer, supply disruption concerns may weigh on aluminium. | SMM reports that LME aluminium inventories fell by 8,400 tons, with roughly 70% of the reduction coming from the Klang warehouse. The drop signals tighter exchange-available supply, which can improve near-term price sentiment for aluminium as buyers face less readily deliverable metal. | Fastmarkets reports UK secondary aluminium ingot prices at record highs, driven by unprecedented aluminium scrap shortages, surging scrap costs, and high UK energy costs. Tight primary capacity and supply-chain disruption from Russia’s Ukraine invasion are boosting import demand (e.g., from Rotterdam). Market participants describe significant volatility and bullish sentiment.

LME aluminium rebounded after losses as uncertainty over Russian supply increased volatility. Aluminium for three-month delivery rose 2.59% to $3,427.5/t, with Russia contributing ~6% of global supply. Wider Russian supply disruptions and market actions affected other metals, while nickel trading was suspended and copper spot was discounted versus forwards. | The piece argues Lun (LME-linked) aluminium has surged on higher European energy costs, Western Europe production reduction announcements, and restrictions on Russian exports after Rusal payment disruptions. It says Shanghai aluminium is weaker due to softer domestic demand but export profits and active overseas orders should support recovery. | SMM’s March outlook says SHFE aluminium and spot prices trended with intra-month volatility, ending Feb up around 2–3%. Supply rose on production resumption in multiple regions but was partly offset by a Guangxi pandemic-triggered enterprise shutdown. Downstream operating rates improved post–Spring Festival, while social inventories grew more slowly. | Rio Tinto plans to suspend bauxite shipments to Russia-owned Aughinish Alumina refinery in Ireland following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and steps to sever ties with Russian businesses. Bauxite feedstock is exchanged for alumina under an existing agreement. After the news, aluminium prices reportedly rose on the LME. | Aluminium prices were volatile and down about 10% on the week despite a Friday rise, after uncertainty over Russian exports and a broader Ukraine-related supply shock drove earlier record highs. Higher energy/coal costs threaten smelter output, while LME inventories and physical premiums tighten conditions, sustaining volatility. Nickel trading remains disrupted. | Dozens of major firms are exiting or scaling back operations in Russia. In the energy and metals section, Rio Tinto says it is terminating commercial relationships with Russian businesses, including its Queensland Alumina/Rusal aluminium-related joint venture. The move could disrupt aluminium/alumina supply and support higher prices. | Shanghai aluminum trended up as spot sentiment strengthened after suspension of production at Rusal’s Ukrainian alumina plant and tightening sanctions-related expectations, though the article says UK sanctions’ immediate impact is relatively small. Demand rebounded after the price fall, inventories fell, and supply was stable, reinforcing bullish short-term bias. | Aluminium is set for a big weekly fall, down nearly 10% despite a Friday rise, driven by volatility after uncertainty over Russian exports. Russia’s war-related supply shock, tighter inventories, higher premiums, and energy-cost risks for smelters are cited. Copper and nickel dynamics are also referenced via stocks and suspension after a surge.

SMM reports mixed overnight base-metals performance. LME copper fell while SHFE copper rose slightly; it cites tighter Chinese copper stocks and improved premiums but also macro headwinds for copper. LME aluminium gained and SHFE held higher on stronger wire/cable and plate/sheet operating rates. Nickel remains undersupplied but is expected to pull back. | LME aluminium fell 1.1% as diplomatic progress on the Russia-Ukraine conflict eased investor nerves and reduced immediate supply-disruption fears. Despite continued fighting and sanctions against Russia, the benchmark aluminium slide coincided with lower LME copper. The piece also notes Russia’s share of aluminium, nickel, and copper output. | SMM says overseas alumina prices jumped to about $500/mt FOB Western Australia (+$40, +8.7% weekly) with ocean freight rising to ~$80/mt. Export demand keeps the export window open and production resumes after a supply hit. SMM expects China alumina to stabilize around 2,950-3,050 yuan/mt. | LME aluminium fell 1.1% as progress in Russia-Ukraine diplomacy calmed investors’ fears about supply disruptions, despite ongoing fighting. Benchmark copper also dropped 1.6%. The article notes Russia’s significant roles in global aluminium, nickel, and copper supply and sanctions disrupting shipments, plus upcoming Fed rate moves. | The article title says that during the Ukraine invasion, Rusal investors lost US$6 billion in a Hong Kong sell-off, while the aluminium producer reportedly deflects sanctions. If sanctions are circumvented or mitigated, aluminium supply constraints ease, which can weigh on aluminium prices over coming days. | SHFE aluminium fell sharply last week and is expected to stabilise this week as smelter output in Yunnan/Guangxi resumed and downstream demand improved, slowing inventory growth. Despite some pressure from higher domestic supply, tighter supply expectations from the energy crisis should keep LME aluminium supported, implying sideways trading. | SMM data shows LME aluminium warehouse inventories fell by about 3,100 tons on March 14, with Klang inventory continuing to decline. Reduced exchange stocks suggest tighter short-term supply and can support expectations for firmer aluminium pricing over the coming days as the market adjusts to lower availability. | Ivanhoe Mines said it secured options for electricity offtake combining solar power and LNG-derived gas to run its Platreef project in South Africa, including a planned 5 MW solar plant. The move aims to reduce reliance on coal grid electricity. It also cites future power needs for phased operations.

The article says Tesla has bought millions of euros of aluminium from Russian producer Rusal since late 2020 for use in its Berlin plant. It also cites Biden urging suspension of normal trade with Russia, which could make Russian metals prohibitively expensive and disrupt supply, supporting higher aluminium price expectations. | LME aluminum edged higher as Russia-Ukraine tensions and EU sanctions revived concerns about disrupted supply. Gains were limited by weaker China demand sentiment tied to a surge in COVID cases. The piece also notes Russia’s share in global nonferrous supply and mentions nickel contract resumption and other metals moves. | LME aluminum rose 0.7% on lingering supply concerns from the Russia-Ukraine conflict and EU sanctions, while China’s soaring COVID-19 cases and demand worries capped gains. China aluminum production also fell in early 2022. LME copper was up modestly, and nickel trading dynamics were noted. | War in Ukraine disrupted LME nickel trading and triggered emergency margin measures, causing nickel to spike 61% before suspension, with spillover into zinc/lead and a drop in aluminium and tin. It highlights Russian supply risks for nickel (Norilsk) and some exposure for aluminium, while copper was least affected. | The piece highlights that Rusal shares fell sharply in Hong Kong, attributing the move to risks linked to the Ukraine invasion. Since Rusal is a key aluminium supplier, the news points to potential supply/trade disruptions or financial stress that can weigh on aluminium price expectations in the near term. | Rio Tinto offered $2.7 billion to buy a 49% stake in Turquoise Hill Resources, which would give it more control over Mongolia’s Oyu Tolgoi copper mine. The deal is described alongside a government agreement to restart Oyu Tolgoi’s underground expansion. Analysts cite strength in copper prices. | SMM reports bauxite prices are firm amid low domestic mine operating rates and production delays until after the Two Sessions. Alumina refineries hold low bauxite stocks and face difficulty buying feedstock, prompting some purchases of imported bauxite. Despite alumina price pullbacks, bauxite tightness should support aluminium-related costs. | LME announced a 15% daily trading range limit on all base metals and set additional nickel-specific rules, including nickel contract resumption at 08:00 London time on Mar 16 and a minimum ±5% nickel limit. It also delayed nickel deliveries to Mar 23 and tightened large-position reporting and margin/credit oversight. | The piece highlights that aluminium is leading other metals lower as China lockdowns threaten economic activity. Such measures can reduce industrial output and consumption, weakening near-term demand expectations for aluminium and contributing to downside pressure in the aluminium market. | An overnight market wrap links metals moves to improved Russia-Ukraine peace-talk hopes, a firmer rates backdrop, and China coronavirus-linked demand worries. LME copper fell ~2.9% and Shanghai copper/aluminium also eased, while Shanghai nickel reversed—rising about 8.5%—making the overall impact mixed across tracked metals. | European and US aluminium PFA premiums climbed to record highs after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. Fastmarkets cited supply concerns tied to Russian producers, potential export bans, depleting PFA stocks, higher energy and input costs, and freight delays. These factors are lifting aluminium primary foundry alloy premiums and remelt-related premia. | Fastmarkets reports the US Midwest aluminium premium rising toward a possible tipping point near 40 cents per lb. Deal indications for P1020 and reported abnormal spot demand reflect ongoing concerns about aluminium supply from Russia after its invasion of Ukraine. Scrap and LME spot prices eased, but primary premiums remained near record highs.

Oil falls and risk appetite improves as markets react to Russia-Ukraine peace talks and easing inflation expectations. A key focus is nickel: LME trading resumes within 24 hours after a one-week halt, with new rules including daily price limits and position disclosures. China lockdowns and softer demand weigh on commodities. | SMM reports aluminium prices were relatively stable in mid-March, with thinner scrap transaction volumes as recyclers showed limited willingness to ship during renewed COVID controls in China. Pandemic measures disrupted domestic shipments and reduced downstream demand, while spot/quoted scrap prices largely held steady despite prior aluminium declines. | Most base metals rose as traders weighed supply risks and hopes for additional stimulus in China. LME copper and aluminium were up early in the session. Nickel’s LME trading is set to resume after its March 8 suspension following a rapid price spike, potentially capping near-term moves. | Reuters reports Rusal diverted bauxite ore shipments from Guinea to its Ireland refining facility after shelling shut its Nikolaev facility. The end-December level was 296,500 tons. | SHFE aluminium prices slipped early, with weak recent futures performance attributed to slower unloading of domestic ingots and rising downstream demand worries. Despite rapid supply recovery, downstream consumption stayed soft under high prices and pandemic effects, keeping aluminium prices pressured. | SMM says the mid-March upward cycle in SHFE aluminium has ended and prices are correcting. Tight global supply and record-low LME inventories support levels, while pandemic disruptions reduce downstream production, slowing ingot/billet shipments and adding bearish pressure. Domestic smelter profits remain strong; inventories are still only slightly higher. | Chinese firms and officials seek granular guidance to comply with US/EU sanctions on Russia, easing some Washington concerns. It also reports traders shipping 27,216 tonnes of alumina to Moscow-based Rusal, indicating continued procurement of aluminium feedstock despite sanctions pressures. | Kaiser Aluminum will break ground on a $150 million expansion at its Newburgh, Indiana plant, adding a 100,000-square-foot production bay and a new 665-foot coating line. The operation produces about 310,000 metric tons of flat-rolled aluminum annually and serves food and beverage packaging.

SMM market tracking reports broad weakness in China’s non-ferrous complex early March 30: Shanghai nickel fell 2.75% and Shanghai aluminum dropped 1.5% (inner market), while Shanghai copper declined slightly. Overseas trade improves marginally for some contracts, but epidemic/logistics frictions and shifting macro sentiment keep pressure on metals near term. | Overnight SHFE and LME aluminium fell sharply after opening higher, with LME down 4.33% on the day. Easing Russia-Ukraine tensions and macro guidance were bearish. Fundamentals were mixed: new capacity ramped up, transport remained restricted, while LME stocks declined 7,800 mt. Overall sentiment turned bearish for aluminium. | MoHUA officials flagged off India’s first aluminium-body metro train for Maha Metro’s Pune project. The coaches use lighter aluminium car bodies (about 6.5% lighter than stainless steel), with 34 trains and 102 coaches planned. Speakers cite energy efficiency, longer life, and Make-in-India/Atma Nirbhar goals. | Rusal said 2021 earnings rose due to higher LME aluminium prices, but new sanctions on Russia and tighter trade (including an Australian real-time export ban on alumina/alumina ores to Russia) may disrupt operations. Rusal also temporarily closed its Nikolaev alumina refinery, raising supply-risk for aluminium/alumina. | LME aluminium rebounded after a near 5% prior-session drop as optimism around Russia-Ukraine talks faded, reducing a supply-related risk premium. A softer U.S. dollar supported metals, but China demand worries from a COVID surge capped gains. Russia sanctions remain a key supply overhang for aluminium, nickel and copper. | Rusal warned that Russia’s economic isolation could disrupt raw-material supply and markets, citing Australia’s ban on sending alumina to Russia (covering about 20% of Rusal demand) and a shutdown of its Ukrainian alumina plant. Uncertainty remains over Aughinish, with potential investment delays and higher financing costs. | SMM data show LME aluminum inventories fell by 8,750 tons on March 30, with the largest decline in the Klang warehouse. Lower exchange stocks can signal tighter near-term supply and tighter prompt availability, generally supportive for aluminum prices over the following days. | Reuters reports Rusal’s bauxite shipments from Guinea have ground to a halt as the Ukraine war disrupts operations and refinery access. With Australia banning exports to Russia and the Nikolaev alumina refinery shuttered, bauxite is diverted or stockpiled, tightening feed supply and likely boosting aluminium prices. | Reuters reports Rusal’s Guinea bauxite shipments have stopped amid Russia-Ukraine war disruptions and trade backlash. The outage affects bauxite supply to Rusal’s refineries (notably Aughinish) and coincides with Australia banning bauxite/alumina exports to Russia. With feed shortages likely, aluminium output could face near-term constraints. | SMM reports aluminium prices moved slightly (down then up then down) over three days, while aluminium scrap prices stayed largely unchanged because recyclers and deliveries were constrained by local pandemic conditions and invoice/tax policy considerations. Scrap trading volumes fell sharply in Shandong, suggesting disruptions but minimal net price direction. | The article’s headline says billionaire Birla will invest $7.2 billion tied to an aluminium demand bet. Such large, stated demand-focused spending can raise near-term expectations for aluminium consumption and tighten sentiment around supply-demand balance, supporting aluminium prices despite limited specifics in the provided excerpt. | Aluminium prices jumped as markets renewed fears of Russian supply disruptions and the dollar weakened. The story cites Russia’s role in aluminium, copper, and nickel supply, potential sanctions and transport issues, and high European gas prices that have forced smelters to cut output. LME aluminium, copper, and nickel rose. | Ball Corporation and Resource Recycling Systems report that raising U.S. aluminum can recycling to 90% would generate jobs, cut emissions, and keep about 1.3 million tons of aluminum out of landfills. The study also claims higher recycled supply would reduce dependence on primary aluminum imports, supporting aluminum availability. | The article highlights that the US copper premium has reached a historic high, pointing to tighter physical pricing conditions and supportive near-term copper sentiment. It also references Fastmarkets’ aluminium differential/premium assessment corrections and notes periods of disrupted regional supply/shipping, which may influence aluminium differentials.

LME aluminium jumped over 3% as traders re-priced renewed supply risks and a weaker US dollar. The piece links higher prices to sanctions and disrupted routes from major Russian producers of aluminium, copper and nickel, alongside higher energy costs and delayed investment, while noting possible demand uncertainty from China. | SMM reports aluminium prices firming overnight (SHFE 2205 up ~1%, LME aluminium up ~2.8%), supported by rebounding oil and concerns over Russia/Rusal risks. However, domestic fundamentals weaken: alumina prices fall, export ban boosts spot alumina supply, and COVID-driven logistics and downstream disruptions lift social aluminium inventories. | LME aluminium rose over 3% on renewed supply concerns and a weaker US dollar, making prices cheaper for non-US buyers. The piece links the broader move to Russia-Ukraine sanctions disrupting supply routes, and cites Russia’s key role as an aluminium, copper and nickel producer, alongside tight market signals. | SMM reports China’s domestic aluminium billet inventory fell to 157,500 mt on March 31, down 7,900 mt week-on-week. Drops occurred in most major regions as buyers shifted cargoes from more affected areas. Spot billet trading was sluggish, and pandemic-driven effects may halt the decline next week. | SMM data show LME aluminium inventories in major global warehouses fell on March 31, with total holdings down 7,625 tons. Singapore warehouses recorded the sharpest decline. Lower stored metal suggests tighter short-term availability and can provide upward support for aluminium prices in the near term. | China’s domestic aluminium ingot social inventory was 1.04 million mt as of March 31, up 2,000 mt from last Thursday but down 11,000 mt from Monday. Pandemic disruptions affected cargo flows across regions. With no easing in sight, total inventory is expected to drop slightly next week, depending on arrivals and consumption. | A study on the U.S. aluminum industry reports stable employment overall, rising North American aluminum demand (+7.7% YoY in 2021), and investments nearing $1 billion in the past year. It also notes primary production job declines but highlights growth in recycling and certain downstream segments. | Talon Metals says President Biden added nickel (and other battery materials) to Defense Production Act Title III support, enabling funding, infrastructure support, and potential offtake tools. The company expects this to bolster domestic production from its Tamarack nickel-copper-cobalt project, supporting near-term sentiment for nickel (and some copper) supply. | Canadian North Resources announced receipt of a final prospectus, conditional TSX Venture Exchange listing, and net proceeds of ~$2.19M released plus ~$20M raised for exploration/development of its Ferguson Lake project. The project is described as a magmatic massive sulfide zone targeting nickel and copper, but no production impact is stated.

LME aluminium closed lower on the day but posted the biggest quarterly gain since 1988, driven by supply disruptions and higher smelting costs tied to Russia-Ukraine conflict and sanctions. Nickel also surged strongly after recent volatility. The piece adds supportive copper signals, including an export license and weaker Chile output. | SMM says domestic aluminium prices face downward pressure. Costs are stable (energy policy-controlled, alumina weak) while smelter profits are high, lifting operating rates and output. Imports are effectively closed and pandemic-hit demand limits consumption. Despite Europe’s energy crisis supporting LME, SHFE is expected to underperform and inventories may rise. | Copper dropped as China’s factory activity data signaled sharper deterioration, increasing near-term demand concerns. A firmer U.S. dollar further pressured dollar-priced metals. Investors stayed cautious ahead of U.S. jobs data. Other base metals weakened too, with aluminium slightly lower and nickel down on Shanghai trading. | Copper prices fell 1.1% as investors worried China’s weak factory activity and Shanghai COVID lockdowns would curb demand. LME three-month copper retreated to about $10,264/t. The article cites supply/energy and other metal moves, but copper remains the clearest downside read. | Washington officials approved funding to upgrade the idled Alcoa Intalco aluminum smelter, but restart hinges on securing affordable electricity from BPA. BPA says it lacks available power and cannot meet desired reduced rates. Continued delays keep supply constrained, while any potential restart depends on power contracts. | Washington officials secured $10 million for Intalco smelter upgrades, but restart hinges on obtaining affordable electricity from BPA. The agency declined reduced-rate power, prompting ongoing talks and debate over hydropower allocation. Supporters say restarting would restore aluminum supply and “green” output, while utilities oppose a subsidy.

London copper rose for a second session as traders worried about supply from Chile after February output fell 7.5%. Gains were tempered by a firmer U.S. dollar and lingering concerns over Chinese demand, with China holiday/lockdown measures affecting sentiment. LME also plans an independent review into last month’s nickel chaos.

SMM reports overseas alumina FOB Western Australia prices fell 8% to $440/mt on April 6 after Australia’s ban on alumina exports to Russia redirected material into the spot market. The overseas-China price spread narrowed, reducing exporter profits, while domestic alumina stayed mostly stable. | SMM’s March composite PMI for China’s aluminium downstream processing came in at 49.1 (down y/y), reflecting COVID-related logistics and production disruptions, lower-than-expected operating rates, and weaker orders. Several segments remain in contraction, with rising finished-product inventories. Easing is hoped in April, but near-term demand looks soft. | LME data show aluminium inventories fell further after a prior surge, reaching 623,325 mt and a new 16-year low. SHFE inventories in Shanghai also declined for a third straight week, down 1.02% to 305,805 mt. The falling stocks suggest tighter near-term availability for aluminium. | SMM reports Chinese aluminium smelters lifted April prebaked anode bids by 850 yuan/mt, pushing total smelter costs up to 18,317 yuan/mt from last week. Meanwhile aluminium prices fell, cutting profits. While coal prices eased in some regions, cost volatility from alumina, freight rates, and anodes remains high. | SMM reports that LME aluminum inventories fell by 3,450 tons on April 6, marking the largest drop in the Klang warehouse. Lower visible exchange stocks can signal tighter near-term availability and improve market sentiment for aluminium prices. | Oleg Deripaska, a major shareholder in Aughinish Alumina via Rusal, is under consideration for EU sanctions. The EU is also preparing wider measures against Russia, including bans on certain imports and restrictions on banks, vessels, and procurement. Such steps could disrupt Russia-linked alumina/aluminium supply. | The article reports Trump plans 25% tariffs on steel and 10% tariffs on aluminum starting next week, with markets falling sharply on the policy. Aluminum is directly targeted, likely tightening import supply. The broader risk-off tone could weigh on industrial demand, creating offsetting forces for copper. | EU is considering sanctions on Oleg Deripaska, a major shareholder in Aughinish Alumina via Rusal, alongside broader measures targeting Russia’s financial sector and trade. Proposed bans on coal, limits on bank transactions, port access restrictions, and potential further action on fossil-fuel revenues increase supply-chain and risk concerns for industrial metals. | China’s aluminium ingot social inventory rose to 1.05 million mt on Apr 6, up 13,000 mt week-on-week. Growth came mainly from Wuxi after buyers couldn’t collect cargos due to truck shipment hindrances. SMM links the rise to reduced transport efficiency during the pandemic and will monitor effects on prices. | Fastmarkets opened an IOSCO-compliant consultation to review methodologies used for publishing aluminium premiums, including MB-AL-0302 and MB-AL-0300 6063 extrusion billet premiums (DDP terms) and the MB-AL-0316 aluminium P1020A premium. The update adds an Italy FCA premium as part of the audited pricing framework. | Reuters reports Rusal exported its first Guinea bauxite shipment in nearly a month, after disruption from the war in Ukraine. Using shipments via Kamsar port, ore feeds Rusal’s Aughinish refinery in Ireland. With Australia having banned bauxite exports to Russia, any resumed Guinea supply supports aluminium refining availability. | The article links a growing aluminium can “can-demic” to higher demand from craft beer and pet food packaging alongside tighter supply. It notes China cutting production and suppliers raising purchase minimums, leaving smaller firms relying on third parties. Can prices nearly doubled pre-pandemic, suggesting bullish aluminium price pressure.

CNN reports shortages affecting grocery items like canned beer and cat food are linked to an aluminum shortage ahead of peak demand. The article attributes the gap to labor and broader supply-chain disruptions and notes China’s pollution crackdown reducing aluminum manufacturing. Experts expect the issue to persist for months. | Rising European off-season electricity prices are expected to keep some European aluminium capacity offline, with 100k–250k tonnes of smelter production at risk. Higher energy and cash costs could also pressure output. China and Russia may partially offset, but the global supply-demand gap is seen widening. | SMM reports Chinese aluminium smelters were profitable in March, with average full costs easing QoQ amid cheaper coal and power in some regions. It forecasts Q2 cost uncertainty: coal/power costs may fall somewhat, but overall costs likely rise due to energy and petroleum-coke volatility and high anode prices, supporting aluminium. | The Irish government says Aughinish Alumina’s immediate parents Rusal and EN+ are unlikely to face the next EU Russia sanctions. However, EU sanctions on Deripaska raise concerns that counterparties could stop trading. Aughinish converts bauxite to alumina for European aluminium smelters, so disruptions could pressure aluminium supply and prices. | Japanese buyers agreed to pay a $172/tonne premium for aluminum shipments in Q2 (April–June), down 2.8% from the prior quarter. The premium decline reflects weak demand in Japan and China, offsetting concerns about possible supply disruptions in Russia. Premiums are linked to LME spot pricing. | SMM reports LME aluminium inventory levels across major warehouses on April 7. A 100t Kaohsiung warehouse inventory reduction occurred, but was largely offset by decreases in Kaohsiung and Singapore. The resulting headline points to slightly tighter aluminium availability in the near-term warehouse system. | SMM’s survey says East China’s aluminium plate/sheet, strip and foil demand is hit by the severe pandemic, with falling orders and disrupted shipments. As a result, some producers in Henan are cutting operating rates, indicating weaker near-term aluminium consumption and likely bearish price pressure. | Rio is set to take full control of its Rusal alumina partnership in Australia. The move is linked to upstream alumina supply, an input for aluminium smelting, which can affect production expectations. However, the provided text lacks specifics on output, disruptions, or delays, limiting near-term price direction. | Japan agreed a Q2 aluminium premium of $172/t over the LME cash price, down 2.8% and below initial producer offers. Sources cite weak auto demand and ample Asian supply as China exports more aluminium. Russia disruption concerns eased as Rusal kept shipments, though traders may divert supply.

Rio Tinto says it will no longer need to consult its Russian partner, Rusal, on operational decisions at a shared Queensland alumina refinery after taking “step-in” rights tied to sanctions. Rusal keeps its 20% ownership, but loses influence over maintenance and operations, potentially supporting supply confidence for aluminium-linked markets. | Rio Tinto took full control of Queensland Alumina Limited after Australia sanctioned Deripaska and Vekselberg and banned export of bauxite products to Russia. Rio will run 100% of QAL capacity and governance until further notice, aiming for uninterrupted operations. Activists welcomed the move, reducing uncertainty for aluminium-related inputs. | Bunting will open a new aluminum extrusion mill in Pennsylvania, redeveloping a large facility and investing in equipment and workforce via state incentives. The expansion targets construction, automotive, and transportation markets, suggesting incremental aluminum consumption from extrusion production. | SMM said China aluminium social inventory reached 1.07 million mt as of April 7, up 25,000 mt on the week. Wuxi rose most (+36,000 mt) due to pandemic-related transportation constraints limiting warehouse shipments. Other regions were mostly flat or down, including Nanhai (-7,000 mt). | DADA Holdings, Concord Resources and Italy’s Invitalia acquired the business unit of Slim Fusina Rolling in Venice, Italy, including a vertically integrated cast house and rolling mills. The operation has about 75,000 t/year rolling capacity, and production and deliveries were maintained through its prior insolvency process. | Shanghai aluminium prices fell to a three-week low as fresh China lockdowns and expectations of tighter monetary policy raised worries about weaker growth and metals demand. A stronger dollar pressured dollar-priced commodities, pulling LME and ShFE copper lower too, with nickel trading/infrastructure concerns adding sentiment drag. | SMM data shows LME aluminium inventories fell by 4,025 tons on April 8, with the biggest reductions in the Klang and Singapore warehouses. The inventory draw suggests tighter available supply in key shipping regions, which can support aluminium pricing over the following days. | SMM reports China’s March aluminium output at 3.315 million mt with no production cuts and operating rates around 90.8%. Capacity in Yunnan and Guangxi is resuming smoothly, lifting expected April output. Recurring pandemic disruptions are hurting transport and demand, leaving a supply-demand mismatch and rising social inventories through late April. | Michigan’s Capitol visitor center opening was delayed because the project could not source aluminum for curtain wall glass frames. The Capitol Commission expects a late-June opening, with delays linked to broader supply-chain constraints and possible access issues to Russian-origin aluminum, though officials cite unknown factors.

Las Vegas craft breweries face an aluminum can crunch as global supply is disrupted, especially after China lockdowns reduced output of the world’s largest aluminum producer. Can costs are reported nearly 125% higher, forcing bigger orders and warehousing three months ahead, delaying expansion. | SHFE 2205 and LME aluminium are down on the week after Qingming, with the macro backdrop bearish (hawkish Fed, strong USD, volatile energy amid Russia sanctions). Fundamentals cite higher China output and capacity growth, weaker consumption, and rising social inventories, extending pandemic-related supply-demand imbalance. | Overseas alumina prices fell sharply after Australia banned alumina exports to Russia, easing supply tightness as material entered the spot market. Domestic alumina prices edged higher on bauxite shortages in Guizhou and restocking by resuming aluminium production, but new capacity release could push the market toward surplus, keeping a mixed outlook. | SMM reports that LME aluminum stocks declined by 5,900 tons on April 11, reaching the lowest level since November 2005. The piece details stock changes across major global warehouses. Lower visible exchange inventories suggest tighter near-term supply conditions, which can support aluminium prices. | Installed Building Products announced the acquisition of Central Aluminum Supply (and Central Aluminum Supply of North Jersey), a distributor of gutter supplies and accessories used in residential and retrofit construction. The deal adds about $45 million of annual distribution revenue. Potentially modest incremental demand support for aluminum-containing building products. | Taxpayers will fund 44% of a $19.7 million trial aimed at reducing the carbon footprint of Australia’s alumina refining. ARENA will contribute $8.6 million for emissions reductions at Alcoa/Alumina Ltd’s Pinjarra refinery in Western Australia. The move targets refining sustainability rather than immediate output changes. | Oil prices fell about 4% on concerns about weakening Chinese demand and expectations of additional IEA strategic reserve releases. The stronger dollar and macro data focus contributed to risk sentiment. Industrial metals dropped, led by aluminium (-3.64% offshore; SHFE -3.09%) and nickel (-2.37%), while copper also fell (~-1.61%). | Oil prices dropped 4% on worries about falling Chinese demand and plans to release strategic reserves. In line with weaker macro sentiment, industrial metals fell: aluminium dropped ~3–3.6% and nickel fell ~2–2.4% on demand-slowdown fears. Zinc was the only gainer on production cuts and lower European inventories.

Guinea’s interim government asked major bauxite miners to submit by May plans to refine bauxite into alumina domestically, with penalties if deadlines are missed. The country is a leading bauxite supplier, so the move could affect near-term alumina supply expectations and support aluminium-linked sentiment. | SMM reports LME aluminum inventory distribution as of April 12, noting aluminum stocks rose for the first time recently, including a 3,725-ton increase at the Singapore warehouse. The inventory build suggests soft prompt demand or improving supply balance, which is generally bearish for near-term aluminum prices. | Six Southern California firms linked to China Zhongwang were ordered to pay nearly $2 billion in restitution for evading U.S. anti-dumping/countervailing duties on aluminum extrusions by misrepresenting them as finished pallets. The metals sat in warehouses with no actual sales, leading to fraud charges and potential supply disruptions. | BMW will use 100% sustainably produced cast aluminium wheels from 2024, with production powered by green power, and suppliers required to meet these conditions. BMW also expands secondary aluminium use (e.g., 70% in some Mini models), positioning demand toward lower-carbon aluminium supply and potentially supporting aluminium market sentiment. | Six Southern California companies tied to a fugitive Chinese aluminum billionaire were ordered to pay $1.83B in restitution for disguising imported Chinese aluminum extrusions to evade 2011 anti-dumping duties. Aluminum extrusions were reshaped into pallet-like forms and stockpiled at multiple warehouses, mainly via Los Angeles and Long Beach ports. | Six Southern California companies were ordered to pay $1.83 billion in restitution for allegedly evading anti-dumping duties on imported aluminum extrusions from China. Prosecutors said aluminum was disguised to resemble pallets to avoid 2011 duties. The case may reduce duty evasion and tighten effective aluminum supply.

The report argues that since the Russia-Ukraine conflict escalated in Feb 2022, European power costs have surged, pushing aluminium smelters into production cuts and tightening spot supply. It also highlights growing sanctions and shipping worries around Russian producer Rusal, with knock-on effects from alumina logistics disruptions. | The report attributes aluminum price declines to epidemic-related weakness in downstream processing starts and delayed demand, alongside expanding supply and only small inventory buildup. It also cites LME governance/clearing reviews after nickel-market events as adding pressure to Lun aluminum. It expects stabilization only if epidemic conditions improve. | SMM reports alumina prices stable in China but falling overseas; the China–FOB spread narrowed sharply as export profits eroded and exporters turned cautious amid COVID disruptions. Tightness from ore shortages and rising aluminium capacity supports demand, but incoming alumina supply and weaker aluminium prices could push the market toward surplus. | SMM reports SHFE and LME aluminum prices fell in early April as macro conditions worsened (hawkish Fed, stronger USD). Domestically, supply rose with restarted and new smelting capacity and profits, while pandemic disruptions reduced downstream production and logistics, leading to inventory accumulation. Prices may stay pressured unless demand rebounds. | China’s March unwrought copper imports fell 8.8% year-on-year to 504,009 tonnes for the third straight monthly decline, attributed to sluggish manufacturing demand and COVID disruptions. Manufacturing PMI slipped below 50. The article also mentions aluminium exports rising in March, but the copper demand weakness is the key signal. | Nature reports a solid-state electrolysis (SSE) process using molten salts to upgrade aluminium scrap into high-purity aluminium comparable to primary metal, with projected energy consumption less than half of primary production. It frames rising demand for high-grade aluminium (e.g., EVs) and reduced demand for low-grade recycled aluminium. | Research warns that Xinjiang-made primary aluminium may be exposed to expanding US forced-labour enforcement under the UFLPA. Some Xinjiang producers are linked to alleged forced labour programmes. If customs restrictions grow or audits tighten, imports and downstream supply chains could face delays or denials.

SMM reports China exported 594,400 mt of unwrought aluminium and aluminium semis in March, up 34% YoY, driven by policy support, a low domestic price vs high overseas prices (SHFE/LME), weak Southeast Asia processing output, and overseas supply constraints. It forecasts high export levels despite Shanghai port disruptions and higher sea freight. | SMM says Shanghai Aluminum rebounded and remained volatile, closing up 1.42%. Exports are strong and overseas supply tightness is supported by new US sanctions/tariffs on Russian raw aluminum, potentially redirecting flows to Asia. Domestic production resumes and inventories are stable, but optimistic consumption expectations underpin support. | SMM says operating rates for China’s aluminium plate/sheet and strip producers were 79.22% in March, with late-March COVID outbreaks disrupting purchasing, shipping and capital turnover. In April, more firms cut or stop production due to raw-material shortages and weaker downstream demand, with operating rates expected near ~74%. | SMM reports March operating rates for China’s aluminium wire and cable sector rebounded sharply as orders increased and production resumed, aided by grid-related UHV demand. April is expected to rise further. Pandemic disruptions affected some regions, but overall demand remains supportive, with weak exports due to high aluminium prices. | Alcoa has begun restarting its Alumar aluminum smelter in Brazil by electrifying the first 20 electrolytic cells, with full capacity targeted later this year. It also notes Hydro restarting capacity at Albras after power outages. Together, the moves suggest improving aluminum output supply and potential near-term price pressure. | SMM says China domestic aluminium billet inventories fell to 146,400 mt on Apr 14, down 8.02% week-on-week. Destocking accelerated in Foshan as downstream producers resumed restocking after aluminium prices dropped to 20,000 yuan/mt. East China flows remain disrupted by the pandemic, while Nanchang inventories rose on weak demand. | Yinbang Clad Material’s shares rose after Valeo Thermal Systems placed a large order worth about USD314 million for aluminum alloy sheets. Deliveries are planned over three years and prices are linked to aluminum ingot costs. The deal is described as boosting earnings, but it is company-specific rather than a sector-wide shock. | SMM’s daily review shows industrial metals mixed on LME but generally higher in China, led by Shanghai nickel (+2.39%) and Shanghai aluminium (~+1.5%). The piece attributes nickel strength to tighter pure nickel supply amid Shanghai epidemic disruptions and weaker demand, while LME copper is slightly up (~+0.45%). | Alcoa announced a supply agreement to deliver low-carbon EcoLumTM aluminum to Speira and said it expects strong growth in its sustainable product portfolio. The news points to incremental contracted demand for aluminum, which can mildly support nearby aluminum price sentiment, though the contract size is not specified. | Vinacomin is seeking government approval to expand bauxite mining at its Tan Rai and Nhan Co complexes by 23%, which would raise alumina production by 150,000 tons per year. Higher alumina availability could support aluminum smelting output and improve near-term supply expectations, affecting aluminium prices. | EU sanctions on Russian oligarch Oleg Deripaska were reported to raise fears for Aughinish Alumina, but the Irish minister confirmed the Limerick alumina plant faces no direct impact. Aughinish is the EU’s largest alumina producer (used for aluminium), with Deripaska described as only a limited shareholder and not receiving dividends. | SMM reported China’s aluminium ingot social inventory at 1.06 million mt on April 14, down from last Thursday, with some markets rising due to restricted shipments. COVID transport controls reduce logistics efficiency and increase costs, while downstream restocking after price pullbacks and spot premiums in Foshan suggest firmer aluminium sentiment. | SMM’s overnight wrap says oil rose on possible action to ban Russian exports and the dollar strengthened. Despite macro headwinds, the article cites expectations of China stimulus supporting metals demand. It reports Shanghai copper up about 0.3%, nickel up about 3.49%, and aluminium up about 1.73%.

Culpeo Minerals completed induced polarisation surveys at its Quelon iron oxide-copper-gold project in Chile, identifying large geophysical anomalies and stating three zones are compelling drill targets. The company is pursuing earn-in stakes and plans a maiden drilling program. The update is exploratory and does not change near-term copper supply. | LME copper rises 1.7% to a two-week peak as low inventories and China stimulus hopes coincide with supply worries. MMG’s Las Bambas mine plans to suspend operations after a local protest begins. Aluminium stabilizes near $3,214 per tonne on technical support, suggesting near-term resilience. | LME data show aluminum inventories fell again after a prior spike, with last week’s level reaching the lowest in 17 years. SHFE aluminum inventories also decreased 2.46% week-on-week to 299,855 mt, the lowest in two months. The inventory draw suggests tightening supply and price support for aluminum. | Portugal will introduce a contribution from 1 July 2022 on disposable plastic or multi-material food packages and in 2023 on packages made of aluminium or multi-material with aluminium. The charge is paid by producers/importers via a customs/e-DIC process and is meant to be passed to final consumers, potentially weighing on aluminium packaging demand.

London copper edged up as hopes for additional China stimulus offset a firm dollar and growth concerns. A suspension at Peru’s Las Bambas mine added a copper supply-tightness risk. Aluminium and nickel in LME and Shanghai were broadly softer, reflecting uneven sentiment tied to China lockdown-related demand worries. | China’s alumina exports to Russia rose more than 90x in March after sanctions over Ukraine reduced Russia’s traditional supply routes. Shipments to Russia reached 9,949 tonnes, while total alumina exports jumped 180% year-on-year. Australia’s alumina and aluminium-ore ban to Russia is cited as the driver. | China’s primary aluminium imports fell 55.1% year-on-year in March to 39,433 mt, though they rose 115% month-on-month. Q1 imports totaled 96,551.5 mt, down 71% year-on-year. The shift in import flows could influence short-term aluminium supply expectations. | SMM reports early-session SHFE aluminium plate trading around 21,800 yuan/mt with narrowed ranges. Spot ingot transactions in East China stayed slightly discounted, reflecting cautious longs and weaker holder pricing after declines. Demand improved modestly with downstream pandemic recovery, but upstream refined output resumed, adding supply pressure. | SMM reports a deal for 30,000 mt of alumina sold at $371/mt FOB Western Australia, down $7/mt from the prior transaction. The shipment is scheduled for late May and the destination is undisclosed. The pricing drop suggests weaker near-term alumina market sentiment, a key input for aluminium smelting. | Grand View Research says the global aluminum casting market should grow to $135.2B by 2030, citing rising demand for lightweight vehicles and equipment. It expects steady use of aluminum components, despite some impact from electrification. China’s scale in primary aluminum and manufacturing growth underpin demand for casting inputs. | Tight aluminium availability pushed Rotterdam duty-unpaid premiums to an all-time high near $500/t, alongside stable but elevated Midwest US premiums at record levels. LME warehouse stocks fell and energy costs remained high, while sanctions-fear around Russian supply supported demand for physical material. | Hawaiian Airlines will replace plastic water bottles on certain routes with Mananalu’s 16-ounce BPA-free aluminum bottles, aiming to remove about 142,000 plastic bottles annually from transpacific operations. The move increases demand for recycled aluminum packaging but offers limited, localized market impact. | Kaiser Aluminum reported Q1 2022 results with strong demand in general engineering and packaging and improving aerospace recovery, while automotive remained muted. Higher freight and inflationary costs were partially offset by contained-metal surcharges. It also cited lingering supply-chain issues affecting magnesium and timely metal supply at its Warrick facility. | Kaiser Aluminum reported Q1 profit of $8 million (EPS $0.51), up from $5 million a year earlier and matching analysts’ expectations (adjusted EPS $0.66). Revenue rose sharply to $949 million from $324 million. With results inline with estimates, the impact on near-term aluminium price expectations appears limited. | The article headline says Alcoa’s aluminum shipments are declining, a sign that demand is waning. Falling shipments suggest reduced orders and weaker consumption for aluminum in the near term. This points to a looser demand outlook for the aluminium market, likely weighing on aluminium prices over the coming days.

Novelis will invest over $30 million in Germany to add a continuous annealing line at its Plettenberg-Ohle facility, doubling capacity for aluminium used in coffee capsules. The expansion targets growing demand for sustainable, high-recycled-content packaging and supports recycled aluminium supply from its Ohle and Nachterstedt operations. | China imported 120,600 mt of unwrought aluminium alloy in March, up 54.5% YoY and 31.3% MoM. Customs data also shows strong Q1 growth. The report links expectations of aluminium supply shortage to a deepening overseas energy crisis, supporting LME aluminium and lifting ADC12 prices, with fewer new orders. | SMM says China’s aluminium ingot social inventory fell to 1.02 million mt on April 21, down 42,000 mt week over week, helped by higher warehouse shipments and stronger demand in some regions. Poor transport temporarily lifted Wuxi inventories, while improved post-pandemic conditions boosted downstream restocking. | SMM said China’s aluminium billet inventory fell to 119,100 mt as of Apr 21, down 11.37% from a week earlier, with declines across major regions. Logistics and pandemic controls continue to weigh on transport efficiency, while destocking and rigid/seasonal demand support expectations of further inventory reductions into Labour Day.

SMM says operating rates for major aluminium processing enterprises fell 3.3 percentage points to 64.5% as East China’s pandemic situation suppressed demand. Downstream firms cut production, finished-product inventories rose, and transportation disruptions made it harder to source inputs and ship output. Rates could rebound if infections ease. | LME copper is on track for a third weekly decline as China’s COVID lockdowns cloud demand, while LME-approved inventories have risen to the highest since last October. Rising inventories and a stronger U.S. dollar add pressure. Limited support comes from hopes of Chinese stimulus and supply disruptions, but overall bias remains down. | Fastmarkets proposes launching a monthly bauxite CIF China price assessment (from accepted origins including Guinea, Australia, Indonesia, and Brazil) and discontinuing existing FOB bauxite assessments for Guinea and Brazil, subject to market feedback. The change is methodological/benchmarking, not an explicit physical market shock. | Citigroup downgraded Alcoa to neutral from buy, citing aluminium price assumptions (around $2,800 mid-spot versus a long-term $2,500) and a more balanced near-term risk-reward after a large stock rally. The move reflects expectations for aluminium rather than a clear new supply or demand shock. | ArcWest Exploration provided an exploration update for its Oxide Peak Cu-Au project, including planned 2022 drilling of an untested porphyry target and prior geophysical/geochemical results indicating copper anomalies. The release is company/project specific with no immediate changes to mine output, inventories, or pricing mechanisms for copper, nickel, or aluminium.

SMM expects short-term aluminium prices to hold near highs, citing improving downstream consumption, falling social inventories from limited ingot transportation to consumption areas, and active restocking by downstream producers. It also notes overseas smelters are unlikely to resume due to high energy prices, supporting aluminium supply balance despite some operating-rate softness. | SMM says China’s alumina prices edged up domestically, but overseas alumina FOB Western Australia fell to $371/mt as previously Russia-bound material entered the spot market. The China–overseas premium shrank sharply, and about 180,000 mt already entered China; future import-window reopening could add more supply and pressure prices. | SMM reports that alumina prices edged up while auxiliary materials were stable, and smelter full costs rose to 18,255 yuan/mt. Despite some relief from falling thermal coal, the article warns aluminium costs are unlikely to decline due to potential increases in alumina and anode prices, plus higher freight rates. | Industrial metals fell as China’s COVID-19 lockdown restrictions raised fears of weaker demand and economic growth. Copper hit its lowest level in over a month, while expectations for larger Fed rate hikes supported a stronger dollar, pressuring dollar-priced metals. Additional headlines include a negative outlook for copper projects and Peru mine restart. | China imported 11.7 million mt of bauxite in March, a record and mostly from Guinea, as Indonesia’s export ban uncertainty and rainy-season disruptions keep Indonesian supply unstable.
