Chinese alumina refineries shifted toward Guinean ore to maintain production and stockpiles, supporting more stable alumina supply and potentially aluminium output. | SMM reports China’s aluminium operating capacity climbed in Q1 with higher operating rates and resumption of previously shut plants, and expects further capacity growth in Q2 to about 40.94 million mt. It also cites capacity replacements reducing net output less than planned, implying supply support and potential near-term price pressure. | Yunnan Statistics Bureau data cited by SMM show aluminium output rose to 377,000 mt in March, up 43.6% month-on-month. By end-March, around 80% of aluminium capacity in the province had been released, implying increased near-term supply and potential pressure on aluminium prices. | China’s aluminum plate/sheet and strip exports rose in March, but companies report April export orders have slumped month-on-month. They attribute weaker shipments partly to pandemic disruptions and also to improving overseas capacity in Western countries as COVID-19 restrictions ease, reducing dependence on Chinese supply. | Vedanta Aluminium’s Lanjigarh unit in Odisha runs a week-long malaria awareness and prevention campaign for local communities, citing reduced malaria cases from prior efforts. The piece mainly covers health outreach and surveillance/testing improvements, with only background details on Vedanta’s alumina refinery and aluminium smelting operations. | Europe’s aluminium semis market is described as extremely tight due to tariffs and sanctions limiting Russian deliveries, with further EU restrictions (including possible import/payments limits) under consideration. At the same time, China is expanding semi-finished output and improving primary smelting, making it the key swing supplier. Premiums remain high.

London copper rebounded 1.4% after hitting a more-than-two-month low, driven by hopes for additional China economic stimulus and a slight retreat in the U.S. dollar. The piece also notes lingering COVID lockdown worries and broader macro uncertainty, with aluminium and nickel referenced via markets and related company updates. | CME Group said it will launch a COMEX-listed aluminum option contract on May 23, pending regulatory approval. The exchange highlighted higher aluminum futures trading volumes and positioned the new contract as additional risk-management and hedging flexibility. The announcement is about derivatives access, not immediate changes to aluminum fundamentals. | The article reviews copper market drivers: a still-large but narrowing refined deficit, Chinese demand slowing tied to real estate weakness, European consumption pressure from high energy costs, and uncertainty from Russia-related sanctions and financing. It also notes limited copper-aluminium substitution, leaving copper volatility high but directionally mixed. | This podcast episode reviews Alcoa’s earnings call and the company’s weaker stock reaction. Speakers cited rising energy costs, disruptions tied to leaving Russia, shipping challenges, and environmental initiatives toward zero-carbon smelters. The operational and cost pressures discussed are likely to weigh on near-term aluminium supply expectations.

LME copper fell as concerns grew that COVID lockdowns in top consumer China will curb demand, while expectations for large U.S. rate hikes lifted the dollar to multi-year highs. The stronger dollar increased costs for non-dollar buyers. China stimulus hopes limited declines. Global risk-off also pressured industrial metals. | Aluminum prices are reported to be holding near a three-month low as concerns about China’s demand weigh on market sentiment. The implication is softer near-term consumption from China, which can pressure aluminium prices over the coming days as traders reassess demand outlook. | Industrial metals rose as hopes for further Chinese stimulus outweighed COVID-related demand worries and fears from potential aggressive U.S. rate hikes. LME three-month copper edged up and LME aluminium gained. Nickel also rose, with investors weighing China’s monetary support amid lockdown impacts on industrial activity. | SHFE aluminium rebounded after two days of declines, rising 0.51% to 20,765 yuan/mt. Moves were mainly driven by macro sentiment, including a rising US dollar that pressured base metals. While smelter output resumed and social inventories fell, the article expects weak fluctuations because consumption recovery lags. | SMM reports aluminium prices fell sharply over Apr 25-27, reaching around 20,730 yuan/mt. Falling prices suppressed trader shipments and, with weaker downstream alloy consumption, reduced aluminium scrap trading volumes. Even as transport pressure eased in some areas, scrap demand is expected to stay weak due to poor demand and long settlement periods. | Guangxi’s DRC issued guidance on tiered electricity prices for aluminium smelters. It said six named smelters in 2021 were not subject to tiered rates because their average electricity use in aluminium liquid production stayed below 13,700 kWh per metric ton, implying their power cost structure was unchanged.

The EU’s proposed batteries regulation would require battery makers and importers to source bauxite (linked to aluminium) and copper responsibly, if included in the final list of regulated raw materials. A coalition argues the rules should cover bauxite, copper, and iron. Negotiations are ongoing with differing positions in EU institutions. | An EU batteries regulation proposal would require battery importers and manufacturers to ensure responsible sourcing of key raw materials used in batteries. A coalition argues the final list should include bauxite (for aluminum) and copper, alongside iron, citing human-rights and environmental risks in mining and refining. | SMM reports Shanghai-area aluminium billet social inventories at 105,900 mt on Apr 28, down 9.88% from a week earlier. Inventory fell across most major regions as downstream producers restocked ahead of the Labour Day holiday, though sluggish demand in Foshan slowed the decline. Stocks are expected to drop further after the holiday. | CRISIL Research forecasts aluminium prices to average about 25% higher year-on-year, linked to LME contract pricing. Despite recent declines from conflict-era highs, the report expects a supply deficit in 2022 supported by extended cuts and robust demand, with demand and export dynamics limiting downside. | The article says aluminium prices rose to ₹300/kg in March but are likely to ease in the second half of the fiscal due to excess supply. It forecasts average domestic prices around ₹225–235/kg and links these to three-month LME contracts, where March’s average was $3,537/tonne. | CME says it will launch options on aluminium physical futures after successful physical trades in March and April. It cites record aluminium futures volumes and rising client use, positioning CME as an alternative to the LME. The move supports aluminium hedging and liquidity, potentially providing modest near-term upside sentiment. | Century Aluminum reported Q1 2022 results highlighting higher shipments and net sales driven by higher LME aluminum prices. It cited a global ~1 million tonne aluminum supply deficit and the lowest LME inventory levels since 2005, plus restart projects at Mt. Holly and Hawesville, supporting tightening sentiment for aluminum. | Century Aluminum Co reported a sharp Q1 improvement, with earnings rising to $16.6 million from a loss a year earlier and revenue increasing to $753.6 million. Adjusted earnings were $60.7 million ($0.59 per share). The update can affect near-term sentiment on aluminium producers.

Century Aluminum reported Q1 2022 results, highlighting stronger shipments and higher net sales driven by higher LME aluminium prices. The company emphasized a global ~1 million tonne aluminum supply deficit and record-low LME inventories, suggesting tight supply conditions and supportive near-term pricing for aluminium. | SMM reports alumina prices have been flat, with spot buying weak ahead of the Labour Day holiday. Pandemic-related logistics disruptions tightened supply and raised bauxite/auxiliary material and refinery costs. However, new capacity release and a potentially reopening import window for ~150,000 mt imported alumina are bearish. Cost support limits downside. | The piece says Iran is expanding aluminium production, with aluminium ingot output “skyrocketing” over recent years and doubling in two years. It also attributes the growth to more than a 13% surge in bauxite production. Overall, it signals rising alumina/aluminium supply. | Copper rebounded 1.4% after China’s Politburo pledged more economic support and the dollar eased, making copper cheaper for overseas buyers. However, a renewed COVID wave and tighter lockdowns are expected to hurt Chinese factory activity, with copper still headed for a sharp monthly fall. Aluminium and nickel also fell on China demand worries. | The article headline says aluminum orders are stalling because U.S. buyers are increasingly concerned about a looming recession. That signals weakening near-term demand and softer order flow, which usually weighs on industrial metals sentiment and can pressure aluminum prices over the coming days and weeks. | European aluminium premiums are at record highs as Rotterdam infrastructure bottlenecks and staff shortages prevent imports from easing physical tightness. Warehouses lack units for May, shipments are delayed, and swaps are difficult. Logistics issues compound already low stocks, with falling LME inventories and a growing deficit outlook. | Fastmarkets says China’s stimulus sparked a risk-on rebound in LME base metals, lifting copper and other contracts early Friday. Traders also cite ongoing supply tightness in 2022, including mining/logistics challenges and Glencore’s lower copper guidance and force majeure on African copper deliveries. | Hydro Aluminum Metals plans to start operations in Cassopolis, Michigan, bringing a new product line backed by recycling technology aimed at the EV market. The project receives local and state support including grants, tax exemptions, and a property tax abatement, potentially boosting aluminium-related output expectations.

Vow ASA’s unit C.H. Evensen won a nearly EUR 1.3 million contract to deliver an electric preheating furnace for aluminium ingots to a leading, undisclosed producer. The company highlights energy efficiency and emissions reduction. The award suggests continued aluminium production investment, with limited but positive near-term demand signaling. | London copper and aluminum dropped to three-month lows as COVID-19 restrictions in China and expectations of aggressive U.S. rate hikes raised fears of weaker global growth. The Fed meeting and potential half-point hike, along with recent weak Chinese data, intensified demand worries, pressuring LME prices. | London copper and aluminum slid to three-month lows as COVID-19 curbs in China and expectations of aggressive U.S. rate hikes raised fears of weaker global growth and metals demand. Copper fell 1.7% and aluminum 0.9% on LME, with the dollar firmer and macro data also weighing. | A white paper from the Aluminum Association says Commerce AD/CVD duties on common alloy aluminum sheet imports from 16 countries have cut imports sharply and increased U.S. producer shipments by about 24% in 2021. It cites $300 million+ in U.S. plant upgrades and expands capacity, supporting bullish aluminum sentiment. | A white paper from the Aluminum Association says U.S. AD/CVD orders on common alloy aluminum sheet from 16 countries have reduced unfair imports by two-thirds and boosted domestic producer shipments in 2021. It cites investment and cites injury findings from the USITC, suggesting improved supply-demand balance for aluminum.

Copper prices steadied near 4-1/2 month lows as traders weighed rising interest rate risk, China COVID outbreaks and slowing factory activity that could weaken demand. The dollar’s strength further pressures dollar-priced metals. Speculators shifted toward price-fall bets. LME aluminium and nickel also posted declines. | The piece describes the history of Britannia Mines near Vancouver, focusing on copper ore concentration, output, disasters, and later environmental remediation. While copper mining and historical production are emphasized, the article contains no present-day supply, policy, or demand event that would likely affect short-term industrial metal prices.

SMM says China’s eight major markets recorded aluminium ingot social inventories of 1.03 million mt as of May 5, up 46,000 mt week-on-week but down 82,000 mt year-on-year. Holiday arrivals lifted inventories in Gongyi and Nanhai; logistics restrictions eased. Suppliers face selling pressure, while downstream buying stays cautious, weighing on aluminium prices. | SMM reports aluminium futures have fallen sharply on the week amid a likely US rate hike, stronger USD and weaker risk appetite. Downstream operating rates remain low with order declines in auto and real estate. Although inventories dropped, they fell due to delayed arrivals, so higher post-holiday arrivals may raise stocks and pressure prices. | SMM reports domestic alumina prices were flat week-on-week despite easier north-China transport after improved pandemic conditions and some COVID recurrence in Guangxi. Smelters stockpiled on logistics concerns, but weak aluminium prices and adequate long-term supply muted upward momentum. Support may be temporary, while future oversupply and imports loom. | SMM reports aluminium smelters’ full costs eased slightly but profits fell sharply as aluminium prices declined. Thermal coal costs dropped after cancelled import tariffs, while power tariffs were mostly stable short term. However, prebaked anode prices are seen rising in May and freight rates increased, implying higher aluminium costs ahead. | SMM reports aluminium prices lost momentum through April, with SHFE and LME both falling. It attributes weakness to rising domestic supply from capacity restarts, muted downstream demand/PMI and higher inventories due to pandemic logistics, plus bearish sentiment from US rate hikes. Aluminium is expected to stay pressured in May. | China’s domestic aluminium billet inventory rose 22,700 mt to 128,600 mt on May 5 after Labour Day. Large arrivals from Yunnan and stagnant trading boosted stocks, especially in Foshan. Downstream buying stayed cautious due to ongoing consumption disruption/pandemic rumours, creating near-term inventory pressure and price downside risk as buyers wait.

Aluminium futures and LME prices fell over May 5-6. SMM data show social aluminium inventories rising and spot premiums weakening, with domestic downstream procurement described as weak and processing operating rates down. Despite some shipments and faster plant resumption, the net view is that aluminium fundamentals remain pressured and prices may stay rangebound at low. | SMM says major aluminium processing operating rates fell 7.5 percentage points to 57.3% week-on-week, hurt by Labour Day shutdowns and pandemic disruptions. Extrusion and secondary producers saw the sharpest declines, with higher transport costs and inventory build. Rates may improve only as holiday effects fade and pandemic improves. | The article reports a record high for the aluminium industry in 2021, attributed to sustained recovery of demand in Europe. It notes deliveries rising 3.9% while exports fell 15%, implying stronger domestic/contract demand versus weaker outward shipments, which is generally supportive for near-term aluminium pricing sentiment. | SMM reports aluminium prices fell 420 yuan/mt to 20,300 yuan/mt. Corresponding aluminium scrap prices also dropped: extrusion scrap by 200–300 yuan/mt and tense scrap by 100–200 yuan/mt, with some areas like Shandong seeing declines up to 400 yuan/mt. Drops varied by local COVID severity. | Copper, aluminium, nickel and other commodities have fallen as China tightened Covid controls. The lockdowns reduce activity in end-use industries, cutting near-term metal demand and weighing on broader sentiment toward the metals complex. The price declines over the past couple of weeks reflect this demand shock. | Copper, aluminium, nickel and other metals have fallen over the past couple of weeks as China tightened Covid curbs. The lockdowns reduce demand from the world’s largest metal consumer and dampen sentiment toward the metal complex, creating negative price pressure across industrial commodities in the near term. | Shanghai’s Covid-19 controls affected bonded metals flows. Copper bonded stocks kept increasing in April but more slowly as transport improved and import arbitrage turned profitable. Aluminium and nickel bonded stocks declined due to closed or loss-making arbitrage windows and limited inflows/outflows, with weak trading and constrained logistics.

SMM data show China’s April aluminium output rose to 3.298 mln mt and capacity utilization stayed high. Demand was weaker in April due to repeated COVID-related logistics issues, leaving delayed ingot arrivals and a clearer backlog. May output and capacity are expected to rise, but improved transport could lift downstream use and reduce inventories later. | SMM says Linyi (Shandong) had strict pandemic freight controls since April, reducing aluminium scrap supply, but shipments rose after the Ministry of Transport allowed compliant drivers to pass through. Despite eased transport elsewhere, recyclers are cautious for May due to lower prices and demand and higher inflows of non-standard ADC12 secondary aluminium. | China’s customs data show total exports and imports rising year-on-year. Specifically, exports of unwrought aluminium and aluminium semis increased 29.2% in Jan–Apr, while copper ore and concentrates imports rose 4.5% over the same period. These trade-flow shifts can influence short-term supply expectations for aluminium and copper. | China’s April exports of unwrought aluminium and aluminium semis were about 596,900 mt, largely unchanged MoM but up sharply YoY, with COVID-impacted transport limiting further gains. April export profits improved as the SHFE/LME ratio recovered. With logistics improving, May exports are expected to stay up about 35% YoY. | SMM reported China’s aluminium ingot social inventories at 1.03 million mt on May 9, down 3,000 mt from a week earlier and 72,000 mt versus a year earlier. Some regions rose (e.g., Gongyi) on delayed arrivals, while others fell (e.g., Wuxi, Hangzhou) as arrivals slowed. | SMM says SHFE aluminium slid to a four-and-a-half-month low near 19,575 yuan/mt after weak fundamentals. April unwrought aluminium and semi exports rose sharply YoY, while output also increased and consumption remains inhibited by severe COVID conditions. Fed-tightening and recession/sanctions fears further pressured LME base metals. | Brazil’s CBA reported record Q1 EBITDA, attributing the result to higher aluminium prices and premiums. While the piece is largely platform/assessment-related, the key financial linkage highlights recent aluminium price strength, which can bolster expectations for further short-term aluminium pricing.

SMM reports Chinese aluminium smelter costs averaged 17,320 yuan/mt in April, with only a portion of capacity above higher cost thresholds. Despite recent aluminium price declines, smelters remain profitable and may stick to output increase plans. Meanwhile, east China spot discounts persist amid increasing supply and weak demand, keeping prices weak and volatile. | LME data show aluminium inventories have resumed falling after a February spike, reaching 560,275 mt on May 10, the lowest in nearly 17 years. SHFE aluminium inventories ended three weeks of decline but still rose only 1.72% to 295,137 mt as of May 6, remaining low versus recent months. | LME three-month aluminium slid to a five-month low as funds bet slowing demand will weigh on prices. Upcoming China lending and social financing data may confirm demand weakness. Higher U.S. interest rates boosted the dollar, pressuring dollar-priced metals, though tight supply concerns from Europe’s high electricity costs may offer limited support. | SMM reports that SHFE metals broadly rose on May 11, with SHFE nickel up about 3%, SHFE aluminium up 2.61%, and SHFE copper up around 0.3%. LME copper and aluminium also increased, while nickel declined, reflecting mixed global signals amid a weaker dollar and higher crude. | SMM reports SHFE aluminium returning above 20,000 yuan/mt, up 2.61% on the day. It cites LME inventory drops to the lowest levels in nearly 17 years and lower Chinese social inventories. Rising smelter costs also support prices, though SMM says fundamentals haven’t materially improved and risks remain. | Copper rose 1.4% as China’s slowing COVID-19 infections eased near-term demand concerns, though “zero-COVID” restrictions remain. The report notes lockdowns and macro worries had pressured prices recently. It also cites disruptions affecting cobalt, nickel, lithium logistics and shows aluminium up while nickel falls. | Blackwolf Copper and Gold provides an exploration and permitting update for its Niblack (Cu-Au-Ag-Zn VMS) and Hyder-area projects (including Cantoo with Cu/Ag/Zn plus gold-silver). The company anticipates permits and plans multi-phase drilling, potentially expanding resources, which may modestly influence near-term copper expectations. No nickel or aluminium impacts are stated. | Governor Kay Ivey said Novelis will invest $2.5B to build a low-carbon integrated aluminium recycling and rolling plant in Alabama, with an initial 600 kilotonnes of finished goods capacity and 1,000 jobs. Construction is underway and commissioning is expected mid-2025, targeting beverage can demand. | Copper prices rose about 1.4% as China’s COVID-19 infections slowed, easing immediate demand concerns, though partial “zero-COVID” restrictions still suppress activity. The piece notes April vehicle and cathode output weakness from lockdowns and logistics disruption, while LME aluminium also gained and nickel fell on the day. | ALX Resources said it completed six of eight planned holes in its Electra nickel-copper project in Ontario, intersecting sulphide mineralization and detecting nickel, copper and zinc via portable XRF. Additional lab results are pending and the update is exploratory, with no production or definitive resource revision. | Rio Tinto and Utah officials said the Kennecott refinery in Utah will begin producing tellurium, a critical mineral used in solar panels, from materials previously discarded as tailings. The U.S.-based plan targets about 20 tons/year and aims to reduce reliance on China, offering limited near-term impact on copper or aluminium prices.

SHFE aluminium rebounded toward 20,000 yuan/mt, with the LME aluminium contract also rising. The piece attributes the move to improved market activity as enterprises resumed work, narrower spot discounts, stronger downstream purchases, and lower secondary aluminium operating rates, outweighing bearish positioning. | SMM reports aluminium scrap throughput at Linyi’s nonferrous metals city rose week-on-week in early May, helped by improved logistics and a small aluminium price increase. However, falling prices and weak downstream alloy demand reduced recycler activity and trading volumes, despite a May 11 rebound in aluminium and taint prices. | Aluminium Bahrain (ALBA) reported Q1 2022 profit of BD146.8m, up 181% YoY, citing higher LME prices driving revenue (LME up 56% YoY) and low LME inventories (~0.6m MT). It also notes demand recovery and improving supply-demand balance, reinforcing positive near-term aluminium sentiment. | Novelis plans a Bay Minette, Alabama, integrated aluminum recycling and rolling plant, positioning it to serve beverage can and automotive markets. The company expects to start commissioning in mid-2025 and discusses future capacity expansion above 600kt, signaling longer-dated aluminum supply and demand changes rather than immediate pricing pressure. | The piece is mainly about dividend-paying gold stocks and a mild rise in gold after US CPI eased rate-hike fears. It also describes Rio Tinto taking full control of an Australian alumina refinery where Rusal faces sanctions, potentially tightening alumina supply and supporting aluminium prices. | The piece highlights aluminium volatility: energy-price pressures and Russia-Ukraine supply tightness have reduced European smelting output and pushed aluminium can and container costs up sharply. It also warns recession fears could cool demand and slow production, creating uncertainty for near-term aluminium availability and pricing.

SMM says China’s domestic aluminium billet inventories rose to 138,700 mt as of May 12, up 10,100 mt week-on-week (+7.84%). Smelter restarts in Yunnan boosted shipments to Foshan, while high conversion margins weighed on demand. Some areas saw poor consumption and inventory growth, though sentiment improved. | SMM said China’s aluminium ingot social inventory fell to about 1.0 million mt as of May 12, down on the week and sharply vs a year earlier. Shipments were smooth, traders sold more, and inventories dropped in several regions (e.g., Wuxi, Foshan, Hangzhou), reflecting relatively strong demand. | SMM says China’s aluminium plate/sheet and strip producers ran at 76.7% in April as COVID restrictions hurt shipping, purchasing, and downstream consumption. Export orders fell as overseas buyers shifted away. With easing in east China, firms expect production and orders to recover in May-June, potentially tightening near-term sentiment for aluminium. | SMM says alumina prices are flat domestically but have eased overseas, with FOB Western Australia dropping to about $365/mt. With newly added domestic capacity gradually coming on stream, the market increasingly expects a surplus and further price declines, pending large-scale transaction guidance. | SMM expects bearish near-term aluminium price pressure in May. China’s macro indicators deteriorated and the Fed raised rates by 50 bps. Fundamentals are negative: record operating capacity and higher output, closed import window, and downstream disruption from COVID lower consumption. Marginal power costs provide limited support. | SMM forecasts alumina prices will remain supported as import economics improve (FOB Western Australia decline narrows China–abroad spread) and new capacity ramps in gradually. Pandemic-related transport constraints in northern China disrupted supply, while southwest demand rose with resumed aluminium smelter operations, lifting alumina locally. Prices expected to hold higher-cost support. | The piece highlights that aluminium warehouse stocks are at record lows, reflecting persistently tight physical supply. With inventories strained, market participants face higher near-term availability risk, which tends to lift prompt aluminium premiums and support higher aluminium prices over the coming days. | The piece flags that aluminium inventories have reached a record low, suggesting tightened near-term physical availability. This type of inventory squeeze often supports prompt pricing as market participants anticipate less stock to draw from in coming days. | The piece reports that aluminium prices moved higher, attributing the rise to low supply conditions in the market. It frames the move as part of broader base-metal trading after a fear-driven week, suggesting improved short-term supply tightness supported aluminium upward momentum. | The piece highlights that aluminum is rising as the market reads signals of tighter supply at the end of a fear-filled week. Tight supply conditions typically reduce availability and support higher prices over the coming days, keeping near-term sentiment bullish for aluminum.

SMM says operating rates of major aluminium processors increased after Labour Day as pandemic-related logistics improved and downstream end demand started recovering. Extrusion and secondary alloy saw notable gains; wire, cable, and foil also stabilized or rose. Despite weak auto demand in places, expectations are for further rate increases in the coming days. | SMM reports bearish macro signals (high US CPI, strong dollar, mixed PMI) have largely been digested. Aluminium fundamentals show easing pressure: downstream resumes post-holiday, buyers restock after spot lows around 19,640 yuan/mt, and inventories keep declining. An aluminium smelter accident could further tighten near-term supply, supporting a rebound in prices. | SMM reports alumina prices in China remaining high, with the domestic index around 3,016 yuan/mt and a narrowed domestic-versus-FOB WA gap. April imports are expected to stay near 180,000 mt, and sporadic COVID-19 slowed port operations. Smelter restart in Yunnan supports local demand, keeping short-term prices elevated. | USW workers at Arconic/Alcoa facilities in Iowa, Tennessee, Indiana and New York are angry at a proposed four-year contract that they view as cutting real wages by removing a pay-for-performance scheme. Workers authorized a strike (May 12) at Davenport Works, raising risk of aluminum production disruption. | China’s eight major markets reported aluminium ingot social inventories of 984,000 mt on May 16, down on the week and month. Wuxi pickup recovered while Gongyi saw more shipments. Downstream demand is improving, and Yunnan smelter output cuts after an accident helped aluminium prices rebound. | SMM reports aluminium smelters’ profits fell as aluminium prices dropped sharply. Electricity costs in Yunnan eased slightly with the rainy season, while anode prices rose and overall smelter full costs increased. Alumina faces downward pressure from growing supply, but falling aluminium prices dominate near-term sentiment.

Favourable COVID policies and improving Shanghai conditions boosted market confidence. Falling aluminium ingot social inventories in China’s major markets and a Yunnan smelter cutting production after an accident supported sentiment. SHFE aluminium recorded five straight gain days, with expectations of further short-term rebound, though longer-term upside depends on consumption recovery. | SMM reports China’s domestic alumina prices staying flat at 3,014 yuan/mt as negotiations stall. North China supply increased, while buyers sought significantly lower prices, but sellers resisted due to rising costs. With supply and pricing expectations stuck, trading volumes and the regional weighted index showed no day-on-day change. | Emirates Global Aluminium says it has successfully started up DX+ Ultra reduction cell technology at 500kA, a first for the Middle East. Higher amperage is expected to lift production by about 5% versus current operating limits, supporting potline upgrades and future capacity expansion. | LME data shows aluminium inventories declining steadily since mid-February, reaching 532,500 mt on May 16—the lowest in nearly 17 years. SHFE aluminium inventories also fell 16.33% week-on-week to 246,931 mt as of May 13, the lowest in seven months, signaling tighter supply. | China’s April primary aluminium production rose to 3.36 million mt, a record high, as eased power rationing allowed smelters to expand to full capacity by month-end. Domestic operating capacity is expected to rise in May with plans to resume and new capacity coming online, supporting higher supply despite early-2024 output weakness.

Legal issues over land acquisition have been resolved for MIDHANI to set up an aluminium alloy plant at Bodduvaripalem near Nellore with NALCO. The project investment is Rs 2,500–3,000 crore and construction can start. It signals additional aluminium processing demand, mainly a medium-term support factor. | Copper fell 1.1% as markets renewed worries about global slowdown after hawkish Fed signals and weak China data tied to Shanghai COVID lockdown impacts. The stronger dollar pressured dollar-priced metals. LME aluminum and nickel also declined, reflecting broad risk-off sentiment toward industrial metal demand.

The article notes that copper premiums in Asia and Europe have increased, while the US market is flat amid quiet conditions. It also describes delays and corrections to Fastmarkets’ European low-carbon aluminium differential and certain aluminium billet premium assessments due to procedural errors, implying data housekeeping rather than a clear new supply-demand shock. | SHFE aluminium fell for two days amid pessimistic sentiment, weak spot demand, and a rising US dollar. Costs were steady as alumina talks stalled. A smelter reduced output after an accident, helping prices rebound, but demand remains subdued. Prices may rise again if pandemic-linked demand recovers. | SHFE aluminium rebounded about 1% and prices rose above 20,600 yuan/mt as east China spot aluminium ingot inventories fell by 38,000 mt to 965,000 mt and billet stocks declined. The Zhongyuan spot discount narrowed sharply on lower ingot supply and steady downstream acceptance demand, drawing new long positions. | SMM reported domestic aluminium billet inventories at 136,400 mt as of May 19, down 2,300 mt (1.66%) on the week. Regional inventories were mixed, with notable declines in Wuxi and Foshan. Downstream buyers increased purchases after aluminium price and conversion-margin drops, supporting destocking. | EGA reports that one of its aluminium production sites has been recertified to the Aluminium Stewardship Initiative. The update is likely to strengthen sustainability credentials and market access for the aluminium produced, which can modestly support expectations for aluminium demand or pricing, though no direct supply shock is mentioned. | Alcoa signed a long-term renewable power purchase agreement with Greenalia to support the planned 2024 restart of its San Ciprián aluminum smelter in Spain. The deal would supply up to 183 MW for baseload electricity (~45% of needed power at full capacity), helping replace curtailed operations.

China’s primary aluminium net imports fell to 2,842.6 mt in April, down 98.2% year-on-year. April imports declined sharply versus last year, while exports were also lower month-on-month but higher versus a very low base. The reduced net import flow may limit near-term supply. | SMM says major aluminium processing operating rates rose slightly week-on-week to 59.1%, helped by improving COVID conditions and transport in eastern China. However, many sectors remained stable due to insufficient orders and cautious downstream ordering, with some producers expecting limited gains. Auto demand weakness and logistics/frieght pressure persist. | Russia exported about 2.938 million mt of primary aluminium in 2021, mainly to Europe and Asia. With Rusal as a major producer and low-carbon output supported by hydropower, Russia’s export flows are highlighted as influential to global aluminium supply expectations. The piece links broader 2022 trade frictions and tight market sentiment. | SHFE aluminium prices stabilized higher on the day, helped by a week-on-week decline in China social inventories and firmer spot demand. The article also cites Indonesia’s planned 2022 ban on bauxite exports as a catalyst expected to increase alumina costs, though noting the policy is not yet fully implemented and timing is uncertain. | Arconic aluminum workers at several plants express anger over a below-inflation USW contract proposal, arguing raises do not keep up with high inflation and bonuses are inadequate. Workers signal readiness to strike, with heightened protest activity around the Davenport Works plant, raising short-term supply disruption risk for aluminum. | SMM reports that in early Shanghai trading, SHFE nickel rose 5.36%, while SHFE aluminium gained 1.69% and SHFE copper rose 0.66%. The broader move coincided with a firmer US dollar, expanding Fed balance sheet, hawkish Fed comments, and crude oil weakness tied to growth concerns. | SMM reports SHFE 2201 aluminium opened at 20,850 yuan/mt and closed at 20,820 yuan/mt, up 0.94%. The rise is linked to a recent decline in the US dollar index and domestic demand stimulus that encouraged restocking, improving short-term sentiment. | China’s aluminium wheel exports fell in April 2022, down 10% month on month and 15% year on year, per customs data. Demand from the automotive sector stayed weak and pandemic disruptions in east China hurt production and shipments. SMM expects May-June exports to drop further and annual volumes to remain relatively low.

China’s primary aluminium imports fell sharply in April (down 77.6% YoY) while exports surged (up ~640% YoY). Limited European aluminium supply from energy problems and constrained Rusal exports boosted overseas demand. With import windows loss widening and reduced orders from COVID, tighter flows supported repeated LME aluminium record highs. | SMM says SHFE and LME aluminium are likely to rise this week as social inventories in China keep falling and downstream activity recovers slightly. It notes a Yunnan smelter shutdown and restarts, Rusal ingot inflows, and growing concern over a potential Indonesia bauxite export ban that may lift alumina and aluminium costs. | SMM reports average aluminium smelter costs fell slightly to 18,239 yuan/mt as alumina prices eased, while profits rose. Lower power costs from Yunnan’s rainy season helped, but an upcoming electricity-demand peak in east and south China may lift coal/energy prices. Overall, aluminium costs are expected to rise short term. | SMM cites International Aluminium Institute (IAI) data showing global primary aluminium production of 5.599 million tonnes in April, with China accounting for about 3.29 million tonnes. The figures inform short-term supply expectations for aluminium markets, but the excerpt lacks comparison to prior months or demand context. | SMM reports China’s alumina prices slightly down week-on-week, with declines in north China but stability in parts of southwest China. April exports jumped sharply, and continued high exports could reduce domestic surplus and slow further price drops. North supply improved post-transport recovery, while some bauxite tightness raised costs. | Copper prices climbed to a more than two-week high on a weaker U.S. dollar and improving China demand expectations as lockdown easing and mortgage rate cuts were seen supporting property activity. Shanghai copper also rose amid falling SHFE inventories. LME aluminium gained as well, suggesting broader industrial-metals uplift. | SMM reports SHFE aluminium staying elevated around 20,800-21,000 yuan/mt. Aluminium ingot inventories fell to 941,000 mt (down 24,000 mt from May 19), with sharp declines in Wuxi and Gongyi. Spot prices remained discounted but discounts narrowed and then widened modestly as rigid producer buying outpaced weaker downstream demand. | Shanxi alumina prices continued falling, pushing the market further into a buyer’s mode. SMM attributes weakness to export disruptions, including Australia’s ban on alumina exports to Russia, which diverted supply into overseas markets. The article notes falling FOB Western Australia prices and domestic alumina index declines. | China’s April primary aluminium exports fell 24.2% month-on-month to 32,034 mt, with tariff 76011010 exports at 333.8 mt and tariff 76011090 imports at 31,700.3 mt. January–April exports rose sharply year-on-year, but the April drop points to reduced export flows. | LME data cited by SMM shows aluminium inventories have steadily fallen since mid-February, reaching 504,275 mt on May 20—the lowest level in 17 years. SHFE aluminium inventories also declined to 288,801 mt, a two-and-a-half-month low, indicating ongoing destocking that can tighten near-term supply.

SMM cites customs data showing China’s aluminium plate/sheet and strip exports at 329,000 mt in April (+1% MoM, +52% YoY). Despite April growth, SMM surveys indicate export orders fell from April because of pandemic disruptions in east China and softer overseas demand, suggesting May–June exports may be below April. | The piece reports that domestic aluminum demand continues to grow, rising 5.3% in the first quarter. The implied increase in near-term consumption supports expectations of tighter aluminum supply-demand conditions, which typically provides upward price pressure for aluminum in the short run.

SMM reports LME aluminium down over 2% and SHFE aluminium falling to around 20,400 yuan/mt. East China spot remains at a small discount, while central China prices rebound on tighter arrivals and rigid downstream demand, with premiums. Overall, futures weakness dominates the immediate tone. | SMM reports SHFE aluminium down 1.99% after three gains, while inventories continue to fall. China social aluminium inventories and LME stocks hit recent lows, providing support. Supply grew more slowly, despite a Yunnan smelter shutdown, and stable smelter operations persist. Short-term low inventories are expected to underpin prices. | Brazil reduced import duties for steel and aluminium, with aluminium duties cut to 4.8% from 5.4% (for included product types) effective June 1 through year-end. The move aims to curb inflation and broaden Mercosur trade. Lower duties can lift aluminium import competitiveness, pressuring near-term prices. | Shadowbriar Capital Partners’ affiliate acquired controlling ownership of Benada Aluminum Products, a Florida-based aluminum extruder with three extrusion lines and a paint line. The deal terms were not disclosed. The update is about ownership and potential growth, without specific near-term capacity or market impact details.

SMM reports the SHFE most-traded aluminium contract moving with tight early-session fluctuations. LME aluminium fell and spot prices in east China stayed slightly discounted, but in central China aluminium ingot premiums rose. Thin arrivals and a 26,000 mt inventory decline supported rigid downstream demand, keeping premiums stable. | SMM’s survey shows China aluminium ingot social inventories at 936,000 mt, down 29,000 mt WoW, helped by lower arrivals in Wuxi and slower declines elsewhere, with tight transport capacity limiting future inflows. Aluminium billet inventories also fell WoW, though Foshan arrivals stayed high amid weak consumption. | Chinese alumina exports to Russia jumped more than 12-fold in April, with Russia likely to keep buying as sanctions limit alternative raw-material sources. Rusal has lost about 40% of its alumina supply since Ukraine’s invasion, and Australia banned alumina/bauxite exports to Russia, tightening feedstock availability. | SMM reports Shanxi logistics largely recovered, but alumina prices still slid another 5 yuan/mt to 2,890–2,950 yuan/mt due to high inventories and market pessimism. North China remains a buyers’ market; further falls are possible if exports disappoint. In the southwest, alumina is supported by aluminium smelter production resumption. | The headline says Manna Capital Partners plans to broaden access to U.S. aluminum can sheet production and recycling through an alliance with Ball. If capacity or supply access increases, it could ease near-term aluminum supply expectations, but the article body provides no capacity/timeline details, limiting confidence in any price direction. | Workers at Rusal’s Guinea bauxite mine COBAD went on strike, halting production, with Rusal calling the strike illegal. Guinea is the world’s second-largest bauxite producer and a key supplier to China. The disruption could reduce bauxite availability for alumina and aluminum output, pressuring aluminum prices. | Fastmarkets said it will launch a CIF China bauxite price, stop publishing the fob Guinea format, and extend consultation on fob Brazil. It also delayed and corrected several aluminium-related assessment methodologies (differentials and billet premiums) due to procedural errors, updating its pricing database accordingly.

China imported 11.13m tonnes of bauxite in April, with faster demand driven by concerns over Indonesia’s potential bauxite export ban. Buyers increased imports from Guinea, which rose to 5.83m tonnes, while Indonesia’s volumes fell MoM. Chinese alumina refiners seek alternative feedstock to mitigate supply uncertainty. | SMM reports a quiet domestic alumina market with traders taking a wait-and-see approach. In the southwest, aluminium smelter production resumed in Yunnan, encouraging local alumina sellers to keep offers firm. The SMM weighted alumina index was 2,995 yuan/mt, unchanged versus the prior day. | Copper rose after a three-day slump as the dollar weakened, while worries about China’s economic slowdown persisted. Nickel on the Shanghai Futures Exchange climbed up to 5% after three days of declines. Separately, a strike at Rusal’s Guinea bauxite mine raised supply disruption concerns for aluminium-linked markets. | Copper rose after a three-day slump, supported by a weaker U.S. dollar, but the article says copper was still set for weekly losses due to worries about China’s demand amid slowing growth. It also references an aluminium-related Rusal mine strike and low Chinese nickel inventories lifting nickel prices. | SHFE aluminium rose 1.22% to 20,775 yuan/mt as downstream operating rates improved and aluminium ingot social inventory fell across major Chinese markets. Billet inventory also declined. Destocking is supported by smelter output dynamics, though auto demand remains weak and macro pessimism could limit further gains. | Copper rebounded after three sessions of losses as a weaker U.S. dollar and hopes of China easing COVID curbs boosted demand expectations. China’s copper futures also rose. The piece also cites an illegal strike at Rusal’s Guinea bauxite mine, supporting aluminium, while aluminium and nickel prices gained on the day. | Manna Capital Partners plans, with Ball, to develop an advanced aluminium can sheet rolling mill and recycling centre in New Mexico. The project targets up to 1.3 billion pounds annual capacity, with construction starting late next year and production in 2026, supported by state incentives and renewable-energy framing. It could bolster aluminium supply chain sentiment. | Manna Capital Partners and Ball Corporation plan to build a state-of-the-art aluminum can sheet rolling mill in Los Lunas, New Mexico, with construction starting late 2023 and manufacturing in 2026. The project includes a long-term supply agreement, renewable power plans, and up to 1.3 billion pounds annual capacity with high recycled content.

SMM reports China’s stabilising-growth stimulus and accelerating production resumption. Fundamentals show slower domestic aluminium supply growth in May, higher liquid-output mix by some smelters, and continued destocking in Wuxi/Gongyi, with consumption improving. Overall, aluminium prices should gain near-term momentum despite medium-term supply/demand constraints. | SMM says average aluminium smelter cost fell 34 yuan/mt to 18,205 yuan/mt due to lower alumina prices. Spot aluminium averaged 20,800 yuan/mt and smelter profits rose 64 yuan/mt to 2,594 yuan/mt. Electricity tariffs fell in multiple provinces, likely keeping costs easing, though power shortages in peak season are a risk. | High ocean freight rates are keeping shipment costs elevated for China’s bauxite imports. Traders report booking difficulties and shortages of available spot supply. Rising imported bauxite prices squeeze alumina refineries’ margins in Shanxi, Henan and Guizhou, prompting possible output cuts, lifting near-term aluminium supply concerns. | SMM reports China’s aluminium ingot social inventories fell to 923,000 mt as of May 30, down 14,000 mt week-on-week, and 64,000 mt cumulatively since May. Regions like Wuxi and Foshan saw faster declines. With downstream production resuming, inventory is unlikely to rise soon, supporting sentiment for aluminium. | SMM says Shanxi alumina prices fell another 15 yuan/mt to 2,860-2,950 yuan/mt, lowering the weighted alumina index to 2,989 yuan/mt. Flooding in Guangxi did not disrupt local supply, while southwest alumina stayed firm as aluminium smelters resumed production.

LME copper held near a three-week high as China demand optimism rose. Better Chinese factory data and easing COVID-19 lockdowns, including Shanghai reopening, improved near-term consumption expectations. The U.S. dollar’s limited strength provided support, while copper was steady near $9,537/tonne and other metals were mixed to slightly higher. | China is expanding aluminium shipments to Europe amid the Russia-Ukraine war, with exports rising far above imports. The article notes US and European criticism of these shifting trade dynamics. More Chinese aluminium supply into Europe is likely to weigh on short-term aluminium pricing through improved availability and weaker local balance. | China is increasing exports of aluminium to Europe, exploiting the Russia-Ukraine war-driven reconfiguration of supply. The article cites higher VAT rebates for exports and a larger export gap versus imports, alongside April growth in aluminium output after power-production curbs eased. ING warns supply growth may exceed soft demand. | SMM reports SHFE aluminium and spot prices were largely flat in May. It cites slowing growth in domestic aluminium supply, a modest decline in social inventories, and expectations of downstream consumption recovery alongside improved market sentiment from China’s financial-support meeting. Overall, SMM expects aluminium to remain rangebound. | Cordoba Minerals begins feasibility-study technical work and a 40,000-metre infill drilling program at its San Matias copper-gold-silver project in Colombia. The company cites prior PFS economics using US$3.60/lb copper and notes the current spot copper price, but provides no new supply or demand changes that would move prices. Nickel/aluminium not mentioned. | Novelis joined the First Movers Coalition to accelerate decarbonization of the aluminum industry, pledging by 2030 to source 10% near-zero primary aluminum and at least 50% aluminum from recycled inputs (it already has 57%). The move targets clean-energy technologies for lower-carbon aluminum production and recycling.

Vedanta Aluminium launched its Restora brand of low-carbon ‘green’ aluminium in India, claiming much lower GHG intensity via renewable energy and ultra-low carbon products made from recovered dross. The move targets growing demand from climate-conscious manufacturers, but no short-term supply or trade change is mentioned. | A ResearchAndMarkets report projects bauxite demand growth through 2030, citing rising aluminium-linked consumption such as aluminum foil packaging and investment in new foil production capacity. Since bauxite is the key feedstock for alumina and primary aluminium, the outlook is supportive of aluminium demand expectations. | Ball Corporation and Novelis joined the World Economic Forum’s First Movers Coalition for aluminum decarbonization, committing to buy 10% of primary aluminum annually as near-zero carbon by 2030 and to source at least 50% recycled aluminum by 2030. The piece emphasizes circularity and clean energy needs rather than near-term price moves. | The UK-US deal removes Trump-era Section 232 tariffs on UK steel and aluminium entering the US, replacing them with tariff-rate quotas. UK aluminum exporters can ship specified volumes tariff-free each year. Cheaper access for suppliers and improved trade conditions are expected to support the near-term aluminum market.

Rusal sued Rio Tinto in Australian federal court, arguing Rio’s April step-in control of Queensland Alumina Ltd unjustly sidelined Rusal and breached obligations. Rio’s move followed sanctions on Russian oligarchs. Australia previously banned alumina/aluminium ore exports to Russia. | SMM forecasts rangebound aluminium prices this week, with SHFE aluminium expected at 20,000–21,300 yuan/mt and LME at $2,700–2,900/mt. Improved COVID conditions support demand recovery in east China, while tight inventories and a supply slowdown counterbalance a temporary “collateral scandal” shock. | SMM reports alumina prices in China falling week-on-week, with bearish sentiment dominating spot trading. It cites reduced spot buying by aluminium smelters, potential output ramp-ups from new alumina capacity, and expected supply glut. Caustic soda and bauxite costs may limit downside, but near-term pressure remains bearish. | London copper prices climbed to the highest level in over a month as China began relaxing COVID-19 lockdowns and sentiment improved ahead of possible U.S. tariff easing. The piece links the move to expectations of a faster demand recovery, alongside a broader positive tone in industrial metals. | Rusal filed a lawsuit against Rio Tinto seeking to regain its 20% access to alumina output at Queensland Alumina Ltd in Australia. Rio took sole control after sanctions triggered by Russian oligarch links following Ukraine. The case challenges whether conditions for the step-in existed and asks for restoration of Rusal’s rights. | LME data show aluminium inventories kept declining last week to 453,875 mt, the lowest in more than 21 years. SHFE aluminium inventories also fell for four straight weeks, dropping 2.25% to 279,128 mt in the week of June 2. The trend is supportive for futures. | SMM reports alumina prices fell 5-10 yuan/mt, while prebaked anode prices rose, lifting smelter full costs by 84 yuan/mt to 18,289 yuan/mt. Spot aluminium averaged 20,540 yuan/mt, but smelter profit fell 344 yuan/mt. Near term, electricity is stable and alumina could drop on surplus, weighing on aluminium prices. | China’s social aluminium ingot inventories fell to 874,000 mt as of June 6, down 8,000 mt from the previous Thursday, led by a 20,000 mt drop in Wuxi. Warehouse shipments rose to 188,000 mt after a collateral scandal prompted cargo pickup. Inventories may decline more slowly due to upcoming arrivals to Gongyi. | A Rusal subsidiary is suing Rio Tinto, saying it was frozen out of a Queensland alumina joint venture after Australia sanctioned Deripaska. Rio moved to take full control to avoid breaches, while ABC seeks reinstatement and damages. The sanctions process is disrupting alumina supply and governance at Gladstone. | Rusal sued Rio Tinto to restore its 20% alumina production share in a Queensland joint-venture refinery. The claim follows Australia’s sanctions on Russia, including bans on supplying alumina and bauxite to Rusal, after which Rio Tinto assumed 100% capacity and management, potentially tightening alumina—and aluminium—supplies. | SMM reports sluggish alumina transactions and little price movement, despite supply pressures: Hebei Wenfeng output was released in May and Chongqing Wanbo has further capacity ramping. Rising caustic soda prices and high costs erode refinery profits, leading sellers to stick to long-term orders. With weaker smelter demand after the Dragon Boat Festival, alumina prices face downside risk. | SMM reports SHFE aluminium gained over 3% on the first day after the Dragon Boat Festival, reaching the highest level in more than a month. It links the move to improving China downstream activity post-COVID lockdown, higher processing operating rates, stronger export demand, and slight declines in social inventories, though near-term volatility remains. | Vedanta Aluminium says its largest smelter cut GHG emissions intensity by ~12% in FY22 versus FY21 while raising production volume by 20%. The company also expands renewables via a power delivery agreement for 380 MW, launches lower-carbon “green” aluminium, and highlights energy-efficiency gains and climate targets. | Infinity Stone Ventures begins a reconnaissance exploration program at its Zen-Whoberi copper-cobalt-(plus platinum/palladium) project in Quebec. The work aims to confirm historical results, including reported copper intercepts, before potential drill work later in 2022. The release does not suggest immediate production effects for metals markets.

Granite Creek Copper announced it is starting a 2022 Induced Polarization (IP) survey at its Carmacks Cu-Au-Ag project in Yukon, Canada, to improve targeting of deeper and offset mineralization. The release focuses on exploration planning and resource/PEA work; it does not indicate production or supply changes. | Shape Corp partnered with Hydro to bring to the US automotive components using Hydro CIRCAL® recycled aluminum with at least 75% post-consumer scrap. The materials are positioned as lower-carbon and suitable for vehicle lightweighting. Shape also references expanding extrusion capacity in Ohio, supporting ongoing aluminum demand.

LME copper ended lower on June 7, falling 0.47% to $9,699/mt, as a stronger US dollar reinforced concerns about China’s demand. While LME copper inventories dropped by 20,200 mt to a multiweek low, aluminium inventories fell to a 21-year low, yet aluminium futures still slipped. | On June 8, SHFE aluminium fluctuated around 20,800 yuan/mt and slipped below the daily moving average. The east China aluminium ingot premium stayed around 50 yuan/mt, but warehouse-related issues kept transactions weak. Zhongyuan ingot prices were quoted at discounts of 70–80 yuan/mt to SMM A00, with some at larger discounts. | SMM says aluminium scrap throughput at East China Nonferrous Metal City in Linyi fell sharply in late May/early June and again for the week. The drop is linked to reduced supply as aluminium’s price shock triggered wait-and-see sentiment and some upstream traders took a holiday. Monitoring A00 inventories is urged.

Rio Tinto will invest in large-scale wind and solar projects in Queensland to supply electricity for its aluminium operations in the Gladstone region. The company issued a call for proposals to meet energy demand for the Boyne smelter and its Yarwun and Queensland Alumina refineries, replacing coal-fired power. | China’s alumina refineries face higher production costs as bauxite and caustic soda prices jumped in April and northern supply tightened. With alumina prices softening amid oversupply, traders say Henan refineries may trim output, but cuts are uncertain and not yet reflected in prices. Possible aluminium cost/supply effects remain mixed.

SMM data show China aluminium production rose in May and more capacity is expected to resume in June, lifting operating capacity and output. Demand remains modest despite easing transport/pandemic issues, with social inventory falling by less than usual. Billet inventory edges higher, implying near-term price pressure for aluminium. | SMM says China’s metallurgical-grade alumina output rose in May, with new capacity driving higher production and shifting fundamentals from shortage to surplus. It notes net imports were negative and alumina prices corrected. More capacity is expected to ramp in Q3, keeping long-term alumina surplus while potentially easing smelter feed costs. | SMM survey data show aluminium billet producers’ operating rates using aluminium liquid rose to 71.2% in May, aided by better aluminium-liquid availability and improving regional supply. North China disruption is expected to improve. Output and downstream demand are forecast to recover in June, lifting operating rates to ~72%. | SMM reports SHFE aluminium rose then fell to close up 0.36% and highlights May’s year-on-year increase in aluminium output. Despite higher production and some capacity gains, social inventories of aluminium ingots kept declining, supporting prices. Net effect is a tug-of-war, implying continued volatility rather than a clear trend.

SMM expects SHFE/LME aluminium to remain rangebound. ECB policy signals and US inflation concerns weigh on sentiment, while China’s aluminium supply slightly rises after a capacity restart and output increases. A collateral scandal triggered trader selling and wider spot discounts, but low inventories and possible consumption recovery support prices. | SMM reports China’s metallurgical alumina prices declining across provinces and overseas benchmarks stabilizing around $360/mt FOB WA. Output rose in May and net exports turned to a surplus, shifting the market from deficit to oversupply. Profit erosion from bauxite and caustic costs limits dumping, while smelters’ near-term alumina demand is subdued. | SMM reports domestic alumina prices fell again and Guangxi will apply preferential grid tariffs, potentially lowering electricity costs by about 0.1 yuan/kwh. Aluminium smelter weighted average power costs dropped, reducing smelting costs and expanding profits. In the near term, falling alumina amid excess supply supports softer input costs for aluminium. | Iran’s IMIDRO reported that alumina powder production rose 11% to 42,406 tons in the first two months (March 21–May 21) versus about 38,000 tons a year earlier. Higher alumina supply can support aluminium refining capacity and weigh on aluminium prices short term. | LME aluminium slid to a six-month low as investors feared weaker Chinese demand from COVID lockdowns, higher inflation and interest-rate hikes, and a stronger U.S. dollar. Inventories are at low levels, but macro-driven demand concerns outweighed support. Copper and nickel also declined during the sell-off. | SMM reports China’s social aluminium ingot inventories totalled 825,000 mt on June 13, down from last Thursday and end-May, with declines led by Wuxi, Shanghai and Foshan. Some warehouses/outlets are missing in stats after a collateral scandal, and arrivals may slow destocking. | Aluminum prices weakened in May as China’s Shanghai lockdown returned, curbing manufacturing and reducing aluminum imports, while primary output stayed high. At the same time, LME inventories fell and oil/energy prices rose, supporting costs. Overall direction remains unclear amid competing demand and supply signals.

Bauxite mine supply in China tightened sharply, worsening after Indonesia announced a bauxite export ban. Alumina plants in Shanxi, Henan and Guizhou faced shortages and had to buy imported ore at high prices. Rising ore costs and reduced output are expected to keep bauxite prices firm, supporting aluminium via constrained alumina supply. | LME aluminium fell to a six-month low on June 13 amid concern over possible additional China lockdowns and a sell-off linked to rising inflation and interest rates. Demand for industrial metals is expected to weaken. The piece also notes falling LME copper prices, while aluminium inventories are at a multi-year low. | Vietnam’s Ministry of Industry and Trade announced a second review of anti-dumping measures on specified aluminium product imports from China. The review covers several HS-coded items and will reassess the duties’ application, potentially sustaining protection for domestic sellers and influencing aluminium import flows in the near term. | SMM says China’s secondary aluminium alloy plant operating rates rose in May, but prices fell further. Weak auto-related demand, declining die-casting orders, and profit erosion from high scrap and freight costs reduced production and quotes. Import volumes eased as the aluminium price ratio worsened, extending downward pressure. | The Ministry of Industry and Trade announced a second review of anti-dumping measures on specified aluminum product codes imported from China. Initial duties imposed in 2020 and continued after a first review in 2021 remain under assessment, with the investigation citing dumping and harm to the domestic aluminum industry. | American Industrial Partners acquired 100% ownership of the Aluminium Duffel aluminium rolling mill in Belgium from Ecnavla 8 UK Ltd. The deal follows administration and court decisions related to refinancing defaults. AIP expects no operational disruption and plans additional capital, including working capital and investments. | Vietnam’s MoIT announced a second review of anti-dumping duties on specific aluminium product codes imported from China. The original duties (2.49%–35.58%) were imposed after an investigation found dumping and harm to the domestic industry. After the first review, duties were continued, and the second review may keep them in place.

China-wide allegations of financing fraud at aluminium warehouses—showing physical stocks below reported levels and suspected double pledging of the same aluminium stocks—have raised concerns about collateral risk and potential forced unwinding. The article links the emergence of irregularities to recent aluminium price weakness and tighter liquidity conditions. | European aluminium 6063 extrusion billet premiums in Germany, Italy and Spain have fallen as a rise in overseas imports creates “extreme oversupply.” Demand has weakened with poor automotive activity and consumers delaying purchases. With aggressive low offers and ample stocks, traders expect billet premiums to continue trending down. | The article claims Section 232 aluminum tariffs are contributing to high inflation by raising aluminum costs faced by U.S. beverage canmakers that rely on imported and taxed aluminum inputs. It urges Senators Toomey and Warner to repeal the tariffs, arguing this would lower aluminum prices and consumer costs.

Australia faces power shortages after AEMO capped wholesale electricity prices, leading to shutdowns of some coal and gas plants and restricting supply. SMM says aluminium smelters are currently producing normally, but high power costs and spot-market exposure could trigger future production cuts, weighing on aluminium sentiment as LME aluminium falls.

Rumours that the same SHFE-approved aluminium ingot batches were repeatedly pledged as collateral triggered panic to ship out cargoes from warehouses in Foshan and other regions. This boosted spot aluminium scarcity and drove large swings, including Gongyi premiums rising from earlier discounts to ~200–280 yuan/mt after the scandal. | China’s May 2022 prebaked anode output rose to 1.88mn mt, with operating rates at 77%. Pre-baked anode prices increased further in June, supported by a petroleum coke rebound. Aluminum smelters are ramping production, boosting anode demand, though carbon profits face pressure from weaker aluminum prices. | Copper prices edged up as traders priced in strike risk at Chile’s Codelco, the world’s largest copper producer, alongside slightly better China activity data. LME copper rose 0.4% on the day, while aluminium weakened on a firmer dollar and softer China prospects; nickel rose modestly. | The Aluminum Extruders Council urged the Biden Administration to defend CBP’s EAPA findings that Kingtom avoided U.S. aluminum extrusion duties by transshipping Chinese-origin extrusions via the Dominican Republic. The group also seeks CBP action to halt imports tied to forced-labor violations, potentially tightening supply and supporting aluminum prices.

SMM says domestic alumina prices were quiet but slightly down, with spot prices falling on abundant supply after new capacity commissioning and worsened cash flows leading traders/refineries to dump cargoes. Overseas prices rose moderately as smelters increased purchases to keep production stable. Some refinery cuts slowed declines, but an outright rebound depends on costs vs. prices. | SMM reports that a 75 bp US Fed hike and additional rate increases abroad have pressured commodities, including SHFE and LME aluminium. Domestically, supply growth is accelerating, with some smelters turning loss-making and traders shifting/cancelling orders, limiting support despite declining ingot inventories. | SMM says alumina prices in China fell but the decline slowed as overseas buying picked up, tightening supply and lifting FOB Western Australia prices. Domestic pressure eased where cash-flow stress forced some refineries to cut output. Smelters relied on long-term supply, limiting spot demand, but costs remain high. | SMM reports a slight rise in China’s domestic bauxite prices due to tighter bauxite availability for alumina refineries. Guinea’s military government asked bauxite miners to submit alumina-refinery construction timetables, considers a bauxite reference price, and raises export uncertainty, potentially lifting imported bauxite costs and widening refinery cost disparities. | SMM reports alumina costs edged lower while thermal coal was slightly firmer, leaving electricity prices largely unchanged for aluminium smelters. Despite anode prices staying high, smelters’ average costs fell but profits dropped sharply as aluminium spot prices plunged. Raw material costs may fall further, though electricity-driven coal strength could keep costs elevated. | China’s aluminium ingot social inventories in eight major markets were 757,000 mt as of June 20, down 28,000 mt week-on-week and 165,000 mt from end-May. The decline was led by Wuxi and Foshan, aided by cargo transfers and stronger downstream purchases after price pullbacks, with limited arrivals helping inventories fall.

Alcoa marked the 120th anniversary of its Massena, New York aluminum smelter, noting 450 employees and long-term production for transportation, construction, defense, and aerospace. The facility is described as the oldest continuously operating aluminum smelter and recently earned ASI certifications, with an emphasis on ESG performance rather than output changes. | SMM reports China’s primary aluminium ingot imports fell 61% YoY in May while exports surged, leaving small net imports. The SHFE/LME ratio rose in June as domestic prices held up versus LME, narrowing import losses and suggesting the import window may reopen, while weaker overseas orders may reduce semis exports. | Japanese firms are reportedly substituting away from copper as copper prices surge. Daikin plans to cut copper usage by about 60% in 2023, and Panasonic is considering air-conditioner models using aluminium instead of copper for core components. The shift may dampen copper demand and mildly lift aluminium demand. | SMM reports SHFE aluminium falling 0.84% to a six-month low amid bearish macro sentiment after multiple central banks’ rate hikes and recession fears, with expectations of further US hikes. Energy supply concerns in Europe and power price pressure add headwinds. Social inventories declined, but rising supply and weak demand cap rebounds. | SMM reports IAI data showing global primary aluminium production (excluding China) rebounded to 2.21 million tonnes in May. The daily average still slipped to 713,900 tonnes, the lowest in about 1.5 years, signaling supply remained constrained but improved versus the trough.

China’s bauxite imports hit a record high in May, rising sharply year-on-year, with large volumes from Guinea and Indonesia. Indonesia export-ban rumors and Guinea regime instability could restrict supply and lift imported bauxite prices. Higher feedstock costs are expected to pressure alumina costs and support aluminium prices. | SMM reports China’s primary aluminium imports in May 2022 totaled about 37,305 mt, up 6.96% month-on-month but down 61% year-on-year. Imports from January to May fell to 168,700 mt, down 71% YoY, indicating significantly reduced primary aluminium inflows.

The article discusses aluminium foil market conditions in Europe, noting first-quarter production fell marginally to 244,700 tonnes. It highlights strong demand within Europe but a sharp export slump, with shipments down by almost a third, suggesting weaker external sales that could pressure aluminium market pricing in the near term. | SMM reports China aluminium ingot inventories fell to 751,000 mt by June 23, continuing declines in Wuxi and Foshan due to dip buying and fewer arrivals, though Gongyi saw accumulation. Aluminium billet inventories also dropped to 111,600 mt. Destocking and improved restocking are expected to extend into next week, supporting prices. | Century Aluminum plans to idle its Hawesville smelter (as stated in the headline). Smelter idling reduces primary aluminium production in the near term, altering supply expectations. The impact on prices depends on whether the outage is supply-driven or reflects weaker demand; the headline implies a net negative near-term effect. | Global markets declined as investors feared further interest-rate hikes to fight decades-high inflation could push economies into recession. Germany’s and France’s recent Purchasing Managers’ Index data showed weaker activity, reinforcing slowdown concerns. This demand outlook weighed on risk assets and industrial commodities such as copper and likely other metals. | Century Aluminum’s Hawesville plant is scheduled to stop operations for roughly a year, starting in August, citing high global energy costs. The shutdown is expected to trigger 628 temporary layoffs (about 500 union members) at the Hawesville smelter. Reduced output may support aluminium prices. | Century will begin idling its Hawesville, Kentucky aluminium smelter on June 27 because US power costs have more than tripled. The closure is expected to reduce US Midwest primary supply, potentially reversing some declines in aluminium premiums. Aluminium prices and US premiums have already fallen sharply amid global sell-off.

Century Aluminum will shut down its Hawesville, Kentucky smelter, cutting over 600 jobs. The company cites energy costs nearly tripling due to the war in Ukraine. The closure is expected to last 9–12 months and comes after prior temporary shutdowns blamed on Chinese overcapacity, implying reduced near-term aluminum supply. | SMM reports sharp declines in SHFE and LME aluminium prices, citing US rate-hike expectations and weaker manufacturing data as bearish for demand. Despite lower Chinese aluminium ingot and billet inventories, spot transactions stay weak. Rising domestic operating capacity and overall macro tightening are expected to keep aluminium prices pressured in the near term. | SMM reports China’s aluminium ingot social inventories fell to 751,000 mt by June 23, down 172,000 mt in a month, driven by sharply lower imports, increased exports of ingots and semis, more smelting output as aluminium liquid, and dip buying by downstream users. Smelters also reduced warehouse shipments after a collateral scandal, aiding destocking. | SMM’s survey finds aluminium plate/sheet and strip demand remains subdued. Many Chinese producers report new orders falling by more than 50% year-on-year, while both domestic sales and foreign trade are sluggish. This indicates weaker near-term aluminium consumption and potential downward pressure on prices. | Century Aluminum plans to temporarily halt its Hawesville, Kentucky smelter for nine to 12 months starting in August, laying off about 600 workers. The company cites dramatically higher power costs. The Hawesville plant is its largest U.S. smelter and a major high-purity primary aluminum producer.

SHFE aluminium fell 4.22% on the week and LME was also down, reflecting bearish macro pressure from Fed/ECB rate-hike expectations. Fundamentals are soft: domestic aluminium capacity is set to rise, but processing sectors receive limited orders and July-August is an off-season. Inventories are low, limiting downside. | SMM reports alumina prices roughly flat week-on-week, with most Chinese regions holding within set ranges. Some Shanxi/Henan losses from higher caustic soda and imported bauxite prompted production cuts, while ore-related outages are gradually resuming elsewhere. Tight balance from stable exports and returning aluminium output underpins current prices, despite long-run surplus risk. | SMM reports aluminium smelters’ profits shrinking sharply as spot aluminium prices fell to a new year low (19,160 yuan/mt as of June 24). Despite a drop in smelting costs, bearish aluminium market factors and potentially sticky coal costs near peak power demand could push aluminium prices lower and risk losses. | ALRO’s Vimetco Extrusion installed and tested a new automated extrusion press line after a ~€10m investment. The 7” line aims to produce higher value aluminium profiles with lower energy and gas use, adding 11,000 tonnes of annual capacity to reach 35,000 tonnes by end-2022. | China’s eight major markets recorded aluminium ingot social inventories of 746,000 mt as of June 27, down slightly on the week but sharply less than end-May. Destocking slowed materially. Wuxi outflows fell as collateral-scan-dal effects eased, while Gongyi inventory rose on arrivals and weak downstream buying. | Codelco says Chile’s tightening environmental and water regulations will force strategy changes, including recirculating water, cutting emissions, shifting to renewables, and previously closing its Ventanas smelter. The drought is still affecting output, while permitting delays and potential glacier rules add uncertainty for future projects. | ALRO’s downstream unit Vimetco Extrusion has completed a new automatic press extrusion line for aluminium profiles, following nearly EUR10m investment since March 2021. The added extrusion capacity may increase near-term demand for aluminium billets/feedstock, supporting expectations for aluminium consumption in the period.

SMM reports SHFE aluminium moving in a broad range amid recession worries but notes cost support and supply tightening. Alcoa’s Hawesville smelter and Century’s Kentucky plant closure were cited, alongside falling LME and domestic inventories. The piece expects SHFE aluminium to trade between 18,900-19,600 yuan/mt near term. | China’s policy briefings and additional measures to stabilize the economy are portrayed as turning the macro outlook more optimistic, lifting non-ferrous prices. SHFE aluminium is reported up 2.34% and spot A00 prices higher, with firmer seller pricing/cost support and some rebound after shorts covered. | Western Copper and Gold reported positive feasibility results for its Casino copper-gold-molybdenum project, including project economics that depend on assumed base-case copper prices. The article details processing design, recovery improvements, and inflation-driven cost increases, but it does not indicate imminent production or supply disruptions that would move metal prices soon.

Romanian alumina producer Alum Tulcea plans to suspend production starting in August, according to its activity plan. Because alumina is a key feedstock for aluminium smelting, the shutdown raises concerns about tighter supply of refining inputs, which can pressure expectations for near-term aluminium prices.

LME copper stayed near a 16-month low as investors weighed hawkish central bank signals and potential rapid rate hikes that could hurt growth and demand. LME nickel futures rose after UK sanctions involving Norilsk Nickel’s major shareholder. Aluminium premiums for July–September were agreed lower, suggesting softer near-term aluminium pricing. | Rising European energy costs, intensified by geopolitical tensions and strikes (including at TotalEnergies), are squeezing aluminium smelter margins and raising expectations of further curtailments. Traders say losses exceed profitability thresholds, limiting supply and supporting Rotterdam aluminium premiums despite declines in the LME aluminium cash price.

China Molybdenum plans to invest over $1.8 billion in the Kisanfu/KFM copper-cobalt project in the DRC, including spending on engineering and construction. The mine is expected to supply about 90,000 tons of cathode copper annually after full output, starting production in the first half of next year. | SMM reports a surge in newly commissioned Chinese alumina capacity, pushing alumina prices into a downward trend since May 16. Inland, high-cost refineries in Shanxi and Henan face bauxite shortages and reduced profits, cutting operating rates or shutting furnaces.
