Falling alumina and ongoing supply tightness may ease smelter costs. | SMM reports that an aluminium rally proved temporary: SHFE aluminium fell 1.42% after improving but still mixed macro data. Weak end demand ahead of the off-season and slower destocking coincide with higher domestic operating capacity (up to ~41mn mt) and only modest inventory declines, weighing on prices. | London copper dropped as much as 3.6% to around $7,959/ton, extending a bear market and marking the lowest level since early 2021. It fell nearly 20% in the quarter amid recession and growth fears, with similar weakness cited for aluminium and nickel.

The piece describes intensified Western Australia oversight of Alcoa’s bauxite mining, including an unprecedented $55 million forest-clearing fine and ongoing investigations over approvals and exemption conditions. It also notes regulator actions that could add stricter constraints, with knock-on concern for alumina output, affecting aluminium supply expectations. | Alcoa said it will begin immediately curtailing one of three smelting lines at its Warrick, Indiana aluminium smelter because of operational challenges. Each line is about 54,000 mt annual capacity. Alcoa plans to protect output from the remaining two lines while reducing total capacity safely. | SMM reports alumina prices are largely stable week-on-week as high-cost refineries in north China cut output due to bauxite shortages and shrinking margins. Regional prices are steady and overseas alumina transactions fell, suggesting falling CIF levels. Aluminium smelters find prices acceptable, so near-term aluminium impact looks limited. | SMM says China’s eight major markets’ aluminium ingot social inventories were 734,000 mt on July 4, flat from last Thursday, indicating the destocking cycle has paused. Foshan and Gongyi rose (Gongyi above 100,000 mt for four straight weeks). Spot trading softened as the off-season nears, and inventories may edge higher. | SHFE aluminium fell below 19,000 yuan/mt amid bearish macro signals from Powell and expected ECB rate hikes. Meanwhile China’s aluminium supply expanded with new capacity coming online. Downstream demand weakened (construction, auto, and wire/cable orders), inventories destocking slowed, and the market risk shifts toward surplus, pressuring prices further. | A StrategyR/GIA market study projects the global aluminum composite panels market to grow to about $10B by 2026 from $7.1B in 2020, with steady growth by segment and region. While it suggests rising end-demand for aluminium inputs, it contains no short-term supply or pricing triggers. | CMOC says its KFM copper-cobalt mine in the DRC will start producing in 1H23, targeting average output of about 90,000 tpy copper metal (and 30,000 tpy cobalt) at full production. The company argues the additional cobalt supply helps EV demand, but near-term price effects are limited. | Japan-based aluminium buyers are set to pay lower premiums for July–September imports. Falling import premiums generally indicate less tightness in the physical market and/or softer demand conditions, which can weigh on aluminium price expectations in the near term by improving availability and reducing buyer urgency.

SMM reports SHFE aluminium up slightly but under mounting pressure as bearish macro concerns suppress demand ahead of the traditional off-season. Aluminium processing rates tick up while social inventories stop falling and may accumulate. Alumina refinery cuts due to bauxite shortage and higher petroleum coke costs provide some cost support. | The title says Russian tycoon Potanin plans to begin merger talks between Nornickel and Rusal. Nornickel is closely tied to nickel output and Rusal to aluminium. While deal talks can affect market expectations for supply and corporate risk, the snippet lacks details, limiting clear direction in the near term. | The piece says Russian tycoon Potanin plans to begin merger talks between Nornickel and Rusal. These companies are major producers of nickel and aluminium, respectively, so consolidation could affect expectations for supply. But without details on timing or capacity changes, the short-term price direction is unclear. | Vladimir Potanin, largest shareholder in Nornickel, said he is ready to discuss a potential merger with aluminium producer Rusal. While Nornickel has not been directly targeted by sanctions, Britain recently sanctioned Potanin, and Potanin cited diversification and possible state support as motives for talks, Reuters reported. | Police in Durban raided two illegal scrapyards and recovered 682kg of stolen copper, arresting three suspects. The operation involved Transnet, Prasa and Telkom and also found aluminium cables and lids. Authorities said further arrests would continue to stop tampering with infrastructure in eThekwini. | Vladimir Potanin, largest shareholder in Nornickel, said he is ready to discuss merging Nornickel with aluminium producer Rusal. The idea is driven by shareholder diversification to withstand sanctions; Britain recently sanctioned Potanin. The proposal comes from Rusal’s management, with no immediate response from Rusal. | SMM says non-ferrous metals are under pressure from recession fears after Fed/ECB rate hikes. For aluminium, overseas demand (real estate/auto) is weak, while domestic output is set to rise quickly with new capacity, likely leading to stock accumulation in Q4. Overseas supply may be vulnerable in winter. | Vladimir Potanin said Norilsk Nickel is ready to begin talks to merge with Rusal, citing shareholder diversification, sustainability/green agenda, and increased resistance to sanctions. Norilsk produces nickel and copper, while Rusal produces aluminium with low-carbon power. The update is only about initiating merger discussions. | Copper prices plunged below $8,000/ton on the London Metal Exchange to a 17-month low, down about 2.3% Tuesday and nearly 20% in the prior quarter. The drop is attributed to global recession fears and rate hikes. Aluminum and nickel also declined amid the broader metals rout. | Major beer CEOs urge President Biden to suspend Trump-era US aluminum tariffs, saying they have cost the industry over $1.4 billion since 2018. Beer producers rely heavily on aluminum cans, and the tariffs reportedly raise end-user production costs through the supply chain, pressuring consumer prices.

SHFE aluminium (2208) hit a 15-month low and LME aluminium fell below $2,400/mt. Weak downstream orders, poor spot trades, destocking ending, and continued capacity/operating momentum are cited. Pandemic curbs, softer oil, and tightening monetary conditions add macro pressure, keeping aluminium bearish in the near term. | Copper extended its retreat as investors priced in worsening recession risks, pointing to weaker demand prospects from Europe’s energy/gas problems, a US slowdown and renewed COVID flare-ups in China. After a 4.2% drop to a 19-month low, copper and aluminium fell again, reflecting broad metals risk aversion and demand concerns. | SHFE aluminium (2208) fell over 4% to 17,975 yuan/mt as prices have trended down since March. Resumed and newly commissioned smelter capacity is set to lift operating supply, while downstream demand is still weak and spot transactions sluggish. Aluminium inventories are stabilizing/increasing, and many smelters face losses near 18,000. | Alcoa said it will reduce alumina production by up to 15% at the San Ciprián refinery in Spain to mitigate rising natural gas costs. The plant has 1.5m tpy alumina capacity and says output can be adjusted. The move follows prior San Ciprián aluminium suspension and pressures alumina prices. | European aluminium supply is described as tight due to capacity cuts, logistics bottlenecks (e.g., Rotterdam port backlogs, trucking shortages, strike-related disruption), and reduced availability including less Russian-origin material. Premiums in Europe have risen to all-time highs, implying sellers hold leverage and negotiations remain supportive for prices.

SMM reports China aluminium ingot social inventories at 723,000 mt as of July 7, down week-on-week and sharply versus last year. Billet inventories also fell weekly. Dip buying increased as aluminium prices dropped and buyers brought forward purchases amid COVID-related transport uncertainty, though demand is weakening into the off-season. | Aloft Holdings, a Dutch company planning to buy and manage aluminium assets globally, is in advanced talks to acquire Alcoa’s Portland Aluminium Smelter. The buyer has completed due diligence and discussed a potential deal for months, but the article provides no immediate operational or production changes. | Copper prices jumped 4.5% to $7,858/t after Bloomberg reported China is considering $220bn in stimulus, helping offset bets on lower prices and forcing position buybacks. Analysts still warn rising rates and recession risks could push copper further down, with aluminium higher too. | Kaiser Aluminum declared force majeure at its Warrick rolling mill due to limited availability of magnesium used in aluminum beverage and food packaging. US Magnesium deliveries stopped after partial supplies, forcing Kaiser to cut output 30%–40% in July and about 50% in the rest of Q3 until supplies resume.

Commodities rebounded after reports China may let local governments issue 1.5 trillion yuan of special bonds to support growth. The rally lifted copper by about 4.5% to around $7,860/t. The stimulus narrative typically boosts near-term industrial demand expectations, supporting base metals. | SMM says operating rates at major aluminium processing enterprises fell to 66.5% (down 0.2pp), with primary and secondary aluminium alloys particularly weak due to off-season demand softness and bearish sentiment from sharply falling aluminium prices. Secondary alloy ADC12 fell sharply on inventory pressure, further hurting downstream purchases. | Benchmark LME copper fell about 1.7% and was set for a fifth straight weekly loss as fresh China COVID-19 restrictions raised fears of slower infrastructure spending and weaker metal demand. The piece also notes declines in LME aluminium and nickel, reflecting broader recession/risk-off sentiment toward industrial metals. | Hydro began producing low-carbon forged aluminium (HyForge) at its Rackwitz recycling plant, adding 25,000 tpy using more post-consumer scrap, and is investing in a new extrusion press in Tønder for EV and automotive components. The move coincides with rising European aluminium scrap prices and higher low-carbon premiums. | Fastmarkets reports alumina price volatility amid rising costs, weaker Chinese domestic demand, and reduced refinery output. The Atlantic premium may narrow if Century’s Hawesville smelter closes, but Alcoa’s San Ciprian and Romania cuts could offset. The LME aluminium price falling below $2,400 trims margins, pressuring aluminium sentiment.

SMM says SHFE and LME aluminium rebounded briefly due to low inventories and marginal costs, but downstream demand is weakening as operating rates fall and new orders stay insufficient. With operating capacity still rising and destocking slowing, the market risks a future surplus, compounded by overseas rate hikes. | SMM reports alumina prices in north China firming slightly as high-cost refineries cut output due to tighter bauxite and high raw-material costs. In the south-west, new alumina capacity release is increasing supply and weighing on prices. Overall alumina is in the “tightening then rebound” phase. | Copper prices fell as new COVID-19 restrictions in China and worries about faster Fed rate hikes heightened fears of slower global growth and weaker metals demand. LME three-month copper slid 1.3% and Shanghai August copper fell 2%. Aluminium also declined, reflecting a broader risk-off demand outlook; nickel was mixed. | Alumex, Sri Lanka’s aluminium profiles exporter, joined the Aluminium Stewardship Initiative (ASI) and will pursue ASI Performance and Chain of Custody standards. The firm also expanded aluminium waste collection and upgraded its recycling facility, targeting higher scrap recycling volumes as commercial operations begin in 2022. | SMM reports China metallurgical-grade alumina output fell in June due to tight raw-material supply and high prices, prompting cuts in high-cost capacity in Shanxi and Henan. Other regions partially increased output. Net imports were slightly negative and a modest surplus is expected, with supply-demand balance easing as old capacity is replaced. | SMM data show China’s June aluminium output rose 4.48% y/y to 3.361 Mt as production resumed and new capacity (e.g., in Guangxi, Gansu, Yunnan) added ~450,000 tonnes. July capacity and output are expected to keep increasing despite weakening prices, demand, exports, and inventories likely staying firm. | Copper prices dropped 3% on Monday as worries about China’s demand from new zero-COVID restrictions intensified, reinforced by higher interest-rate expectations, a soaring U.S. dollar, and rising warehouse stocks. Aluminium also slid 2.4% in the broader base-metals complex amid recession fears.

Credit Suisse’s recession scenarios point to downside for copper and aluminium: copper could drop for longer amid incoming supply, while aluminium faces negative demand pressure despite possible support from constrained supply and higher energy/input costs. It also discusses broader iron ore resilience and a bauxite outlook that is mainly longer-term. | Credit Suisse analysis flags recession scenarios as negative for copper and aluminium: copper could fall longer due to incoming supply, while aluminium faces demand weakness though it may get support from constrained supply and higher energy/input costs. The piece also discusses bauxite supply prospects, which could ultimately support aluminium demand. | Australia’s Anti-Dumping Commission recommended ending low-level (1.9%) anti-dumping duties on Vietnamese aluminum extrusions, citing evidence that dumping had little or no impact on the domestic industry in the inquiry period. Duties imposed on June 27 are expected to expire, likely increasing import competition and weighing on aluminum prices. | Australia’s anti-dumping authority concluded that aluminum extrusion imports from Vietnam and Malaysia were not causing significant harm to the domestic industry. The 1.9% duty on Vietnamese extrusions is set to expire from June 27, 2022, implying reduced import cost pressure for buyers and potentially more competition for local producers. | Freedonia Group reports that US pipe market values surged in 2021 alongside 38% PVC, 48% steel, and 49% copper price increases driven by inflation and supply-chain shortages. It also notes pipe usage rebounded in oil & gas and construction. Forecasts through 2026 expect average metal prices to moderate.

China’s bauxite imports in May reached 11.97 Mt, a new record, led by Guinea and also higher Australia/Indonesia-linked volumes. With Indonesia repeatedly rumored to ban exports and Guinea’s regime instability, supply uncertainty persists. Strong refinery demand is expected to push up bauxite costs and support aluminium prices. | SMM reports alumina prices have bottomed out as high-cost refineries cut output amid losses. In northern China, supply tightens and local spot prices hold firm, while the south-west faces renewed buyer-favourable pressure from new capacity. Overall, alumina downside is limited in the short run due to curtailments. | SMM reports aluminium prices falling sharply as domestic supply rises with resumed production and new capacity, while demand weakens due to July seasonality, high southern temperatures, and sluggish real estate. Inventory declines are slowing, and overseas semi exports growth may fade. SMM expects SHFE aluminium to drop further in July-August. | SMM says China’s molten aluminium casting billet operating rates fell to 72.4% in June, with weaker terminal consumption and lower order volumes in several regions. Billet social inventory rose amid supply-demand tension, conversion margins were pressured, and conditions are expected to deteriorate in the July off-season. | Copper prices fell to a 20-month low as economic slowdown fears intensified. A stronger U.S. dollar, renewed COVID-19 curbs in China, and expectations of aggressive U.S. rate hikes ahead of inflation data reduced demand outlook. China’s growth cuts and market caution before China data reinforced selling pressure. Aluminium also fell; nickel was mixed. | Proud Source Water, Ball Corporation, and Replenysh launched a Florida pilot to improve aluminum recycling infrastructure, targeting recovery of at least six million pounds of aluminum over 12 months. The program places schools as collection “host locations” to divert aluminum cans from landfill, increasing recycled supply.

SMM reports secondary aluminium operating rates rose 4.3% in June to 43%, supported by easing pandemic effects and improving auto supply chains. However, low new order volumes and weak die-casting demand left downstream purchasing cautious. Inventories increased and firms cut prices, suggesting operating rates may fall in July. | SMM data show China’s aluminium ingot social inventories fell to 697,000 mt as of July 14, driven by downstream dip buying, reduced warehouse arrivals due to pandemic impacts, and active cargo outflows. Billet inventories also eased overall, though arrivals rose in some regions. Lower prices boosted purchasing sentiment.

London and Shanghai copper prices are set for their biggest weekly loss in over two years, pressured by a stronger US dollar and weak demand outlook tied to slowing growth and imminent rate hikes. China’s COVID-hit activity also weighs. Nickel sharply falls too; aluminium edges up slightly. | Vedanta Aluminium announced on World Youth Skills Day it is expanding training and placement-linked skill programmes for rural youth in Odisha and other states. It highlights work near its alumina refinery and aluminium smelter operations, including farmers’ training and rural women’s cooperatives, but no direct production, pricing, or trade changes are cited. | S&P Global says copper is the bottleneck for 2050 net-zero, projecting demand rising sharply and supply failing to meet it due to long mine lead times and constraints on expanding production and recycling. With limited substitution—including insufficient aluminium and recycling—near-term expectations for tight copper supply could support higher prices.

SHFE 2208 and LME aluminium prices extended declines, with contracts down about 6.6% on the week. The report attributes the fall to bearish macro—US CPI and higher rate-hike odds—and aluminium fundamentals: many smelters are loss-making, downstream orders remain weak, and destocking slows, keeping prices pressured. | SMM reports China’s aluminium ingot social inventories at 682,000 mt as of July 18, down from last Thursday and significantly lower than a year earlier. Shipments from warehouses increased, and downstream buyers became more willing to purchase after prices dipped near 17,000 yuan/mt. Arrivals in Wuxi should normalize after pandemic disruption. | SMM reports that aluminium smelters face higher power costs as coal supply tightens during peak summer electricity demand. Electricity prices rose 1.6% to 0.439 yuan/kwh, pushing real-time smelter costs up 58.6 yuan/mt. Meanwhile, A00 aluminium prices fell 5.5%, expanding smelter losses and risking further short-term deterioration. | SMM reports alumina prices diverging across China as ocean FOB Western Australia prices fall on abundant supply, while north China gets partial refinery recoveries but bauxite costs constrain output. South-west prices weaken with new capacity releases. Record aluminium smelter operating rates suggest only a slight alumina surplus, limiting sharp aluminium moves. | Copper fell 7.8% over the week to $3.2 per pound, its worst rate since November two years ago. Nickel declined 11% and aluminium dropped 3% (zinc also down). The move is linked to recession fears and wider commodity weakness, alongside European gas supply concerns from Gazprom. | Copper jumped 3.3% on LME after investors covered shorts following Chinese government support for the property sector and signs of monetary easing, helped by a weaker dollar. CMOC’s Tenke Fungurume suspended exports. Aluminium rose 3.8% and nickel gained 6.5%, reflecting broad industrial metals strength.

Culpeo Minerals says assays from the seventh drillhole at its Lana Corina copper project in Chile extend high-grade copper mineralisation by 100 metres to depth and shows strong copper (>2%) and molybdenum intersections. Drilling continues on nearby breccia pipes. The update is exploration-focused, with no immediate production impact. | China imported 9.42 million tonnes of aluminium ore and concentrate in June, down 7.5% year-on-year, while first-half imports rose. Alumina exports jumped to 190,000 tonnes in June, up sharply YoY, with much higher first-half exports, suggesting shifting supply balances that may support aluminium prices. | SMM reports that East China Nonferrous Metal City in Linyi saw aluminium scrap throughput fall sharply while aluminium prices dropped by 1,040 yuan/mt last week. After aluminium rebounded by 320 yuan/mt on Monday, aluminium scrap prices rose (taint +300, tense +200). Demand stayed weak, limiting transaction recovery. | Steel Dynamics authorized construction of a $1.9–$2.2 billion low-carbon recycled aluminum flat-rolled mill with 650,000 tonnes capacity, plus two recycled aluminum slab centers, mainly using aluminum scrap. Production is targeted for Q1 2025. The project is expected to expand aluminum processing and scrap utilization. | Steel Dynamics will invest $2.2 billion to build a recycled aluminum flat-rolled mill and two satellite slab centers, aiming to address a reported 2+ million ton North American supply deficit in aluminum flat roll. The facilities will rely on the company’s scrap aluminum recycling platform and ramp from 2024-2025.

The piece forecasts growth in the aluminum cable market, citing increased use of aluminum wire in lightweight vehicles, power distribution, and electric/hybrid vehicles in Asia-Pacific. It describes aluminum’s lightweight and corrosion-resistant properties as drivers. It acknowledges copper’s substitutability but presents it as a dampener, not the main theme. | SMM expects China’s alumina oversupply to ease in H2 as supply-demand balances improve and new capacity timing shifts economics. It forecasts domestic alumina prices to fluctuate around production costs despite high bauxite/energy/caustic soda inputs. Overseas, weaker cost support may pressure prices. | Europe’s power crunch is reportedly cutting aluminum capacity by half. Reduced operating rates from high energy costs can tighten near-term aluminum supply expectations, affecting producer output and sentiment across the aluminum market. | The article argues that aluminium smelting is highly sensitive to power and gas prices. It cites Europe idling over 900,000 tonnes of primary capacity due to soaring energy costs and warns some curtailments may become permanent. It compares past energy shocks in the US and Japan that led to closures. | SDI will invest $2.2bn to build an aluminium rolling mill (650,000 tpa) plus two recycling facilities supplying aluminium slab, targeting beverage can and automotive sheet. The firm cites a growing North American flat-rolled aluminium supply deficit and says the projects will start from 2024/2025.

Japan’s Denkai America plans a $150 million initial investment and up to $430 million for a North American headquarters and manufacturing plant in Augusta, producing copper foil for printed electronics and lithium-ion batteries. The buildout implies incremental near-term demand for copper materials, which can be mildly supportive for copper prices. | SMM expects aluminium prices to face downside risk in H2 as China’s operating capacity resumes and supply grows faster than downstream demand recovery. It notes slower consumption post-pandemic, rising ingot/billet supply with inventories likely turning up, and cost support from high costs, but net supply-demand pressure dominates. | SMM said China aluminium ingot social inventory fell to 668,000 mt on July 21, down from both the prior week and a year earlier, as delayed arrivals and deliveries resumed selectively. Billet inventory also dropped to 95,400 mt, helped by weaker north-west smelter shipments and downstream restocking, likely continuing to fall next week. | Steel Dynamics will build a $1.9 billion low-carbon flat rolled aluminium plant in the Southeast, with 650,000 metric tons/year capacity, starting production in early 2025, and will own most of the joint venture with Unity. The project also includes two supporting recycled slab centers, expanding North American supply. | Rio Tinto signed an exploratory memorandum with Ford to develop and potentially supply low-carbon materials for next-generation vehicles, including battery and lightweight components. The deal explicitly mentions aluminium and copper, and supports expectations of increased demand as EV and truck production expand in North America, Europe and China. | Alcoa executives say surging natural gas prices have forced European smelters to cut capacity, including a two-year curtailment at its San Ciprian smelter in Spain and a 15% drop in refinery production. With spot-driven costs, operations are loss-making and aluminum supply remains under pressure despite demand growth. | Kentucky’s Gov. Andy Beshear is trying to salvage a deal for a proposed aluminium mill after the previous plan to locate the plant in Kentucky failed. His administration is reaching out to Steel Dynamics, which will build the mill with Unity Aluminum but has not specified a Kentucky site. Financing issues are cited.

The Unity Aluminum/Braidy project in Kentucky is in dispute, with Gov. Andy Beshear criticizing the state investment and saying it has received nothing so far. Steel Dynamics is scouting for a planned $1.9 billion plant with Unity, but no site commitment is confirmed. The outcome could influence aluminum supply expectations. | Aughinish Alumina in Limerick avoided Deripaska/Rusal sanctions but faces rising energy costs. The article notes Romanian alumina producer Alum was forced to halt output for 17 months, laying off 70% of staff, while Alro Group (a major aluminium smelter) warns energy-driven production cost damage affecting calcined alumina and electrolytic aluminium. | Romanian alumina producer ALUM plans to temporarily suspend its Tulcea alumina operations from Aug. 1 for about 17 months, citing very high and scarce electricity and natural gas, plus rising CO2 certificate prices. The shutdown follows losses and cost escalation and implies lower alumina supply for aluminium production. | Europe’s energy crisis is reported to risk slashing aluminum production. With electricity and power-intensive smelting facing higher costs or instability, aluminum output could be curtailed, affecting near-term supply expectations. The headline implies bearish pressure for aluminum markets as production risk rises.

SMM said North China alumina prices rose mildly as cost support remained and loss-making refineries reduced spot supply, focusing on long-term orders. Overseas alumina prices slipped slightly, narrowing the domestic-FOB spread, while exports helped ease surplus concerns. Overall, prices are expected to stabilise short term. | SMM reports SHFE 2208 aluminium has rebounded on the week after bottoming, while LME aluminium is also higher. Fundamentals cite mixed demand and rising domestic supply/normal smelting, with low inventories supporting prices. ECB rate hikes and weak European PMIs weigh on commodities, leading to a sideways-to-down weekly outlook. | SMM reports aluminium smelter costs were mostly stable but remained high versus last year. Real-time smelting costs increased to 18,025 yuan/mt, while losses narrowed to 35 yuan/mt after spot aluminium rebounded to 17,990 yuan/mt. However, aluminium prices remain under pressure and profitability is unlikely to improve soon. | Chalco plans to acquire a 19% stake in Yunnan Aluminium for 6.66 billion yuan, taking its ownership to 29.10% after completion. The deal (at a 5.5% premium) aims to resolve horizontal competition, improve synergies, and expand “green aluminium” output capacity, benefiting stakeholder profitability and sentiment for aluminium. | SMM said China’s eight major markets’ aluminium ingot social inventories rose to 671,000 mt on July 25, up 3,000 mt from last Thursday, while spot trade eased after prices rebounded. Shipments from key warehouses fell, but arrivals into Wuxi are expected to increase, keeping inventory growth risk elevated. | SMM reports Linyi aluminium scrap shipments rose to about 7,800 mt in July 18–24, up 2,000 mt week-on-week, alongside a weekly rise in aluminium prices to 17,990 yuan/mt. Improved purchasing demand ahead of peak season and slightly eased supply pressure supported prices, though bearish sentiment could return if prices fall. | Kaiser Aluminum reported a smaller Q2 loss than the prior year, with improved EPS and adjusted earnings, alongside higher quarterly revenue. Analysts’ revenue expectations were also exceeded. As an aluminium producer, the results may signal firmer near-term operating conditions and support aluminium price sentiment over the coming weeks.

SMM reports aluminium prices stabilizing after a July 14 low but still drifting sideways amid Fed-meeting caution. SHFE aluminium is down 0.64% and spot A00 ingot prices have eased. Inventories in major Chinese markets rose modestly while downstream demand stayed sluggish, keeping aluminium under pressure. | Freeport-McMoRan said the sharp copper price fall stems from economic uncertainty and higher rates, and could prompt adjustments to operating plans. The article compares today to 2015, noting potential production curtailments and lower LME inventories. It adds that escalating power and diesel costs have pressured aluminium output and costs. | The Northern Miner podcast reviews Alcoa’s Q2 earnings call, highlighting rising energy costs, a strong U.S. dollar, and ongoing struggles for smelter profitability. It also notes that aluminum is expected to remain in deficit and demand continues to grow, which tends to underpin near-term price expectations for aluminum. | The piece reports aluminum prices have fallen despite supply disruptions. It attributes European smelter and refinery closures to surging power and natural gas costs, which are expected to stay high through 2024. Yet recession fears and potential gas rationing imply demand destruction, outweighing supply tightness and pressuring prices lower globally.

SMM says customs inspection of imported aluminium scrap was relaxed in late June, lifting June imports to 124,600 mt (+220% y/y, +24% m/m) as order backlogs cleared and domestic scrap supply stayed tight. It expects imports to stay stable in July-August, easing supply pressure. | Aztec Minerals began a 10-hole, 3,000m core drilling program at its Cervantes gold-copper project in Sonora, Mexico to expand and test oxide cap and deeper copper-gold targets. While it highlights copper-bearing intercepts, it does not change near-term production, so price impact is likely limited. | Tiwai Point, New Zealand’s largest aluminium smelter, has begun negotiations with power companies to secure a new electricity contract beyond 2024. Because a closure would remove aluminium demand and hurt power-company investment plans, the start of talks supports expectations of continued output, modestly bullish for aluminium.

SMM said Shanghai aluminium ingot social inventory was 671,000 mt, up on the week, while billet inventory rose to 107,600 mt. Some regions saw increases due to intensive cargo arrivals and sluggish downstream demand. Spot transactions were weak, and inventories are unlikely to fall near term. | Ellwood Group said it will invest in its Ellwood Specialty Steel plant to add an aluminum tooling plate (ATP) production line. The automated facility includes sawing, machining, quality control, lamination, storage and packaging, aiming to supply customers more reliably with reduced lead times and inventory uncertainty. | Hydro Aluminum plans a $15 million investment in its Henderson, Kentucky recycled-aluminum facility, including a new homogenization furnace and a baghouse dust/air system. The upgrades aim to boost output of certified low-carbon aluminum and enable processing of higher scrap volumes, supporting production for the automotive market.

Copper prices rose Tuesday, supported by a weaker dollar and supply risk from Peru. Three-month LME copper gained 0.8% and Shanghai September copper rose 1.3% amid ongoing demand worries. The article also reports gains in LME aluminium and ShFE nickel, suggesting broadly positive metal sentiment. | SMM reports China’s aluminium wheel exports fell in June to 71,500 mt, down 6% month-on-month and 10% year-on-year. With major destinations accounting for most flows and an off-season slump, wheel plants cite insufficient orders from domestic and overseas buyers, suggesting exports won’t improve in July-August. | Copper is set for a fourth straight monthly decline as recession fears, a weaker demand outlook in China, and an elevated U.S. dollar weigh on prices. Copper is down about 6% this month. Nickel production at Nornickel fell QoQ. LME aluminum is up modestly, indicating weaker bearish spillover to aluminium. | The piece forecasts steady expansion of the global aluminum cans market (notably in North America and China) driven by rising beverage and processed-food consumption and sustainability/recyclability trends. It also notes manufacturers focusing on increasing production capacity to meet demand, implying supportive near-term aluminum demand.

SMM reports regional alumina price divergence in China, with north China supported by higher costs and expected Shanxi production cuts for environmental reasons. FOB Western Australia prices eased slightly as overseas supply improved. South-west prices remain firm but may soften with future output releases. Alumina is seen fluctuating narrowly this week. | China’s bauxite imports fell to 9.42 million mt in June, down 21.4% MoM, with Australia and Indonesia both declining sharply. Weather disruptions hurt shipments, and Indonesia’s export ban risk has intensified, prompting Chinese alumina refineries to seek alternative ores. Supply tightness could support aluminium pricing. | SMM reports China exported 340,800 mt of aluminium plate/sheet in June 2022, down 6% month-on-month but up 44% year-on-year. Exports weakened as overseas fabrication recovery did not translate into sustained orders, and SMM expects continued MoM export declines in coming months, pressuring aluminium demand expectations. | SMM said China’s aluminium ingot social inventories totaled 678,000 mt on Aug 1, up 7,000 mt week-on-week but below last year. Foshan inventories rose for two weeks amid weak trades and steady arrivals. With low arrivals elsewhere, demand remains poor, and inventories may increase further as shipments arrive. | Koba Resources completed induced polarisation (IP) surveys over its historic Blackpine cobalt-copper project ahead of maiden drilling in September, following an earlier maiden program at the nearby Colson project. The work targets extensions of copper-cobalt mineralisation, but no production changes are reported.

Aluminium prices fell sharply on both SHFE and LME after the prior rally, with spot discounts widening. SMM links the weakness to accumulating domestic social inventories, poor downstream demand and lower operating rates, and smelter production pressures from loss-making conditions. High energy costs provide some support, but upside momentum appears limited.

Hydro Aluminum Metals USA will upgrade and expand its Henderson, Kentucky plant with a new homogenization furnace and baghouse to improve automotive specs and increase recycling of broader post-consumer scrap. Work starts August and finishes by September 2024, supported by Kentucky tax incentives tied to investment and job retention. | Copper prices eased as U.S.-China tensions over Pelosi’s Taiwan visit raised fears of weaker industrial demand. Markets also discounted the prospect of more Fed rate hikes and recession risks. LME three-month copper fell 1.6%, with aluminium down 1.3% and nickel down 0.8%, reflecting broad metals pressure.

SMM expects aluminium prices in August to fluctuate but remain pressured by weak downstream demand in an off-season, falling profit-driven reluctance to sell/produce by high-cost smelters, and inventory accumulation risks for ingot and billet. Costs may ease via lower electricity and anode prices, but demand is slow to improve. | SMM reports August aluminium fluoride tender prices were cut to 8,500 yuan/mt, with weaker cost support from falling sulphuric acid prices. Lower bidding by aluminium plants reduced aluminium fluoride producers’ profits, prompting some to switch to hydrofluoric acid. SMM expects aluminium fluoride prices to face short-term downside and muted transactions. | AMAG and Audi developed a new recycled aluminium casting alloy (AlSi7.REC) for car wheels with over 70% recycled content, aiming to meet safety and aesthetic requirements while cutting CO2 versus primary metal. The piece also cites recent Fastmarkets aluminium pricing levels, but provides no large-scale volume impact. | Murchison Minerals says it has commenced Phase III diamond drilling at its HPM Quebec nickel-copper-cobalt project, targeting expansion and delineation at the Barre de Fer zone and the nearby Syrah target. The company plans ~7,500 m and references assay grades, but no production or supply changes are announced.

Customs data cited by SMM shows China exported about 652,200 mt of unwrought aluminium and aluminium semis in July 2022, down 5.01% month-on-month but up 39.05% year-on-year. January–July exports rose 34.9% YoY to 4.16 million mt, indicating weaker near-term outward shipments. | SMM reports SHFE and LME aluminium ended the week lower/flat-to-higher but with weak momentum. Europe’s energy/power crisis and poor profitability slow smelter restarts and raise expectations for cuts, while weak downstream demand and rising ingot inventories widen spot discounts. Net effect is rangebound price action. | SMM reports that aluminium smelter costs fell after rounds of cuts to prebaked anode and aluminium fluoride prices. Despite stable power tariffs for most firms, the average smelter cost dropped to 17,884 yuan/mt and profits rose to 635 yuan/mt as A00 aluminium prices increased to 18,520 yuan/mt. Risks remain from price volatility. | SMM said Chinese aluminium ingot social inventories totaled 687,000 mt as of Aug 8, up for three consecutive weeks (+17,000 mt since early August) and higher than end-July. Several regions saw stock increases, while warehouse shipments fell to 128,300 mt and spot discounts widened amid sluggish demand.

German KfW IPEX-Bank reiterated full support for Altech Chemicals’ high-purity alumina (HPA) project in Malaysia, including a planned US$190 million senior loan, with Euler Hermes renewing export credit cover. The update follows discussions on financing and bankable progress, enabling work to resume after pandemic-related delays. | State Grid announced it will start multiple Ultra High Voltage transmission projects and accelerate additional projects, investing over 150 billion yuan. UHV lines in China use aluminium bare overhead conductor (ACSR), implying substantial aluminum rod demand (SMM estimates 700,000 mt+ over construction). Analysts expect supportive effects on aluminium demand and prices. | A German aluminium foundry is preparing for temporary shutdowns to survive Europe’s gas crisis, with gas costs more than doubling and likely tripling. Aluminium smelters/foundries depend heavily on natural gas and may reduce output, raising concerns about downstream delivery disruptions across auto supply chains. | SMM says China produced 3.5m tonnes of aluminium in July, up 6.7% y/y, with domestic operating capacity continuing to rise. August output is expected to increase further, while demand for sheets/plate, strip and extrusion weakens. Inventories of ingots and rod may keep rising, weighing on aluminium prices. | A German aluminium foundry plans rotating shutdowns to survive Europe’s gas crisis as energy costs have doubled and gas prices have surged on reduced Nord Stream 1 flows. Regulators urge further cuts, and carmakers warn output depends on suppliers. Aluminium foundries may curb operations, tightening near-term supply.

SMM’s Aug 10 outlook says aluminium prices face two pressures: new domestic supply/resumed idle capacity and weaker overseas consumption concerns. Costs remain firm (cash ~16,500 yuan/mt) while inventory is low but ingot/billet stocks have risen recently and may rise through late August, limiting upside. | Copper rose about 1% after U.S. inflation came in weaker than expected, easing fears of aggressive rate hikes and supporting a weaker dollar. Comex futures also climbed. The article notes aluminium fell on the same day, while nickel gained sharply, suggesting mostly supportive macro demand sentiment for copper. | Century Aluminum idled its Hawesville, Kentucky aluminum smelter for 9–12 months, laying off about 600 workers, citing “untenable” electricity and energy prices after power costs tripled. Alcoa is also curtailing smelting at its Warrick, Indiana plant, with broader energy-driven industrial slowdown implied.

SMM reviews July–August alumina pricing in China, with northern output reduced at times due to high costs and tight spot supply, while later expectations of cuts were not fully met. Southern China saw new capacity ramping up in Guangxi/Chongqing, leading to steadier but slightly softer alumina prices, pressuring deals toward lower levels. | Alpha Copper reports results and ongoing drill progress at its Indata copper-gold project, targeting vein gold and porphyry copper-gold mineralization with associated molybdenum. It describes chalcopyrite mineralization and encourages copper-bearing intercepts, which can affect near-term copper market sentiment. No nickel or aluminium impacts are indicated. | SMM’s survey shows domestic aluminium billet producer operating rates fell to 71.3% in July and are expected to decline further in August. Downstream extruder rates dropped, and Sichuan faced power rationing and an aluminium smelter accident disrupting 190,000 mt of aluminium and 40,000 mt of billets, hurting demand. | SMM says aluminium ingot social inventories across eight Chinese markets rose to 693,000 mt as of Aug 11, accumulating for three straight weeks, while downstream demand remains weak and the price rebound curbs buying. Aluminium billet inventories also increased for five consecutive weeks, suggesting further inventory growth next week. | Century Aluminum laid off hundreds at its Hawesville, Kentucky primary aluminum plant, citing “untenable” energy prices. The plant will stay idle for nine to 12 months until power costs are expected to normalize. The curtailment tightens supply forecasts for primary aluminum and is likely bearish for near-term prices.

The PRNewswire piece summarizes an Allied Market Research report on global aluminum spacers demand, citing substitution from metal, construction and transport end-use, and manufacturing emphasis in Asia-Pacific. It also notes feedstock availability at reduced prices but provides no short-term supply disruption or policy shock that would materially move aluminium prices immediately. | Copper prices gained around 1.56% in New York as the US dollar pulled back, supporting a firmer Australian dollar. The piece also highlights higher energy prices and a weak Rhine water level affecting coal transport and stockpiling, reinforcing broader commodity strength that can lift industrial metals sentiment. | China’s bauxite imports fell to 9.42 million mt in June, down 21.4% MoM, with declines from Australia and Guinea and a steep drop from Indonesia amid export-ban fears. Weather reduced production and shipments in Australia/Guinea, while Indonesia supply looks tighter, potentially pressuring alumina availability and supporting aluminium prices. | SMM reports alumina prices in China are largely stable within a narrow range as northern refineries face high-cost pressure and production reductions, tightening spot supplies, while new capacity in the southwest adds supply and pressures prices. Overall transaction activity is thin, with expectations of stable prices in the north and slight declines in the south. | SMM reports volatile aluminium trading, with SHFE prices rising despite sluggish consumption. Supply is supported by unexpected Sichuan smelter cuts and ongoing Europe energy-related capacity limits, while downstream operating rates remain weak and inventories keep rising. Exports are strong but overseas semi demand is expected to stall, keeping prices range-bound. | SMM says Chinese aluminium plate/sheet and strip producers’ average operating rate fell to 74.99% in July amid sluggish end consumption and insufficient new orders. Producers cut output or margins. Although inventories fell after late-July price rebound, firms expect continued weak off-season demand, potentially worsened by power rationing in August. | Sky-high European energy costs are forcing aluminium smelters to cut output further, potentially reducing winter capacity. LME aluminium inventories are at very low levels, adding supply-fear support. While recession-driven demand risks are noted, the supply contraction from energy constraints is expected to be price supportive in the near term. | Sky-high European energy costs are pressuring aluminium and zinc smelters, with analysts warning of further capacity cuts this winter and already low LME inventories. The resulting tighter supply deficits could outweigh demand worries, supporting expectations of relative price outperformance, particularly for aluminium, if power curbs occur.

SMM reports domestic molten aluminium casting billet firms operated at 71.3% in July, down for a second month. Downstream extruder rates fell sharply, prompting billet output cuts, while Sichuan power rationing and an accident disrupting 190,000 mt smelter capacity and 40,000 mt billet capacity may weigh further in August. | SMM reports alumina prices fell across North and South China, with weaker bids and a gradual tilt toward a buyer’s market. Overseas FOB Western Australia prices also declined. Supply risk is tempered near term, but new capacity is set to ramp up in September-October, reviving oversupply concerns. | SMM reports Sichuan’s aluminium smelter was shut after an accident, and 285,000 mt of capacity was cut due to power rationing. Despite some easing oversupply, downstream demand remains weak, social inventories keep rising, and overseas demand falls amid high energy prices and rate-hike expectations. Aluminium seen ranging widely this week. | Imperial Aluminum in Minerva seeks to expand by reactivating a nearby former industrial building. Because the structure contains lead and asbestos, officials are pursuing state grant funding to pay for hazardous-material cleanup before demolition and site use. The project is expected to support Imperial’s operations and potential hiring. | SMM says China’s aluminium ingot social inventories totaled 698,000 mt on Aug 15, up 5,000 mt week-on-week but down 44,000 mt year-on-year and still at a low level. Wuxi fell on fewer arrivals; Foshan declined with higher shipments. Sichuan production is cut due to power issues, potentially reducing southbound arrivals. | The piece attributes pressure on the Australian dollar to stronger USD and recession fears, citing weak U.S. data and China’s softer retail sales and industrial production. It specifically says copper prices came under pressure, weighing on risk sentiment. RBA minutes and wage expectations are also in focus.

The LME suspended Russian nickel from approved UK warehouses unless exported before July 20, citing high additional costs from UK sanctions. It follows earlier April steps banning Russian copper and aluminium-related products in warehouses. LME said no Russian nickel was currently stored, though Liverpool held 198 tonnes of nickel. | The LME suspended Russian nickel from approved UK warehouses unless it is exported by July 20, citing significant additional costs risk under UK sanctions. It follows an April 1 ban on other Russian metals, including copper and primary aluminium/aluminium alloy, from LME warehouses. | Hydro Extrusions’ aluminium plant in Cressona, Pennsylvania, is investing over $50 million in renovations, including a new press installation, demolition of an old boiler house, and added scrap-receiving and casting capacity. The company expects to produce 50,000 more tons of cast metal while cutting emissions, supporting aluminium demand/supply balance.

Sichuan expanded emergency industrial electricity cuts due to extreme heat and drought lowering hydropower. Aluminum smelters are forced to stop or reduce production; SMM cites output reductions reaching 395,000 mt and expect slower recovery of operating capacity. SMM flags potential future supply gaps, supporting aluminium price strength. | Hydro’s Slovak Slovalco smelter will close its primary aluminium production, citing high electricity prices and an uncompetitive CO2 compensation framework. Operations will end by late September 2022 after prior curtailments. Recycling continues at 75,000 tpy, but primary supply loss is supportive for aluminium prices. | Norsk Hydro is shutting an aluminum plant as Europe’s power woes deepen. The closure reduces aluminum supply from a major producer, worsening short-term availability. With electricity costs cited as the driver, markets may expect more curtailments, supporting aluminium prices in the near term. | SHFE and LME aluminium prices rose after news of production cuts: Hydro will shut Slovakia’s Slovalco primary aluminium plant and Sunndal strike begins Aug 22, while Sichuan power rationing has reduced provincial aluminium output. The supply outlook tightened, lifting SHFE aluminium by 3.75% and LME by 1.30% despite mixed demand signals. | Reuters reports Shanghai aluminium prices rallied on supply concerns tied to European and China production disruptions. Norsk Hydro will close a Slovak primary aluminium plant due to high electricity prices, and China’s heatwave power controls disrupted aluminium production. ShFE aluminium rose sharply, with nickel also edging higher.

Biden’s federal climate and health legislation includes tax credits and grants aimed at lowering the cost of producing low-carbon aluminum in the U.S. It may help the Intalco Works smelter’s reopening in Washington, though the timing and ultimate restart remain uncertain. Overall support is likely for near-term aluminium sentiment.

SMM reports LME aluminium inventories dropped by 2,350 mt on August 18 to a new record low. Most of the decline came from the approved warehouse in Klang (-1,500 mt), with additional falls in Kaohsiung (-625 mt), Baltimore (-125 mt), and Bilbao (-100 mt). | Norsk Hydro will stop primary output at its Slovalco aluminium smelter by end-September due to high electricity prices, while possible strikes at Hydro and Alcoa Norwegian plants could cut additional production. With falling LME warehouse stocks and stressed logistics, European aluminium premiums are pressured but supply tightness is seen as supportive.

SMM reports mixed alumina pricing but highlights that power rationing mainly impacts aluminium smelters more than alumina refineries. If smelter output cuts escalate, alumina demand should fall, putting significant downward pressure on alumina and potentially weighing on aluminium market sentiment. Regional supply dynamics also vary. | SMM reports ongoing aluminium production curtailments as power shortages and high energy prices persist. In Sichuan output is cut sharply, leaving only 230,000 mt operating, while Europe faces additional reductions from strikes and energy-cost-driven shutdowns. Downstream may recover faster than smelters, keeping supply tight Aug-Dec. | The analysis argues that China’s electrolytic aluminum output capacity rebounded more than expected in H1 2022, while future growth slows but still leads to Q4 2022 conditions where domestic supply grows faster than consumption. It suggests a move toward inventory accumulation, implying downward pressure on aluminium prices. | Sichuan activated a first-level energy security emergency, extending industrial power outages and production shutdowns through Aug 25, potentially affecting up to 800,000 mt of aluminium smelting and even fully shutting local output. Spot aluminium (A00) rose and SHFE gained, though downstream operating rates eased slightly. | China’s aluminium ingot social inventories were 683,000 mt on Aug 22, up week-on-week but far below last year. Cargo outflows rose, while arrivals in Wuxi and Hangzhou kept totals slightly higher. Sichuan power shortages nearly shut local smelters; Chongqing output was cut slightly, which should underpin aluminium prices. | The news reports that aluminium smelters in Europe have shut down due to soaring electricity (power) prices. The curtailments reduce near-term aluminium production, altering supply expectations and increasing market volatility, which can pressure aluminium prices over the next several days to weeks. | Europe’s soaring power costs are forcing metal producers to cut supply. Norsk Hydro plans to shut its Slovalco aluminum smelter in Slovakia at end next month, citing electricity prices showing no near-term improvement. The article also notes strike-linked aluminum production risk in Norway, reinforcing tighter supplies.

SMM cites China customs data showing primary aluminium imports of 51,037.4 mt in July, down 82.1% MoM and 97.2% YoY. Exports reached 7,588.4 mt, up 14.6% MoM and 16x YoY, with much higher export totals from January to July. | Alumina Limited’s managing director says the inflationary pressures that raised its unit costs by 32% over the past year have likely peaked. The firm notes alumina prices have fallen from an average of about $398/tonne over six months to roughly $330/tonne recently, easing cost pressures and supporting the aluminium value chain. | BNN Bloomberg notes that an aluminium rally has helped Emirates Global Aluminium (EGA) achieve record profits. This suggests favourable aluminium price levels and/or demand conditions for the producer in the near term, supporting market expectations for aluminium. | Wildpack Beverage announced a long-term purchase and supply agreement with Ball Corporation for sustainable aluminum beverage packaging. The deal expands Wildpack’s printed can and warehousing services across its U.S. locations, supporting ongoing demand for aluminum packaging and potentially improving near-term sentiment for aluminium prices. | A report says a German aluminum smelter is considering halving production because energy costs are too high. If implemented, lower primary aluminum output could affect regional supply expectations and market pricing in the near term, depending on how quickly alternative production or inventories can offset the reduction.

China’s unwrought aluminium alloy imports fell 13% MoM in July but rose strongly YoY, while exports surged nearly 50% MoM. Import losses were easing as aluminium prices rallied since mid-July. An opened import window in early August pushed Malaysia ADC12 quotes up, despite losses widening to 300–400 yuan/mt. | Speira is considering reducing output at its German aluminium smelter to about half to cope with soaring electricity and natural-gas costs, deciding in September. With many European smelters already curtailing, the risk of tighter regional aluminium supply is rising, which supports near-term LME aluminium price strength. | Aluminium markets are pressured by Sichuan power rationing that is pushing more Chinese smelters to cut or suspend output, while overseas producers face strikes (Hydro Sunndal, Alcoa Mosjoen) and energy-price-driven reductions. Despite some eased supply, weak downstream demand limits upside, keeping prices volatile but supported by supply tightness. | S&P Global/IHS Markit research warns a copper supply gap will open by 2025, with demand surging from electrification and recycling/substitution not closing the shortfall. It argues higher prices are the likely market response to persistent deficits, potentially constraining the net-zero energy transition. Aluminium substitution is limited to about 1% of demand. | Aluminium prices saw a modest rise as smelter closures and temporary shutdowns—driven by high energy costs in Europe and drought/heatwave in China—tightened supply. However, Reuters notes worsening macro data, high rates and inflation are weakening demand, likely capping further upside despite falling LME inventories. | Monster says port congestion and high freight costs have pushed it to continue importing aluminum cans to avoid stockouts, despite aiming to shift more sourcing to U.S. suppliers. The company incurred higher aluminum can costs in Q1 and Q2 and expects cost-of-sales relief via more domestic can usage, while raising prices Sept. 1.

South32 posted record profit, saying higher realised prices—especially aluminium and metallurgical coal—boosted earnings. The company also highlighted moves to low-carbon metals, adding copper exposure via Sierra Gorda and expanding aluminium capacity through Mozal and a restarted Brazil smelter. No direct near-term price shock is announced. | Trimet France upgraded its Marseille alumina import unloading system, replacing an older pneumatic method with a new electric Siwertell next-generation road-mobile unloader. The equipment reduces berth time, meets strict dust/noise/emissions rules, and is reaching about 150t/h discharge, improving operational efficiency for aluminium production. | SMM reports China’s July primary aluminium imports jumped MoM as price spreads narrowed, but expects imports to slow in August (net ~30,000 mt) due to further price declines, bearish macro after US rate hikes, weak downstream consumption, and higher inventory. European energy costs also weigh on smelter output, affecting semis demand. | SMM outlines global aluminium capacity and reports production cuts: Europe (about 1.16m mt) and North America (about 0.304m mt) hit by high energy costs and strikes, while Sichuan sees near-total smelter suspensions (~1.0m mt). Overall supply is tighter, potentially lifting aluminium prices short term. | Reuters (Aug 25) cites ANZ analysts saying China’s energy shortage could accelerate grid and renewable projects, lifting demand for copper used in solar/wind and power cabling. It also expects aluminium benefits from ultra-high-voltage line and electrical cable needs. The report forecasts deficits that could pressure prices higher. | ANZ says China’s energy shortage and power constraints may accelerate grid and renewable projects, increasing demand for copper used in solar, wind and cabling. The report also expects higher aluminium demand from ultra-high-voltage transmission lines and electrical cables, widening deficits and supporting higher prices.

SMM says aluminium scrap throughput at Linyi Huadong rose sharply to about 15,300 mt in Aug 22-28. Domestic A00 aluminium prices increased to 18,860 yuan/mt and added another 100 yuan to 18,960. Scrap recyclers sold more amid weak momentum, but downstream demand—especially for tense scrap—remains strong. | CRISIL Research says aluminium prices have likely bottomed and are set to rise as a global deficit looms. It attributes the rebound to limited additions to smelter capacity and a pickup in demand, which should tighten supply and support higher aluminium prices over the coming weeks. | Construction is underway for a new Hydro Aluminum plant in Cassopolis. The facility will use raw aluminum and recycled scrap to produce aluminum bars, targeting demand including the automotive sector. Hydro says the project will create about 100 jobs and expects operations to start in fall 2023, implying limited immediate pricing impact. | Fastmarkets reports that aluminium extrusion billet premiums in the US and Europe declined as demand weakened and supply stayed ample. Underlying primary aluminium premiums eased too, while producers faced higher energy costs that compressed margins. Thin spot activity and cautious buying further pressured premiums across multiple regions. | Fastmarkets’ preview highlights ongoing European aluminium smelter cuts from high energy costs and Ukraine-related disruption, while demand remains uncertain and consumers are not buying against full order books. Aluminium premiums have fallen from record highs, and LME stocks are low, with macro recession fears adding bearish sentiment toward near-term prices.

Rio Tinto plans a new aluminum recycling plant in Saguenay-Lac-Saint-Jean, Quebec, aiming to remelt locally sourced post-consumer aluminum scrap into billets at the Arvida smelter. The facility is expected to start in Q2 2024 with 30,000 tons/year capacity, boosting low-carbon recycled supply.

SMM reports a Shandong alumina refinery shut one 900,000 mt/year alumina line after an accident, removing an estimated 70,000–75,000 mt of alumina output. Production is expected to resume mid-to-late September. The temporary alumina supply tightening is likely supportive for aluminium prices over the next several weeks. | SMM said China’s social aluminium ingot inventories rose to 683,000 mt (+4,000 mt WoW) but remain lower vs a year ago, led by gains in Wuxi and transfers from Foshan. Billet inventories slightly fell (-500 mt WoW). Demand is weak, and Sichuan smelter output remains constrained, limiting further accumulation. | MacDonald Mines reported Phase 1 drill results at its SPJ property, including a 0.41% CuEq (with copper values and associated gold/silver) over 90.44m at the Alwyn trend and cobalt anomalies. While it highlights potential for broader polymetallic systems including nickel, no nickel/aluminium results are given. | Novelis has excluded Russian metal from a key supply deal, signaling reduced availability of Russian-origin aluminum material for customers. This kind of sourcing shift can tighten regional supply in the near term and support expectations of firmer aluminum prices over coming days to weeks.

SMM says Yunnan’s aluminium smelters are mostly operating normally, but hydropower shortages and lower reservoir water raise concerns of power rationing and output curtailment after the dry season. Yunnan’s growing smelter capacity could be cut if 20–30% of capacity is curtailed, easing domestic supply pressure. | Copper prices touched a one-month low earlier amid China’s extended COVID restrictions in Shenzhen and a lockdown in Chengdu, raising recession and demand concerns; potential smelter shutdowns could reduce supply. Risk sentiment improved after U.S. jobs data eased Fed hike fears. Aluminium support came from Aldel mothballing capacity due to energy costs.

SMM reports alumina prices were slightly lower week-on-week amid mixed supply moves: some Chongqing and other plants resumed after power restoration, while others cut output due to accidents, ongoing bauxite shortages, or delayed availability. Aluminium smelters are restarting in Sichuan, but Yunnan hydropower concerns may curb output and raise risks for alumina demand. | SMM reports SHFE and LME aluminium prices fell on the week amid hawkish rate expectations and stronger USD, plus weak macro demand signals. While Sichuan power is restored, Yunnan reservoir declines may trigger smelter load cuts and supply uncertainty. Overall, aluminium is expected to remain volatile with support and downside risks balancing. | Shanghai (SMM) said China’s aluminium ingot social inventories in eight major markets fell to 681,000 mt on Sep 5, down slightly week-on-week and more than last year. Regional moves were small. Despite Sichuan smelter shutdowns in August, inventory didn’t drop sharply, but downstream purchases improved after price declines, supporting demand. | European aluminium producers face potential smelter shutdowns as an energy crisis—linked to Gazprom supply cut-offs and soaring electricity costs—makes production uneconomic. Norsk Hydro’s Slovak plant is at risk, with European output at the lowest since the 1970s and analysts expecting large capacity closures, some permanent. | SMM reports weaker downstream appetite for aluminium scrap in Linyi, with aluminium scrap throughput falling sharply week-on-week due to reluctance to buy after September stocking. Aluminium scrap prices and overall aluminium prices dropped last week, reflecting bearish sentiment and inventory dynamics.

SMM reports aluminium fundamentals turning supportive: potential power rationing in Yunnan could reduce aluminium output and support SHFE prices. It also notes weaker bauxite/alumina supply stability concerns, overseas producer capacity cuts (Alcoa, Aldel), slightly falling aluminium inventories, and wider spot discounts despite thin transactions. | Copper futures rose on a weaker dollar and expectations that China will accelerate infrastructure spending. Reuters cites the shift as the main driver for improved metals demand. LME cash/three-month and Shanghai copper gained, alongside price increases in aluminium and nickel, reflecting broad bullish sentiment across industrial metals. | Kaiser Aluminum lifted force majeure at its Warrick rolling mill after magnesium shortages constrained production of certain aluminum packaging products in 2022. Its largest supplier, US Magnesium, stopped deliveries in June 2022, but Kaiser secured qualified magnesium from alternative sources and aims to return to full production. | EU and U.S. imports of Russian aluminium and nickel rose sharply in March–June, despite wartime logistical issues and sanctions. Trade data show aluminium and nickel shipments increasing up to ~70%, with supply still reaching ports. Continued Russian export availability likely reduces supply-risk premiums and pressure for higher prices. | Reuters data show EU and U.S. boosted Russian aluminium and nickel purchases by up to ~70% in March–June despite logistical issues and sanctions threats. Ports and traders report continued deliveries because metals are largely spared. The near-term supply outlook improves, weighing on expectations for aluminium and nickel prices. | Europe’s top aluminum plant plans to cut output 22% because energy costs make production uneconomic. The supply reduction is expected to tighten aluminium availability in the near term, supporting aluminium prices over coming days to weeks as buyers anticipate less production and potential restocking. | EU and U.S. boosted purchases of Russian aluminium and nickel since Russia’s invasion, with imports rising as much as 70% in March–June versus a year earlier. Russian shipments continued despite war-related logistics issues and broad sanctions, suggesting supply pressures have eased, weighing on price expectations. | EU and U.S. have increased purchases of Russian aluminium and nickel since the Ukraine war despite sanctions and logistics concerns. Reuters trade data show EU/U.S. Russian aluminium and nickel imports rising as much as 70% in March–June. Analysts attribute steady flows to workable deals mainly driven by price. | Europe’s top aluminum plant plans to reduce production by 22% because higher energy costs make operating uneconomic. The output cut should lessen near-term aluminum supply in the region, tightening availability and providing upward support to aluminum prices over the coming days to weeks.

Aluminium Dunkerque Industries France, Europe’s largest aluminium smelter, plans to cut output by 22% as high electricity prices threaten profitability. The shutdown begins next Monday and should finish before October 1, amid broader European smelter curtailments and uncertain restart timing without losses. | China’s August unwrought copper and copper product imports rose year-on-year as July-August price declines prompted producer buying and arbitrage between Shanghai and London increased inflows. Electricity rationing during a heatwave tightened domestic supply and lowered SFE stocks, supporting copper availability, though overall demand remained soft amid property weakness. | Reuters data show EU and U.S. imports of Russian aluminium and nickel rose sharply from March to June, with U.S. aluminium up 21% and nickel up 17% year-on-year and some increases up to ~70%. The shift reflects ongoing sanctions-driven trade rerouting after the Ukraine war. | EU and U.S. imports of Russian aluminium and nickel rose markedly in March–June, with values up to $1.98 billion and monthly aluminium imports up around 21% (U.S.) and nickel imports up 17% (top destinations). Analysts link the shift to lessons from disruption after earlier sanctions, suggesting increased supply into Western markets. | A report says a German aluminum smelter has cut production by half because power costs have surged. The output reduction points to immediate curtailments in primary aluminum supply, which can tighten near-term availability and likely provide upward pressure on aluminium prices over the next several weeks. | Boeing said it stopped buying aluminum from Russia in March after suspending operations in the sanctions-hit country. It follows an earlier pause on Russian titanium sourcing. Boeing cited widespread aluminum sourcing but the move, alongside Russia’s major role via Rusal, may tighten near-term supply expectations for aluminium. | Irish regulator approved Aughinish Alumina’s plan to expand by-product storage at its Askeaton alumina refinery, raising red mud pond and salt cake storage heights. The expansion supports deposition of about one million cubic metres per annum and helps avoid a projected 2030 operational stop, supporting alumina supply continuity.  | Brazil’s alumina exports rose 62% year-on-year in August to 908,008 tonnes, with higher shipments via São Luís and Belém. Export revenues nearly doubled and average FOB prices jumped to $386.14/t. Fastmarkets’ fob Brazil alumina index also increased, indicating firmer alumina pricing that can support aluminium cost expectations. | News describes widespread force majeure and shutdowns across metals smelting and fabrication, citing sky-high energy prices and weak demand. Examples include an aluminum smelter closure in Montenegro and primary aluminum cutbacks in Slovenia, plus copper mine/smelt-related disruptions (e.g., Cuajone suspension, Kennecott force majeure), implying near-term supply tightness. | The article says several copper and aluminium production sites worldwide have halted or declared force majeure, citing high electricity costs, weak demand, and disruptions such as protests, earthquakes, or strikes. Examples include an aluminium smelter shutdown and copper cathode/ mine curtailments, implying reduced near-term supply. | Multiple metals smelting and production operations are shutting down worldwide under force majeure and citing very high electricity prices and weak demand. Examples include an aluminium smelter closure in Montenegro, copper mine/smelter disruptions in Peru and Chile, and an electrochemical strike affecting a nickel refinery in Europe.

Europe’s energy crisis is forcing aluminium smelters to cut output, highlighted by Dunkirk Aluminium’s 22% production reduction. Soaring electricity costs make production loss-making and lead to broader curtailments. The article notes domestic aluminium prices rose nearly 10% since mid-July, supporting bullish price pressure. | SMM reports that Indonesia may halt bauxite exports, alongside earlier nickel ore export restrictions. Chinese alumina refineries relying on Indonesian ore are considering alternatives such as Guinea, Australia, Montenegro, Jamaica, and other sources. The move raises near-term expectations of tighter bauxite/alumina supply for China. | Europe faces soaring electricity costs after Russia’s Nord Stream 1 suspension, with natural gas power a key input to generation. European aluminium smelters are already cutting output—over 1 million mt curtailed—and additional cuts are expected before winter. Dunkirk, Alcoa Norway and Hydro plan sizable reductions, temporarily supporting prices. | Fastmarkets reports aluminium P1020A premia weakening across key regions. Japan’s MJP premium fell as traders noted low deliverability and heavy inventories. Europe and Brazil premiums continued downtrends amid weak spot demand and ample availability, despite reported energy-related smelter curtailments. US premia were flat but at very low levels. | York Harbour Metals reports preliminary assays from Phase 3 drilling at its Newfoundland York Harbour Copper-Zinc Project, including a drill interval with 2.86% copper over 14.77 metres. Management says the discovery expands the D Zone and could extend along strike and downdip. Results for additional core are pending. | Boeing has stopped buying Russian aluminum again, ending sourcing of a “crucial metal” from Russia for the second time since the Ukraine invasion. The move reduces demand for Russian aluminium supply, potentially tightening near-term availability and supporting aluminium prices as buyers shift away from Russia. | The piece argues aluminium prices remain volatile as energy costs and inflation force many smelters in Europe and the USA to curtail or shut down due to uneconomic production. It examines scrap and secondary aluminium trends, noting recycling can cut energy use and emissions, but depends on local, sufficient scrap availability. | Speira will curtail 50% of aluminium smelter production at Rheinwerk, Germany, cutting output to 70,000 tonnes/year from October through November, citing high European energy costs. The curtailed output will be replaced via external supplies. The move follows other energy-driven aluminium curtailments and occurs amid falling premiums. | Soaring European power and gas prices have driven widespread aluminum smelter curtailments, including cuts at Neuss (Germany) and Dunkerque (France). Despite the supply reductions, aluminum prices are still falling to a 17-month low, with recession fears and rising Chinese output capping upside. Eurometaux warns the crisis also affects copper and nickel.

Yunnan has large, active aluminium smelting capacity and recently produced record-high output. Low reservoir water levels raise the risk of power rationing after the Mid-Autumn Festival. If implemented, electricity limits could reduce smelter operating rates, lowering near-term aluminium supply expectations. | Alumex launched low-carbon aluminium billets (“Ozon”) backed by a new state-of-the-art melting facility at Sapugaskanda, with over Rs. 1 billion investment. The process claims much lower energy use for billet production and targets export demand from the USA, UK, Europe, and Australia, supporting improved aluminium market competitiveness. | South32 said it will transition its Worsley alumina refinery in Western Australia from coal to gas as the state moves out of coal-fired power. The firm expects a further shift to renewables only after viable alternatives emerge and dependent technologies develop, implying near-term production cost impacts for alumina-linked markets.

Electricity restrictions in China’s Sichuan/Yunnan region have been extended, keeping industrial power cuts in place through Thursday. The disruption raises concerns of reduced aluminum smelting output in a major supply area, which can tighten near-term market balance and lift aluminum prices. | Yunnan aluminium smelters are ordered to cut output by about 10% initially due to power rationing, potentially rising to 20% and lasting until May next year. Given Yunnan’s role as China’s aluminium hub and power-driven production constraints, the market expects tighter aluminium supply and higher SHFE prices.

SMM reports aluminium prices surged on week amid continuing supply disruptions. Yunnan smelters are expected to cut output 10–20% after the Mid-Autumn Festival, while overseas cuts exceed 1.5 million tonnes this year. Social inventories are falling, and downstream restocking is improving, though demand remains softer. | SMM reports China’s molten aluminium casting billet operating rates fell to 67.84% in August, citing weaker downstream extruder orders, COVID disruptions, and power rationing. Higher silicon and magnesium costs raised billet costs, while some regions faced extended restart delays. September/October demand pessimism implies further operating-rate declines. | SMM reports China’s metallurgical-grade alumina prices mostly range-bound, but overseas deals rose due to frequent smelter output cuts. In China, some refineries face pandemic-related closed-off management, potentially tightening supply, while aluminium smelters have already started small-scale output cuts. Demand weakening may cap further gains. | Rio Tinto cut Rusal out of its Gladstone, Queensland alumina refinery operations to avoid breaching Australian sanctions on Russian oligarchs linked to Rusal. Rusal’s subsidiary sued, but the dispute underscores an immediate disruption to Russian-linked alumina bauxite processing and alumina export flows, which can tighten aluminium-related supply. | SMM says China’s aluminium ingot social inventories rose to 696,000 mt on Sep 13, up from the prior week, driven by holiday arrivals and slower pickup. Downstream buyers stayed cautious given high aluminium prices. Sichuan smelters are restarting slowly, while Yunnan cut fears eased, adding supply clarity. | Constellium said it will supply aluminium auto body sheet solutions for Mercedes-Benz C-Class vehicles produced in Europe, China, and South Africa, including hood, roof, tailgate and fenders, using plants in France and Germany. The update indicates ongoing aluminium demand from automotive, but provides no volume data to gauge price impact. | Aluminum prices have slipped as European energy costs force additional smelter cuts (e.g., a 22% capacity reduction at Dunkerque and halving Speira output), and production hits multi-decade lows. However, weakening demand, falling premiums, and rising LME inventories alongside lower prices suggest net bearish pressure.

Alumex PLC (Sri Lanka) launched a low-carbon aluminium billet brand (“Ozon”) backed by a new state-of-the-art melting facility at Sapugaskanda. The firm claims major energy reductions and capacity expansion to grow exports to the USA, UK, Europe and Australia, supporting higher aluminium billet demand. | BNN/Weekend Edition reports that Alcoa is being squeezed by stubbornly high costs while aluminum prices are lower. The coverage implies ongoing weakness in the aluminum market and potential margin pressure for producers, which can weigh on near-term sentiment toward aluminum prices. | SMM says domestic bauxite prices in northern China fell slightly in mid-September, with lower bids attributed to August reductions in alumina refinery production (high costs) and weaker bauxite demand. Nevertheless, it expects alumina output to grow while domestic bauxite remains tight, so prices should stay high. | Technavio’s report forecasts growth for the copper alloy wire market (about USD 18.6B incremental growth) driven by electrical/electronics demand and APAC’s large market share.
