SMM reports that LME-tracked A00 aluminium prices started weak, falling alongside declines in aluminium scrap prices. Lower scrap costs eased billet conversion margins, but demand is faltering as downstream firms close early for Chinese New Year and logistics slow. Conversion margins vary by region (e.g., south China 270–390 yuan/mt). | SMM said China’s aluminium ingot social inventories rose to 576,000 mt on Jan 5, after a sharp weekly increase, despite still-near historical lows. Aluminium billet inventories also climbed to 79,700 mt. Muted spot demand around the New Year and expected further stock builds point to bearish near-term price pressure. | Guizhou Power Grid imposed a third round of winter power rationing on local aluminium smelters due to severe heating demand and falling hydropower output. Five producers must cut combined consumption by 500,000 kW within five days. Prior rounds already curtailed output, cumulatively affecting ~940,000 mt and likely extending shutdowns into early January.

SMM reports Rusal’s view that low-carbon aluminium demand is accelerating, with premiums versus thermal-power aluminium remaining in China ($20-40/mt) and Europe ($40-60/mt). It links demand to China’s carbon peak/neutrality and EV makers’ adoption, and notes stable low-carbon supply and certifications. | Copper prices rose as expectations grow that China will support its ailing property sector, improving demand outlook and helping inventory restocking. LME copper was up 1.2%. Nickel fell sharply to a one-month low on supply expectations after Tsingshan’s new electronickel project started production. Aluminium also edged higher. | Copper prices rose on hopes China will add property-sector support measures, improving the near-term demand outlook. LME three-month copper and SHFE February copper both increased, while Chile production fell. In contrast, Shanghai nickel fell on supply expectations from Tsingshan’s new project starting production. Aluminium also edged up. | Copper rose to a three-week high on a weaker U.S. dollar and optimism that China will boost property demand. LME three-month copper climbed about 1.2%. The piece also references Chile output declines supporting copper, while nickel weakness reflects Tsingshan project-related supply expectations and aluminium advanced. | A mineral trade body is urging tariff removal on bauxite exports. Since bauxite is the key raw material for alumina and ultimately aluminium production, removing export tariffs could ease supply expectations and support aluminium prices in the near term. | British Columbia expanded its blue-bin recycling list to include additional aluminum items such as foil baking dishes and pie plates, alongside many single-use plastics. The policy aims to divert disposable materials from landfills and cites improved recycling technology, but provides no quantified supply effect for aluminum.

Alcoa’s Australia unit signaled a 30% production cut at its alumina refinery. The reduction in alumina output can tighten supply of refining feedstock for primary aluminium, potentially raising expectations for higher aluminium prices over the coming days to weeks. | Alcoa has reduced output at its Kwinana alumina refinery in Western Australia, taking a refining unit offline as a Chevron-related outage worsens gas availability. Alinta Energy tapped stored gas to boost domestic supply, but gas shortages still forced lower alumina production. South32 also curbed gas use and awaits a Wheatstone restart. | SMM reports that Guizhou imposed a third round of power rationing on local aluminium smelters, cutting domestic operating capacity to about 40.1 million mt, while new/restared output has not yet materialized. Demand softness and rising social inventories drove the earlier selloff, but shrinking supply and cost support may stabilize prices this week. | SMM reports prebaked anode benchmark purchase prices fell 400 yuan/mt MoM, helping reduce some smelter power costs as coal prices dropped. Nonetheless, aluminium A00 prices plunged to around 17,700 yuan/mt and turned smelters from profit to losses, implying weak profitability and continued pressure. | SMM reports alumina prices changed marginally week-on-week (SMM weighted index +2 yuan/mt). Overseas quotes were flat. With bauxite still tight, north China refineries stayed cautious, while Guizhou curtailments from power rationing slightly supported southern prices. Aluminium smelters completed winter stockpiling, so spot trading remained subdued and prices are expected stable. | Alcoa’s Australia unit expects production at its Kwinana alumina refinery to be cut by about 30% after a gas supply shortage. An JV unit was taken offline, process flows were hit, and the site is using diesel instead of gas. The shortage stems from a Wheatstone gas plant outage. | Aluminum Dynamics plans a groundbreaking in early March for a $2.5 billion aluminium mill near Golden Triangle Regional Airport in Mississippi. Steel Dynamics will own the mill and construction site activity may begin within weeks, including infrastructure work and permitting. The project includes planned roads upgrades and job creation, supporting aluminium-related market expectations.

Alcoa will reduce production at its Kwinana alumina refinery in Western Australia by about 30% after a natural gas supply shortage leads to a unit being taken offline. With an estimated 1.5-month persistence, alumina supply tightens, potentially boosting expectations for aluminium prices. | The Aluminium Association of India urged the Finance Ministry to cut customs duties on critical raw inputs (calcined/raw petroleum coke, caustic soda lye, aluminium fluoride, anodes, calcined alumina) while increasing duties on primary aluminium and aluminium scrap. It also requested removing coal cess to ease power costs and concessional freight for bauxite/alumina. | India’s Aluminium Association of India (AAI) urged the Budget to remove taxes on raw materials, arguing that aluminium demand could surge from 4 million tonnes per annum to 10 MTPA by 2030. The group estimates large investment needs to expand capacity, implying cost relief would support production and pricing. | Metal Energy reported assay results from five Phase Two drill holes at its Manibridge project in Manitoba, intersecting shallow, stacked zones of high-grade nickel sulphide mineralization. Results include thick intervals and continuity along strike and dip, supporting potential resource expansion. Copper-cobalt is mentioned, but aluminium is not. | Copper prices rose to a 6-1/2-month high on expectations that China’s reopening and stimulus-backed lending will boost metals demand. LME futures extended gains despite a holiday-related demand slowdown. Aluminium also climbed and nickel edged up, reflecting broad base-metal optimism tied to China’s improving demand outlook. | Aluminium prices eased from an early-December peak and broader downside pressure remains dominant despite a short-term bounce. China raised aluminum export tariffs, adding some support and volatility, while energy constraints in Europe and China’s demand weakness weigh on prices. Indonesia’s bauxite export ban is June 2023 and may help later. | Copper extended gains to a 6-1/2 month peak as investors priced in a quicker China reopening and further stimulus, supported by unexpectedly higher bank lending. LME three-month copper rose 0.8% to $8,926/t. Aluminium also climbed, while nickel edged up on the same demand optimism.

China’s bauxite imports rose to 11.79 million mt in November, with Guinea supplies surging as Indonesian exports are set to be banned from June 2023. SMM expects the ban’s effect on supply to be diluted by other sources and growing Guinea output, implying limited near-term aluminium-price impact. | SMM expects aluminium to fluctuate widely in January. Guizhou smelters face power rationing, cutting local capacity, though costs may fall with lower coal and anode prices. Demand remains weak in the off-season and social inventories start accumulating, while off-season inventory expectations weigh on prices. | SMM says China’s aluminium ingot social inventories reached 622,000 mt as of Jan 9, up from Jan 5 and end-December, after a December dip tied to delayed arrivals. Early January saw a spike from heavy arrivals and sluggish consumption. Seasonal inventory is expected to build into post–CNY weeks. | Golden Arrow commenced an IP-resistivity survey and plans its first diamond drilling program in Q1 at the San Pietro Cu-Au-Co project in Chile. The company aims to refine drill targets for copper and cobalt using geophysics and prior drilling data. It is exploration activity with no immediate production impact. | Alba, one of the world’s largest aluminium smelters, plans to install solar PV panels over 37,000 m2 at its Bahrain site, delivering more than 6 MW capacity and over 10,000 MWh per year. The project is intended to support ESG/net-zero goals and likely lower power costs for smelting. | Maaden’s aluminium smelter has reportedly cut production after operating problems. Reduced output from an aluminium producer can tighten near-term supply expectations, affecting balance and pricing sentiment. If the disruption lasts, it would likely be supportive for prices, but the immediate market reaction is expected to be bearish on uncertainty. | The piece indicates Maaden’s aluminum smelter is cutting output because of operating issues. Reduced production from an aluminum smelter can affect near-term supply expectations, potentially supporting price weakness if the disruption suggests lower tonnage availability or longer restart timelines.

Alcoa said it has reverted to gas for fuelling its alumina refineries in Western Australia. The change in refinery fuel sourcing can alter immediate operating costs and production economics, potentially tightening near-term alumina supply expectations and supporting aluminium price sentiment over the coming days. | SMM reports that remelted aluminium billet producers in China will take Chinese New Year holidays in phases between early January and later January. Weak off-season orders, sluggish downstream demand, price fluctuations, and COVID disruptions have pressured operating rates. Companies plan to resume around Jan 30–31, with limited finished inventories. | SMM says China’s aluminium ingot social inventories rose to 642,000 mt on Jan 12, up from the prior week, while billet inventories increased to 95,000 mt. Demand is muted around the CNY holiday and prices attract fewer buyers. Inventories are expected to grow further next week, adding pressure to aluminium prices. | SMM says Chinese aluminium extruders’ average operating rate fell to 36.5% in December, down sharply YoY, with construction extruders—especially in northern regions—cutting production ahead of early CNY. Industrial output was steadier but inventories rose. January operating rates are expected to fall further as plants close. | The article discusses recent aluminum market moves, noting that China’s decision to lift COVID restrictions coincided with an immediate jump in aluminum prices. It also references prior recession fears, slowing demand, and rising LME aluminum stockpiles, plus consumers delaying 2023 orders due to inflation and supply-chain issues.

SMM reports aluminium futures and spot prices rising after eased US dollar pressure and supply-side production cuts, including further Guizhou reductions and slow Sichuan/Guangxi restarts. However, off-season demand is weak, and social aluminium inventories continue climbing into January, limiting further price gains as CNY approaches. | China’s December copper imports fell 12.7% y/y to 514,049 tonnes as COVID infections and shrinking factory activity cooled demand. The Caixin/Markit PMI stayed in contraction, and some metal-fabrication plants shut or cut plans early. Copper prices in London were higher than Shanghai, also weighing imports. | A Norwegian project led by Hydro and partners (HyForge) is developing low-pressure casting that can replace an extrusion step for aluminium suspension components. The process aims to cut defects, scrap, cost, energy use, and carbon footprint, and may increase confidence in using more cast aluminium in car production. | China’s faster-than-expected reopening is described as a “game-changer” for commodity markets, driving bullish repositioning and lifting raw-material prices. Softer US inflation reduces pressure for aggressive Fed tightening, weakening the dollar and supporting expectations for global growth, which underpins rallies including copper and likely industrial metals demand.

Alcoa will cut Kwinana alumina refinery production by 30% in Western Australia because of natural gas shortages, with one unit already suspended and restart timing undecided. SMM expects output disruptions to last ~1.5 months, reducing supply and supporting higher overseas alumina prices, a bullish signal for aluminium-linked markets. | SMM reports alumina prices rose slightly while smelter full costs fell 72 yuan/mt to 17,721 yuan/mt, mainly due to modest electricity price declines. Spot A00 aluminium rebounded to 18,460 yuan/mt on Friday, lifting average smelter profits to 738.7 yuan/mt. Short-term costs should remain fairly stable. | SMM reports SHFE and LME aluminium rose over the week amid cooling US inflation and less fear of aggressive rate hikes. However, domestic aluminium output is still falling due to power rationing and downstream operating rates weaken ahead of CNY. Social inventories are rising, keeping prices range-bound. | SMM reports China’s eight major markets’ aluminium ingot social inventories at 690,000 mt as of Jan 16, up 48,000 mt from Jan 12 and 197,000 mt from end-December. Seasonal build-up is concentrated in Wuxi, Foshan and Gongyi, with spot trading near-stagnant as buyers paused before the break. | SMM reports alumina prices rising domestically and overseas as natural gas shortages limit roasting furnace operations in several Chinese regions. Reduced alumina supply pushes northwest China aluminium smelters to buy from higher-priced areas. Overseas prices also lifted after a refinery production cut, supporting near-term aluminium input costs. | Rio Tinto indicated it may resume growth in export volumes after a softer year, citing high stockpiles at its Western Australia network and a major mine ramping. It also said it plans higher volumes across its alumina, aluminium and copper businesses, adding supply outlook pressure on prices. | Rio Tinto signaled it may restart growth in export volumes, citing high stockpiles at its Western Australian network and ramping a new mine. It also suggested higher 2023 volumes for its alumina/aluminium and copper businesses, implying increased near-term supply that could weigh on copper and aluminium prices.

China’s 2022 aluminium production reportedly hit a record high of 40.21 million tonnes. Record output signals higher supply availability and can weigh on aluminium price expectations over the coming days to weeks, unless demand improvements or inventory tightness offset the additional production.

Chile’s government rejected the $2.5bn Dominga open-pit iron and copper mine and port project near the Humboldt penguin reserve, citing insufficient mitigation for marine and nature impacts. The decision removes a potential copper supply development in a key producer region, likely tightening near-term expectations.

Indonesia says it will stop exporting bauxite from June, aiming to expand domestic refining into alumina and higher value manufacturing. The policy shifts bauxite supply away from global traders, likely tightening feedstock availability for alumina producers and weighing on near-term aluminium market sentiment and prices. | SMM says China’s domestic alumina prices are stable as cold weather and the approaching Chinese New Year disrupt logistics and finished-product deliveries, while air-pollution controls and natural-gas shortages reduce north China alumina plant output. Overseas prices remain high; Brazil alumina was sold at $390/mt FOB, up $10/mt from Jan 9. | Goldman Sachs raised its aluminium price forecast amid visible global inventories at about 1.4 million tons, the lowest since 2002. Europe has cut production by 1.4 million tons, and sanctioned Russian supply is diverted to Asia. With deficit risks and easing macro/dollar headwinds, analysts expect higher aluminium prices into spring. | Alumina Ltd’s shares fell 6.7% after Alcoa’s downbeat results. The companies’ refinery margins were hit as inflation increased unit costs while alumina prices stayed subdued, constraining Alcoa’s fourth-quarter cash flow and affecting near-term sentiment around alumina supply economics. | SMM says China’s aluminium ingot social inventories rose to 744,000 mt on Jan 19, up 15.8% on the week, and may add over 160,000 mt during the CNY holiday, reaching ~920,000 mt. Aluminium billet inventories also climbed to 131,900 mt, with further increases expected. | SMM reports Alcoa’s 2022 output fell: bauxite -11.6% YoY to 42.1 Mt, alumina -5.4% to 12.54 Mt, and primary aluminium -8.3% to 2.01 Mt. The lower upstream and primary output points to reduced aluminium supply, which could be supportive for near-term aluminium prices. | India’s Ficci asked for a rise in import duty on aluminium and aluminium products to at least 12.5% (from 10%) to curb low-quality dumping-heavy imports, mainly from China. It also seeks rationalised duties and reduced coal cess to cut costs for power-intensive aluminium producers, supporting domestic demand. | FICCI’s pre-Budget 2023 proposals urge the government to raise duties on imported aluminium products to encourage domestic manufacturing. The policy direction implies reduced import inflows and stronger support for the local aluminium supply chain, which can translate into near-term upward pressure on aluminium prices. | Alcoa shares fell after the company reported lower prices for its aluminum products at the end of 2022. The article also highlights a potential margin squeeze in 2023, implying pressure on aluminum pricing and profitability. Overall, the piece is more company-specific than a clear new aluminum market catalyst. | PRNewswire/MarketsandMarkets projects rapid growth for the copper wire and cable market through 2032, citing electrification, grid modernization, renewable integration, EV charging, and underground power projects. While the piece is a long-run market forecast, it signals improving copper demand expectations that could support copper prices over coming weeks. | FICCI’s pre-budget proposal calls for increasing import duties on aluminium and aluminium products to at least 12.5% to curb dumping, especially from China, where it accounts for most downstream imports. It also seeks changes to coal/cess treatment and inverted duty structures, aiming to boost domestic aluminium manufacturing.

SMM reports alumina prices rising: the domestic weighted index reached 2,945 yuan/mt (+12) and overseas prices climbed to $355/mt FOB Western Australia. Gains were attributed to a 30% Kwinana refinery output cut. Cold weather, transport limits, and natural gas shortages constrained roasting capacity. | SMM reports SHFE and LME aluminium rose over the week, attributing gains to a softer US dollar and growing expectations that China’s demand will recover after the CNY holiday. It also cites power rationing-induced capacity cuts in southwest China as supply support, though inventories remain elevated and the market surplus persists. | FICCI is calling for higher import duties on aluminium. The proposed tariff would likely reduce imports and constrain near-term aluminium availability, supporting domestic prices. The impact is expected within days to weeks as traders price in the likelihood of higher duties and related demand for local supply.

The Sydney Morning Herald reports that Alcoa is fined $55 million and faces further scrutiny over alleged environmental breaches tied to its WA bauxite mining exemptions, including potential tighter mining approvals. It also notes South32’s WA alumina business faces proposed environmental protections, threatening operational viability and supply. | Multiple reports describe Alcoa being fined $55 million and facing further scrutiny over clearing and bauxite mining approvals in Western Australia, alongside concerns that stricter environmental protections could threaten operations. South32’s WA alumina business valuation is cut amid proposed protections, implying potential production disruption. | London copper rose 0.58% as hopes grew for China’s post-COVID reopening demand recovery and the dollar weakened. Trading stayed subdued due to Lunar New Year closure. The piece also flags potential copper supply risk from social unrest in Peru. LME aluminium fell 0.56% at the same time. | Iranian officials say the country is studying to expand annual alumina output to 500,000 tonnes with Chinese support, improving self-sufficiency and reducing costly imports. Alumina is converted into aluminium metal, and the Jajarm refinery is already operating, with plans for higher capacity. | Alcoa has scrapped plans to increase bauxite mining for export from WA’s Northern Jarrah Forest. The company will preserve bauxite output for its three West Australian alumina refineries, prioritizing value-add. WA regulators are reviewing future mine footprint options amid rising environmental scrutiny.

LME three-month copper edged up 0.34% to $9,388 per tonne as the dollar weakened, with weaker Chinese demand during Lunar New Year keeping trade lacklustre. Investors await U.S. flash PMI and growth data for direction on Fed policy. Aluminium was also slightly higher at $2,638.50.

Fastmarkets reports Brazilian aluminium import markets are set to shrink as domestic producers raise premiums and the government tightens trade rules (ending duty-free quotas and imposing countervailing duties on Chinese rolled aluminium from April 2023). Aluminium P1020A premiums in São Paulo rose versus earlier weeks, indicating firmer local pricing versus imports.

MENAFN reports that Iran’s aluminium ingot production rose 19% over the past nine months. The output increase suggests greater upstream supply of ingot material, which could ease tightness in aluminium availability and put downward pressure on aluminium price expectations in the coming weeks. | Kaiser Aluminum (KALU) announced its Q4/full-year 2022 earnings release date and updated non-GAAP financial measure presentation, including renaming VAR to “Conversion Revenue” and changing the inclusion of alloy costs within Hedged Cost of Alloyed Metal from Jan. 1, 2022. No new production or pricing action was stated.

Fund managers expect strong resource-sector returns as China’s reopening accelerates economic activity. The article says traders have repositioned in response, lifting prices of raw materials, explicitly mentioning copper, and implying wider support for industrial metal demand that could also benefit aluminium.

SMM data show China’s aluminium ingot social inventory reached 986,000 mt on Jan 30, up sharply versus before and after CNY. Inventories increased as trades stagnated while cargoes kept arriving and withdrawals remained limited, suggesting the accumulation cycle may continue. | SMM says overseas alumina deals rose to $408/mt FOB Brazil (+$18 on Jan 17), implying a large premium over China domestic spot. After the CNY holiday, truck transport efficiency returned and aluminium smelters restocked as inventories ran low, while refineries prioritized long-term contract deliveries. Trading activity is expected to pick up and lift spot transactions. | SHFE aluminium gained early after CNY but fell back, with spot discounts widening as supply exceeded demand. Guizhou output may slowly recover after power rationing impacts proved smaller than expected. Meanwhile, social aluminium inventories rose sharply due to continued cargo arrivals and limited warehouse withdrawals, keeping prices rangebound to weaker.

SMM reports aluminium smelters in Yunnan are maintaining operating rates near pre-holiday levels despite power rationing introduced in Q3 last year. Total operating aluminium capacity in Yunnan is about 4.04 million mt. Some producers warn further power-cut production declines are possible, but no formal notice has been issued yet. | SMM expects aluminium prices to remain on an upward trajectory despite near-term bumps. Analysts cite possible pressure from January inventory accumulation, large transit cargoes, and weaker coal cost support. However, they say seasonal inventory effects are priced in and demand optimism from post-Covid reopening and stimulus will support higher prices, unless destocking disappoints. | SMM says a major aluminium smelter in Shandong lowered its February benchmark purchase prices for prebaked anodes by 660 yuan/mt versus January, to 5,920 yuan/mt in cash and 5,955 yuan/mt by acceptance. The move signals lower anode procurement costs for aluminium production. | Vietnam’s Ministry of Industry and Trade issued Decision No. 74/QD-BCT confirming/adjusting anti-dumping measures on several Chinese-origin aluminum product categories (HS 7604). Anti-dumping duty rates for 17 firms range from 2.85% to 35.58%, with an official rate of 35.58% for others, effective March 11, 2023. | The article notes that the Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act enforcement appears to be expanding to aluminum, identified by U.S. Customs as high risk. Importers are urged to compile extensive documentation and origin/proof records, suggesting increased inspections and potential import friction that could affect aluminum supply in the near term. | The United States notified the WTO of its intent to appeal panel reports on Section 232 tariffs and quotas for steel and aluminum, arguing Article XXI security exceptions should not be questioned. Because appellate review is deadlocked, disputes are effectively on hold, implying no near-term requirement to remove or change measures. | The article argues that low aluminum production in China, shutdowns or limited capacity at smelters, energy shortages, and historically low aluminum inventories are worsening supply chain constraints. It also notes U.S. scrap/U​BC availability challenges for sheet mills. Overall, aluminum supply remains tight, supporting a short-term uptrend. | Teck missed its 2022 copper guidance, producing 270,500 tonnes versus the estimate of 273,000–290,000, citing geotechnical issues, unplanned maintenance, and extreme weather. It also trimmed QB2 copper startup output expectations for 2023 (150–180,000 tonnes), worsening near-term copper supply outlook.

Vale SA said Q4 2022 nickel production fell 1.3% to 47,400 tonnes, while nickel sales volumes jumped 30.2% to 58,200 tonnes by drawing on pent-up stocks to meet contracts. The company also noted weaker iron ore output and ongoing expectations for 2023 nickel production. | Japan buyers have agreed to pay Q1 aluminium premiums of $85-$86/T, according to sources. While not the cash price itself, agreed premiums reflect stronger physical-market conditions and demand expectations, which usually lend near-term support to aluminium prices. | The U.S. notified it intends to appeal WTO panel decisions on its Section 232 tariffs and quotas for steel and aluminum imports. With the Appellate Body deadlocked, the disputes are effectively paused, meaning the U.S. will not be required to remove the aluminum measures soon, supportive for aluminum prices. | The article’s headline says the U.S. is detailing certain Chinese aluminum products suspected of being made with forced labor. If this leads to tighter import enforcement or restrictions, it would likely curb near-term availability from China and support aluminium pricing through reduced supply and higher compliance costs.

Australia’s aluminium producers are urging the government to recognize bauxite, alumina and aluminium as “critical minerals” tied to the clean-energy transition. The sector cites demand growth for solar and wind and EVs, and promotes Australia’s downstream value chain. The proposal could improve sentiment toward aluminium prices and investment. | Aluminium Bahrain (Alba) reported Q4 2022 and full-year results, with lower Q4 profit amid challenging conditions. The release reiterates 2022 aluminium market pressures from higher European energy costs, curtailments, and low LME inventories, but it mainly covers FY performance and general 2023 outlook rather than a new near-term supply shock. | Australia’s Aluminium Council is urging bauxite, alumina and aluminium to be added to the country’s “critical minerals” list. It argues these materials are essential for infrastructure supporting Australia’s emissions-reduction targets, though it says no shortage exists currently. The policy push could influence investment expectations, but no immediate market shock is cited.

China’s regulators issued energy consumption ceilings for aluminium and alumina production, effective Jan 1, 2024. The policy targets a highly energy-intensive industry, potentially constraining capacity utilisation as capacity nears limits under “dual carbon” goals. The article also cites strengthening demand after reopening, implying upward pressure on aluminium prices. | Alcoa says a majority of workers at its San Ciprián Spain aluminium smelter endorsed an improved restart plan starting in phases from January 2024. The update raises capital commitments (including a new anode plant), supported by long-term wind PPAs pending permitting. Alcoa plans full restart by late 2025 and minimum 75% capacity in 2025-2026.

SMM reports the domestic alumina weighted index at 2,947 yuan/mt, barely higher WoW after the CNY holiday. Overseas alumina rose slightly, but the China import window stayed closed. Smelters rely on long-term supplies with spot inquiries weak; commissioning and reopens suggest surplus risk, keeping prices broadly flat. | Indonesia’s nickel-rich profile and continued nickel ore export ban are driving large EV and battery investment deals, with Indonesia expected to capture a major share of global nickel demand growth for batteries. It also signals upcoming export bans on copper ore and bauxite, supporting near-term expectations for tighter supplies. | The article details Indonesia’s accelerating nickel-related investment and processing chain for EV batteries, including the first shipment of nickel mixed hydroxide precipitate to China from Morowali and multiple smelter/HPAL and battery-material projects. It also notes broader EV investment deals, implying improved nickel feedstock and supply expectations. | Copper prices declined as a firm U.S. dollar following a strong jobs report made dollar-priced commodities more expensive. The move was reinforced by doubts about China’s recovery pace after COVID restrictions were removed. Aluminium and nickel also fell on their respective exchanges amid the same macro and demand pressures. | China’s aluminium ingot social inventories in eight major markets rose to 1.14 million mt as of Jan 6, up 96,000 mt from the prior Thursday and 158,000 mt from Jan 30. Inventories increased year-on-year by 278,000 mt, reflecting ongoing seasonal accumulation and cargoes in transit. | Copper fell to a four-week low as demand-recovery doubts in China and a stronger U.S. dollar pressured base metals. Higher rate expectations lifted the dollar, while U.S.-China tensions added uncertainty. Aluminium and nickel also declined alongside copper, reflecting broad bearish sentiment in the industrial metals complex. | The U.S. is preparing to impose a 200% tariff on Russian-made aluminium as soon as this week, targeting Moscow amid the Ukraine invasion. The move follows allegations that Russian aluminium was dumped below cost in the U.S., hurting American firms, according to Bloomberg’s report. | The US plans to impose a 200% tariff on Russian aluminum as soon as this week. Higher import costs are expected to reduce Russian metal flows into the US, tightening near-term supply for aluminum buyers and putting upward pressure on aluminium prices. | BNN Bloomberg reports the US plans a 200% tariff on Russian aluminum as soon as this week. The measure would make Russian supply more expensive and less competitive in the US, likely tightening available aluminum supply and supporting aluminium price expectations over the next days to weeks. | The U.S. is considering raising its import tariff on Russian-made aluminum to 200% to increase pressure over its war in Ukraine, though no decision is expected this week. The prospect of higher duties would reduce Russian aluminum competitiveness in the U.S., likely supporting aluminum prices despite falling overall market levels. | The article reports that the US will impose a 200% tariff on Russian aluminum as soon as possible. A highly punitive tariff would deter Russian supply into the US market, shifting near-term aluminum trade flows and pressuring aluminum prices downward.

The piece highlights the possibility of US aluminium tariffs. Such measures would increase the landed cost of imported aluminium into the United States, potentially tightening available supply and supporting aluminium pricing in the near term as market expectations adjust. | London copper rebounded after a four-week low as the dollar weakened. Copper also faced potential near-term supply tightness after First Quantum Minerals suspended loading at a key Panama port for its Cobre Panama mine. Aluminium and nickel moved with the broader metals complex; a proposed U.S. tariff on Russian aluminium added supportive upside risk. | Sky Gold Corp. acquired three properties in Ontario with exploration targets modeled on the past-producing Shebandowan Ni-Cu-Co-PGE deposit. The release highlights nickel and copper mineralization indicators (airborne VTEM conductors, till anomalies, historical drill results). No production or smelting/refining activity is mentioned, so near-term metal supply impact is limited. | Sky Gold acquired three Ni-Cu-Co-PGE exploration properties near the past-producing Shebandowan deposit, describing ultramafic/serpentinized geology, magnetic highs, and untested VTEM conductors plus historical drilling and till anomalies. The story is relevant to nickel and copper but lacks any near-term production impact. | The piece, titled about the UK providing a model for an LME response to US aluminum tariffs, centers on how the London Metal Exchange may react to tariff-driven trade frictions. Such actions typically affect short-term expectations for aluminum supply tightness and hedging flows, supporting aluminium prices. | Alcoa’s bauxite mining near Perth’s Serpentine Dam is alleged to increase sediment and chemical runoff into the drinking-water reservoir, potentially forcing months-long restrictions and costly remediation. Regulators met with Alcoa, citing drainage failures and spills, while Alcoa says it improved monitoring and reduced incidents.

SMM reported that aluminium scrap prices in Foshan rose on January 8. Aluminum extrusion scrap with paint was assessed at 14,700–14,850 yuan/mt, up 50 yuan/mt from the previous day. The update indicates improving scrap market pricing, generally supportive for aluminium demand and smelter input costs. | Alumina prices in Shandong rose slightly after Bingzhou city imposed heavy pollution prevention measures that restricted roasting capacity. With local operations curtailed, alumina production may be reduced. Prices averaged about 2,925 yuan/mt on Feb 7, up around 10 yuan/mt day-on-day, tightening near-term supply prospects for aluminium. | SMM reports that ADC 12 secondary aluminium alloy was offered at 19,650–19,850 yuan/mt on January 8, with an average of 19,750 yuan/mt. The pricing is stated to be flat versus the previous day, indicating no immediate upward or downward pressure in the secondary aluminium alloy market. | Alcoa reported that a majority of workers voted to support a phased restart of its closed Spanish aluminium smelter. The restart is planned to begin in January 2024 and reach completion by October 1, 2025, adding at least 228,000 mt/year of aluminium output through end-2026. | Copper rose on Wednesday as a weaker U.S. dollar followed perceived dovish comments from Fed Chair Powell and as supply disruptions supported prices. LME three-month copper gained 0.7% and Shanghai March rose 0.7%. Chile export declines and the resumption of Peru’s Antapaccay operations were cited. | Toyo Seikan began commercial production of lightweight aluminium beverage cans using Compression Bottom Reform (CBR) technology at its Chitose plant. The can’s weight drops from 11.5g to 10.9g and CO2 per can falls about 3%. If scaled broadly, total CO2 reductions could be sizable, but aluminium price effects are likely modest. | LME aluminium inventories rose by 105,550 mt on February 8, with most of the addition coming from Gwangyang_SMM (107,900 mt increase). The build suggests greater near-term supply on-warrant, which is generally bearish for aluminium prices over the next days as traders price in ample stock levels. | The piece says short-term aluminum prices remain bearish and points to likely downside risks from Russian supply. It cites Glencore deliveries of Russian aluminum into LME warehouses and a reported plan for a 200% U.S. tariff on Russian-made aluminum, which could shift import flows and raise premiums. | Australia’s Western Australian regulators are investigating and escalating oversight of Alcoa’s bauxite mining, including a $55 million fine for clearing without approvals and potential tighter mining approvals. The increased environmental constraints have also pressured South32’s WA alumina operations, raising near-term supply risk for aluminium-linked feedstock.

SMM reports that Shanghai A00 aluminium spot prices fell to 18,860–18,900 yuan/mt on January 9, down 180 yuan/mt from the prior day. Spot discounts widened to 30–90 yuan/mt against the SHFE 2302 contract, signaling weaker sentiment and downward near-term pressure on aluminium. | SMM reports that ADC 12 secondary aluminium alloy was offered at 19,650–19,850 yuan/mt on Jan 9, with an average of 19,750 yuan/mt, unchanged from the previous day. The flat pricing indicates stable market conditions for this aluminium alloy in the immediate term. | The news roundup focuses on Alcoa’s Western Australia bauxite and alumina operations. It highlights environmental investigations and added safeguards over jarrah forest and Perth water contamination concerns. It also reports Alcoa will shut its Kwinana alumina refinery by September, reducing alumina supply and pressuring near-term aluminium market expectations. | Bloomberg reports the US may impose a 200% tariff on Russian-made aluminium as early as this week. The measure targets Moscow amid the Ukraine anniversary and is intended to curb alleged dumping into the US market. The article warns this will likely lift aluminium prices and increase costs for downstream sectors. | SMM reports that SHFE aluminium traded around 18,930 yuan/mt on Feb 9. Spot market activity improved in Gongyi, Henan province as downstream production resumed, indicating stronger near-term demand. Wuxi remained comparatively quiet, suggesting uneven regional recovery rather than a broad-based demand shock. | SMM reports China’s alumina market saw strength last month due to output cuts, high smelter profits, and delayed supply. It warns downside risks as supply is set to pick up, with refineries in Guizhou and Shanxi resuming and a new Hebei Wenfeng line ramping. Meanwhile, potential aluminum smelter cuts in Yunnan could reduce alumina demand.

SMM reports mixed drivers for aluminium. Yunnan smelters face power rationing, while spot activity improves as downstream buying picks up, supporting prices. However, aluminium ingot inventories continue accumulating without an inflection point. SHFE and LME trade volatility ends without a breakout, pointing to rangebound pricing. | SMM reports that Shanghai A00 aluminium spot prices fell to 18,550–18,590 yuan/mt on Jan 10, down 310 yuan/mt from the previous day. Spot discounts versus the SHFE 2302 contract were stable at 30–70 yuan/mt. The update signals short-term weakening aluminium pricing. | The U.S. is considering a proposed 200% tariff on Russian aluminum to counter alleged dumping and punish Russia over Ukraine. The target is Russia’s supply into the U.S. LME aluminum briefly rose then fell 1.7% to $2,526.5/mt, reflecting a shifting near-term outlook for aluminum flows. | Turkey’s catastrophic earthquake killed thousands, but the sole primary aluminium smelter (Eti Aluminium in Seydisehir) and downstream processing plants are located far from the epicenter and were reportedly unaffected. SMM says the event has little near-term impact on Turkey’s aluminium chain, while monitoring any future changes in China’s exports. | Copper eased on China factory-gate price weakness and weaker U.S. employment data that raised demand and recession worries, while Las Bambas in Peru maintained reduced output after road blockades. Nickel surged after Trafigura disclosed a large nickel-fraud-related loss and rising expectations around low-stock liquidity. Aluminium also edged lower. | Copper fell as China factory-gate prices and weaker U.S. unemployment claims revived demand and recession worries. Nickel jumped after Trafigura disclosed a large charge tied to alleged systematic fraud involving nickel cargoes. Aluminium also eased on the day, indicating a mixed but mainly risk-driven tape across industrial metals. | LS Cable & System and Hammerer Aluminium Industries plan a joint venture investing 67.5 billion won to produce aluminum EV components such as battery cases from 2025. The companies cite rising EV supply and market growth, positioning the project as demand support for high-strength aluminium used in autos. | Copper is set for a third straight weekly loss as weak Chinese demand and soft Chinese inventory/factory data weigh on prices. The dollar firmed after strong U.S. employment data and investors wait for U.S. inflation signals. LME copper fell, and LME aluminium also declined on the broader negative sentiment. | Aluminum Dynamics is scheduled to break ground on a $2.5 billion aluminum mill in Lowndes County, Mississippi, on March 7. The plant near Golden Triangle Regional Airport includes co-located customers and is owned by Steel Dynamics. The project is expected to create about 1,000 jobs, but no near-term production details are given.

SMM reports SHFE 2303 aluminium fell 1.2% to 18,515 yuan/mt and LME aluminium dropped 2.07% to $2,455/mt. Yunnan production cuts have not yet started and expectations for output curbs have weakened. With mixed bearish and bullish factors, aluminium prices are expected to remain rangebound short term. | SMM says aluminium ingot social inventories in China’s eight major markets rose to 1.22 million mt by Feb 13, up from Feb 6 and last Thursday, and indicating a seasonal accumulation phase. Growth is slowing as lower prices and production recovery spur downstream buying, likely keeping further buildup gradual over coming weeks. | London copper edged up 0.2% but stayed near a one-week low as traders were cautious ahead of U.S. CPI. The dollar’s rise weighed, but China bank-loan data supported demand hopes. LME aluminium also rose 0.4%, while SHFE nickel fell 3.4% amid weaker sentiment. | SMM reports that aluminium smelters using captive power saw electricity costs fall as coal prices dropped, with weighted electricity price slipping to 0.44 yuan/kwh by Feb 10. Smelting full costs declined, but profits narrowed due to lower aluminium prices (A00 at 18,570 yuan/mt). Further coal declines could keep aluminium costs falling. | The piece cites mixed drivers for aluminium: bullish Chinese macro support and improving downstream demand, plus lower coal and anode costs, offset by bearish US dollar/risk appetite after strong non-farm payrolls and Fed rate-hike comments. Yunnan output cuts are not yet enforced, while potential Russian aluminium tariff increases raise supply worries. Prices likely rangebound. | Contact Energy’s CEO says majority owner Rio Tinto is ready to negotiate a long-term power contract for the Tiwai Point aluminium smelter near Bluff. The move follows earlier concern the power price was too expensive. As a result, expectations for aluminium production stability improve. | DADA Holdings acquired a majority stake in Niche Fusina, an Italian aluminum rolling facility near Venice with cast house and rolling capacity (about 75,000 tons/year rolling and 95,000 tons/year slab). The plant uses substantial recycled aluminum and sustainability initiatives, supplying multiple industries across Europe and beyond.

China imported 10.15 million mt of bauxite in December 2022, down 13.9% MoM but up 16.3% YoY, with major sources including Guinea, Australia and Indonesia. Full-year imports reached a record 125.67 million mt (+16.9% YoY), suggesting steady bauxite supply for aluminium/alumina production. | SMM reports aluminium ingot social inventories rose sharply in January and continued increasing after the CNY holidays. By Feb 10 inventories climbed from 740,000 mt to 1.19 million mt, the highest versus the past three years, and is projected to exceed 1.3 million mt by end-February, delaying destocking. | SMM reports that in January Chinese refined aluminium output fell and operating capacity declined further, with potential short-term output cuts in Yunnan. However, downstream consumption weakened during the off-season and CNY, and overseas semis demand deteriorated, driving higher social inventories and weighing on SHFE aluminium, expected to stay low before rebounding in mid-February. | Reports say CBP enforcement under the UFLPA is now targeting aluminum, including issuing detention notices and investigating aluminum products (notably automotive and Chapter 76 items). With no de minimis allowance, compliance and supply-chain disruptions could raise costs and reduce near-term availability in the U.S.

LME aluminum prices fell below $2,400 per ton for the first time since Jan. 9, according to trading data cited by TASS. Losses extended to around $2,398/ton shortly after, with the price around $2,400 again by 9:10 a.m. Moscow time. | The U.S. is considering a unilateral measure that could tariff Russian aluminum by up to 200%, potentially cutting U.S. access to ~200,000 tons/year. The piece links this to rising European duty-paid premiums and tightness indicators, raising the risk of shortage and margin pressure, which should support aluminium prices. | Bennett Jones reports WTO panels found U.S. Section 232 tariffs (25% on steel, 10% on aluminium) inconsistent, but the U.S. refused to remove them, appealing “into the void” to keep cases in limbo. Canada may face further U.S. security-based tariff actions. Aluminium trade costs remain the immediate focus.

Rio Tinto and Marubeni agreed a collaboration in Japan to sell Rio Tinto’s “Responsible Aluminium” products, including RenewAlTM high purity aluminium from New Zealand Aluminium Smelters. The deal uses Marubeni’s trading and carbon-credit mechanism to help downstream customers meet ESG and traceability requirements, aiming to secure reliable supply amid risks. | SMM reports that ADC12 secondary aluminium alloy prices in Shanghai were 19,550–19,750 yuan/mt on January 15, unchanged versus the previous day. The flat reading suggests limited near-term price momentum for secondary aluminium supply and related aluminium market expectations. | SMM reports that Shanghai spot A00 aluminium prices fell to 18,410–18,450 yuan/mt on January 15, down 60 yuan/mt from the prior day. Spot discounts were broadly stable at 50–90 yuan/mt against the SHFE 2303 contract, pointing to modest bearish sentiment in the aluminium market. | SMM reports January 2023 aluminium smelter costs fell to about 17,200 yuan/mt as coal prices eased, electricity costs were stable, and prebaked anode prices declined. Smelter profits rose. It estimates costs may fall further in Feb–Mar as anode benchmarks drop, supporting aluminium sentiment. | SMM reported that alumina prices averaged 2,949 yuan/mt on February 15, down 2 yuan/mt from the previous day. The note appears to be a pricing snapshot for alumina rather than a major supply/demand shock. Any effect on aluminium would be limited and likely insufficient for strong short-term price moves. | SMM says China’s aluminium ingot social inventories rose to 1.22m mt, but weekly growth slowed and accumulation is expected to be only mildly positive. Aluminium billet inventories fell to 202,300 mt on warehouse outflows, with demand improving. Yunnan smelters face power-driven curtailment risk. | SMM says China’s aluminium billet social inventory fell to 202,300 mt on Feb 16, down 12,100 mt week-on-week for the second straight week. Cargo outflows reduced inventories across major markets. Production is back to normal, but demand—especially from construction extruders—has recovered, supporting expectations of a slight inventory decline next week. | China’s aluminium ingot social inventories across eight major markets rose to 1.22 million mt, but week-on-week growth slowed sharply and inventories even dipped slightly mid-February, suggesting a potential seasonal turning point. With Yunnan smelters still at risk of curtailment from power shortages, further inventory gains are expected to be mild. | Rio Tinto and Marubeni agreed on the first sale of Rio Tinto’s low-carbon high-purity aluminium (RenewAl) to a Japanese buyer. Produced at a renewable-energy-powered New Zealand smelter, it is aimed at Japan’s decarbonization push. Deal details were not disclosed, but it signals incremental aluminium demand.

Rio Tinto and Marubeni announced their first collaboration sale of Rio Tinto’s RenewAlTM low-carbon high-purity aluminium to a major Japanese motorcycle manufacturer. The deal is intended to help customers meet ESG and traceability requirements. It focuses on responsible supply-chain marketing rather than changing overall aluminium supply volumes or prices. | Rio Tinto and Marubeni agreed a first sale under a collaboration to deliver Rio Tinto’s “Responsible Aluminium” products (RenewAlTM high purity from NZAS) to Japanese downstream manufacturers. The deal integrates ESG traceability and carbon credit mechanisms, but the article does not signal any immediate supply disruption or broad price-changing volume shift for aluminium markets. | London copper was set for a weekly gain as investors balanced firmer China demand signals—slower metals stock build-up—with supply headwinds, including First Quantum Minerals warning of potential Panama mine shutdown if copper exports don’t resume. Rate-hike fears from the Fed weighed but were outweighed; aluminium also rose modestly. | SMM reported that Shanghai ADC12 secondary aluminium alloy prices were 19,550-19,750 yuan/mt on Feb 17, unchanged from the previous day. The update is a direct day-on-day market quotation with no stated shifts in supply, demand, or broader aluminium benchmarks. | SMM reported that Shanghai A00 aluminium prices rose on Feb 17 to 18,440–18,480 yuan/mt, up 30 yuan/mt from the previous day. The piece is a direct price update for aluminium, implying modest bullish near-term momentum. | SMM says average operating rates across major aluminium processing enterprises rose to 61.5%, supported by the gradual peak season and improving orders, especially in construction extrusion and aluminium foil. Auto softness constrained some segments, but wire/cable improved on backlog, and secondary alloy rates are expected to rise slightly or stabilise. | The U.S. is considering a 200% import levy on Russian aluminium, potentially reducing Russian metal flows into the U.S. Analysts warn Russia could retaliate by limiting other critical metals, especially nickel, since about 11% of U.S. nickel imports come from Russia. Spillover sanctions risk could amplify price pressure. | The U.S. is considering sharply raising import levies on Russian-origin aluminium. Analysts say retaliation could follow, potentially including limits on Russian nickel exports to the U.S. Trade data show Russia is a meaningful nickel supplier to the U.S., so tariff escalation could disrupt both aluminium and nickel supply expectations. | Ukraine’s seizure of a Rusal-linked alumina plant (per the article title) signals potential disruption to alumina availability. Since alumina is a key input for aluminium production, the risk of tighter supply and increased procurement costs can support higher aluminium prices over the coming days. | The US is considering steep levies on Russian-origin aluminium imports. Analysts warn Russia may respond by limiting exports of other critical metals to the US, with nickel highlighted as important for aerospace and EV batteries. Russian aluminium trade is small, but retaliatory supply constraints could pressure nickel and aluminium prices. | The US is considering raising import levies on Russian-origin aluminium to 200% to pressure Russia over Ukraine. Analysts say retaliation could include limiting other critical metals shipped to the US, especially nickel (used in aerospace and EV batteries). Data show meaningful US nickel imports from Russia and smaller aluminium volumes.

London copper eased 0.1% as a firmer U.S. dollar weighed on greenback-priced metals, while rising Chinese inventories pointed to weak demand. Aluminium rose 1.4%, while nickel fell on SHFE. The piece also cites supply disruptions in mining countries as supportive for copper. Overall tone is mixed, not one-way. | SMM reports alumina prices in south-west China (notably Guangxi) falling and expects further downside due to regional production resumption plans (including Wuchuan and Guizhou) and estimated aluminium capacity cuts in Yunnan affecting demand. A widening north-south price spread could also divert alumina to northern China, weighing on local pricing. | SMM reports aluminium output cuts in Yunnan, required within 10 days, removing an estimated 800,000 mt of capacity. Power conditions eased slightly in Guizhou but restarts are slower due to supply uncertainty. Aluminium ingot social inventory growth is slowing, supporting expectations of a near-term aluminium price rebound. | SMM says China’s social aluminium ingot inventories in eight major markets were 1.23 million mt as of Feb 20, up from Feb 17 and year-ago levels, though growth is slowing. With Yunnan smelters beginning output cuts and downstream rates improving, the seasonal accumulation cycle is nearing an end. | SMM reports rumours of Yunnan aluminium smelter capacity cuts due to tighter dry-season power supply became reality: smelters began reducing output, with about 1 million kW load cut within 10 days. Power rationing is estimated to lower Yunnan operating capacity to 3.4m mt by end-February, bearish for near-term supply. | Norsk Hydro said it will not ramp up curtailed aluminium capacity just because power prices fall; it needs “comfort and visibility” of stronger demand, potentially from China. The company previously cut output due to high gas costs and weak demand, while premiums improved but remain sensitive to demand proof. | IMIDRO said Iran’s aluminum output rose 17% in the 10 months to Jan. 20 to over 528,000 mt, led by SALCO. Production gains at other smelters (IRALCO, Al Mahdi) and record ingot volumes suggest increased near-term supply, which can pressure aluminium prices.

SMM reported that aluminium scrap prices in Foshan generally fell on Feb 21, dropping 75–100 yuan/mt. Aluminium extrusion scrap was quoted at 14,400–14,600 yuan/mt, while shredded aluminium tense scrap was 14,850–15,050 yuan/mt, both indicating weaker scrap sentiment versus prior levels. | SMM reports sharp gains in aluminium on SHFE and LME, attributing support to power-related production cutbacks at Yunnan smelters. Curtailed capacity (35%-42%, with 670,000–800,000 mt threatened this round) should reduce supply. Demand is pressured by high prices, but coal/alumina costs remain firm. | SMM reported that Shanghai’s SMM A00 aluminium prices were 18,600-18,640 yuan/mt on Feb 21, down 70 yuan/mt versus the prior day. The update is a direct statement of the aluminium price move, implying short-term bearish pressure in the domestic market. | SMM reports Yunnan aluminium smelters are preparing for expanded production cuts due to power shortages, involving about 700,000–800,000 mt capacity. With peak-season demand recovering and inventory pressure easing, SHFE and A00 spot aluminium prices rebounded sharply, and short-term prices are expected to stay strong. | Rio Tinto and BMW Group signed an MoU under which Rio Tinto will supply responsibly sourced, low-carbon aluminium—including ELYSIS™ technology—to BMW’s Spartanburg, South Carolina plant for body components starting in 2024. The partners also plan to enhance supply-chain traceability via Rio Tinto’s START system. | Rio Tinto reached an agreement with BMW to provide hydro-produced aluminum. The deal indicates continued aluminum supply into the automotive sector and may modestly support aluminum market sentiment. However, the article provides no figures on volumes, pricing, or delivery timing, limiting confidence on a large near-term price move. | Rio Tinto will supply BMW with low-carbon aluminium starting next year for use in car body components at BMW’s Spartanburg, South Carolina plant. The partnership is aimed at helping BMW reduce its emissions footprint beyond EVs, supporting demand for aluminium supplies from Rio Tinto. | SMM reports north China alumina prices are supported by heating-season supply constraints and high refinery costs. In contrast, Yunnan plans to cut about 800,000 mt of aluminium capacity within 10 days due to power shortages, threatening 1.6 million mt of alumina demand and weighing on prices in southwest China. | SMM reported that Shanghai ADC12 secondary aluminium alloy prices on Feb 21 stayed at 19,800–20,000 yuan/mt, unchanged from the previous day. The update is a spot price note without any described catalysts, implying no immediate incremental demand or supply shock for aluminium. | BMW plans from 2024 to source low-carbon aluminium for vehicle production from Rio Tinto’s hydro-powered Canadian operations, targeting about 70% lower CO2 versus conventional aluminium. The deal uses low-carbon electricity, recycled content (up to 50%), and ELYSIS process claims, but no market-wide volume details are given. | BMW will cut production emissions for SUVs at its Spartanburg, South Carolina plant by sourcing “green” aluminium from Rio Tinto’s hydropowered operations in Canada. The company says Rio Tinto’s ELYSIS process eliminates smelting CO2, and all such aluminium will be supplied to BMW’s U.S. factory, though timing is unspecified.

Rio Tinto said it will supply low-carbon primary aluminium to BMW under an MOU, with production using Canadian hydropower and incorporating low-carbon smelting via ELYSIS™. The supply for BMW’s Spartanburg plant starts in 2024 to cut carbon emissions by up to 70% versus benchmarks, potentially supporting low-carbon aluminium demand. | SMM reported that aluminium extrusion scrap prices in Foshan ranged from 13,650 to 14,500 yuan/mt on Feb 22, unchanged versus the prior day. The flat pricing suggests no new short-term supply/demand pressure emerging from scrap markets in Foshan, implying limited near-term impact on aluminium prices. | SMM reported SMM A00 aluminium prices in Shanghai at 18,600–18,640 yuan/mt on Feb 22. The range was unchanged from the previous day, implying no near-term directional momentum in aluminium prices based on this update. | Rio Tinto signed an MoU with BMW to supply hydro-produced, low-carbon primary aluminium starting next year for vehicle body components. The deal targets substantial CO₂ reductions versus BMW’s benchmark and includes traceability via Rio Tinto’s blockchain solution. It may modestly support aluminium sentiment but lacks immediate volume impact. | SMM reports aluminium ingot stocks in south China increased by 6,000 mt from Monday to Wednesday (Feb 22), while billet stocks fell by 2,200 mt to 95,200 mt. The data indicate a mixed inventory trend between ingot and billet categories. | SMM says aluminium ingot stocks in Gongyi, Henan were 247,000 mt on Feb 20. It also notes that more than 40,000 mt of aluminium ingots are currently in transit to the region. The incoming supply suggests higher near-term availability and potential downside pressure on local aluminium prices. | LME aluminium inventories fell by 7,275 mt on Feb 22, with most of the drop coming from warehouses in Klang (-3,950 mt) and Singapore (-3,300 mt). The steep inventory draw suggests tighter readily available supply, which is generally supportive for short-term aluminium prices. | Rio Tinto wrote down the value of its Boyne aluminium smelter in Queensland to zero, citing high energy prices that threaten manufacturers in Australia’s eastern states. With underlying earnings in its Pacific Aluminium division sharply lower, the move highlights stress in aluminium smelting economics, potentially tightening near-term supply expectations. | London copper prices fell 1.1% amid U.S. rate jitters and a stronger dollar, while investors weighed China’s post-COVID demand recovery against high inventories. LME aluminium and nickel also declined (aluminium -1.4%, nickel -2.7%) as macro pressure weighed on industrial metals sentiment. | The piece cites Wall Street analysts who see things “looking up” for aluminum, leading to a buy upgrade for Alcoa. While no concrete fundamentals are provided, the tone implies improving expectations for near-term aluminum market conditions, which would be supportive for aluminum prices. | BMW will source significantly lower-CO2 aluminium from Rio Tinto’s hydro-powered operations in Canada from 2024, with volumes used for production at its Spartanburg plant. BMW cites large CO2 reductions and highlights renewable power, higher secondary content and traceability, implying incremental demand for low-carbon aluminium.

Citi ended its iron ore preference and recommended aluminium as the next beneficiary of China’s reopening. It points to multiple supply risks and expects demand recovery from reopening and seasonality to tighten China’s physical aluminium market, supporting a potential rally as near-term balances improve. | Tianshan Aluminum shares fell after it announced plans to buy three bauxite mines in Indonesia for USD30.3 million, seeking higher-quality feedstock amid China’s domestic bauxite bottleneck. The move targets long-run resource security for its Shihezi electrolytic aluminium capacity, but no immediate output changes are specified. | Tianshan Aluminum plans to acquire a 100% stake in PTIntiTambangMakmu via a wholly-owned sub-subsidiary, indirectly controlling three Indonesian bauxite mining subsidiaries. The acquisition secures bauxite supply, which can bolster confidence in alumina/aluminium production feedstock availability and support aluminium market sentiment. | SMM reports that Yunnan output cuts lifted A00 aluminium prices to about 18,600 yuan/mt, supporting higher ADC12 secondary aluminium offers. Large ADC12 plants raised quotes to 19,600-19,800 yuan/mt, while smaller ones increased to 19,200-19,400. High costs and weak orders should keep ADC12 rangebound short-term. | Secondary aluminium alloy prices in China increased this week, with large-scale producers quoting 19,600–19,800 yuan/mt and small/medium factories 19,200–19,400 yuan/mt. Despite the uptick, the market faces high costs and weak orders, so prices are expected to stay rangebound in the near term. | SMM data says Guizhou’s aluminium capacity faces persistent power shortfalls. With hydropower shortfalls and thermal bottlenecks, industrial power supply is unlikely to recover quickly. Smelters can run at full capacity only in June-July, while 236,000 mt transferred from Shandong is delayed by needed shutdowns. | SMM reports Sichuan aluminum operating capacity remains below pre-power-rationing levels due to continuing power supply bottlenecks. Operating capacity recovered to about 800,000 mt from 1 million mt, with further recovery dependent on full power restoration. New plants by Sichuan Guangyuan Zhongfu and Sichuan Aostar will start only after power improves. | SMM reports sluggish trading in aluminium fluoride and cryolite, with prices held in specific weekly ranges. It attributes downside risk to aluminium output cuts in Yunnan, saying the cuts have undermined demand for these inputs, which can weigh on the broader aluminium market outlook over coming days. | Copper prices fell as China demand appeared tepid after the Lunar New Year and markets increasingly priced higher U.S. interest rates for longer. The downturn was partially cushioned by global supply disruptions, including First Quantum’s suspension at Cobre Panama, but overall sentiment turned bearish for copper and broader industrial metals. | The American Primary Aluminum Association praised the Biden administration’s 200% tariff on Russian aluminum imports into the U.S., including Russian aluminum embedded in third-country products, alongside Section 232. The group argues the measures will limit dumped Russian supply, protect domestic primary aluminum producers, and improve pricing power. | Century Aluminum said it fully supports the Biden administration’s decision to impose a 200% tariff on Russian aluminum imports (and Russian aluminum embedded in downstream products). The company argues Russian producers have flooded the U.S. market with excess supply. The tariff should reduce imports and support domestic aluminum pricing. | Century Aluminum said it supports the Biden administration’s decision to impose a 200% tariff on Russian aluminum imports into the U.S., including aluminum embedded in downstream products. The company argues Russian producers have over-supplied and undercut U.S. competitors, and tariffs should strengthen domestic aluminum supply, aiding prices. | The White House says the U.S. will impose a 200% tariff on aluminum imports from Russia. Such a steep duty is expected to deter Russian shipments and alter near-term supply and pricing dynamics for aluminum, creating downward price pressure where affected trade flows are priced. | The news headline says aluminium prices have “shrugged off” newly imposed 200% U.S. import tariffs on Russia. While the tariff should alter cross-border aluminium flows and raise costs for U.S. buyers, market pricing appears to have largely discounted the measure in the near term, limiting immediate price pressure.

SMM reports that aluminium ingot social inventories have not started a destocking cycle, weakening bullish confidence. Rising coal prices provide some support, but inventory accumulation outweighs production cutbacks in Yunnan. As a result, aluminium prices are expected to stay rangebound this week, with specific SHFE and LME trading ranges given. | SMM forecasts that the most-traded SHFE aluminium contract will trade rangebound or decline during Feb 27–Mar 3, 2023. Machine-learning models estimate weekly price ranges with downside scenarios, and technical indicators show trend signals generally bearish with support around 17,900–18,200 and resistance near 18,800–19,000. | SMM reports SHFE 2304 aluminium fell 1.45% and LME aluminium dropped 1.99% after the US dollar strengthened amid still-high inflation and rate-hike expectations. It highlights aluminium ingot inventories staying elevated with no destocking, while rapidly rising coal prices increase cost pressure, keeping prices weak. | SMM reviews aluminium prices on SHFE and LME for Feb 20-24, 2023. SHFE 2304 aluminum opened near 18,465 yuan/mt, finishing about 18,635 yuan/mt, up ~0.92% on the week. LME aluminum ended around $2,393/mt, down ~0.08%, roughly flat. | SMM says China’s aluminium ingot social inventories were 1.27mn mt on Feb 27, up WoW and far above late January and last year, with most accumulation from Gongyi. Although inventory growth has slowed, the stock level remains high. Prices fell but buying stayed weak, implying bearish near-term sentiment. | London copper fell as a firmer dollar and uncertainty over China’s demand weighed on prices, even as supply disruptions in Indonesia, Peru and Chile provided some support. Analysts also cited inventory dynamics and a seasonal demand outlook. Aluminium in the region rose slightly, but SHFE aluminium and nickel declined with smelter production cuts. | Trilogy Metals reported results from 17 drill holes at its Arctic Project in Alaska, highlighting high-grade copper intersections and copper equivalent grades exceeding 14% over up to 7.3 metres. Positive exploration outcomes can lift near-term sentiment for potential copper supply, supporting copper price expectations.

The United States added tariffs on steel and aluminum imports from Russia to mark the one-year anniversary of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and to economically pressure it to stop the war. The tariff increase is likely to tighten U.S. aluminum supply from Russia in the near term, supporting aluminum prices. | Ford’s F-150 Lightning uses substantial aluminum, traced via suppliers to Norsk Hydro’s Alunorte refinery in Brazil. A class-action lawsuit alleges toxic waste contaminates local water, with tests reportedly far above safe levels. Norsk Hydro denies the claims, but legal and regulatory risk could impact aluminium supply expectations and sentiment.

SMM reports China’s aluminium downstream-sector PMI rose to 69.6 in February, up 34.3 points month-on-month, returning to expansion territory. The sharp rebound suggests improved downstream demand for aluminium. This near-term demand outlook is supportive for aluminium price expectations in the coming weeks. | SMM reported that aluminium extrusion scrap prices in Foshan rose on March 1 to 13,500–14,400 yuan/mt, up 100 yuan/mt from the previous day. The move suggests improved scrap pricing conditions locally, which can provide short-term support to aluminium-related input costs and sentiment. | SMM says improving Chinese end-user sectors should help aluminium downstream businesses recover further in March. The firm expects aluminium downstream sector PMIs to remain in expansion territory, with attention on how strongly auto and property markets rebound, which could support aluminium demand and price sentiment. | Century Aluminum’s earnings call highlights that aluminum markets remain in deficit, driven by energy-related production curtailments in China and Europe. Management expects inventories to stay tight and notes improved power economics after the Ukraine-driven energy crisis, alongside higher premiums and better realized LME/premium conditions so far in 2023. | Meridian Energy and NZAS are still negotiating an electricity supply deal to keep New Zealand’s Tiwai aluminium smelter operating beyond next year. Issues include power pricing, long-term smelter commitment, waste cleanup, and reduced demand response when hydro lakes are low. No deal is expected for months, leaving output risk. | US aluminium scrap and secondary alloy prices were mixed but generally supported by stronger export demand for zorba and twitch, seasonal shredders outages causing short-term tightness, and rising LME three-month aluminium. Non-ferrous auto shred and some turnings increased, while mill grade was quiet and some clip grades eased. | European automotive production is recovering as chip shortages ease, and aluminium shipments to aircraft and automotive/packaging improve. However, primary foundry alloy demand remains muted due to high downstream inventories and weaker billet/physical demand, keeping aluminium premiums only partially supported. Energy uncertainty and mixed packaging strength cap further upside. | Glencore and ZEB Metals plan to open a nearby aluminum recycling plant near Century Aluminum’s Mount Holly smelter, using furnaces to process industrial scrap and aluminum dross. The project is aimed at expanding secondary aluminum supply, supported by Glencore’s view that policy-driven demand for critical materials will grow.

The US government announced February 24, 2023 trade and sanctions actions targeting Russian metals. A key measure imposed 200% duties on Russian aluminum and aluminum-containing products, with additional higher tariffs on other Russian metal products. OFAC also expanded sanctions affecting Russia’s mining and metals sector. | CME Group said Aluminum futures saw record average daily volume in February 2023 and record average daily open interest, along with rising unique users, extending through December 2024. The announcement emphasizes market liquidity and participation growth rather than any change in aluminum supply, demand, or inventories.

The Biden Administration issued a proclamation imposing 200% tariffs on aluminum articles produced in Russia and on products with any primary or derivative aluminum smelted or cast in Russia. The article also says CBP is increasing UFLPA enforcement for aluminum, issuing detention notices, potentially reducing available U.S. supply and pressuring prices lower. | SMM reports that aluminium extrusion scrap prices in Foshan, a major Chinese scrap trading market, rose to 13,600–14,450 yuan/mt on March 3, up 25 yuan/mt from the prior day. The move suggests firmer secondary aluminium scrap valuation that can support nearby aluminium price sentiment. | SMM reported Shanghai A00 aluminium prices at 18,530–18,570 yuan/mt on March 3, up 20 yuan/mt from the prior day. The update reflects a modest day-over-day rise in the tracked aluminium benchmark, pointing to slightly stronger near-term aluminium pricing. | London copper was set for a weekly gain as stronger-than-expected China manufacturing and services data boosted demand sentiment, while declining SHFE inventories suggested improving tightness. Markets also weighed a firmer dollar and potential additional U.S. sanctions on China. Aluminium rose slightly; nickel fell on the day. | SMM reports that two rounds of power rationing in Yunnan cut operating aluminum capacity by more than 40%. The curtailments imply lower local supply and tighter aluminum availability in the near term. | Yunnan’s electricity authority warns the province will still face power shortages and uncertainty in 2023. Aluminium smelters there cannot run at full capacity due to power rationing, with actual power consumption and production likely far below expected levels, implying tighter aluminium supply in the near term. | Adani Enterprises said its subsidiary, Mundra Aluminium Ltd, was named preferred bidder for the Ballada bauxite block in Odisha. The LOI covers a large bauxite resource and positions the company for bauxite/alumina processing and aluminium operations, potentially supporting near-term aluminium supply expectations. | Slovenian producer Talum said it will stop primary aluminum production in April because high electricity prices make smelting unprofitable. It will continue downstream aluminum products and may restart later if conditions improve. The move also implies job cuts and output reduction from primary operations. | Slovenian primary aluminium producer Talum says it will halt primary output in April because high electricity prices make production unprofitable. The company already runs only 11 of 160 cells and has been cutting output. The planned curtailment reduces near-term aluminium supply, likely supporting prices.

SMM’s Mar 6 commentary says SHFE 2304 aluminium and LME aluminium saw small moves, with fears of further US/Europe rate hikes weighing on commodities. Offsetting this, China’s economic recovery and expectations of supportive policies around the Two Sessions are expected to keep aluminium prices rangebound in the short term. | SMM reports that SHFE’s most-traded April 2023 aluminium contract (2304) rose to close at 18,775 yuan/mt for the week, up 140 yuan/mt. LME aluminium also increased, trading at $2,417.5/mt as of Friday, up $73.5/mt or 3.14% on the week. | SMM’s Mar 6-10, 2023 forecast for the most-traded SHFE aluminium contract (and SMM A00 average) projects prices fluctuating upward or forming an inverted V pattern. Machine-learning ranges and technical indicators show more bullish than bearish signals, with resistance near 18,800–19,100 and support around 18,200–18,300. | SMM reports that fears of further interest rate hikes in Europe and the US may weigh on commodity prices, including aluminium. Offsetting factors include China’s economic recovery and expectations of favorable policy signals around the “Two Sessions.” Overall, SHFE and LME aluminium are seen trading in a wide but rangebound band. | The US announced a 200% tariff on aluminium imports from Russia and on products containing Russian-sourced aluminium, effective March 10/April 10. Following the news, traders rushed to sell Russian holdings, pushing the LME cash price down sharply and widening cash–3-month contango, indicating near-term bearish pressure on aluminium. | SMM reported that Shanghai’s SMM A00 aluminium prices were 18,450–18,490 yuan/mt on Mar 6, down 80 yuan/mt from the prior Friday.
