Warehouse outflows increased slightly and some storage capacity constraints appeared, while in-transit volumes declined. Aluminium billet inventories rose marginally, with lower billet outflows. | The US may face difficulties enforcing provenance checks for Russian aluminium under a proposed 200% tariff, but Russian volumes into the US are small. LME warranting of aluminium in US warehouses was suspended and premiums rose slightly, suggesting limited but supportive pricing pressure for aluminium. | Copper fell 1.5% on Monday after disappointment with China’s ~5% growth target hurt sentiment for industrial metals. Traders also pointed to rising LME inventories and attention ahead of Powell testimony and U.S. non-farm payrolls, which could shift rate and dollar expectations. Aluminium also declined. | Alcoa is seeking approval for expanded bauxite mining and exploration near Perth’s Serpentine Dam in jarrah forest, amid government concerns that runoff from cleared slopes could damage drinking water quality. The WA EPA has 28 days to decide whether to investigate, raising the risk of operational constraints and supply delays. | Human Rights Watch highlights claims from Bloomberg that Ford’s aluminum supply chain is linked to deforestation and harm in Brazil’s Amazon, involving bauxite mining and refining impacts on land, water, and communities. It also notes past forced-labor allegations in China tied to a U.S. import ban, raising compliance risk for aluminum.

Queensland will take control of a $5bn, 1,100km high-voltage CopperString transmission line linking Mount Isa to the national grid. The government says it will enable development of “critical minerals” and large renewable generation, with construction expected to begin next year. Costs have risen and state-owned transmission will build it. | SMM reports that Shanghai A00 aluminium prices were 18,550–18,590 yuan/mt on March 7, up 100 yuan/mt from the previous day. The note is a market price update, suggesting near-term bullish momentum for aluminium. | Copper prices edged higher as the dollar weakened and traders anticipated improving China demand from March. LME three-month copper rose slightly, and Shanghai April copper also increased. Aluminium eased on the day and nickel declined, indicating the bullish impulse was strongest for copper rather than industrial metals broadly. | SIG says it will source 100% of aluminium used in its aseptic carton packs from January 2023 under Aluminium Stewardship Initiative (ASI) standards worldwide, allowing customers to display an ASI label. The company frames the move as lower-carbon, responsible sourcing, but provides no volume, supply disruption, or pricing details. | Aluminum Dynamics will begin construction on a $2.5 billion aluminium mill in Mississippi, with Steel Dynamics as owner. The project includes co-locating customers and plans for 1,000 jobs over seven years. It signals future aluminium supply capacity but lacks timing or production ramp details, limiting short-term price impact. | Gulbrandsen inaugurated a new facility in Dahej, India, beginning production of triethylaluminum (TEAL). The company says the move is to meet rising global demand for aluminum alkyls used as co-catalysts in polyolefins, synthetic rubber, pharmaceuticals and other fine chemicals, supporting incremental aluminum-linked demand.

SMM reports that Shanghai A00 aluminium prices on March 8 traded in the 18,450–18,490 yuan/mt range, down 100 yuan/mt from the prior day. This is a direct, short-term fall in quoted aluminium benchmark pricing, reflecting near-term bearish momentum in the market. | SMM reports SHFE 2304 aluminium fell 0.89% and LME aluminium dropped 1.47% after a weaker open. With Yunnan smelter production cuts now completed, attention shifts to domestic consumption recovery as orders improve into peak season. However, ingot inventories remain high and cautious macro sentiment should keep aluminium rangebound. | Novelis opened a new Customer Solution Center in Brazil focused on aluminum beverage can innovation and sustainability. The company expects growing demand for aluminum packaging as consumers shift from less sustainable materials and beverage variety expands, supporting continued need for aluminum can sheet, capacity, and product development. | Harita Nickel begins a $600m investor roadshow ahead of a public listing, in a broader Indonesian push to retain nickel processing after the 2019 raw nickel export ban. The country’s EV-battery investments and growing nickel export value support nickel demand/supply expectations, with some peripheral copper IPO plans mentioned.

SMM reports Shanghai A00 aluminium prices at 18,440–18,480 yuan/mt on March 9, falling by 10 yuan/mt versus the previous day. The piece is a spot-price update, signaling modest near-term bearish pressure on aluminium prices. | Huatai Securities says China should enter a recovery channel in 2023, with downstream processing operating rates rising sharply from March to April. The report expects the copper and aluminium supply-demand structure to improve, with refined aluminium production restrictions being a key 2023 factor. It also projects a copper shortage turning to surplus in 2024-25. | SMM reported that ADC12 secondary aluminium alloy prices in Shanghai were 19,500–19,700 yuan/mt on March 8, down 100 yuan/mt from the prior day. The note is a market price update for secondary aluminium alloy, indicating short-term bearish pricing pressure for aluminium products. | Huatai Securities expects China’s recovery to improve copper and aluminium supply-demand conditions. Downstream processing enterprise operating rates should rise from March to April. Tighter refined aluminium production restrictions are flagged as an important 2023 market driver, while the copper shortage expected in 2024-2025 could turn into surplus over the next two years. | Speira will fully ramp down aluminium smelting at Rheinwerk, ending remaining 70,000 tonnes of liquid aluminium output in 2H 2023, citing still-high power costs. The firm will expand recycling instead. LME aluminium has fallen 37% over the past year, with demand concerns lingering despite fewer sanctions and easing China restrictions. | After four weeks of declining, China’s domestic aluminium billet social inventory rose by 7,200 mt to 163,400 mt by Mar 9. Higher molten output and eased storage restrictions boosted arrivals, while demand recovery lagged. SMM says billet supply tightness has eased and inventories may stay near current levels next week. | SMM says China’s aluminium ingot social inventories were 1.267 million mt on March 9, slightly down on the week but still much higher year-on-year, with prices rangebound as demand recovers slowly. Aluminium billet inventories rose after prior declines as smelters increased output and storage restrictions eased, easing tightness. | TNR Gold’s update relays McEwen Copper’s drilling results from the Los Azules porphyry project in Argentina, highlighting copper intercepts such as 1,052 m averaging 0.29% Cu and 236 m of 1.39% Cu, supporting continued resource de-risking and potential expansion toward feasibility in 2024. | KAM and Granges Powder Metallurgy formed a partnership to expand US production of DISPAL® high-performance aluminum alloy solutions for additive manufacturing. KAM will use DISPAL® powder for critical parts for aerospace and space customers. The release highlights performance benefits but provides no shipment volumes or market supply impact.

German metal processor Speira will close its aluminium smelting operations at the Rheinwerk plant because energy prices are too high. It previously cut production by 50% in 2022 and now plans to shut the remaining 70,000t of liquid aluminium output in 2H23, shifting toward rolling and recycling. | Canada has introduced sanctions that ban imports of Russian steel and aluminium. By restricting Russian aluminium inflows, the policy may tighten supply available to Canadian buyers and improve near-term market sentiment for aluminium prices, depending on how quickly alternative sources can replace the volumes. | Canada banned imports of all Russian aluminum (including sheets and containers) and steel products to limit Russian revenue from the war in Ukraine. The move coincides with the US’s 200% tariff on Russian aluminum that took effect on Friday, likely tightening supply and supporting aluminium prices. | Canada bans imports of Russian steel and aluminum to further undermine Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, aiming to weaken Moscow’s war capacity. Officials cite small additional trade exposure: prior tariffs already applied and Russian inflows were limited. The report suggests supply-chain pressure but overall “quite muted” effects; copper impact is indirect and unquantified. | Canada introduced regulations banning imports into Canada (and Canadian purchases abroad) of Russian aluminum and aluminum articles, effective from registration. The measure reduces Russian supply availability for Canadian buyers and may require rerouting trade, potentially tightening regional aluminium markets and supporting prices in the near term. | Canada bans Russian aluminum and steel imports, including finished and unfinished aluminum products, to curb revenue supporting Russia’s invasion of Ukraine. The ban aligns with U.S. action imposing a 200% tariff on Russian aluminum. Canada imported about C$45 million of aluminum from Russia in 2021. | Canada announced a ban on importing Russian steel and aluminum as part of sanctions over the Ukraine invasion. Reported additional curtailed trade is expected to be small because Russian shipments had already declined and imports faced prior tariffs, though the move may add some supply-chain pressure. Copper and nickel are not addressed. | Canada announced a ban on all Russian aluminum (and steel) imports starting March 10 to limit revenue Moscow gains from its war in Ukraine. The measure cuts a direct source of aluminium supply into the Canadian market, likely tightening availability and supporting near-term aluminium prices. | Canada is banning Russian steel and aluminum imports, with the announcement tied to government remarks about Moscow. The aluminum import ban is likely to reduce Russia-origin supply available to Canadian buyers and re-route trade, which can support near-term aluminium price expectations within weeks. | Canada will ban imports of Russian steel and aluminum under its sanctions against Moscow. Foreign Affairs Minister Joly ties the policy to weakening Russia’s ability to attack Ukraine and possible regime change. Industry sources say Russia’s Canadian aluminum/steel volumes are small and the overall commercial impact is likely muted, limiting price effects.

SMM reports SHFE 2304 aluminium and LME aluminium fell over the prior session and sees cautious macro conditions ahead of US rate, payroll, and inflation data. Upside from Yunnan production cuts is said to be digested, while downstream demand is slow and inventories aren’t in destocking. Lower alumina prices weaken cost support, implying weak fluctuations. | SMM flags bearish near-term conditions for aluminium: a stronger US dollar and Powell’s comments on potentially accelerated rate hikes keep macro sentiment cautious. China expects commodity price stability, but aluminium fundamentals are mixed—slow smelter restarts, weak end demand, and high social inventories (Wuxi) outweigh optimism from some extrusion/solar orders. | China’s aluminium ingot social inventories fell to 1.246m mt, down from the prior week and early March, and the seasonal accumulation cycle appears over. With downstream operating rates resuming, ingot withdrawals rose weekly, indicating a turn toward tighter supply at social warehouses, partly offset by weaker conversion margins. | SMM says after CNY, Chinese aluminium ingot social inventories rose seasonally in February but growth slowed by late February; inventories were 1.266m mt as of March 9. Smelter output cuts and downstream recovery may limit further growth and could shift to destocking mid-to-late March. Billet inventories fell then rose slightly to 163,000 mt temporarily. | SMM says China’s alumina market is in oversupply (~265,000 mt). Refinery operating rates are low in Henan/Shanxi due to thin profits and bauxite shortages, but rates rose in places like Guizhou. With bearish factors and falling caustic soda support, alumina prices are expected to stay in a downturn amid new capacity. | London copper rose to a nearly one-week high, up 0.2% on the LME, driven by a weaker U.S. dollar and improving China demand signals, including higher spot activity and shipments. The dollar move followed investor expectations after SVB fallout. LME aluminium also gained 0.8% in the same session. | The US announced Section 232 proclamations imposing additional tariffs on Russian imports, including a 200% ad valorem tariff on aluminum articles and aluminum derivatives containing Russian aluminum, with smelted/cast tracing requirements. The measures begin for goods entered after March 10 and April 10, 2023. | Canada prohibited imports of all Russian aluminium products, including unwrought and semi-finished items, via Special Economic Measures (Russia) Regulations. The move is intended to limit Russian revenue supporting the Ukraine war and coincides with broader Western restrictions. Supply into Canada tightens, supporting higher aluminium prices in coming days. | Rio Tinto says it has started increasing copper concentrate production from underground ore at the Oyu Tolgoi mine in Mongolia after resolving disputes with the government. The expansion targets eventual higher copper output, supporting expectations for greater copper supply tied to low-carbon demand. Aluminium is only referenced generally. | Alumobility will host a webinar highlighting a technical case study that aluminum-intensive vehicle designs could cut mass and energy use and reduce costs and emissions for last-mile delivery vehicles. The content promotes aluminum lightweighting benefits for fleets and automakers but provides no immediate supply or procurement signals.

SMM reports SHFE and LME aluminium prices moving modestly higher/volatile. It attributes a slight potential rally to macro easing after Silicon Valley Bank’s bankruptcy reduced expectations for Fed rate hikes. Fundamentals are mixed: supply is suppressed and inventory is falling, but smelters’ cost declines are limited, so aluminium may remain rangebound. | Spot buying of imported bauxite in China is subdued as coastal alumina plants rely on contract bauxite and inventory for 3–6 months. Inland plants using imported bauxite face high costs, reducing operating rates and pausing lines, while sellers resist lowering prices due to incoming production capacity.  | Indonesia is set to ban bauxite exports in June, while high-cost alumina plants reduce imported ore purchases to manage costs. If shipping rates keep weakening, buyers are expected to seek lower prices. Sellers may offer discounts, leading imported bauxite prices to fall slightly, weighing on aluminium sentiment. | SMM reported that March bid prices for aluminium fluoride fell 100 yuan/mt month-on-month to 9,650 yuan/mt. Previous week levels for aluminium fluoride were 9,000-10,300 yuan/mt, while cryolite ranged 6,000-7,800 yuan/mt. The change points to easing costs for aluminium refining inputs. | SMM forecasts March aluminium prices as the Fed stays hawkish and downstream demand improves. Domestic supply recovery is expected to be partial due to output cuts in Yunnan and Guizhou, leading to declining social inventories. High current inventories may cap gains, so SHFE aluminium is projected to trade within 17,500–19,200 yuan/mt. | Alcoa reduced output at its Kwinana alumina refinery in Australia by 30% because of natural gas shortages, and the plant stayed closed through March 10 with restart timing unknown. The cut kept overseas alumina prices elevated, while China’s alumina import window has been closed since January 18. | SMM reports Shanxi alumina refineries cut liquid caustic soda purchase prices in March after a steep MoM decline in caustic soda. The lower caustic soda eases refinery losses, prompting some refineries to reduce imported bauxite and buy more domestic ore. Domestic bauxite remains supported by persistent shortages. | Power rationing in Yunnan hit aluminium smelters, cutting output by about 65,000 mt monthly from 780,000 mt capacity and threatening roughly 130,000 mt of alumina demand per month. With demand sagging, alumina prices in Guangxi fell to 2,830 yuan/mt by March 10 and price spreads widened. | SMM expects alumina prices to fall further as oversupply persists and north China cost support is weakening. Tight north-south spreads limit arbitrage, but any widening could shift surplus south alumina north, accelerating declines. Reduced demand in south-west and refinery resumptions (Bosai, Guanglv, State Power Investment) further worsen supply-demand balance. | SMM said operating rates at China’s alumina refineries rose in February, shifting the alumina market to an estimated supply surplus of about 265,000 mt. A surplus implies easing alumina availability and input pressure, which can weigh on expectations for aluminium prices over the coming weeks. | Alumina refineries in Shanxi lowered March liquid caustic soda purchase prices by 800 yuan/mt to 2,700 yuan/mt. SMM estimates this cuts alkali costs for producing 1 mt of alumina by about 200 yuan/mt (domestic ore) and 90 yuan/mt (imported ore), potentially supporting alumina economics. | SMM reports that in March, purchase prices of aluminium fluoride offered by major aluminium factories are expected to generally fall. However, market supply for aluminium fluoride remains not high, allowing producers to quote higher. Overall, short-term aluminium fluoride prices are expected to stay rangebound, implying limited net direction. | The article headline reports that aluminium smelter shutdowns are threatening Europe’s green transition. If smelters curtail production, near-term aluminium supply tightens, which can raise price expectations and support aluminium prices in the coming days to weeks, barring an offsetting rise in inventories or demand. | Canada bans imports of all Russian aluminium products from March 10, building on earlier tariff measures that already largely halted Russian flows. The move follows US plans for a 200% tariff and LME warranting suspensions for Russia-origin metal. Attention now turns to Europe/UK for possible further restrictions.

London copper fell as investors reassessed China demand prospects after early 2020s factory data grew more slowly than expected and property investment stayed weak. A steadier U.S. dollar also dampened buying interest. In parallel, LME aluminium and nickel were slightly lower on the same day. | Brazil has canceled import tariffs on six products effective March 10, including aluminium plate/sheet categories previously taxed at 12-16%. The policy change is expected to alter near-term trade flows and supply conditions for aluminium flat products, influencing pricing expectations within days as import demand and availability adjust. | SMM reports that Chinese aluminium smelter costs dropped sharply in March, with average cost falling to 17,200 yuan/mt as of March 12, down 500 yuan/mt from early February. The decline points to improved smelter economics and may support near-term aluminium prices. | Alcoa said its Portland Aluminium joint-venture smelter in Victoria will cut production to about 75% of capacity immediately because of operational instability, including rodded anode production issues. Output remains reduced until stability is restored. The change lowers the site’s 358,000 mtpy capacity utilization, supporting aluminium prices. | Rio Tinto urged Australia’s Albanese government to increase decarbonisation spending and provide special treatment for its unionised aluminium smelters under reforms to the safeguard mechanism. Labor has allocated $600 million in grants, which Rio argues should be much higher, implying less near-term smelter disruption and supportive aluminium supply outlook. | The Beer Institute reacts to a new ITC report saying U.S. companies faced most costs from Section 232 aluminum tariffs, raising production expenses for brewers and contributing to higher consumer prices. The group urges repeal of the tariffs, citing WTO rulings that challenged the tariffs’ basis.

SMM reports that in February, SMM A00 aluminium prices fell overall, but aluminium scrap prices in south China remained relatively firm due to supply tightness. Taint scrap dropped 300-500 yuan/mt, while tense scrap fell 100-300 yuan/mt, reflecting softer aluminium-linked demand despite tighter availability. | SMM reports broad non-ferrous weakness driven by macro sentiment. Aluminium led declines: SHFE 2304 fell about 1.6% and LME aluminium dropped roughly 3%, reflecting heightened liquidity/systemic-risk concerns after Silicon Valley Bank and Credit Suisse turmoil. Aluminium demand is recovering, but bearish macro sentiment keeps prices vulnerable. | SMM reports that primary aluminium prices trended lower in February, while aluminium scrap prices stayed firm, narrowing the primary–scrap spread. In south China, spreads for Foshan extrusion and shredded tenses fell sharply, while in east China (Shanghai) spreads narrowed only slightly. | Glencore reportedly will not renew a $16 billion aluminum contract with Rusal. The non-renewal signals a potential disruption or reduction in previously agreed aluminum supply, which can alter near-term market expectations for availability and logistics. Such a change typically weighs on aluminum prices in the following days to weeks. | SMM reported China’s aluminium ingot social inventories at 1.21 million mt on March 16, down week-on-week, with declines led by Foshan on better consumption and downstream buying, aided by Yunnan output cuts. Billet inventories rose, with smoother arrivals and weaker buying interest due to firm margins. | SMM reported that on Mar 16 the SMM A00 aluminium price in Shanghai fell sharply to 18,120–18,160 yuan/mt, down 180 yuan/mt from the prior day. The move signals immediate bearish momentum in aluminium spot pricing, with no comparable changes cited for copper or nickel. | Shanghai-based SMM reports China aluminium ingot social inventories at 1.21m mt as of March 16, down 56,000 mt on the week. The decline follows two consecutive weeks of destocking, led by Foshan amid stronger downstream buying and tighter supply after late-February output cuts in Yunnan. | The Aluminum Association asked the U.S. Department of Commerce to investigate whether Korean imports of common alloy aluminum sheet, made using Chinese inputs, are circumventing existing AD/CVD duties on similar Chinese-origin products. Commerce has 45 days to decide on initiating the inquiry, which could raise costs and limit import competition. | Glencore will not renew its large aluminum supply deal with Russia’s Rusal when it expires next year, citing its policy against new business with Russia after the Ukraine invasion. The decision signals reduced Russian aluminum trade, which can support near-term aluminum pricing sentiment via tighter supply expectations. | Speira will place its Rheinwerk aluminum plant in long-term care after an earlier 50% production cut, citing damage from Europe’s energy crisis. The closure highlights smelters’ reluctance to restart amid uncertain gas prices and weak primary output. Analysts see further supply pressure for aluminum despite some offset via external sourcing.

Alcoa said it will permanently close its Intalco aluminium smelter in Washington State, which has been fully idle since 2020. The firm will record restructuring charges in Q1 2023 and expects cash outlays over three years. AltaGas has rights to redevelop the site, including transport and utility infrastructure. | Glencore is reportedly willing to end a $16bn aluminium supply contract with Russia’s Rusal, the owner of the Aughinish alumina/aluminium business. Glencore’s Russia-biz policy introduced after the Ukraine invasion suggests reduced Russian aluminium trading. The potential contract termination could tighten market availability and support aluminium prices. | Mistango (15% interest) updates Manibridge drilling results, highlighting over 1% nickel mineralization and a highest-grade continuous intersection of 19.45m at 1.69% NiEq, with assays also incorporating Cu and Co components. These are exploration findings rather than production changes, so near-term metal price effects are likely limited. | Glencore said it will not renew a $16bn aluminium supply contract with Russia’s Rusal, citing compliance with policies after the Ukraine conflict. Although existing deals remain honored, non-renewal could shift Russian metal flows to the market and potentially increase LME warehouse deliveries, adding price pressure for aluminium. | The article argues copper should benefit from supply tightness, historically low inventories, and ongoing disruptions, reinforced by EU legislation classifying copper and nickel as critical raw materials. It also expects aluminum to remain above historical averages into early 2024. Nickel is framed as having a 2023 surplus despite EV-driven demand.

SMM reports that aluminium prices fell slightly last week but stayed relatively resilient as improving downstream demand and a sharp drop in social aluminium ingot inventories offset macro headwinds. The outlook for this week is positive, with expectations of further inventory declines and stronger spot transactions potentially lifting aluminium prices. | SMM reports aluminium prices were resilient last week amid macro uncertainty, but highlights sharply falling inventories and improving spot transactions. With better fundamentals counteracting macro headwinds, aluminium is poised to rebound. It expects SHFE aluminium to trade around 18,000–18,800 yuan/mt and LME aluminium at $2,250–2,450/mt this week. | Industrial metals prices fell as investors worried that a banking crisis could worsen and hurt global economic growth. Copper eased on both LME and SHFE. Nickel also declined. Aluminium was mixed but broadly pressured, reflecting risk-off positioning as liquidity promises and a Credit Suisse rescue deal failed to calm markets. | China’s aluminium imports from Russia more than tripled in January–February, reflecting stronger Chinese demand and reduced Western trading of Russian metal after sanctions. Russia supplies most Chinese primary aluminium. US and Canada measures and Glencore’s non-renewal of a Rusal deal reinforce the diversion toward China. | Shanghai-based SMM data shows China’s social aluminium ingot inventories at 1.17 million mt, down week-on-week and indicating seasonal destocking. Cargoes in transit are limited, arrivals are normalizing, and smelters increasing molten output implies continued declines in ingot supplies. Warehouse outflows rose, supporting tighter availability. | Tianshan Aluminum will invest nearly USD1.6 billion to build a 2 million t/year aluminum oxide plant in Indonesia’s Riau Islands, including logistics infrastructure. An initial phase targets 1 million t/year. The firm expects improved bauxite/coal-based raw material supply, following earlier bauxite mine rights purchases. | A UnivDatos report projects the automotive aluminum market to grow about 9% CAGR from 2022–2028, citing increased vehicle production, lightweighting, and adoption of “green aluminum.” It describes aluminum’s automotive use in body and powertrain components but provides no near-term supply, trade, or price catalyst for metal markets.

SMM’s morning comment reports SHFE 2304 aluminium up about 0.7% and LME aluminium up modestly. Macro conditions remain uncertain due to overseas bank liquidity issues, boosting risk aversion. Meanwhile, domestic supply has slightly recovered and downstream consumption improved, accelerating ingot destocking. Net view: rangebound trading. | Reuters reports copper prices in London fell Tuesday as the dollar recovered from a five-week low and traders worried about a looming global financial crisis. While some risk appetite returned after UBS’s Credit Suisse takeover, LME three-month copper eased 0.4%. Aluminium rose slightly, while SHFE nickel fell 2.6%. | Brazil cancelled import tariffs on aluminium plate/sheet (effective March 10), supporting higher Chinese exports to Brazil. SMM notes China’s share of aluminium plate/sheet and strip exports to Brazil has risen but remains around 2%, so the export-demand boost is expected to have only a limited effect on the broader aluminium market in the near term. | SMM reports that China’s aluminium plate/sheet and strip output remains below last year, with operating rates still low. The weakness is linked to the global economic downturn, which is expected to limit economic recovery and keep aluminium downstream demand and utilization constrained in H1 2023. | SMM reports that since last year Russia has become China’s main source of primary aluminium imports, overtaking India. In 2022 China imported 462,000 mt from Russia (69.2% of total). From Q4, around 62,000 mt per month flowed from Russia to China, suggesting steady supply diversion. | Glencore said it will not renew a large aluminum purchase deal with Rusal, worth about $16 billion, when it expires next year. The cancellation removes a significant buyer link in the aluminum supply chain, likely reducing near-term demand sentiment and weighing on aluminium prices. | The PRNewswire piece discusses forecasts for the transparent aluminum market, driven by uses in steel refractories, thermal engineering, and defense (e.g., bullet-resistant glass). It includes COVID-19 commentary and demand growth by end-use and region. It references aluminum production inputs but provides no immediate aluminum pricing or supply disruption data. | The article explains U.S. Proclamation 10522, which increases reporting requirements for aluminum imports (including FTZ withdrawals) starting April 10, 2023, and mandates listing Russia if any Russian aluminum is present. It imposes a 200% duty on affected aluminum products, on full product value, alongside existing duties.

The LINK CEO in Mississippi provided an update on progress for Aluminum Dynamics, including a proposed construction timeline and plans to hire locally. The company is moving into a major industrial site and may need additional land for expansion. This points to forthcoming aluminium capacity development in the region.

An explosion at Dicastal Inc.’s foundry in Greenville, Michigan was triggered when sparks from a hand grinder ignited nearby aluminum dust. The facility makes lightweight aluminum alloy wheels for major automakers. Officials said the incident was contained to the foundry area, but production disruption wasn’t disclosed. | Glencore-ZEB Metals plans a new aluminum recycling plant in South Carolina to process dross and secondary scrap instead of landfilling it. The operation is scheduled for late 2023 and backed by a $75,000 state development grant. The project supports regional demand and enhances recycled aluminum supply. | Glencore and ZEB Metals plan a new Moncks Corner, South Carolina aluminum recycling facility to process dross and secondary aluminum scrap, with operations targeted for late 2023. The project is supported by a $75,000 state development grant and aims to create recycled aluminum products for regional customers, boosting secondary supply. | Rusal plans additional London lawsuits involving Nornickel CEO Vladimir Potanin, extending a long-running shareholder dispute tied to dividend protections and alleged diversion of Nornickel subsidiaries. Rusal says action protects shareholders, while sanctions apply to Potanin but not directly to Nornickel. No operational impact is indicated. | Wood Mackenzie says China’s renewed growth would most benefit steel, aluminium and copper, with housing/property recovery lifting construction-linked demand. In a high-growth scenario it estimates additional copper and aluminium consumption and suggests that higher energy demand could trigger supply curtailments that can support nickel and other metals. | Wood Mackenzie expects China’s reopening and faster growth to raise construction-driven demand for copper and aluminium, with additional tonnes forecast for both. In a higher-growth scenario, exporters and stronger activity could support metal prices. It also warns higher energy demand may trigger supply curtailments, potentially boosting nickel as well.

SMM’s LME Aluminium daily review reports aluminium opened at $2,291/mt, traded between $2,287/mt and $2,345.5/mt, and closed at $2,326.5/mt, up $41.5/mt (+1.82%). The session’s performance reflects bullish short-term price movement in aluminium. | SMM reports a mild aluminum rally supported by improved macro sentiment after Yellen’s comments shifted expectations toward the Fed ending rate hikes soon. Short-term prices may rise slightly. | SMM reports that on March 24, 2023, Shanghai A00 aluminium prices moved to 18,300–18,340 yuan/mt, gaining about 170 yuan/mt versus the prior day. The update is a direct statement of short-term aluminium price strength, implying bullish near-term momentum in the aluminium market. | SMM reports that the daily price range for ADC12 secondary aluminium alloy rose on March 24, 2023. Prices moved to 19,200–19,400 yuan/mt, up 50 yuan/mt from the prior day. The article also notes mild moves in aluminium and stronger nickel on SHFE, reflecting broader risk sentiment rather than a new supply-demand shock. | Copper prices rose to a three-week high for a second day as banking-sector unease kept the U.S. dollar near seven-week lows. SMM reports LME aluminium trading on March 24: it opened at $2,329/mt, ranged from $2,312.5/mt to $2,364.5/mt, and closed at $2,344/mt, up $17.5/mt (+0.75%). The piece provides no new drivers beyond the price review. | SMM reports the SHFE 2305 aluminium contract opened at 18,405 yuan/mt in the prior night session, traded between 18,485 and 18,405 yuan/mt, and closed at 18,435 yuan/mt, down 35 yuan/mt or 0.19%. It cites a slowing pace of Fed rate hikes that increases uncertainty, but notes fundamentals improving as aluminium smelters resume production in Sichuan/Guizhou and downstream consumption strengthens. Domestic aluminium ingot social inventories fall rapidly, supporting a near-term rally. | Reuters reports copper holding a narrow range with small gains on both LME and SHFE as investors weigh global economic headwinds and banking-system worries. LME nickel resumes Asian hours after a prior suspension and cancellations, while LME aluminium rises modestly. Overall moves are mild, implying limited net impact. | Copper traded in a tight range as investors weighed global economic headwinds and banking risks, with LME copper up 0.3%. LME nickel resumed Asian hours after a year-long suspension, but fell 0.6%. SHFE nickel rose sharply, while aluminium was up modestly. | Copper prices held in a tight range, with three-month LME copper up 0.3%. Nickel resumed LME Asian trading after a long suspension; LME nickel fell 0.6% while SHFE nickel rose 3.3%. Aluminium also inched higher on both LME and SHFE amid macro and bank-stability worries. | Copper prices were steady-to-slightly higher on the LME and SHFE as investors weighed metal stocks against global economic headwinds and concerns over the banking system. LME nickel resumed Asian hours after a prior suspension, but fell on the day. Aluminium rose modestly on both exchanges. | The International Aluminium Institute launched the Aluminium Forward 2030 coalition, bringing together aluminium producers and downstream customers to accelerate net-zero and circularity initiatives across the value chain. It outlines collaboration on decarbonization and recycling, but provides no immediate supply, tariff, or pricing changes that would directly move aluminium markets. | Copper held a tight range but posted small gains as falling inventories and improving demand outweighed macro worries. LME nickel slipped after resuming Asian-hours trading, with concerns over liquidity, margins and deliverable constraints. Aluminium was also slightly higher on both LME and SHFE, supported by broader metal firmness. | Reuters reports LME/SHFE copper trading slightly higher on lower inventories and improving demand sentiment despite macro concerns. LME nickel resumes Asian-hours trading after a suspension, but the contract slipped, reflecting volatile/liquidity risks. SHFE aluminium also gained modestly alongside broader base-metals moves. | SMM reports that aluminium smelters’ real-time full costs barely changed last week, supported by stable alumina prices. As of March 22, smelter costs were 17,218 yuan/mt versus SMM A00 prices at 18,320 yuan/mt, boosting profits to 1,102 yuan/mt (+167). | SMM reports that despite volatility in spot coal, long-term contract coal prices at thermal power plants are mostly stable. Some smelters with captive power have enough coal and buy on dips, keeping thermal costs steady. In addition, prebaked anode prices face steep declines without cost support, potentially lowering aluminium smelter costs in the short term. | SMM reports that while macro sentiment is stabilising, aluminium markets expect near-term support from robust destocking. Despite rising domestic supply and imported ingot inflows, downstream demand is in peak season and social aluminium inventories have declined sharply. This should help aluminium prices this week, though longer-term supply pressure and weaker costs may cap gains. | The International Aluminium Institute launched “Aluminium Forward 2030,” a coalition of primary producers and downstream companies aiming to decarbonise and improve circularity across end-use sectors. Participants including major manufacturers and technology firms plan collaborative roadmap work toward net-zero ambitions, but no immediate supply or policy changes are specified. | Copper traded in a tight range but was slightly higher, supported by falling inventories, improving demand, and expectations around a possible pause in U.S. rate hikes. LME nickel resumed Asian hours after a suspension, with the contract slightly lower and liquidity concerns highlighted. Aluminium rose marginally alongside broader metal sentiment. | Copper prices were steady to slightly higher on Monday, supported by falling inventories, improving demand, and a potential pause in U.S. interest-rate hikes. LME nickel resumed Asian-hours trading after a long suspension, easing logistics for arbitrage, though liquidity remains limited. Aluminium also edged up, but without clear catalysts. | SMM reports that alumina prices in North China face downside risk as perceived supply increases and South China shipments are expected to fill a persistent local supply deficit. North prices are currently supported by production cuts due to high costs and environmental checks, but the balance should shift and prices may decline. | SMM says Guizhou and Sichuan smelters continued resuming production, lifting China aluminium operating capacity to 40.2m mt. Monthly daily output is still below February, while net primary aluminium imports reached 146,000 mt in Jan–Feb and imports from Russia may add 50,000–60,000 mt/month. Ingot inventories fell and processing orders improved.

Copper held steady to slightly higher in Asia after a deal to buy SVB’s assets improved risk appetite, supporting dollar-priced metals. However, gains were capped by weak Chinese demand tied to overseas macro factors and higher prices. Aluminium and nickel also rose modestly, reflecting the same sentiment move. | Power and water shortages in China’s Yunnan province are disrupting hydroelectric supply, prompting authorities to order aluminum smelters to cut electricity consumption and production, most recently by 40%. Yunnan is a key smelting hub, and repeated curtailments suggest near-term supply tightness likely supportive of aluminum prices.

Copper prices edged lower in Asia as investors stayed cautious after U.S. banking turmoil, though fears of a full crisis eased. Nickel slid but was recently supported by falling inventories and short-covering. Aluminium fell. Support from a weaker dollar and mixed Shanghai moves suggest no strong net direction. | LX Hausys and Belgium’s Reynaers Aluminium signed an MoU to cooperate on design, manufacturing technology, and development of high-end aluminium windows for the Korean market. New ultraslim and insulated window products are planned for the second half of this year, supported by rising demand for renovations and premium facilities. | Copper, nickel and aluminium prices fell as investors stayed cautious after banking-sector turmoil. Reuters cites position cuts and risk-off trading by funds and companies, described as short-term deleveraging. Even with a weaker U.S. dollar, LME copper, nickel and aluminium retreated in the session. | Ghana’s Lands and Natural Resources Minister says efforts to establish an integrated aluminium industry via GIADEC are progressing, with plans to develop bauxite supply, build refineries, and modernise/retrofit VALCO. A downstream workshop focuses on policy and incentives to raise local consumption and reduce aluminium imports. | Ghana’s minister and GIADEC highlight plans to build an integrated aluminium downstream industry, leveraging large domestic bauxite resources and AfCFTA market access. They stress policy incentives, including power and tax support, to enable refining and downstream fabrication, which could raise near-term expectations for aluminium production and demand.

Ghana’s Lands and Natural Resources Minister says the country’s integrated aluminium industry plans are progressing under the 2018 GIADEC Act. A two-day workshop focused on downstream policy as Ghana pursues bauxite development, refinery builds, and VALCO modernisation. Higher integration expectations can support aluminium market sentiment. | SMM launched new discount factors for nickel, cobalt, and lithium metals contained in China’s ternary battery scrap to better align scrap settlement prices with underlying metal benchmarks. The methodology updates how nickel and cobalt element values are derived from sulphate/carbonate price indices and applies spot transaction-based assessment daily. | Freeport Indonesia was advised by the government to ship about 2.3 million tonnes of copper concentrate before June. Indonesia plans to ban exports of ROM, including copper concentrate and bauxite, from June to push domestic processing. The front-loading could tighten near-term copper/aluminium supply expectations ahead of the ban. | Copper prices edged lower in London as a firm dollar and economic uncertainty weighed on base-metal sentiment after three days of gains. Analysts cited high inflation, expected tightening, and slower-than-expected China factory activity. The article also notes mild declines in LME aluminium and nickel alongside copper. | Copper rose on LME, supported by signs of recovering Chinese demand, supply disruptions, and a weaker dollar as banking fears eased. Chinese smelters agreed lower Q2 treatment/refining charges, with spot TC/RCs also down, implying tighter concentrate availability and potential market tightening amid low inventories. | LME announced measures to revive its nickel contract after last year’s chaotic trading, including a new lower-grade “class 2” nickel contract with Qianhai Mercantile Exchange, fast-track listings, and a fee waiver to boost liquidity. It also made copper and aluminium price caps tighter. The nickel contract disruption followed a mismatch between high- and low-grade supplies. | Flying Nickel Mining reported initial Minago project drill core assay results, highlighting intersections grading about 1.1–1.5% nickel and small copper percentages, alongside Pt/Pd/Au. The company is continuing a 2023 PGM assay program. The update is primarily sentiment-relevant for nickel, with no direct aluminium link. | LME announced a two-year plan after the 2022 nickel saga, including fast-track listing and fee waivers to boost liquidity in LME Nickel, potential changes to margin methodology, and making daily price limits permanent. It also plans revised calibration methodology for copper and aluminium daily limits and considers broadening nickel delivery. | LME proposes measures to revive nickel trading, including shortening approval time for new nickel brands, expanding eligible nickel forms (including nickel powder), enhancing reporting of eligible stocks, and considering changes to margins and trading platform rollout. It also makes permanent daily price limits for all metals, tightening caps for copper and aluminium.

SMM data for the week ending Mar 31 shows aluminium inventories falling in China’s bonded zones. Shanghai bonded stocks were 50,000 mt (up from 49,300 mt in Guangdong), while combined stocks reached 99,300 mt, down 380 mt versus the prior week, implying slightly tighter supply conditions. | Pampa Metals began a diamond-drill program at its Buenavista target within the Block 4 project in northern Chile, aimed at testing a porphyry copper-molybdenum-gold system. The company expects assay results in June. This is early exploration activity with limited near-term implications for copper prices. | GIADEC and Ghana’s Ministry of Lands and Natural Resources are planning a policy framework for the downstream aluminium industry, emphasizing power costs and tax incentives. The government highlights value addition from bauxite to alumina and primary aluminium, aiming to expand refining and downstream manufacturing. This should be modestly supportive for aluminium market expectations. | GIADEC’s CEO says Ghana is preparing a policy framework to govern the aluminium downstream industry, with incentives and power-cost measures highlighted. The plan aims to expand refining and downstream production to serve sectors like construction, foil and car manufacturing, supported by AfCFTA market access.

Copper prices fell on Monday as China’s Caixin manufacturing PMI weakened to the 50.0 level, raising demand concerns. A firmer U.S. dollar tied to renewed inflation worries after oil producers’ output cuts further pressured dollar-priced commodities. LME and SHFE aluminium also eased, while nickel was mixed. | Copper prices eased on a stronger dollar after higher oil prices revived inflation and potential U.S. rate-hike fears. China’s Caixin/S&P manufacturing PMI fell to 50.0, suggesting demand slowdown. LME warehouse stocks declined, offering some support, but copper still traded lower. Aluminium rose on electricity-cost worries.

Jamaica’s Industrial Disputes Tribunal issued a no-strike order for over 600 workers at UC Rusal Alumina Jamaica, requiring normal operations while the dispute is heard. The stoppage had been planned over wages and benefits after failed talks, lowering immediate risk to aluminum supply. | Alcoa announced it is expanding its EcoSource™ low-carbon alumina brand from smelter-grade applications to include non-metallurgical alumina grades such as hydrates and calcined materials. The company says EcoSource is sourced from its refining system with low CO2e intensity, aiming to attract more customers and reduce emissions. | Bonnell Aluminum (Tredegar subsidiary) plans to expand TSLOTS aluminum structural framing operations by adding a new manufacturing and distribution facility in Newnan, Georgia, to better serve the U.S. Southeast. The project aims to improve access, reduce shipping costs, and shorten lead times, implying higher aluminum processing demand. | Bonnell Aluminum, a Tredegar subsidiary, plans to expand TSLOTS aluminum structural framing operations by adding a new manufacturing and distribution facility in Newnan, Georgia. The move is intended to increase market access, reduce shipping costs, and shorten lead times, following prior investments in the Midwest. | Copper prices struggled to hold gains as investors worried that OPEC+’s surprise output target cut could reignite inflation, while global growth remains weak. Analysts cited strong mine supply and easing Chinese demand, alongside credit concerns in the U.S. and Europe. Aluminium and nickel also slipped modestly.

Meridian and NZ Aluminium Smelters agreed to give the Tiwai Point smelter flexibility to reduce electricity demand when the New Zealand grid is under stress. As the smelter uses about 12% of current generation, such demand curtailment implies potential production adjustments that could pressure aluminium prices. | Indonesia’s Inalum has been separated from the MIND ID holding structure and is now focused on aluminium smelting and developing the national aluminium industry. The split follows government regulations transferring controlling shares. While it strengthens governance and planning, the article does not cite near-term changes to aluminium output or supply. | Copper prices fell toward a one-week low on weak U.S. economic data and worries about demand, but a further drop looked limited due to thin liquidity from China’s Qingming holiday (SHFE closed). Analysts cited a potentially stronger demand/d tighter supply outlook, while LME aluminium and nickel were also slightly lower. | New Zealand Aluminium Smelter (NZAS) and Meridian agreed to a demand-response deal allowing Meridian to request up to 50 MW reductions during grid stress or hydro shortages, with Meridian paying fees per reduction. The agreement is subject to Electricity Authority approval and aims to maintain reliable smelter power supply through next winter. | Vedanta said its aluminium production in Q4FY23 rose to 574,000 tonnes, slightly above the prior year. However, alumina output at its Lanjigarh refinery fell 7% QoQ and 18% YoY due to a planned calciner shutdown. The update is specific to aluminium production levels. | Copper prices rose on LME support from falling inventories, lower cancelled warrants, and drawdown dynamics, despite worries over weaker U.S. and China manufacturing and a stronger dollar. It also cites reduced global smelting activity and lower Chile production. Aluminium was reported lower during the session, and macro sentiment weighed broadly. | Copper prices in London rose 0.5% to around $8,790/tonne as LME inventories continued to decline, with warehouse stocks down almost 15% over two weeks and on-warrant stocks at the lowest since late 2021. Despite weak U.S. and China manufacturing signals, supply disruptions and inventory drawdowns are supporting downside protection.

Copper prices rose as LME warrant stocks fell to the lowest since 2021, supporting the market, and China demand/price premiums offered additional support. However, the rally was capped by global recession fears and macro concerns tied to U.S. jobs data, with aluminium up and nickel down. | SMM reports the daily changes of Shanghai A00 aluminium prices for Apr 6, 2023. The quoted range is 18,590–18,630 yuan per metric tonne, up 20 yuan/mt on the day. The note indicates SMM’s data are for reference and not trading recommendations.

Copper edged higher on a softer U.S. dollar, but gains may be limited ahead of U.S. inflation data that could affect Fed rate expectations. China’s slow consumer inflation and ongoing deflation point to demand weakness. LME aluminium fell while nickel was slightly lower, with all moves modest. | Copper prices edged higher as a softer U.S. dollar provided support, but gains may be capped ahead of U.S. CPI. Markets are focused on how inflation affects Fed rate expectations. The piece also notes small declines in LME aluminium and nickel and highlights weaker China demand indicators. | ABx Group says it plans to resume Tasmanian bauxite mining, while its subsidiary Alcore received a $7.5 million grant toward a Bell Bay aluminium smelter bath recycling plant. The project targets converting excess bath into aluminium fluoride for new smelters, but the timing and market size impact on aluminium prices is uncertain. | Copper rose 0.6% as the dollar softened, but concerns over weak Chinese economic activity limited upside ahead of U.S. CPI data. Aluminium fell to a multi-week low on softer demand from transport, packaging and construction. Nickel increased 3.3% while overall macro sensitivity remained key for industrial metals. | Copper prices climbed to a one-week high after a softer U.S. dollar made dollar-priced metals cheaper. LME three-month copper was up 0.59% to $8,852/t, though analysts warned stronger-than-expected U.S. inflation could curb gains. China inflation demand signals were weaker. | LME data show Russian aluminium dominates LME warehouse stocks (53% or 220,575 tonnes) and Russian copper and nickel are also significant. After US import tariffs, LME suspended Russian inflows into US-registered warehouses, complicating use of Rusal aluminium. Resulting supply tightness expectations support aluminium prices near term. | Los Andes Copper filed a positive pre-feasibility study for its Vizcachitas copper-molybdenum project in Chile, highlighting 0.36% copper reserves and an after-tax NPV of $2.776 billion (24.2% IRR) based on a $3.68/lb copper price and large projected annual copper output over a 26-year mine life. | The piece reports that Russian aluminum is now the dominant share of metal stockpiles held in LME warehouses. Concentration of inventories on the exchange can imply improved near-term supply visibility, often weighing on aluminium sentiment and prices in the short term. | The U.S. issued presidential proclamations expanding punitive tariffs on Russian aluminum. A 200% ad valorem duty applies to aluminum and derivative aluminum articles when primary aluminum is smelted or cast in Russia, later extended broadly. A separate Column 2 increase can add up to 70%, raising landed costs.

London copper eased as rising supply and economic pressure offset support from a weaker U.S. dollar ahead of inflation data. Antaike cited a 14% year-on-year jump in China refined output. Aluminium rose modestly amid increasing Russian LME stock share; nickel and other metals were mixed. | Copper prices steadied at a one-week high after China posted stronger-than-expected bank lending and faster credit growth, supported by continued policy measures post-COVID curbs. Rising copper supply expectations tempered gains. The same macro tone lifted LME aluminium and nickel slightly, alongside cautious sentiment ahead of U.S. inflation data. | Copper climbed on Wednesday, helped by a weaker U.S. dollar and stronger-than-expected Chinese credit growth ahead of inflation data. However, gains were tempered by rising refined copper output in China, driven by new capacity and adequate raw-material supply. LME aluminium and nickel also rose alongside copper sentiment. | London copper prices fell from a one-week high as China’s refined output rose 14% year-on-year, increasing supply, while macro-economic pressure weighed on demand expectations. The weaker U.S. dollar offered some support. Aluminium was slightly higher, while nickel traded with a larger swing amid mixed broader base-metals pricing. | Western Copper and Gold said Rio Tinto will exercise its participation right in full, subscribing for 878,809 shares and keeping its ~7.84% stake, alongside Mitsubishi’s larger investment. The company is developing the Casino copper-gold project, implying marginally improved copper project funding visibility near term. | Glencore reportedly added more Russian aluminium to LME-registered warehouses in Gwangyang, lifting Russian stocks’ share and total inventories. Concern is that larger Russian inventory overhang could weigh on LME benchmark aluminium pricing used in contracts. | Latin Metals Inc. said mapping and prospecting at its Auquis Project in Peru identified two new mineralized zones, including porphyry-style copper and skarn mineralization. Grab samples reported high-grade copper in multiple areas, with associated silver and some lead/zinc. No aluminium or nickel impact is mentioned. | Advance United reports final drill assay results from its Buck Lake VMS property, highlighting high-grade copper intersections (e.g., 4.59% Cu over 3.2 m) and associated silver and zinc values. This exploration update may slightly influence copper sentiment, but lacks production/inventory implications, so near-term price impact is likely limited. | Eurobattery Minerals is advancing the Hautalampi underground nickel-cobalt-copper project in Finland, expanding ownership through acquisitions and completing a pre-feasibility study. The deposit has meaningful nickel and copper reserves and planned flotation processing, but it is still at development stage, limiting short-term supply impact on prices. | Western Australia’s environmental regulator allegedly learned of Rio Tinto exploration risks to drinking water only via Facebook, highlighting a “gap” in inter-agency handling. The story raises concerns that standard permit conditions are inadequate for sensitive water catchments, potentially leading to tighter restrictions affecting bauxite/mining operations.

SMM reports SHFE and LME aluminium prices rising after softer-than-expected US inflation weakened expectations for May rate hikes and a weaker US dollar. Domestically, aluminium supply is only slightly higher and downstream demand is recovering slowly, so the market is seen as rangebound, with Yunnan power supply and inventories in focus. | SMM reports continued declines in aluminium fluoride prices, with April bids cut to 8,900 yuan/mt and March 11–April 10 seeing further falls. While some aluminium smelters in Sichuan and Guizhou are resuming production, demand support is limited. Abundant supply may pressure prices further in the near term, with uncertain spillover to aluminium. | SMM reports that aluminium scrap prices rose in March but weak demand capped further gains. Aluminium extrusion scrap increased by 300–500 yuan/mt and aluminium tense scrap by 100–300 yuan/mt. With both supply and demand growing, aluminium scrap prices are expected to remain rangebound in April. | China’s Jan–March unwrought aluminium and product exports totaled about 1.4 million tonnes, down 15.4% versus the prior year, according to customs data cited by Reuters. The same report notes aluminium exports of 497,412 tonnes in March. Copper demand expectations may soften, while aluminium impact is less definitive without fuller trade context. | SMM says aluminium ingot inventories fell to about 1 million mt as of April 10, down over 200,000 mt from early March. Inventories are expected to keep declining in April, per the SMM ML model. | Aluminum prices have been rangebound after a February decline, with March support and a mild upward bias into April. China’s imports of Russian aluminum nearly tripled in early 2023, offsetting Western trade restrictions amid China’s slow demand from water shortages. Premium signals in Japan and Europe are mixed, keeping prices largely sideways. | MetalX and Manna Capital plan to invest over $200 million in a new greenfield aluminum rolling slab facility and recycling campus, targeting 220 million pounds per year of ultra-low carbon aluminum with more recycled content. Operations are expected in 1H 2026, implying limited immediate impact on near-term aluminum prices.

Copper reached a six-week high after US dollar weakness driven by unexpectedly lower US producer prices raised expectations that the Fed is nearing the end of its rate-hike cycle. The weaker dollar supported dollar-priced metals. The article also notes gains in LME/SHFE aluminium and nickel. | Copper rose to a seven-week high as the dollar slumped after U.S. producer prices unexpectedly fell, strengthening bets that the Fed is nearing the end of its rate hikes. This makes dollar-priced metals cheaper for buyers. LME aluminium and nickel also rose alongside copper. | SMM reports weak automobile consumption has driven poor orders for secondary aluminium alloy plants, with other end-sector demand failing to improve. Market liquidity is thin, and while some large producers keep stable output, smaller plants may cut production due to falling orders and shrinking profits. Operating rates are expected flat to lower. | SMM reports that East China A00 aluminium prices rose on Apr 14, quoting 18,690–18,730 yuan/mt, up by 210 yuan/mt on the day. The note presents the daily price range and change, indicating immediate bullish momentum in aluminium in the region. | SMM reports that imported ADC12 secondary aluminium alloy for future arrivals was offered at $2,370–2,400/mt, but importers still posted small losses. Nickel and aluminium also rose intraday. | Mitsubishi says it is appraising investments in nickel projects, citing rapidly rising EV battery demand, and is verifying profitable options given technical and cost hurdles. It is also considering initiatives to increase copper output through mine expansions and new projects, potentially allocating sale proceeds toward copper and battery metals. No clear aluminium link.

SMM reported that SHFE’s most-traded aluminium contract (2305) rose slightly and LME aluminium ended higher, with LME up about 1.1%. The price strength was attributed to macro sentiment: markets expected the US Fed to pause further rate hikes amid recession fears, boosting aluminium. | SMM says macro sentiment improved as markets bet the US Fed may pause rate hikes amid recession fears, supporting aluminium. With ingots in a destocking cycle and mixed demand, prices are expected rangebound short term, with both LME and SHFE aluminium up last Friday. | Sierra Leone’s Vimetco (Sierra Mineral Holdings) met government ministries to plan a new alumina tri-hydrate value-adding plant at its bauxite concession. The project is scheduled to start in 2020 with output expected in June 2022, funded with Afrexim Bank support, aiming to increase local industrial production and export revenue. | SMM reports that alumina transactions are softening: a Gansu smelter bought 35,000 mt at 2,900 yuan/mt, down versus prior deals, while tighter north supply from Shandong is outweighed by inflows from southwest China. Reuters reports aluminium shortages driven by hydro power problems in China and lingering smelter cuts in Europe after high energy costs and reduced Russian supply. By avoiding energy-intensive heat treatments and improving scrap quality, recycling costs could fall and increase aluminium component adoption.

LME tin retreated after Myanmar mining suspension concerns eased. LME aluminium and nickel also eased, with SHFE aluminium and nickel showing opposing gains. | Copper fell in Asian trading as traders weighed prospects of additional U.S. Federal Reserve rate hikes, supporting the dollar and pressuring non-ferrous prices.
