The contract boosts EGA’s alumina security beyond its refinery output and offers low-carbon EcoSource options, supporting aluminium production continuity. | Alcoa and Emirates Global Aluminium (EGA) signed an eight-year agreement starting in 2024 for Alcoa to supply smelter-grade alumina, with options for up to 15.6 million tonnes from Western Australia. EGA’s Abu Dhabi refinery met 47% of its needs in 2022, and the pact strengthens long-term alumina security, including low-carbon EcoSource.

Copper prices edged higher off a 5-1/2-month low, but the drop was driven by concerns over sluggish Chinese demand. Reuters cites weak April industrial output and shrinking metals-intensive manufacturing. It also warns aluminium demand has fallen in Europe and China, implying further downside risk despite near-term fluctuations. | SMM’s daily wrap shows most SHFE/LME base metals closed lower. SHFE nickel fell over 3% and LME nickel fell 0.9%, while SHFE copper and LME copper dropped about 0.7–0.9%. Aluminium was the exception: SHFE aluminium rose ~0.4% and LME aluminium edged up ~0.24%. | London copper edged higher from a 5-1/2-month low after bargain buying, but prices remain pressured by weak China demand signals. April industrial output and retail sales underperformed, and metals-intensive manufacturing shrank. The report also flags recent aluminum demand slumps in Europe and China as a downside risk. | Copper trades near a 5-1/2-month low as investors weigh weaker-than-expected China economic data, including softer industrial production and shrinking metals-intensive manufacturing. The slowdown reduces demand expectations and contributes to further copper selling. Aluminium demand is also flagged as slumping in Europe and China, maintaining bearish sentiment. | Canada announced nearly CAD 360 million to support research, development and expansion in key minerals, including copper, nickel and aluminium, and plans to simplify mining licensing. It also issued a Canada-U.S. joint statement on building a key mineral supply chain, potentially improving medium-term supply expectations and sentiment. | Alloy Enterprises raised $26M to expand its digital aluminum fabrication process and ramp production of high-throughput, fully dense aluminum parts in the U.S. The company cites a gap between complex aluminum component demand and supply, driven by EV lightweighting and industrial equipment needs, and plans production deliveries this summer. | Reuters reports analysts expect a steep global deficit in copper concentrate during 2025-2027 as Asian smelters expand faster than new mining projects. China’s refined copper imports are forecast to fall as domestic smelting rises. It also says State Grid’s ultra-high voltage focus may increase aluminium usage, supporting aluminium demand. | Kaiser Aluminum announced that union members at its Warrick rolling mill ratified a new four-year labor agreement effective May 15, 2023. The pact covers about 850 employees and runs through May 15, 2027. The company frames it as supporting sustainable operations for its packaging and other aluminum products.

Lindian Resources signed a six-year Guinea bauxite supply deal with C&D Logistics for 23 million wet tonnes from its Gaoual project, with annual renegotiation from 2025. The firm cites rising demand after Indonesia’s bauxite export ban, but start-up timing limits near-term impact on aluminium prices. | Copper prices fell in London as a stronger U.S. dollar reached seven-week highs and LME-registered on-warrant stocks rose to a six-month peak. The article also describes weakness across base metals, with nickel touching a fresh multi-month low and aluminium trading lower, reflecting tighter financial conditions and softer demand expectations. | China’s April aluminum imports rose 27.1% year-on-year to 222,851 tonnes as domestic supply stayed constrained by lingering power and hydropower issues in Yunnan. Output in the province fell with lower hydropower generation, and analysts do not expect rapid regional production resumption, supporting import-led demand. | WBMS reported March 2023 copper cathode output below consumption, leaving a supply shortage of 74,400 mt, while primary aluminium posted oversupply of 148,200 mt and refined nickel oversupply of 5,600 mt. These figures point to tight copper but substantial surplus conditions in aluminium and nickel. | WBMS reported March 2023 copper cathode output of 2.1Mt versus consumption of 2.17Mt, leaving a 74,400t supply shortage. Primary aluminium ran in oversupply (148,200t), and nickel also showed a small oversupply. Overall, the copper deficit is the clearest near-term supportive factor. | The UK introduced sanctions prohibiting imports of Russian-origin copper, aluminum, and nickel, alongside a wider ban on Russian diamonds. The measure directly restricts Russian metal supply into the UK/market, raising expectations of tighter availability and supporting near-term price strength for industrial metals. | Rishi Sunak at the G7 summit in Japan announces expanded sanctions banning imports of Russian-origin copper, aluminium and nickel, alongside a wider package restricting Russian diamonds. Such measures reduce access to Russian metal supplies for the market and can tighten short-term availability, potentially lifting prices. | Rishi Sunak says G7 leaders will announce wider sanctions on Russia, including a ban on Russian-origin imports of copper, aluminium and nickel. The measures aim to restrict financing and war capacity tied to Russian supply chains, with the UK and allies expected to announce further steps over the weekend. | The UK government announced sanctions banning imports of Russian-origin copper, aluminium, and nickel, alongside Russian diamonds, as part of wider restrictions on 86 Russian individuals and firms linked to Russia’s military-industrial complex. The UK’s import ban is expected to tighten supply availability and support prices for the affected metals. | Rishi Sunak said the G7 will ban or restrict imports of Russian diamonds and also extend sanctions to Russian-origin copper, aluminium and nickel. The measures aim to pressure Russia after the invasion of Ukraine. The news signals tighter trade access for Russian metal supply into allied markets.

UK Prime Minister Rishi Sunak announced an embargo on imports of Russian diamonds and also copper, aluminium and nickel. Reporting indicates additional restrictions on Russian diamond trade and sanctions targeting firms tied to Russia’s military-industrial sector. The move is aimed at cutting Russian economic inputs into global markets. | The UK Prime Minister announced an embargo banning imports of Russian diamonds and also Russian copper, aluminium, and nickel. UK reporting says sanctions include a broader market freeze and additional measures. Such trade restrictions on Russian metal supply can tighten availability and provide upward price pressure for copper, aluminium, and nickel. | The UK Prime Minister is reported to have announced an embargo on imports of Russian diamonds, including copper, aluminium, and nickel. The measure is part of sanctions related to Russia’s actions in Ukraine, alongside asset freezes and individual/entity restrictions. The EU is also considering diamond trade restrictions. | Copper is set for a fifth straight weekly decline as the U.S. dollar stays near two-month highs and markets price in additional Fed rate hikes. Weak Chinese demand dampens sentiment. Aluminium and nickel show intraday gains, but the broader macro backdrop caps industrial metals, leaving weekly momentum bearish. | Ahead of the G7 Japan summit, the UK is set to unveil sanctions and export controls targeting Russia. Trade Data Monitor shows Britain imported little or no Russian copper, but did import unwrought aluminium and refined nickel from Russia in 2022-2023. Such curbs can reduce Russian metal flows and affect short-term pricing expectations. | Britain and other G7 states plan new sanctions and export controls against Russia. Factbox data shows UK Russian imports of refined copper were effectively absent in 2021 and tiny via 15,252 tonnes last year; aluminium imports from Russia fell versus 2021; refined nickel imports were much lower or absent in earlier periods. | The article outlines Britain’s recent imports of sanctioned Russian metals before new G7 sanctions and export controls are announced in Japan. It notes some Russian-origin refined copper was imported previously (none in 2021/2022 refined), aluminium supply from Russia, and Russian refined nickel. Curbs on such flows can tighten supply and lift prices. | Lindian Resources signed a six-year bauxite supply agreement to deliver 23 million wet metric tonnes from Guinea’s Gaoual project to C&D Logistics. The contract specifies alumina- and silica-quality limits and follows an Indonesia bauxite export ban, which the company says increased demand for Guinea bauxite. | UK Prime Minister Sunak plans new import bans on Russian copper, aluminium and nickel as part of fresh G7 sanctions. The factbox details limited UK prior imports from Russia (nickel none in early 2023; aluminium and copper past volumes). Reduced Russian trade is likely to tighten supply and support prices. | The UK said it will announce new import bans on Russian copper, aluminium and nickel alongside fresh G7 sanctions at a summit in Japan. Trade data show the UK imported limited Russian volumes—none for refined copper, none early-2023 for aluminium and nickel—while refined nickel and unwrought aluminium imports occurred in 2022. | The UK plans new import bans on Russian copper, aluminium and nickel, alongside new G7 sanctions targeting Russia over the war in Ukraine. Britain imported no Russian refined copper in recent years, while it bought unwrought aluminium and some refined nickel from Russia in 2022. The bans could tighten supply expectations. | Copper rebounded 1.2% on LME on optimism that U.S. debt talks will avert a default, lifting broader risk assets and supported by a slightly weaker dollar. The article also notes gains in aluminium and a strong rise in nickel, reflecting macro-driven sentiment despite headwinds from China demand and recession risks. | Copper rebounded as investors grew more optimistic that U.S. debt talks will avert a federal default. LME three-month copper rose about 1% and Comex futures gained, supported by a softer dollar and traders covering shorts. The piece also flags headwinds from weak China demand and recession risks ahead.

SMM says overseas alumina prices fell $5/mt to $340/mt FOB Western Australia last week, or about 2,974 yuan/mt CIF major Chinese ports after freight. The yuan impact is adjusted by ocean freight, with the article noting the US dollar strengthened and the import window has been closed since Jan. 18. | SMM reports aluminium prices have weakened in recent trading. It cites macro headwinds from prolonged US debt-ceiling talks increasing market uncertainty and notes fundamentals remain soft: end-user consumption is only recovering while the market is oversupplied. Lower prebaked anode and coal prices reduce cost support, increasing downside risk. | Vietnam is reported to have the world’s second-largest bauxite reserves (5.8 billion tonnes). Bauxite is refined into alumina and then smelted into aluminium. The province of Dak Nong plans to expand alumina and aluminium smelting, positioning itself as a bauxite-to-aluminium hub, but the article doesn’t cite near-term output changes. | BNN Bloomberg reports Russia is raising aluminum exports to China to near-record levels, attributing the move to sanctions tightening other trade routes. The larger flow into a major consuming region is likely to increase near-term supply expectations and weigh on aluminum prices over the next several days to weeks. | Reuters reports copper fell on deteriorating demand expectations in China, alongside rising supplies and climbing LME stocks (up 80% since mid-April). Mine resumptions in Peru and a DRC royalty agreement supporting copper exports eased supply worries, widening the cash discount. Other metals were mixed; aluminum slid and nickel edged up. | Novelis announced the startup of a new roll forming development line in Michigan to accelerate R&D and enable large-volume production of high-strength aluminum automotive parts. The company says the technology supports electrification and demand for lighter, sustainable components, potentially supporting higher aluminum usage expectations.

Diageo funded a consortium (BACALL) to develop a UK-based recycled aluminium recycling and manufacturing plant for flat rolled sheet used in Guinness and Gordon’s cans. The initiative targets localization of recycling and reduced energy versus prime production. It’s currently at design and site-selection stage. | Copper touched a near six-month low as speculators boosted bearish positions over recession risks and weaker China demand. The cash–three-month spread widened sharply, signaling ample near-term supply, while a firm dollar and fragile risk sentiment weighed on industrial metals broadly, including aluminium and nickel, which also declined. | The G7/EU/US announced fresh sanctions aimed at closing Russia-related loopholes, with metals explicitly targeted. Russia’s large roles in global aluminium output and LME aluminium stocks raise warrant and delivery risks, while traders may pull non-Russian inventory. Russia’s copper and nickel supply shares add further near-term tightening expectations for LME base metals. | Goldman Sachs lowered its 2023 price forecasts for aluminium to $2,431/mt (from $2,700) and copper to $8,689/mt (from $9,750). It also cautioned that nickel could fall sharply amid surging supply from Indonesia and China, pointing to softer expected prices and downside risk for the metal complex. | Goldman Sachs cut its 2023 price forecasts, lowering aluminium to $2,431/mt from $2,700/mt and copper to $8,689/mt from $9,750/mt, while still expecting 12-month targets to materialize. It also flagged potential sharp nickel downside as supply from Indonesia and China rises.  | Goldman Sachs lowered its 2023 forecasts for aluminium and copper and reiterated 12-month targets may still materialise later. It highlighted potential sharp declines in nickel due to increased supply from Indonesia and China. Overall, the revised outlook weakens near-term price expectations across aluminium, copper and nickel. | Hydro’s CEO says customers are increasingly targeting Scope 3 emissions, enabling producers to earn premiums for low-carbon aluminium. Hydro’s Alunorte partnership with Glencore aims to cut refinery emissions via fuel substitution and electrification. The EU CBAM transition and Scope 3 traceability requirements are cited as drivers, while low-carbon aluminium differentials have risen. | The European Commission is discussing a Critical Raw Materials Act in response to the US Inflation Reduction Act, with Europe’s aluminium sector urging reinstatement of aluminium to the strategic materials list. The piece stresses energy-intensive production pressures, supply risks from outsourcing, and the need for secure, low-carbon domestic aluminium to meet EU green goals. | The piece argues Western aluminum production is in crisis, with Europe’s smelters curtailed due to high energy costs and the U.S. running at about half capacity with rising import dependence. It notes strong green-demand growth (solar/EVs) but says policy funds favor demand over low-carbon supply, worsening the mismatch. | Novelis began operating a new in-house aluminium roll forming development line in Michigan to accelerate R&D for producing large volumes of high-strength aluminium auto parts. The company expects electrification-driven demand for lighter components and aims to work with customers on aluminium sheet technology, but it is mainly a development step rather than immediate market supply changes. | Diageo is funding a UK consortium (BACALL) to build an aluminium recycling and manufacturing plant. The planned facility aims to roll hundreds of thousands of tonnes of aluminium sheet in the UK, increasing recycled aluminium use and reducing carbon intensity. The project is at design and site-selection stage. | Chinalco Peru’s president said Peru’s government approval is expected within 30 days to restart construction of the Toromocho copper mine expansion, targeting building commencement by end-June. The project is expected to raise Chinalco’s production, including an incremental 244k tons scale cited, supporting near-term copper supply expectations.

Congo plans to boost its stake and control in the Sicomines infrastructure-for-minerals deal with China, seeking higher equity for Gecamines, a larger infrastructure loan envelope, and lump-sum compensation over alleged undervaluation of copper and cobalt sales. Negotiations with Chinese firms are expected to intensify after Tshisekedi returns from China.

Tomago Aluminium workers rejected a management pay offer of 11% over two years, with union leaders calling for renewed bargaining and highlighting inflation-driven pay erosion. As Tomago is a large Australian smelter (up to 600,000 tonnes/year), the dispute could raise disruption risk and near-term aluminium supply concerns. | Workers at Rio Tinto-majority Tomago Aluminium rejected an 11% pay offer over two years, with about 90% of workers voting no. The Electrical Trades Union called for renewed bargaining amid inflation concerns. With Tomago able to produce up to 600,000 tonnes annually, labor unrest could disrupt smelting output and weigh on aluminium prices. | Workers at Tomago Aluminium rejected an 11% pay offer over two years, with most workers voting against the deal. The smelter can produce up to 600,000 tonnes annually and is majority owned by Rio Tinto. Union leaders call for renewed bargaining amid inflation-related pay erosion, raising disruption risk for aluminium output. | Fitch Solutions’ BMI and Citi both cut their aluminium average price forecasts, with BMI expecting prices to remain under pressure this year due to weak global demand and increasing Chinese supply. Inventories are still expected tight, but softer manufacturing recovery and macro outlook weigh on pricing sentiment. | SMM data shows global alumina capacity and output rose sharply from 2012 to 2022, with China driving most of the growth. China’s share of global alumina output increased from 47% to 58% over the decade. Global alumina capacity utilization stayed around 80%, implying continued steady production levels. | Workers at Tomago Aluminium rejected an 11% pay offer over two years, with most of the 200 workers voting against it. The smelter can produce up to 600,000 tonnes annually. Union officials urged management to return to bargaining amid inflation concerns, increasing risk of labor disruption. | Workers at Rio Tinto–majority owned Tomago Aluminium rejected a proposed 11% pay rise over two years, urging a return to bargaining. Roughly 90% of the workforce voted down the offer, citing inflation and retention concerns. Potential labor disruption at the 600,000 tonnes/year smelter could pressure near-term aluminium supply. | Indonesia’s mining minister said an export ban will start in June, but copper concentrates and some other ores will continue until smelters are ready, with duties and exemptions for delayed projects. In contrast, bauxite shipments will stop in June, potentially tightening near-term aluminium supply expectations. | Indonesia’s energy minister told parliament that Indonesia’s June export ban will allow continued shipments of some copper raw ores (plus anode mud and other concentrates) until smelters can process them. Bauxite exports, however, will stop in June, as existing smelters can absorb supply. | Workers at Rio Tinto majority-owned Tomago Aluminium rejected an 11% two-year pay offer, with about 90% of employees voting against it. Union officials cite inflation and urge management to return to negotiations. A potential labor dispute at the aluminium smelter could disrupt output, increasing short-term supply risk. | Workers at Rio Tinto–majority owned Tomago Aluminium rejected an 11% two-year pay offer, with union officials urging management to return to bargaining. The vote suggests heightened risk of labour unrest at a smelter capable of up to 600,000 tonnes annually, potentially tightening near-term aluminium supply expectations. | Workers at the Tomago Aluminium smelter rejected an 11% two-year pay offer. About 90% of staff voted against, with unions citing inflation-driven pay erosion and urging management to return to bargaining. Tomago can produce up to 600,000 tonnes annually, so labour disruption risk could weigh on aluminium prices. | Tomago Aluminium workers rejected a proposed 11% pay increase over two years, with the union arguing wages haven’t kept up with inflation. The workforce at the smelter—capable of up to 600,000 tonnes annually—has called for renewed bargaining, heightening the risk of disruption. | Workers at Tomago Aluminium (major smelter, up to 600,000 t/year) rejected an 11% pay offer over two years, with union officials calling management back to negotiations. The dispute could lead to labour disruption, reducing near-term smelting output and worsening aluminium supply expectations, likely pressuring prices lower. | Workers at Rio Tinto-majority-owned Tomago Aluminium rejected a management pay offer, with union representatives citing inflation-driven erosion of living standards. Rejection signals potential labor disruption and adds near-term risk to smelter output, potentially reducing aluminium supply expectations and putting downward pressure on prices. | SMM says China’s aluminium ingot social inventories fell to 657,000 mt as of May 25, while billet inventories dropped to 141,300 mt. Cargo arrivals in south China declined sharply and some smelters shifted shipments to east China as premiums cooled, slowing but not reversing stock reductions. | EIA reported a much larger-than-expected fall in US crude inventories, while the dollar rose for several days. Metals trading closed mixed: SHFE nickel surged 3.08% and copper dipped 0.77%, with aluminium down 0.4%. LME nickel gained 2.33%, copper rose 0.34%, and aluminium ended flat. | DR Congo plans to raise its stake in the Sicomines copper-and-cobalt venture with Chinese partners to 70% and seeks more infrastructure funding and compensation, arguing the 2008 deal was lopsided and priced minerals below market. Renegotiations are expected to start after President Tshisekedi returns from China. | Alcoa said its Portland Aluminium joint venture smelting and casthouse operations in Victoria, Australia, received Aluminium Stewardship Initiative (ASI) Performance Standard certification. ASI also recertified Alcoa’s Western Australia assets, including bauxite mines and alumina refineries. The news highlights responsible production but no immediate capacity or pricing changes. | DR Congo plans to raise its stake in the Sicomines copper-and-cobalt JV with Chinese partners from 32% to 70% and seek greater management control, citing limited gains under the 2008 infrastructure-for-minerals deal. Reuters reports groundwork for renegotiations ahead of discussions, potentially affecting copper supply expectations.

China’s exports of unwrought aluminium alloy fell to 16,400 mt in April, down 35.2% YoY and 16.5% MoM. January–April exports rose, but April was weaker. Japan and South Korea absorbed over 70% of April shipments (mainly via tolling), implying reduced export supply to key buyers. | SMM reports SHFE 2307 aluminium opened around 17,750 yuan/mt and LME aluminium around $2,209/mt, both up modestly. However, debt-ceiling negotiation failure, weaker consumption recovery, rising local aluminium ingot inventories, and lower prebaked anode/coal costs weaken support. Analysts watch for a possible fall below 18,000 yuan/mt. | Overseas and domestic quotes for ADC12 aluminium alloy ingots have fallen, with Malaysia and South Korea pricing around $2,260–2,280/mt. A weaker yuan increases import losses, while slowing domestic consumption reduces traders’ import appetite. ADC12 imports are reported near 90,000 mt, with arrivals expected to stay 80,000–100,000 mt. | Marubeni said its Portland, Victoria aluminium smelter was awarded ASI Performance Standard certification, recognizing environmental and safety initiatives and integrity of operations. The firm also plans to increase renewable power use to cut emissions and support responsible aluminium supply. No immediate production, capacity, or logistics changes are stated. | Crude oil prices rose as expectations shifted on OPEC+ production-cut signals, while the U.S. dollar weakened. Reflecting this risk-on macro backdrop, SHFE and LME base metals increased broadly: SHFE aluminium +3.38%, copper +2.17%, nickel +1.27%; LME aluminium +0.85%, copper +1.31%, nickel +1.29%. | PRNewswire promotes a 2023 report on the low-carbon aluminum market, projecting growth and citing stronger adoption in transport/EVs and supportive government pressure for decarbonization. It highlights investment trends toward low-emissions production and recycling partnerships, which could improve near-term outlook for aluminium demand and supply expectations.

SMM reports mixed LME/SHFE performance after a U.S. debt-ceiling agreement boosted risk appetite and weakened the dollar, but inflation and expected Fed hikes limit upside. Copper rose slightly yet sentiment may fade amid weaker consumption. Aluminium fell with inventories dropping but fundamentals seen unchanged. Nickel weakened due to falling premiums, rising stocks, and softer demand. | Copper fell after the U.S. dollar hit a two-month peak, making dollar-priced metal more expensive for non-USD holders. Risk sentiment improved temporarily, but the stronger dollar kept pressure on the non-ferrous complex. LME nickel and aluminium also declined, suggesting broad near-term bearish momentum for industrial metals. | SMM reports China exported 228,000 mt of aluminium plate/sheet and strip in April, down 8.56% month-on-month and 30.7% year-on-year. With overseas production normalizing and global demand cooling, exports have trended lower since late 2022. SMM expects 200,000–250,000 mt monthly in the future. | Copper prices stayed near a six-month low as investors prepared for Chinese data expected to show continued factory contraction. Demand concerns were reinforced by weak construction activity, low Yangshan import premiums, and LME stocks rising to about 100,000 tonnes. Aluminium and nickel also fell as broader metals sentiment deteriorated. | Copper trades just above a six-month low as investors brace for China’s manufacturing PMI, expected to show a second month of contraction. Demand concerns are reinforced by stagnant construction, low Yangshan import premiums, and LME stocks near 100,000 tonnes. Aluminium is also weaker on the broader demand/risk sentiment. | Indonesia’s export-ban policy on nickel ores, smelter buildout, and potential further restrictions (bauxite ban; possible heavy tax if WTO appeal fails) are positioned to increase processed nickel output while potentially tightening effective supply. Rising EV battery demand is cited, supporting a bullish near-term nickel outlook. | Aluminium Bahrain (Alba) met Egypt’s public enterprises minister and discussed enhancing cooperation with Egypt’s aluminium producers, including Egyptalum. The companies plan to study feasibility of potential partnerships, with bauxite highlighted as a key raw material. The piece is about investment and integration rather than immediate production or trade changes.

Indonesian copper miner Amman Mineral’s $880m IPO will finance debt repayment and projects, including completing a copper smelter in Sumbawa with planned cathode output. The Indonesian government’s temporary raw export exemption supports revenue needed to finish the project, reinforcing near-term copper sentiment. | Copper prices slid after China’s manufacturing PMI came in weaker than expected, below 50 indicating contraction, reinforcing concerns about demand in the world’s top consumer. Copper was set for a second monthly loss and fell 5.8% so far this month. LME aluminium and nickel also declined alongside risk sentiment. | ALRO signed an MoU with Azeraluminium to explore cooperation on raw material supply (baked anodes and alumina), aluminum product sales, and processing/marketing of continuous casting coils. The parties also discuss developing a gas-fired power plant on ALRO’s premises, aiming to improve technical and economic capabilities. | Copper prices edged toward six-month lows after May China factory activity contracted faster than expected, reinforcing concerns that the rebound is losing steam. Japan and South Korea output also fell. A weaker yuan pressured dollar-priced metals, while physical premiums rose and LME inventories increased. | SMM’s May 31 market wrap says crude oil slipped and the U.S. dollar rose, with supply-side headlines around Iran/OPEC. Base metals mostly fell: SHFE nickel plunged 4.81%, SHFE copper was slightly down, and SHFE aluminium rose modestly. LME metals were largely lower, with aluminium nearly flat. | BHP’s Escondida in Chile will use AI and Azure-based analytics on real-time concentrator data to improve ore processing and grade recovery. The initiative aims to extract more value from existing resources as copper is expected to face future supply shortfalls from green energy and battery demand. | LME launched two consultations to reform market rules after last year’s nickel crisis, including expanded warehouse reporting on off-warrant inventories and changes to how closing prices are set. The VWAP-based closing method would include more aluminium, copper and nickel contracts. Timing is June–July, not immediate rule changes.

Rio Tinto’s Pacific Aluminium subsidiary reported 2022 results tied to a global aluminium price surge in the first half of 2022 after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and higher energy costs. Aluminium prices later fell and stabilised. The smelter operated at 333,689 tonnes and continued Tiwai remediation, seeking energy solutions beyond 2024. | Pacific Aluminium Smelters’ underlying profit fell year-on-year as Tiwai Point aluminium prices cooled in the second half of the year. Earlier gains followed Russia’s invasion and higher energy costs. Management expects cost control amid a cyclical aluminium environment and notes prices have dropped from post-invasion highs. | Commerzbank lowered its 2023-2024 price forecasts, reducing expected end-2023/end-2024 levels for copper, aluminium, and nickel versus prior estimates. Copper and nickel forecasts fell materially, while aluminium was also marked down. The change reflects revised expectations, not an immediate physical market disruption. | Commerzbank cut its end-2023 and end-2024 forecasts for copper (to $9,000/$9,400), aluminium (to $2,600/$2,800), and nickel (to $23,000/$20,000) versus prior estimates. The revisions imply weaker expected market conditions, typically bearish for metal sentiment and pricing. | Commerzbank cut its forecasts for industrial metal prices, including copper, aluminium, and nickel, for end-2023 and end-2024. Copper and nickel targets were reduced materially, while aluminium was lowered versus prior estimates. The revision may affect market sentiment, but it is largely a medium-term view rather than an immediate shock. | Rio Tinto’s Pacific Aluminium reports 2022 results for New Zealand Aluminium Smelters, stating profits were driven by a first-half 2022 spike to record-high aluminium prices after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and higher energy costs. Prices later fell and stabilised. The release focuses on financial performance and remediation, not new price-moving developments. | Avante Mining announced it is commencing preparatory work on the TL Nickel Project, including re-logging and targeted sampling of historic core ahead of planned 2023 drilling. The release cites historic grades for nickel (and associated copper) and expects resampling results soon, but provides no production or immediate supply impact. | SMM’s daily comments show broad base-metal strength alongside firm energy and mixed FX. SHFE aluminium jumped 2.04% and SHFE copper gained 1.4%, while SHFE nickel slipped 0.17% by end of intraday. The piece suggests near-term bullish pressure for aluminium and copper. | The article argues copper is in a “buy zone,” citing declining LME warehouse stocks, tight supply, and rising demand tied to decarbonization (EVs, wind). It expects copper to avoid breaking key support around $3.15 and sees bullish upside despite bearish technicals. Spillover to other base metals is suggested. | Novelis signed a long-term supply agreement with Coca-Cola Bottlers to provide aluminum beverage can sheet for North American Coca-Cola brands, supported by closed-loop recycling. The deal ties to capacity expansion at the Bay Minette, Alabama plant. Continued contracted capacity and expected demand growth support aluminum demand sentiment. | Shandong Nanshan Aluminum ordered a new cold rolling mill with Primetals UCM technology for its Longkou plant, targeting expansion into higher-end, including automotive, aluminum products. The mill’s capacity is about 180,000 tons, with improved rolling stability and reduced spare work-roll costs. Startup is planned for mid-2025.

Eramet said LME lost benchmark status in a key nickel market segment after a major short sale, with SMM becoming the benchmark for NPI pricing. The piece also notes Commerzbank cutting price forecasts for copper, aluminium and nickel for 2023-2024, signaling a bearish outlook for these metals. | SMM discusses trends in nickel structural supply tied to high-nickel ternary cathode pathways, with weighted nickel consumption from Chinese precursors rising toward 2025. It also notes Commerzbank has lowered price forecasts for copper, aluminium, zinc and nickel for 2023-2024, suggesting weaker price expectations. | Markets ended higher: the article cites a two-day fall in the US dollar and two-day rise in crude oil, with SHFE nickel up 4.04%, SHFE aluminium up 1.71%, and SHFE copper up 1.5%. LME copper, aluminium and nickel also rose. Commerzbank lowered longer-term forecasts for copper and aluminium. | Copper prices were set for their first weekly gain in seven weeks after a surprise rise in China’s Caixin manufacturing activity boosted demand hopes. A U.S. debt-default deal, Senate debt-ceiling legislation, and expectations of no further Fed hikes weakened the dollar and lifted risk assets. Aluminium and nickel also rose. | Jamaica’s Mining Minister Floyd Green met with JISCO Alpart to discuss restarting and reopening the Alpart bauxite-alumina plant in Nain, St Elizabeth. Talks focused on the stalled modification process, feasibility of continued mining, and a prospective timeline for operations resuming, with government support indicated.

Reuters reports copper fell on Monday due to demand worries, notably in top consumer China, and a stronger dollar. Copper later partially recovered as the dollar retreated. The piece cites rising LME warehouse stocks and supply concerns from cancellations, while aluminium and nickel also declined. | Reuters reports another raid and volatility in LME aluminium stocks after 132,700 tonnes were cancelled in May. Russian aluminium inventories have surged, boosted by weaker Western self-sanctioning and displacement after US 200% import duties. Cancellations are concentrated in Port Klang, raising competition and contract differentiation, though outright prices remain in $2,200–2,300 range. | SMM reports the alumina index at 2,844 yuan/mt, down week-on-week, with deals showing falling price levels in north China. New alumina capacity ramp-ups in Hebei and Shandong are increasing pessimism amid oversupply. Refineries cut caustic soda and reduce costs, but prices are declining faster, and alumina is expected to stay weak this week.

Firefighters responded to a smouldering fire at Aurizon Port Services at Carrington, where 10m-high piles of copper and lead concentrate began to self-combust. Crews cooled the material for about seven hours while staff removed the top layers. The fire did not damage buildings and was extinguished the same day. | The weekly outlook notes that despite the US passing the debt ceiling bill, recession fears persist. It says low aluminium inventories should support prices, but weak demand may cap gains. SMM forecasts SHFE aluminium around 17,900–18,800 yuan/mt and LME aluminium at $2,170–2,350/mt this week. | Copper prices in Shanghai and London rose toward a four-week high as investors priced in potential Chinese stimulus and supportive policy for property, while LME on-warrant copper inventories fell sharply. Nickel also increased, reflecting broader metal demand expectations, whereas aluminium was slightly lower in the session. | Rumours in China’s Yunnan province suggest up to 2 million kW of power will be granted to local aluminium smelters to restart production after power rationing. Increased rainfall boosts hydropower generation, and some capacity could resume in late June, increasing near-term aluminium supply expectations.

China’s net imports of primary aluminium in Q1 2023 rose to 213,000 mt, up 804% year-on-year, with Russia supplying 172,500 mt. Russian producers expect sustained demand from Chinese downstream users for low-carbon aluminium. The article implies China will rely heavily on Russia going forward, supporting aluminium sentiment. | Altemira outlines its integrated aluminum can-to-can recycling system: used beverage cans are collected, melted and processed by group entities to make new cans, using UBC scrap instead of primary ingots and cutting CO2 by about 40%. The company cites Japan’s ~70% can recycling rates and expects carbon-neutral policy updates. | Copper prices slipped after China reported weaker-than-expected May exports and contracted imports of unwrought copper and products, signaling softer global demand. A weaker U.S. dollar and hopes for additional Chinese property-sector stimulus supported prices. Analysts also expect refined copper tightness to ease as smelters restart production and port stocks rise. Aluminium rose modestly. | Aluminium Bahrain’s chairman discussed the firm’s decarbonisation efforts at an Arab-German business forum, highlighting Power Station 5 Block 4 and solar farm projects to reduce greenhouse-gas intensity, alongside reforestation initiatives. The message supports Bahrain’s net-zero target but does not indicate immediate changes to aluminium output or sales impacting near-term prices. | U.S. Trade Representative Katherine Tai met France’s Bruno Le Maire and said she is hopeful the U.S. and EU can reach an agreement this year on steel and aluminum trade. A deal could reduce trade friction and tariffs, likely improving near-term expectations for aluminum supply-demand and supporting prices.

India and the U.S. are negotiating an exemption for U.S. tariffs on steel and aluminum that were imposed under Trump’s Section 232 regime. India would withdraw some retaliatory tariffs on agricultural goods. U.S. negotiators are viewed as not very flexible, but the talks hinge on potential aluminum relief before Modi’s visit. | London copper fell 0.4% as expectations of higher-for-longer interest rates and slowing global growth outweighed hopes for Chinese stimulus. Analysts noted weak demand fundamentals and warned a delayed package could restart the downtrend. Nickel also slipped 0.8% while aluminium edged up 0.3% on inventory-driven factors. | India sought a U.S. exemption from Section 232 steel and aluminium tariffs in return for removing some agricultural tariffs, but sources say Washington is not seriously considering it. India’s negotiators were quickly turned down, with continued tariff uncertainty likely to weigh on aluminium-linked trade sentiment near term. | SMM announced a suspension of Philippines and Indonesia-related price quotations during the Good Friday holiday. The article is tied to SMM A00 aluminum ingot pricing in Shanghai/Wuxi. The move may reduce quote availability temporarily, but it does not indicate a fundamental change in aluminum supply or demand. | India sought a U.S. exemption from Section 232 steel and aluminum tariffs by offering to drop retaliatory agricultural tariffs. Sources say Washington is not seriously considering an exemption. U.S. officials have indicated they will not be flexible, and industry leaders support keeping tariffs, sustaining trade friction. | The Beer Institute praised reintroduction of the Aluminum Pricing Examination (APEX) Act, seeking CFTC/DOJ oversight of aluminum benchmark price publication, including the Midwest Premium. It also cites high beer input costs from Section 232 aluminum tariffs and calls for tariff repeal, noting aluminum can/bottle usage dominance in beer packaging.

The Beer Institute asks the Biden administration to repeal Trump-era aluminum tariffs, saying tariff costs remain embedded in aluminum pricing used for beer cans even when imports from some partners are exempt. The group cites higher input costs and claims brewers bear the burden, not foreign producers. | SMM reports expectations for China aluminium ingot social inventories to decline to around 500,000 mt at end-June. Lower visible inventory levels can signal reduced near-term supply buffer and support tighter market conditions for aluminium, potentially providing upward price pressure over the coming days. | Copper prices rose on Friday toward a second weekly gain as hopes for Chinese demand stimulus increased and LME/Shanghai inventories fell. Three-month copper gained 0.4%. Aluminium edged higher on improved Yunnan hydropower conditions. Nickel also advanced, reflecting overall supportive sentiment across base metals. | Indonesian authorities plan to proceed with an imminent bauxite export ban, citing a 2020 mining law and a June 10 implementation timeline. Bauxite miners and industry groups warn domestic alumina capacity is insufficient to absorb output, with refiners able to process far less than national production. | Statkraft will supply 0.44 TWh/year of renewable hydropower under a long-term PPA to Hydro Energi’s Norwegian aluminium plants from 2024-2038. The deal secures 6.6 TWh total, supports future power needs, and reduces the plants’ aluminium carbon footprint by ~75% versus global average. | BT says its Exchange Clearance Operation will extract over 200 tonnes of copper cable in 2023 as part of shifting customers from legacy PSTN/3G copper to FTTP. The program targets recycling and resale of telecom equipment; aluminium is only mentioned as a small potential residual in some areas.  | Taber Extrusions and the Aluminum Extruders Council back Senator Tom Cotton’s call for the U.S. Department of Commerce to reform tariff exclusion rules for aluminum extrusions under Section 232. They cite increased foreign import penetration and risk to domestic production, urging revocation of GAEs to bolster U.S. aluminum supply.

Indonesia is set to ban bauxite exports from Saturday, aiming to boost domestic alumina refining. Industry groups argue existing refinery capacity (four smelters) can’t absorb output (more than 27 million tonnes), potentially forcing producers to cut output or delay shipments. The government says it will evaluate impacts after implementation. | SMM reports the weighted alumina index at 2,843 yuan/mt, down just 1 yuan/mt week-on-week. The key near-term driver is whether aluminium smelters in Yunnan can restart amid unstable hydropower. Partial restart could leave alumina supply excessive, pressuring prices further; full restart could absorb surplus and stabilize. | SMM’s daily comments note three straight days of crude oil declines and a largely flat U.S. dollar. At intraday close, SHFE copper was down 0.07% and aluminium down 0.87%, while SHFE nickel rose 2.22%. Other metals also moved, but the key tracked effects are mixed. | Russian aluminium registered with London Metal Exchange warehouses rose to 68% of total holdings in May (from 52% in April), with 263,125 tonnes in warehouses. The LME said Russian metal continues to flow and be accepted. Concentrated stocks raise concerns about price distortion, likely weighing on aluminium sentiment. | Rio Tinto will invest $1.1 billion to expand its AP60 low-carbon aluminium smelter in Quebec, adding 96 new AP60 pots and about 160,000 tonnes of primary aluminium capacity per year. The project offsets closures at Arvida and aims to meet demand for low-carbon aluminium, with commissioning starting in H1 2026. | Rio Tinto will invest $1.1 billion to expand its low-carbon aluminium smelter at Complexe Jonquiere, Canada, adding about 160,000 tonnes/year and offsetting capacity lost from potroom closures. The project runs over two-and-a-half years and fully ramps by end-2026, supported partly by Quebec. | Rio Tinto will invest $1.1 billion to expand its low-carbon aluminium smelter at Complexe Jonquière in Quebec, with up to $113 million from Quebec government support. The expansion will align with potroom closures at Arvida. Construction runs 2.5 years, with new pots commissioning in 1H 2026. | Copper fell 0.7% as traders worried about China’s demand outlook and awaited U.S. Fed and inflation data. Earlier gains were linked to covering shorts and Chinese stimulus optimism. Falling LME stocks offer some support, but expectations of elevated rates and China uncertainty weigh on copper and aluminium. | Rio Tinto will invest $1.4 billion to expand its Quebec aluminum smelter by adding 96 AP60 pots, with Quebec funding up to $150 million. The project targets lower greenhouse-gas emissions and is expected to take about 2.5 years to build, boosting aluminum supply capacity. | Canada signed a MoU with Rio Tinto to expand its Complexe Jonquière aluminum smelter by investing CAD $1.4 billion. The project will scale low-carbon AP60 electrolysis and deploy ELYSIS technology, adding new Western smelting capacity and increasing jobs during construction. | Rio Tinto will invest $1.4 billion to expand its Quebec aluminum smelter, adding AP60 pots and raising primary aluminum capacity to about 220,000 tonnes per year. The project is supported by a Quebec loan and includes plans to partially close older lines while adding recycling capacity. | Rio Tinto said it will invest $1.4 billion to expand its aluminium smelter complex in Saguenay, Quebec, adding 96 new pots using low-carbon AP60 technology. The move targets greener aluminum production capacity at the Jonquiére/Arvida site, which may support longer-run supply expectations but is unlikely to strongly move prices immediately. | The Aluminum Extruders Council says U.S. Department of Commerce rules grant tariff-free treatment to foreign extruded aluminum via broad exclusions, while domestic producers face higher proof burdens. It claims untariffed imports rose 82% and penetration exceeded 25%—a record—prompting calls to revoke the General Approved Exclusion process. | Rio Tinto will invest $1.4B to expand its Jonquière aluminum smelter in Quebec with 96 new low-carbon AP60 pots, targeting 220,000 tonnes of primary output and replacing jobs affected by the Arvida closure in 2025. Quebec will provide support contingent on job retention and emissions targets. | Rio Tinto will invest $1.4B to expand its Jonquiere, Quebec aluminium smelter, adding 96 AP60 low-carbon pots and increasing capacity by roughly 220,000 tonnes per year. The project is intended to replace jobs as the older Arvida plant closes in 2025, supported by Quebec funding.

Copper and other base metals in London climbed after China cut a key borrowing rate for the first time in 10 months to support growth. Traders expected improved industrial demand, while a weaker dollar also made greenback-priced metals cheaper for non-dollar buyers. LME aluminium, nickel, and copper rose modestly. | An analyst discusses how Indonesia’s decision to ban bauxite exports creates policy uncertainty for mining and metals investors. Because bauxite is a key input for alumina and then aluminium production, reduced export availability is likely to tighten feedstock supply and support aluminium prices over coming weeks. | The article examines how Indonesia’s ban on bauxite exports could affect metals markets. Analysts note investor concern over policy uncertainty in Indonesia’s mining and metals sector. Because bauxite is crucial for alumina and aluminium production, the export restriction is expected to tighten supply and pressure aluminium prices in the near term. | SMM reports China’s alumina supply surplus in May was only 78,000 mt, well below earlier 200,000 mt expectations, helped by refinery maintenance and slower new project output releases. Prices stabilize as hydropower in Yunnan supports potential aluminium smelter restarts in late June, boosting alumina demand expectations. | Copper prices rose after China cut borrowing costs for the first time in 10 months, supporting growth expectations and risk sentiment. A softer dollar also helped. The Reuters snapshot showed LME aluminium and nickel moving higher alongside copper, though China’s total copper demand remained weaker month-on-month. | Rio Tinto said it will invest $1.1 billion to expand aluminium production at its Complexe Jonquiere smelter in Canada, supported by up to $111.3 million in Quebec funds. The capacity increase implies greater aluminium supply, which may pressure near-term aluminium price expectations. | Appian agreed to sell Atlantic Nickel and Mineração Vale Verde to ACG Electric Metals for $1.065 billion, supported by EV and trader financing including Glencore and Volkswagen’s PowerCo. Atlantic Nickel is a nickel sulphide producer, and MVV includes the Serrote copper-gold project, but no immediate production impact is stated. | The LME aluminum contract is increasingly backed by Russian-origin metal: its warehouse share rose from 52% in late April to 68% by end-May. US 200% duties and other sanctions have redirected flows, including more Russian sales to China. LME says demand remains adequate, despite concerns about price distortion. | A global aluminium producer is reducing its Q3 premium offer to $170/T from an initial $180/T. Despite the cut, the premium is still 31–36% above the current quarter’s $125–$130. Japan sets regional benchmarks via premiums paid over the LME cash price, indicating firmer near-term aluminium pricing. | Yunnan’s hydropower shortages previously forced aluminium smelters to cut output by roughly 40%, with curbs reducing capacity by 1.65 million tonnes. With rainfall improving, Yunnan plans to resume power and allow restart of about 20% of halted capacity in late June, subject to weather and demand. | Aston Bay and American West report new high-resolution gravity anomalies at the Storm Copper Project in Nunavut, identifying large, shallow copper sulfide targets coincident with historical EM/IP signals and previous drill intercepts. The news is exploration-oriented and does not indicate near-term supply changes, so market price impact is likely minimal. | Aston Bay reported new gravity and inversion results at its Storm Copper Project in Nunavut, identifying large, shallow (to ~200m) anomaly targets coincident with historical IP/EM signals and known copper mineralization. A 2022 drill hole intersected ~68.8m of chalcopyrite-bearing sulfides, suggesting potential for a larger copper system. | Golden Arrow reported Phase 1 drill results from its San Pietro IOCG project in Chile, including 34m at 0.41% Cu and high cobalt intercepts, plus additional shallow cobalt targets. It is exploration-focused and does not indicate near-term production changes, so near-term effects on copper prices are likely limited. | MacDonald Mines began a 1,200m drilling program at its Alwyn Cu-Au trend near Sudbury, reporting iron-silicate alteration with chalcopyrite that may indicate iron-rich IOCG-style mineralization. The release is exploratory rather than supply-related, with only limited, indirect relevance to copper and potential nickel. | Indonesia has enforced a bauxite export ban, prompting miners to seek a price floor and more funding for smelter development to avoid large layoffs. With domestic production and smelter capacity mismatched, the ban could reduce bauxite availability, but authorities expect absorption via rising smelter utilization. | Rio Tinto will invest CAD 1.4bn to expand its AP60 aluminium smelter at Jonquière, adding 96 AP60 pots and about 160,000 tonnes of primary aluminium capacity per year, alongside commissioning a recycling facility. The company highlights lower-carbon production supported by Quebec.

China’s bauxite supply is shifting to Guinea after Indonesia’s export ban. Guinea’s rainy season may slightly reduce second-half shipments, but 2023 imports from Guinea are estimated to exceed 90 million mt, up about 30% YoY. More bauxite feedstock is generally supportive for aluminium supply expectations. | Indonesia implemented a bauxite export ban from June 10, 2023, with no bauxite exports to China in April. Some Chinese alumina refineries cut output, while others secured alternative bauxite sources. The ban is expected to keep bauxite prices high, limiting margin relief for refineries and supporting aluminium-linked costs. | SMM reports that Yunnan is likely to issue a power load distribution notice for local aluminium smelters, offering hope for partial restarts of about 2 million mt of idled capacity. Clearer power guidance can quickly influence production expectations and near-term aluminium supply dynamics, supporting short-term price sentiment. | Shanghai copper rallied to a seven-week high as China’s demand outlook improved following a central bank rate cut and as tight supply conditions emerged. Refined output fell on maintenance and import activity weakened. A softer U.S. dollar provided additional support. LME aluminium and nickel also rose. | SECO/WARWICK says two European manufacturers (Poland and the Czech Republic) ordered additional EV/CAB continuous brazing lines for electric-vehicle battery coolers. The company highlights growing EV demand and aluminum brazing furnace usage for mass production, suggesting firmer near-term aluminum heat-exchanger demand. | A Jamaican court decision lifted an injunction that had restricted parts of Noranda/Discovery Bauxite’s mining operations. The company expects bauxite shipments to the Gramercy U.S. alumina refinery to return toward normal levels after a prior decline of about 40% during the legal wait. | SMM’s daily comments say base metals rose across SHFE and mostly higher on LME. SHFE nickel climbed 5.43% and SHFE aluminium rose 1.56%, while SHFE copper gained 1.07%. LME nickel and aluminium also increased. Softer USD and slightly higher oil prices supported commodity sentiment. | Meridian Mining UK reported results from a Mise-a-la-Masse geophysical survey and additional drill intersections at its Cabaçal copper-gold project, defining a 650m conductive feeder corridor and reporting multiple copper-bearing zones (CuEq). It also notes adding rigs to accelerate drilling, which can support bullish copper sentiment. | Copper rose on LME after a weaker dollar and hopes for additional China stimulus to lift growth in the top consumer. The report cites expectations of further Chinese rate easing and softer U.S. inflation supporting that view. Aluminium and nickel were also higher as base metals gained from improved sentiment. | Fastmarkets reports improving service activity but highlights ongoing aluminium price weakness drivers: weak construction and consumer demand in Europe, a failure of billet demand to materialize with lower spot/third-quarter offers, and rising aluminium warrant stocks in LME warehouses. It keeps a bearish view on US Midwest aluminium premiums and overall aluminium price bias.

Norsk Hydro says it has produced a first industrial batch of recycled aluminium using hydrogen-fueled production instead of natural gas. The hydrogen switch test, run at a casthouse in Navarra, Spain with Fives, is aimed at decarbonizing energy for recycling and studying effects on metal quality. | Norway is reported to be producing the world’s first aluminum using green hydrogen. The development points to potential reductions in carbon and energy costs versus conventional smelting pathways. Even if ramp-up is limited, it supports bullish sentiment toward aluminium production economics in the near term. | Emirates Global Aluminium says BMW Group is the first customer for CelestiAL-R, EGA’s solar-powered blend of primary and recycled aluminium. BMW has sourced CelestiAL-R since 2023 for electric drivetrain and structural parts. The product lowers carbon intensity and requires longer, more rigorous production and testing. | Red floods and river overflow in Kolaka district (Southeast Sulawesi) have inundated hundreds of hectares of rice fields near a giant Indonesia nickel mine area. The “red mud” resembles mining tailings from processes used for aluminum extraction and nickel operations. Damage is reported, but no direct output disruption is stated. | Emirates Global Aluminium (EGA) says it has begun supplying BMW with CelestiAL-R, an aluminium product made using solar energy and blended with recycled metal. EGA highlights significant CO2 reductions versus typical production and mentions a UAE recycling initiative and plans for a new recycling plant. | Kommersant/Reuters says Rusal will build a 4.8 Mt/year alumina refinery near St Petersburg to reduce reliance on imported alumina (65% of needs, mostly from China). Recent disruptions, including Ukraine supply halts and Australia’s alumina/bauxite export ban, have pushed Rusal’s first-half aluminium production costs up 33%. | Copper rallied to a five-week high on a weaker dollar and hopes that China will add economic stimulus after disappointing May output data. LME warehouse stocks fell and warrant cancellations suggest supply could leave the exchange soon, supporting tightness. Aluminium dipped, while nickel rose modestly. | Aluminum prices are range-bound, with the article emphasizing LME’s growing Russian inventory problem: Russian-origin primary aluminium rose to 68% of LME warehouse stocks in May. The LME has not restricted inflows despite sanctions, keeping supply ample and pressuring LME pricing versus CME. | Rio Tinto will expand its Quebec, Canada aluminum smelter, adding 96 AP60 pots and increasing primary aluminum capacity by about 160,000 metric tonnes per year (full ramp-up by end-2026). The project targets low-carbon “green” aluminum and follows potroom closures at the Arvida smelter site.

Copper hovered near a five-week high and was set for a third weekly gain as the dollar weakened and investors bet on additional China stimulus. Better U.S. retail-sales data boosted sentiment. The piece also notes rises in Shanghai and LME aluminium and nickel, reflecting supportive demand expectations. | SMM reports SHFE and LME aluminium fell modestly. It links the tone to weak domestic real estate/industrial data and tighter macro conditions. Fundamentals cite rising water levels in Yunnan, enabling potential smelter production resumption, but argues short-term output gains are limited and oversupply may build in Q3 amid weak consumption.

Shanghai alumina futures on the SHFE jumped up to 3.8% on debut, driven by high alumina production costs and improved demand outlook. Analysts cite elevated conversion costs versus the SHFE base and Indonesia’s June 10 bauxite export ban, while smelter activity in Yunnan is expected to lift demand. | London copper fell after a three-week rally as a firmer U.S. dollar reduced demand and data showed higher China refined copper production and rising LME inventories. SHFE stocks rose less, while aluminium and nickel prices also eased in LME and SHFE trading on the day. | London copper fell as investors assessed tepid near-term demand despite record-level production in China. LME stockpiles rose, offset by tight SHFE supplies and limited downside room cited by some analysts. Aluminium and nickel also declined on Monday, reflecting softer overall sentiment across industrial metals. | Shanghai Futures Exchange began trading the world’s first physically delivered alumina futures. Eight contracts were traded on the first day, with benchmark pricing reported. Alumina, mainly used for aluminium smelting, is central to aluminium production; the contract is intended to improve risk management amid volatile market prices. | Shanghai Futures Exchange listed the world’s first physically delivered alumina futures, starting with benchmark trading around 2,665 yuan/ton. Since alumina is the main feedstock for aluminium smelting (about 95% use), the launch may influence aluminium risk pricing and hedging, though no immediate supply or demand disruption is cited. | Chile’s President Boric tasked Codelco to lead lithium development while boosting copper output, as copper prices remain near multi-decade lows. Codelco’s internal turmoil after CEO Andre Sougarret’s resignation and uncertainty over whether lithium negotiations will absorb resources cloud near-term copper production prospects. | Shanghai Futures Exchange listed the world’s first physically delivered alumina futures, with Glencore buying in the first trade. Alumina is the key raw material for aluminium smelting, and China dominates alumina production and trade. The launch aims to improve risk management; no immediate production or demand shock is cited. | Indonesia said it is prepared to defend its bauxite export ban, potentially facing a World Trade Organization case, with China the biggest importer. Because bauxite is the key raw input for alumina and aluminium production, uncertainty over enforcement and legal outcomes may tighten near-term supply expectations and support aluminium prices.

Indonesia is enforcing a bauxite export ban and says it is ready to fight any WTO challenge from China, its biggest bauxite buyer. The ban leaves about 17 million tonnes of bauxite idle due to limited smelter capacity, raising expectations of tighter alumina feedstock availability and potential aluminium price support. | China’s SHFE began trading the world’s first physically delivered alumina futures on June 19. SHFE reported an initial benchmark around 2,665 yuan/tonne and trades between Glencore and a Chinese seller. As alumina is the main input for aluminium smelting, the listing supports hedging but may not immediately shift prices. | Copper prices fell on concerns about the lack of clear economic stimulus in China, alongside a firmer U.S. dollar and thin trading during a U.S. holiday. Falling Shanghai Futures Exchange-monitored stocks provided limited support. Aluminium and nickel also dropped on the same day’s demand sentiment. | Mayville Engineering Company agreed to acquire Mid-States Aluminum, a vertically integrated aluminum extrusions and fabrication manufacturer, for about $96 million. The acquisition is expected to be accretive and expand MEC’s lightweight materials capabilities. The release does not mention aluminum pricing, inventories, or supply disruptions, so near-term market impact appears limited. | Copper prices slipped on Tuesday as disappointing disappointment with China’s rate cuts hurt industrial-metal sentiment. The softer U.S. dollar provided some support, but copper still fell on the LME. Aluminium and nickel were also down, reflecting weaker broad risk appetite ahead of upcoming Chinese data and technical levels. | EURACTIV reports criticism that the EU’s Critical Raw Materials Act omits aluminium, despite expected demand growth from EVs, solar and power networks. The piece urges including aluminium in the strategic list to attract investment, support recycling, and stem EU primary smelter closures, implying improved supply outlook. | MTB Metals has begun field work to prepare drilling at its Telegraph porphyry copper-gold project in British Columbia’s Golden Triangle, using geophysics, hyperspectral analysis, and on-site XRF to rank targets. The update is exploratory and does not indicate production disruptions or supply changes, implying limited near-term price impact.

Innovation New Material said it will invest nearly USD191 million to build and operate an aluminum alloy sections plant in Vietnam via a local unit. The project targets Southeast Asia 3C electronics demand and leverages low labor and logistics. Shares fell 2.3%, while capacity growth could influence aluminum alloy supply expectations. | Copper prices in London fell as optimism faded over the scale of potential Chinese stimulus to spur demand for power and construction metals. The stronger US dollar ahead of Fed-related remarks further weighed on dollar-priced metals. LME copper stocks declined. Aluminium also dropped on weak technicals and falling inventories. | Ardagh and Crown Holdings fund additional grants for aluminum can-capture equipment at U.S. material recovery facilities. The program targets improved recovery of used beverage cans that are valuable in recycling. Reported results indicate materially higher annual can capture, supporting more recycled aluminum supply into canmaking. | Ardagh Metal Packaging and Crown Holdings are funding additional grants to install aluminum can capture equipment at MRF recycling facilities. Performance data shows nearly 140 million aluminum beverage cans captured annually, improving recycled aluminum availability and carbon savings. The program targets better sorting to reduce missorted can losses. | Indonesia will keep its bauxite ore export ban despite controversy and potential lawsuits. The policy aims to force downstream processing, but only four of 12 targeted bauxite smelters are operating, leading miners to cut output. The ban may reduce exports while adding value via refining capacity. | Indonesia will keep pushing a bauxite ore export ban introduced June 10, despite controversy and possible legal challenges. With only four of 12 targeted bauxite smelters operating, miners may cut output. The policy restricts bauxite feedstock supply while aiming to increase downstream processing and investment in refining capacity.

Copper prices eased in thin Chinese holiday trade as China had no fresh stimulus news and its market was closed for June 22-23. Reuters notes 3-month copper down while aluminium and nickel were slightly higher, with traders citing disappointment versus expectations and some support from a weaker dollar. | Copper prices rose in tepid Chinese holiday trade as a weaker dollar and falling LME inventories supported tight supply. Three-month copper gained on the London Metal Exchange. Aluminium and nickel were also higher, reflecting broadly supportive currency and inventory dynamics despite concerns over insufficient Chinese stimulus. | Morgan Stanley downgraded Alcoa (a major global aluminum producer) to underweight, citing sluggish global growth, a stronger dollar, and concerns about weaker commodity demand. The firm also adjusted its outlook for U.S. and global growth, expecting continued pressure on mining equities and related aluminum fundamentals. | Copper hit a two-month peak as LME-registered warehouse inventories fell sharply to the lowest since 2021 and cancelled warrants reduced available material. Aluminium and nickel rose modestly alongside copper. | Queensland’s government announced $20m in early works for CopperString 2.0, a 1,100-km transmission line linking the North West minerals province to the grid, plus a vanadium battery trial. The transmission build is likely to boost near-term copper demand expectations, while broader grid investment supports sentiment.

Reuters reports London copper is on track for its first weekly fall in four, with the three-month contract down around 0.5% after a firmer dollar and hawkish central-bank signals boosted recession fears. Nickel and aluminium are also flagged for their biggest weekly declines, reflecting broader base-metal pressure. | Copper was set for its first weekly decline in four weeks as the dollar jumped, making dollar-priced metals costlier. LME three-month copper fell, while nickel also dropped and aluminium slid. The moves were driven by hawkish central-bank comments, risk aversion, and weak China consumer stimulus expectations post-COVID lockdown slowdown. | Copper prices are set for a weekly drop as hawkish rate-hike expectations and a stronger U.S. dollar weigh on growth-sensitive commodities. LME stocks keep falling, but price weakness dominates. Aluminium and nickel are also down on the week, indicating broad risk-off pressure across industrial metals.

SMM’s morning comments report aluminium prices fell in both SHFE and LME trade ahead of the Dragon Boat Festival. It expects further pressure post-holiday from prospects of US rate hikes. Supply is set to rise due to restarted idled capacity in Yunnan and more ingot arrivals, while demand remains weak. | SMM reports both SHFE and LME aluminium fell around the Dragon Boat holiday. It attributes renewed downside risk to expected US rate hikes, capacity restarts in Yunnan boosting supply, and larger post-holiday ingot arrivals pressuring spot premiums. Softer demand outlook and low inventories may cap losses. | Amarc Resources and Freeport launched a 2023 airborne and ground exploration program across the JOY Cu-Au district in British Columbia, including surveys and road/bridge rehabilitation for planned drilling. The release is copper-specific but exploration-only, with no production changes, so any short-term impact on metal prices is likely small. | China’s unwrought aluminium alloy imports fell sharply in May 2023 (down 27.7% YoY and 11.6% MoM), reflecting weak domestic demand and intense price competition. Overseas ADC12 quotes declined and the yuan weakened, narrowing the import window. Arrivals are expected to stay near 80,000 mt, pressuring domestic prices lower. | Copper rose 0.4% on the LME as declining warehouse stocks and rain-impacted mining in Chile offset a strong dollar and growth concerns. The cash-to-3-month premium increased on tighter availability. Aluminium and nickel fell with other base metals on disappointing macro data, including weaker manufacturing and growth outlooks.

Copper prices rose as low supplies outweighed tepid demand concerns. The LME cash premium to the three-month contract widened, while on-warrant copper inventories fell to the lowest level since Oct 2021. Chinese SHFE and bonded inventories also declined. Aluminium and nickel rose too, suggesting broader support. | EDF and Trimet signed a long-term electricity supply contract for Trimet’s French primary aluminium smelter in Saint-Jean-de-Maurienne. Copper rose on LME and SHFE as investor sentiment improved on hopes for China stimulus after weaker-than-expected factory contraction. SMM reports muted aluminium billet trading in China, with soft downstream demand weighing on conversion margins. A00 aluminium prices edged up during the week, but the weekly average fell. Mayville Engineering Company completed its acquisition of Mid-States Aluminum, a vertically integrated custom aluminum extrusion and fabrication producer. The change targets volatility rather than underlying supply-demand. | The LME will cut daily price limits for outright aluminium and copper contracts to 12% from 15% across its venues starting July 24. The exchange says the tighter limits apply to permanent daily limit rules introduced after the March 2022 nickel spike, aiming to curb extreme volatility.

SMM reports mixed overnight moves in copper: LME copper fell 0.44% while SHFE rose slightly; spot premiums dropped on low-priced importer shipments in east China and weak downstream demand, with inventories in south China falling due to lack of arrivals. SMM reports SHFE and LME aluminium futures closed lower overnight. London copper slipped after China inflation data missed expectations, deepening demand worries. The dollar firmed on expectations for further Fed rate hikes. Nickel fell; aluminium rose slightly. | London copper fell 0.5% as disappointing China inflation data signaled slower recovery in the top metal consumer, weighing on demand expectations. SHFE and LME aluminium finished higher on the day, but the piece flags negative macro data (China CPI/PPI decline) and off-season demand. Rio Tinto will trial hydrogen processing and retrofit calciners at its Yarwun alumina refinery to replace natural gas with clean hydrogen, supported by ARENA funding. If successful, it could reduce alumina decarbonization costs and support aluminium production prospects. | Rio Tinto’s Yarwun alumina refinery in Queensland received $32 million for a pilot to replace natural gas with renewable hydrogen, including electrolyser and burner retrofits. Copper hit a three-week high as the dollar fell, making dollar-priced metals cheaper for non-USD holders. LME three-month copper was near unchanged but earlier rose to a recent high. China’s June primary aluminium output rose to 3.46 million tonnes as smelters in Yunnan resumed production after power curbs were eased. It also notes El Nino could disrupt copper supply from South America and create aluminium supply risks in China, but net near-term signals remain cautious. | Copper edged up on the LME as the weak dollar supported USD-priced metals, but sentiment was pressured by weak Chinese economic data. The announcement mainly concerns company asset reshuffling rather than market-wide aluminium fundamentals. | Copper fell in London as weak China GDP growth raised concerns about consumption and metals demand. Rio Tinto expects stronger iron-ore shipments but cut 2023 guidance for alumina to 7.4–7.7 million tons, citing plant stability and bauxite disruption from heavy rainfall, and reduced refined copper output to 160,000–190,000 tons due to a broader Kennecott smelter rebuild. The changes may weigh on copper and aluminium-linked markets. | Rio Tinto raised its iron-ore shipment outlook but cut production guidance for alumina and refined copper. Alumina guidance was lowered amid efforts to improve plant stability and disruption at Weipa due to heavy rainfall. The company plans additional geophysics and deep drilling in 2023, but no production or supply changes are stated. | Chalco said its chairman, Liu Jianping, resigned for a “work adjustment.” Reuters notes this comes days after Bloomberg reported a Chalco unit terminated some aluminium contracts during a government-led investigation into market deals. ARENA announced $32.1m for Rio Tinto and Sumitomo to trial hydrogen calcination at the Yarwun alumina refinery in Queensland.
