It points to tariffs and CBAM reshaping trade flows, energy volatility affecting smelting, geopolitical risk and production inflexibility, and the role of low-carbon aluminium. It frames these as key drivers of aluminium pricing discussions.

Norsk Hydro cut 2025 capex guidance and froze hiring as U.S. tariffs roiled aluminium trade flows, pushing physical premiums to record highs and raising import costs for U.S. buyers. The company’s Q2 core profit rose on higher aluminium and energy prices, suggesting near-term price support for aluminium amid volatile demand. | China’s unwrought aluminum alloy imports fell 12.3% YoY in June and 11.6% YoY in H1 2025. The decline is linked to squeezed import profits from persistent domestic-overseas price spreads, plus off-season demand constraints. After mid-June, losses eased but volumes are expected to stay low in July-August. | China imported 18.116 Mt of bauxite in June, with Guinea the main source. The Guinea rainy season is expected to start affecting supply from August, reducing imported bauxite. Meanwhile, demand is stable and inventories are high, keeping a surplus and limiting major aluminium-related price moves; bauxite CIF likely oscillates. | Guangxi, a major aluminium hub in China, plans cooperation with Vietnam’s Cao Bang on aluminium mining and processing. Discussions cover exploration, production and processing models, and emphasize environmental controls such as reduced red mud discharge. The feasibility of mineral projects is being assessed in Cao Bang.

Alcoa’s CEO urges a U.S. carve-out for Canada from 50% aluminium tariffs, arguing levies have made Canadian exports unprofitable and cut Quebec smelter profits. Canada supplies most U.S. primary aluminium imports. A potential exception or later trade deal could change near-term aluminium supply expectations. | Japan and the US agreed to keep a 50% tariff on steel and aluminium under their bilateral trade deal, NHK reported. Maintaining high duties can weigh on aluminium import demand and regional pricing expectations. The steel portion may add some broader industrial-metals sentiment, though aluminium is the direct link. | Guangxi province in China’s largest aluminium hub seeks cooperation with Vietnam’s Cao Bằng province on aluminium-based product exploration, mining and processing. Discussions include environmental waste treatment for bauxite mining and the use of advanced red-mud reduction technology. The cooperation models are flexible, with feasibility exploration planned, limiting immediate supply effects. | SMM data shows China’s June 2025 net primary aluminium imports fell 9.4% MoM to 172,700 mt, while domestic spot transactions stayed thin due to off-season demand and high prices. Import margins are negative (~1,400 yuan/mt), making the import window closed and expected net imports remain low in July. | New Zealand’s H1 building code updates raise energy-efficiency requirements for windows, pushing builders toward thermally broken aluminium frames to meet insulation performance standards. The article argues adoption is accelerating as suppliers expand and thermally broken joinery moves beyond a luxury upgrade, implying increased aluminium demand.

The US is considering 50% tariffs on copper imports from Aug 1, with businesses warning it will raise copper prices paid by US construction, factories, and power/EV-related users. The tariff scope is still unclear and may affect refined versus intermediate products, while traders reportedly front-loaded supplies ahead of implementation. | SMM says domestic aluminum scrap faced upward pressure WoW, but off-season weak orders kept transactions sluggish. Spot primary and ADC12 prices rose then fell, while cost support from higher aluminum and silicon limited downside. Despite tight supply, weak demand and rising social inventories are expected to keep secondary aluminum rangebound. | Brixton Metals began a second drill at its Catalyst copper-gold porphyry target within the Thorn Project in NW British Columbia. The company reports a refined copper-in-soil anomaly, supportive IP geophysics, and encouraging initial rock assays, with results pending. It is exploration activity without near-term supply effects for metals. | A Russian-owned alumina refinery in Limerick has been suspended from the energy market, according to the article. The outage or restrictions on energy supply can interrupt alumina availability for smelters, tightening immediate feedstock expectations and supporting aluminium prices over the coming days to weeks. | The U.S. raised Section 232 aluminium tariffs to 50%, immediately increasing duties on Canadian imports and squeezing exporter margins at Alcoa and Rio Tinto despite higher Midwest premiums. Producers rerouted cargo to Europe/Asia, cut capex and costs, and lobbied for relief, warning prolonged trade friction could hurt aluminium demand.

Trump’s move to double U.S. aluminum import tariffs to 50% is reshaping Canadian trade flows, with Canadian aluminum redirecting toward Europe where margins are tight. Producers are delaying capex and some aluminum product makers are cutting hours and seeking liquidity help, weighing further demand weakness.

Indonesia’s MIND ID, via INALUM, plans to expand aluminum capacity to about 900,000 tonnes per annum by 2029, including a new smelter in Mapawah and SGAR alumina refinery Phase I commissioning with Phase II planned. The move targets reducing Indonesia’s aluminum supply shortfall amid rising EV and solar demand. | The New York Times, citing a US official, says the US-EU trade deal does not remove the existing 50% US tariffs on steel and aluminum applied globally outside the EU. A reduction would require further talks. The background includes reciprocal tariff orders and temporary suspensions of higher rates. | Ghana cancelled a $1.2 billion bauxite lease for the Nyinahin Hills that would have fed a mine and alumina refinery JV tied to alumina/aluminium production. The government is seeking new partners such as EGA, but no binding deals are set, implying delays and weaker near-term supply expectations for aluminium. | SMM discusses how China’s aluminum industry may adopt a PV + energy storage model to enable more renewable power despite intermittency. It cites lower PV and storage costs versus coal and subsidies/mandatory storage rules, but also highlights high capex, declining incentives, and low green aluminum premiums, limiting immediate incentives. | SMM reports China’s primary aluminum ingot inventory in mainstream consumption areas rising to 533,000 mt, with weak weekly outflows and high in-transit volumes. Despite some regional easing, overall supply pressure remains high while demand is soft, suggesting continued inventory buildup through mid-August and bearish aluminum price pressure.

World primary aluminium production rose 1.4% YoY in Q2 2025 and lifted H1 output to 36.459 million tonnes. The report notes bullish LME aluminium price support, but rising alumina (Platts) costs above $363/t and a seasonal June production dip. Overall, it’s more descriptive than a clear forward catalyst. | Researchers at Montanuniversität Leoben, with industry partners, are developing a thermally assisted process to shred, clean, and smelt used aluminum coffee capsules into aluminum ingots and 0.1mm foils. The goal is industrial closed-loop reuse of capsule aluminum, potentially reducing demand for primary aluminum. | Ghana cancelled a $1.2bn bauxite lease for the Nyinahin Hills, ending a JV intended to mine bauxite and build an alumina refinery. GIADEC now seeks new partners, including EGA and Chinese firms, aiming for extraction in Q1 next year. New investment prospects may support aluminium-linked supply expectations. | Ghana terminated Rocksure’s lease for the Nyinahin bauxite deposit, ending an operator role in a planned $1.2B mine and refinery with GIADEC. Authorities may seek a new foreign partner, with Emirates Global Aluminium mentioned. The reasons are undisclosed and near-term production impact is unclear. | Ghana cancelled a $1.2bn bauxite lease tied to a joint venture to build a mine and alumina refinery in Nyinahin Hills. With the lease void due to lack of parliamentary ratification, GIADEC is courting new partners such as Emirates Global Aluminium. The change adds development uncertainty for aluminium feedstock. | Indonesia’s MIND ID plans to raise national aluminum production capacity to about 900,000 tonnes per year by 2029, from 275,000 tonnes, including a new 600 KTPA facility in Mempawah and expanded alumina refining. The goal is to cut supply-demand gaps and imports, supported by downstream demand growth.

A newspaper piece claims Australia’s all-renewables energy policy has increased power prices and threatens Tomago Aluminium’s 24/7 operations. It warns that when the smelter’s potlines are shut due to grid needs, restarting could take weeks. The article suggests this could impair aluminium supply and competitiveness. | The piece claims Australia’s all-renewables energy policy has sharply increased power prices and threatens grid stability for Tomago Aluminium. It warns that potline shutdowns may be hard to restart, risking reduced aluminium output. It also criticizes a proposed low-carbon aluminium credit scheme and argues reliability is essential. | The piece argues Australia’s all-renewables energy policy is raising power prices and threatening grid reliability for Tomago Aluminium, a ~1,000 MW smelter. It warns that powering down potlines may delay restart by weeks, potentially disrupting aluminium output. It also notes a low-carbon aluminium subsidy scheme. | Aluminum prices rose to a four-month high on the LME, supported by renewed optimism in Chinese demand and global supply constraints. Key factors cited include China nearing its production cap and energy-policy curtailments, sanctions limiting Russian supply, and tariff disruptions reshaping flows, alongside infrastructure and EV/renewables demand.

Trump announced a 50% tariff on certain copper imports, starting Friday, ending the de minimis small-package exemption and using a Section 232 copper investigation. The duty targets semi-finished copper products and copper derivatives while sparing raw cathodes and concentrates, likely raising U.S. costs and pressuring copper prices downward in the market. | Ghana’s Ministry of Lands denies reports that a $1.2bn bauxite lease to Rocksure International was cancelled, saying no valid parliamentary-ratified lease existed. It disputes the $1.2bn valuation and says GIADEC will manage bauxite development, including alumina refining, aluminium smelting, and VALCO retrofitting. | Trump has signed an order imposing a 50% import tariff on copper, effective as early as Friday. The announcement immediately drove U.S. copper prices down as much as 18%, the largest intraday drop since 1989, and pressured copper miners’ shares. The tariff follows similar measures on steel and aluminum imports. | Prysmian said a new U.S. copper tariff scheme will raise costs for importers of copper-intensive cables and support pricing, with management upgrading its outlook. The tariff includes 50% levies on semi-finished copper products from August 1. Analysts expect benefits for U.S. low-voltage margins; aluminium may face similar logic. | The SBS podcast says the US Fed held rates as tariffs remain a concern and discusses fresh US tariff announcements that triggered a sharp copper drop (reported as the biggest since 1988). It links the volatility to ad hoc tariff changes and uncertainty about inflation and growth impacts. | Guinea revoked mining licences for under-performance, forcing Axis Minerals’ bauxite sites in the Boffa–Fria corridor into care-and-maintenance and furloughing workers. SD Mining is seeking to restart while GIC appeals. The article suggests global bauxite remains surplus, limiting immediate aluminium price pressure. | SMM reports China’s July primary aluminium output rose slightly, but the proportion of liquid aluminium at smelters fell by 2.06 percentage points to 73.77%, reflecting strong off-season cuts in downstream alloying. Despite high operating capacity into August and some replacement commissioning, end-use demand weakness keeps liquid share expected low. | Unifor says workers at Dajcor Aluminum in Chatham started a strike after rejecting a tentative agreement and accusing the company of preparing to remove equipment and bring in scab labor. The dispute follows past public funding for capacity expansion, and aims to protect jobs at the aluminium facility. | SMM reports July’s primary aluminium alloy PMI and operating rate improved, supported by billet production cuts and conversion to alloys, with new export orders rebounding. However, it warns demand remains weak in the off-season, high aluminium prices reduce demand, and US-China tariffs keep export expectations uncertain. Aluminium wheel exports held up but may need deeper adjustment. | Trek will make nearly all its aluminum bicycle frames from October 2025 using aluminum sourced from renewable-powered facilities, recycled content, or both, limiting emissions to 11 kg CO2e/kg or less. Trek frames this as a major shift in its aluminum supply chain and supports broader adoption. | U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said trade talks with Canada focus on aluminum tariff levels as firms such as Ford report large tariff costs. Canada faces a 50% aluminum tariff into the U.S., and talks aim to resolve the tariff row before a deadline. Possible relief would support aluminum sentiment. | Trump announced a 90-day extension of the U.S.-Mexico trade deal that keeps steep tariffs on industrial metals. Mexico will pay 50% tariffs on aluminum and copper (along with steel) and 25% on fentanyl, alongside requirements to eliminate non-tariff trade barriers. Cartel-finance sanctions add pressure on Mexico.

Fastmarkets reports Mexico’s primary aluminium market has thin spot liquidity, with producers reducing capacity and imports/exports constrained by tariff uncertainty. Participants expect imported premium pressure to stay downward as inventories linger. The market is also “in limbo” pending US-Mexico talks, suggesting additional downside risk for aluminium prices and premiums. | Unionized workers at Dajcor Aluminum’s Chatham facility began a strike after rejecting a tentative wage agreement. Unifor alleged the company planned to remove equipment and use scab labour, risking job losses and production integrity. Dajcor said it will still meet customer requirements but strikes may disrupt aluminium extrusion, fabrication and anodizing output. | Unionized workers at the Dajcor aluminum plant in Chatham are on strike, according to the report. The work stoppage is expected to interrupt aluminum production, potentially tightening near-term supply and supporting aluminum prices over the next days to weeks, depending on outage duration. | Union workers at Dajcor Aluminum’s Chatham plant are on strike after rejecting a tentative contract, alleging the company plans to remove equipment and use scab labour. The stoppage risks disrupting aluminum extrusions, fabrication, and anodizing deliveries. The dispute is ongoing while both sides negotiate wages and terms.

Trek will source nearly all aluminum bicycle frames from facilities powered by renewable energy and/or using recycled content, making low-emission aluminium the default across its alloy lineup from October 2025. The change covers over one million bikes annually and aims to cut aluminium’s lifecycle emissions, potentially boosting demand for lower-carbon supply. | Vedanta expects primary aluminium cash (hot-metal) costs to fall below $1,700/t in H2FY26 as Train II at Lanjigarh ramps captive alumina and the Sijimali bauxite mine nears approval. Alumina cash costs are targeted to drop with higher captive mix, alongside debottlenecking and smelter capacity upgrades.

The Trump administration says it investigated copper imports under Section 232 on national-security grounds and then imposed new tariffs on copper and copper-intensive derivative products. The policy targets greater domestic copper mining, smelting and refining, aiming to reduce reliance on imports and support US copper businesses. | SMM data show 20 new secondary aluminium projects in China in H1 2025, adding 3.31mn mt of planned capacity. Most are still in environmental assessment or construction, so limited capacity is currently operational. Secondary wrought aluminium is expanding faster than cast, improving economics via recycling and lower costs. | Molson Coors cut its annual profit outlook, blaming weak U.S. beer demand and aluminum tariff impacts. The company’s guidance suggests higher aluminum-related costs and softer use of aluminum packaging, which can dampen near-term demand expectations and weigh on aluminium prices over coming days to weeks. | Ball Corp lifted its annual profit forecast, attributing the upgrade to strong global demand. As an aluminum can manufacturer, higher expected sales and improved demand outlook for aluminum packaging can boost near-term market sentiment toward aluminum prices over the coming days to weeks. | The White House issued executive orders imposing a 50% Section 232 tariff on imports of semi-finished copper and copper-intensive derivative products effective Aug 1, while excluding copper ores, cathodes and scrap. It also adds tariffs on Brazil effective Aug 6 and suspends de minimis entry, likely pressuring demand and pricing for imported industrial inputs. | Molson Coors cut its profit and sales outlook, citing softer U.S. demand and higher can costs from U.S. tariffs on aluminum. The company expects incremental costs of $20m–$35m in the second half as levies lift the U.S. Midwest Premium used for aluminum pricing, pressuring near-term aluminum-related economics. | Molson Coors cut its forecast, citing softer U.S. consumer demand and higher costs from aluminum tariffs. The brewer expects incremental costs of $20m–$35m in the second half as levies boost the Midwest Premium used for U.S./Canada aluminum pricing. Aluminum price expectations are mentioned as potentially reverting later. | Molson Coors cut its annual profit outlook, citing higher costs from Trump’s aluminum import tariffs. Aluminum duties doubled to 50% since June, raising the price of the metal the company uses for beer cans. The tariff spike also pressures demand and net sales as consumers reduce spending on alcohol. | PyroGenesis will supply plasma torch technology for Constellium’s demonstrator aluminum remelting furnace as part of a decarbonization initiative to replace traditional heating sources with all-electric plasma torches. Terms are undisclosed and commissioning is expected by Q1 2026, suggesting mainly longer-dated technology development.

Canada’s exports of unwrought aluminum to the U.S. fell more than 11% in June after the U.S. doubled Section 232 tariffs on aluminum to 50%. The article notes aluminum accounted for about 60% of U.S.-collected duties on Canadian imports, signaling near-term pressure on aluminum trade flows. | Aluminium Bahrain (Alba) reported Q2 2025 profit of $65.3 million, down sharply YoY, while revenue rose. The company cites higher landed alumina costs as a key drag. It notes LME aluminium averaged about $2,447/t in Q2, dipping earlier on tariffs but recovering, remaining volatile. | Kyrgyzstan significantly increased its unprocessed aluminium shipments to China in the first half of 2025, with exports more than doubling in both value and volume. The data, cited from China’s customs administration, suggests greater incoming supply to China, which can weigh on aluminium price expectations over the next few weeks. | Guinea revoked Emirates Global Aluminium’s bauxite mining concession and moved assets to a state-backed entity, citing non-compliance with refinery-planning requirements. EGA’s Guinea operations had already seen export and mining suspensions. The action threatens bauxite supply from a major producer (about 2–3% globally), supporting aluminium prices. | The Trump administration issued a Copper Section 232 investigation and then a proclamation imposing universal 50% tariffs on over 70 categories of semi-finished copper products and copper-intensive derivatives effective Aug. 1, 2025. Copper ores, concentrates, cathodes, and anodes are exempt. Policies aim to strengthen US domestic copper supply. | Guinea revoked Emirates Global Aluminium’s bauxite concession for alleged mining-code violations and transferred the assets to a state-backed firm without compensation. The move escalates a dispute over a planned alumina refinery. Despite ongoing uncertainty, analysts said the quick transfer could calm short-term bauxite supply fears, muting near-term aluminium price impact. | SMM announces the methodology and launch timing (8 August 2025) for its European Aluminium P1020A Rotterdam duty-paid and duty-unpaid spot premium assessments. The premium is defined versus LME official cash. Data collection and publication mechanics are detailed, supporting physical contract settlement and risk management expectations.

Rio Tinto approved a US$180 million Norman Creek access project at its Amrun bauxite mine and is progressing early works on the Kangwinan capacity expansion. The projects aim to increase and secure long-term bauxite supply and port export capacity for Weipa operations, supporting future aluminium raw-material availability. | Rio Tinto approved a $180 million investment for its Norman Creek bauxite area within the Amrun operations in Queensland. The project includes building infrastructure such as a haul road and worker accommodation, with first production expected in 2027. Rio says the investment supports future bauxite output and confidence in operations. | India and Russia signed a protocol to deepen cooperation, explicitly including aluminium, along with mining technology and railway transport. The working group discussed capacity building and technology transfer in mining and other areas. The aluminium cooperation could influence short-term market expectations for supply and trade, though details are not provided. | India and Russia signed a protocol to deepen cooperation in aluminium, along with fertilisers, railways and mining technology. The working group discussed capacity building and technology transfer in mining and industrial waste management. The aluminium focus could influence near-term supply/trade expectations, though no volumes or timelines were specified. | The UAE says it has delivered its first batch of low-carbon aluminium (MinimAL) produced by Emirates Global Aluminium using electricity from the Barakah nuclear plant. EGA/ENEC partnership and early supply to Egypt’s Canex aim to expand low-carbon aluminium availability, supporting demand for lower-emissions aluminium and potentially supporting prices. | Emirates Global Aluminium (EGA) delivered the UAE’s first low-carbon aluminium batch using 24/7 electricity from the Barakah nuclear plant, certified under I-REC protocols. The material, MinimAL, will be supplied to CANEX Aluminum for infrastructure and solar uses. The move supports decarbonisation and low-carbon aluminium demand.  | SLAM Exploration reports additional IP survey results over its Goodwin copper-nickel-cobalt zones, extending deep conductive targets and identifying chargeability/resistivity anomalies east of the Granges and Logan zones. It also references prior drilling with 1.49% copper-equivalent over 64.9 meters. The update supports future potential but isn’t a current supply change. | Copper prices rose as China’s July trade data beat forecasts, lifting sentiment for industrial metals. Chinese exports and copper/copper concentrate imports increased, supporting smelter demand. Optimism around a potential U.S.-China trade deal also helped, while aluminium and nickel prices advanced in tandem. | SMM reports ADC12 secondary aluminum prices rose sharply as aluminum scrap supply tightened and scrap prices climbed with primary aluminum. Despite weak end-use demand from seasonal off-season holidays and limited new orders, cost pressure and improved theoretical margins supported upward bias. Inventory is flat-to-slightly destocking, keeping prices well supported. | The US will impose a 50% Section 232 tariff on the copper-content value of semi-finished copper and intensive copper-derivative imports effective Aug 1, 2025, with CBP entry guidance and no in-transit exceptions. Raw copper (anode/cathode/scrap) is excluded, pressuring copper-linked supply and landed costs for US buyers. | APAA praised President Trump for imposing a Section 232 aluminum tariff, claiming it helps U.S. primary aluminium producers. Century Aluminum, the largest U.S. primary producer, plans to raise output by 10%, invest $50 million, and return its Mt. Holly facility to full capacity, creating over 100 jobs.  | The American Primary Aluminum Association praises President Trump’s Section 232 aluminium tariff, saying it enables Century Aluminum to increase U.S. primary production by 10% after a capacity restoration at its Mt. Holly facility. The announcement cites a $50 million investment and job creation, signaling improved domestic aluminium supply conditions.

A Port of Oswego FTZ allows aluminum importers to temporarily cushion the impact of Trump’s 50% tariff on Canadian raw aluminum. Companies previously stockpiled ahead of tariff increases and use bonded handling and storage solutions. Importers order less due to logistical limits, but FTZ relief helps sustain supply access.

Tomago Aluminium warned it could close in 2028 unless it reaches an affordable long-term energy deal for its large smelter. It rejected a renewables-based power quote and faces a contract ending in 2028. The Australian government is offering $2 billion in aluminium production tax credits as talks continue. | Tomago Aluminium told workers it may close as early as 2028 unless it can negotiate an affordable renewable-based power contract after its AGL supply agreement ends. The current operator rejected a renewables quote as too expensive, risking higher costs and potential downtime, while government credits are discussed. | Tomago Aluminium told smelter workers it may close in 2028 unless it can negotiate an affordable renewable-based power contract after its AGL deal expires. The company rejected a renewal quote as infeasible, citing high energy prices that could add over $100 million to bills, while government transition credits are discussed. | Tomago Aluminium warns it may close in 2028 without an affordable post-2028 renewable power contract. The smelter relies on a large, steady power supply and has rejected a high renewables pricing offer. Government support via production tax credits is discussed, but no deal is reached. | Tomago Aluminium told workers it likely will close in 2028 unless it can negotiate an affordable renewable power contract. It rejected an offer for post-2028 supply as too expensive, and high electricity prices are jeopardizing the plant. The smelter produces 600,000 tonnes annually, with government credits discussed. | Pakistan’s commerce minister says a US reciprocal tariff deal will be more favorable for exporting refined, value-added copper because refined copper is exempt from the earlier 50% tariff. He expects US interest to invest in Pakistan’s copper mining and processing, supported by regulatory and infrastructure upgrades. | Alvance British Aluminium took delivery of 20 refurbished powder tank wagons to improve alumina transport from Blyth port to its Fort William smelter. The higher-payload wagons and upgraded loading facility aim to reduce journeys and support predictive maintenance, ensuring uninterrupted 24/7 smelting operations. | A report explains that Trump’s 50% aluminum import tariff has pushed U.S. aluminum prices to record premiums over the global LME price. It argues recycling and scrap supply can’t quickly replace lost imports, so higher costs will likely persist as stockpiles deplete and firms pass through expenses. | Grid Metals announced a grassroots surface discovery of semi-massive nickel sulfide (“Pavo” anomaly) at its Makwa Ni-Cu-PGE project, with grab samples up to 1.1% nickel and plans for follow-up EM surveys and drilling in Fall 2025. The update is exploration-stage with no immediate supply effect. | Guinea nationalized UAE-linked bauxite assets by terminating GAC/EGA’s Boké concession and transferring it to a state entity, citing legal violations including failure to build a promised refinery. The government also seized stockpiled bauxite and signaled new partner talks, disrupting expected bauxite supply continuity for aluminium markets. | Bonnell Aluminum reported a 20% decline in US extrusion orders after Section 232 tariffs on steel and aluminum rose to 50% from 4 June. Customers paused orders to evaluate the higher tariff, while Bonnell implemented Q3 price increases to offset related cost increases. Lower extrusion demand drove the order fall.

MMG reported a sharp rise in interim profit and cash flow, driven by higher copper output across its mines and stronger copper (and other metal) prices, alongside lower unit costs at Las Bambas. It also cited record payable copper sales. Nickel is referenced for an acquisition, with no immediate impact detailed. | U.S. aluminum tariffs have lifted the Midwest premium to record highs, but investors are cautious as trade talks with key suppliers (especially Canada) remain unresolved. Any duty rollback could cut the premium substantially. Separately, aluminum demand is soft, with sluggish service-center buying and weaker ISM manufacturing conditions. | UC Rusal is repairing damaged rail infrastructure in Bog Walk Gorge to retrieve derailed rail cars used for alumina extraction operations linked to its Windalco bauxite plant. The incident also caused a temporary gorge closure and power outages. Authorities ordered investigations and safety measures. Repairs may disrupt alumina supply logistics in coming days. | A bauxite train operated by Windalco derailed in St Catherine’s Bog Walk Gorge, prompting track and utility repairs and a temporary closure of the area. UC Rusal and JPS crews are restoring rail infrastructure so vehicles can extract alumina from submerged cars while investigations into the derailment continue.

A Wisconsin aluminum foundry says Trump’s 50% tariffs on imported aluminum are raising costs because it relies on imported primary aluminum as a key input. The higher tariff-driven price reduces demand, leading to layoffs and slower hiring, indicating near-term bearish pressure for aluminum markets. | NEPA said its assessment of UC Rusal’s derailed bauxite train in Jamaica found low environmental risk: minimal alumina spillage confined near the embankment, no hazardous materials, no oil contamination, and no fish deaths. Cleanup is ongoing and investigations will determine the derailment’s cause and safety improvements. | SMM survey data show secondary aluminium alloy producers’ operating rate rose to 42.2% in July, helped by stronger orders (e.g., NEVs, motorcycle parts) and aluminium futures-triggered buying of ADC12. For August, demand is weaker with off-season conditions and high-temperature holiday breaks, expected to pressure operating rates. | AriZona’s founder says the brand’s 99-cent can price may rise for the first time in 30 years if proposed U.S. aluminum tariffs take effect. The company uses aluminum in its tallboys, with part imported from Canada that would face a 50% tariff, increasing costs and potentially supporting higher aluminum prices.

Alcoa faces public scrutiny over proposals to expand bauxite mining and increase alumina production in Western Australia’s jarrah forests. Local councils oppose the plans citing water security and rehabilitation concerns, while the company argues emissions impacts are overstated and largely tied to overseas smelting. Regulatory outcomes could affect aluminium supply expectations. | SMM reports Southeast Asia’s scrap aluminum exports fell sharply in 2025 and are unlikely to recover short term. Export licensing probes, border disruptions, port congestion, and rerouting raise costs and slow flows. Meanwhile, U.S. tariffs up to 50% curb demand, worsening the contraction despite stronger local recycled-aluminum demand. | Tightened aluminum scrap supply pushed prices higher, with SMM A00 scrap up and shredded tense scrap especially firm due to difficulties sourcing raw material. Policy adjustments and higher costs (including rising auxiliary inputs like silicon and copper) lifted ADC12 alloy prices, while weak demand and inventory buildup limit upside. | South32 says its Mozal Aluminium smelter will curtail pot relining and later move to care and maintenance from March 2026 because affordable, sufficient electricity supply has not been secured despite negotiations with Mozambique’s utilities. The move is expected to cut output to about 240,000 tonnes in FY2026. | Ontario launched a $1 billion loan program for firms affected by increased U.S. tariffs under Section 232, focusing on auto, steel and aluminum supply chains. The loans help cover payroll, leases and utilities to prevent layoffs and closures, potentially cushioning aluminium-related demand and production disruption in the short term. | Norsk Hydro said it will cut 750 white-collar jobs to reduce annual costs by 1 billion Norwegian crowns and previously announced a hiring freeze and reduced capex. The cuts affect support and commercial functions, with blue-collar jobs unchanged, indicating no immediate aluminium supply disruption. | South32 warned it may mothball its Mozambique Mozal aluminium smelter unless it secures affordable power as its supply agreement expires around March 2026. It cited potential cash-flow negativity under current terms and is curtailing activities like pot relining. Reduced output expectations and power uncertainty may tighten aluminium supply.

Rusal, the world’s largest aluminium producer outside China, swung to a first-half net loss as costs surged, interest expenses rose, and FX losses hit results. Higher alumina and raw material prices plus elevated electricity and transport costs weighed on margins, while Western sanctions have discouraged new contracts for Russian metal. | China’s downstream aluminium processors’ weekly operating rate rose 0.8pp MoM to 59.5%, with gains in primary alloy, plate/sheet and strip, wire and cable, and foil. Drivers include improved cargo pick-up, power-grid tenders, and peak-season restocking. Secondary aluminium weakened slightly due to off-season demand and losses, limiting upside. | Wanji Holding will move aluminium smelting capacity from Henan to Xinjiang, replacing a 580,000 t/yr smelter with a new facility using 600 kA electrolytic baths. Construction is planned to start March 2026 and production in December 2027. The move aligns with China’s aluminium capacity cap and greener-energy upgrades.

The industry ministry says it will expand SME support in response to the US adding 407 more steel and aluminum derivative items to its 50% tariff regime starting Monday. The ministry expects further tariff widening next month and plans expanded consulting, verification of alloy content, and lower cost-sharing to help firms comply. | The article headline says the government will expand support for industries as the US broadens 50% tariffs on steel and aluminum. The tariff move is likely to disrupt aluminum trade flows and near-term demand expectations, creating downward pressure on aluminum prices, though mitigation efforts could cushion the impact. | The report states that Washington expanded a 50% tariff scope covering steel and aluminium, prompting Seoul to announce support measures for businesses. The tariff widening is expected to change near-term aluminium trade flows and reduce import-driven demand, creating downward pressure on aluminium market sentiment over days to weeks.

Trump’s administration expanded tariffs on steel and aluminum imports, adding a 5% tariff to hundreds of goods containing steel or aluminum components. The May doubling of metal tariffs to 50% is already feeding into producer prices, with aluminum and steel higher year-on-year. Consumers may face higher costs for items using aluminum inputs, supporting metal prices near term. | The US Department of Commerce expanded the list of steel and aluminium derivative products subject to US tariffs, adding 407 new HTSUS codes to the 50% rate. This broadens duties on a wide range of consumer and industrial goods like appliances and vehicles, which can dampen near-term demand for metal-intensive inputs. | Tsingshan Holding Group will invest $800m to expand its Dinson Iron and Steel Company in Zimbabwe, building blast furnace and steel plants and a 50MW thermal power unit. The project aims to double carbon steel output and reduce steel imports. The nickel producer’s deeper presence may support nickel-related supply expectations, with weaker spillover to copper and aluminium. | Trump expanded steel and aluminum tariffs by adding 400 more items, according to the report. The additional tariff coverage is likely to reduce or delay aluminum imports and trading activity, worsening short-term demand expectations and export flows. This can pressure aluminum prices lower over coming days and weeks. | The Trump administration announced high-priority enforcement under the Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act for additional Chinese imports, including copper. It also designated caustic soda and red dates for enforcement. This limited relief could ease aluminum supply-cost pressures versus the earlier 50% tariff. | Nagoya is considering an ordinance to stop unauthorized collection of aluminum cans and other recyclables from designated points.
