Reports say a significant explosion occurred at an aluminium plant in Greene County. The incident raises concerns about damage and production downtime, potentially reducing near-term aluminium output. Traders may adjust expectations for short-term supply availability, which can weigh on aluminium prices over the coming days. | Nigeria and Africa Finance Corporation signed a $1.3 billion deal to build a gas-powered alumina refinery, processing 1 million tons of bauxite annually and targeting about 19 million tons of alumina over 20 years. The government links it to boosting mining revenue, reducing oil reliance, and accelerating exploration. | Nigeria’s federal government and the African Finance Corporation signed a MoU for a $1.3 billion alumina refinery project, including bauxite processing and a Bayer-process flowsheet with power generation. The project is projected to produce significant alumina volumes over a 20-year period, boosting value addition and potential alumina supply. | Nigeria’s Federal Government, via the Solid Minerals Development Fund, signed a MoU with Africa Finance Corporation for a $1.3bn alumina refinery and related solid-minerals initiatives. The refinery would process bauxite using a Bayer process, improving domestic alumina supply prospects and supporting aluminium value-chain expectations. | Copper prices have reversed sharply after an early-January peak driven by supply constraints and U.S.-tariff-related pre-emptive stockpiling. Traders may scramble for aluminium units, supporting a higher LME aluminium price and pushing Rotterdam premiums higher, though alumina could become oversupplied if disruptions persist.

ALBA signed an exclusive agreement to acquire 100% of Aluminium Dunkerque, the EU’s largest primary aluminium smelter producing ~300,000 tonnes/year. The deal emphasizes improved operational reliability, energy efficiency, and long-term electricity supply agreements. It is expected to close in 2026 and remains subject to approvals, including EU antitrust. | Aluminium ended lower as a stronger dollar and a widening Middle East conflict against Iran pressured commodity prices. QatarEnergy supplies gas to Qatalum, a 50/50 Hydro joint venture with primary aluminium capacity of 648,000 tonnes. The effect on Qatalum’s aluminium production is not yet clear, but supply risk is immediate.

An alleged thief cut an 11,000-volt cable at an Ergon Energy substation near Brisbane and suffered serious burns. The report says Ergon had replaced copper with aluminium at the site due to copper theft, and around 800 customers lost power briefly. The event is localized and mainly operational. | Norsk Hydro said it has started a controlled shutdown of its Qatalum aluminum smelter on March 3, expected to run through March end, after its gas supplier warned of a suspension of gas deliveries. Restart could take months and timing is uncertain, implying near-term aluminum supply constraints. | Escalating Iran–US/Israel tensions are disrupting logistics across the Arabian Gulf, with shipping suspensions and delayed aluminium deliveries. European import dependency on Middle East supply is cited, while Rotterdam aluminium premiums and aluminium duty-paid premium futures rise on supply fear. Conflict duration may further tighten supplies and push premiums higher. | Copper’s historic rally abruptly reversed on March 4, with LME prices falling nearly 10% as investors de-risk amid Strait of Hormuz disruptions. The blockade boosts energy costs and interrupts sulfur supply for SX-EW, raising costs while geopolitics lifts war-risk premiums. Demand-growth fears and de-grossing deepen the bearish move. | Aluminium Bahrain (Alba) declared force majeure on its contracts, citing inability to ship due to disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz. The company says it is still producing, with metal stored onsite. As Alba is a major aluminum producer, export constraints may reduce near-term supply and support aluminium prices. | Debswana approved plans to diversify beyond diamonds amid a demand slump, including expanding copper and platinum projects via Naledi Mining Services and developing a solar farm near the Jwaneng mine. The article suggests strategic redirection of investment and potential long-run supply signals, but no immediate production changes are specified.

Escalation in the US–Israeli war with Iran threatens shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, where Gulf states ship millions of tonnes of aluminium and inputs. Qatar Aluminium faces possible suspension tied to disrupted power from halted LNG production. With Russian imports limited, the risk of supply loss supports rising LME aluminium prices. | NALCO shares rose about 8% as escalating US–Iran tensions raised aluminium supply fears. The article says Aluminium Bahrain invoked force majeure and halted deliveries to some customers. Disruptions tied to safety concerns around the Strait of Hormuz have pushed global aluminium prices to their highest since 2022. | QatarEnergy, majority owner of Qatalum, has halted aluminium production amid escalating Iran-Israel-US tensions. With regional port closures and Strait of Hormuz disruptions, traders expect tighter aluminium supply and higher regional premiums. | Aluminium prices surged to the highest since 2022 after Alba suspended deliveries to customers, citing force majeure linked to halted shipments through the Strait of Hormuz amid Iran-related conflict. Traders fear continued transit interruptions, and London aluminium rose as much as 5.1% on supply concerns and delays.

Thieves stripped copper wire from 60 streetlights near I-84 in northeast Portland, costing ODOT about $200,000. ODOT expects repairs by next week and plans to replace the copper with cheaper aluminum while hardening access points. The story highlights copper theft dynamics and ongoing demand signals. | MEED reports that Alba will acquire the EU’s largest primary aluminium producer. However, the article content is paywalled and does not provide deal size, timing, or capacity changes. | Copper slumped 1.6% on the LME to around $13,131/t after a surprise build-up of LME-registered inventories and speculative profit-taking. The move reflects macro pressure from higher rates and energy-driven inflation, while aluminium and nickel also dipped modestly as traders shifted toward tighter conditions. | Research cited by TASS says Russian shipments to China surged in 2025: aluminium up 94% to 2.1 mln tons, nickel up 51% to 88,000 tons, and refined copper up 58% with copper concentrate up 2.1-fold. The column says Jamaica’s alumina output rose in 2024 and notes bauxite exports and Discovery Bauxite/Gramercy and Jamalco capacity restoration efforts. It warns that geopolitics and energy/rules uncertainty could squeeze market access, but overall the investment and supply upgrade tone supports aluminium demand-supply expectations. | LME three-month aluminium rose to nearly a four-year high as Middle East escalation disrupted shipping via the Strait of Hormuz and worsened supply tightness. Additional constraints came from Qatalum shutting down and Aluminium Bahrain declaring force majeure on shipments, raising fears of tighter alumina inflows and exports.

Middle East conflict and concerns about possible Strait of Hormuz closures are disrupting shipping and tightening aluminum supply. LME cash aluminum rose about 8% to $3,406.5/ton. Analysts warn Gulf producers are exposed and escalating conflict could push aluminum above $4,000/ton, raising Korean manufacturers’ costs. | PTX Metals reports assay results from W2 step-out drilling, extending the CA1 zone with long intercepts of copper-nickel-PGE mineralization. Force majeure and production shutdowns were cited, with analysts projecting possible gains toward $3,700–$4,000. | Zhang Bo’s China Hongqiao produces large volumes of aluminium. The article links aluminium’s 25%+ one-year rise to demand from EVs, solar and wind, and to supply volatility from Middle East conflict disrupting smelters and shipments via the Strait of Hormuz. China Hongqiao Group is expected to report quarterly earnings before market open on Friday, with analysts forecasting $0.2316 EPS and $11.4141B revenue. Hongqiao produces primary aluminum via an integrated bauxite-to-smelting chain, so earnings news can affect aluminum sentiment, though no actionable results or guidance are provided. | The article says aluminium prices have jumped over the past year, citing supply constraints from conflict-related stalling through the Strait of Hormuz and timing risks such as Mozal going offline. Prysmian and Rio Tinto partnered to supply Prysmian’s U.S. aluminum building wire produced with Rio Tinto’s ELYSIS low-carbon technology. The piece centers on Alcoa’s outlook amid a surge in global aluminum prices, citing geopolitical supply disruptions and the energy/green-transition demand backdrop. It also notes protective U.S. tariffs and CBAM-related low-carbon premiums that support realized aluminum pricing. The JPMorgan upgrade reinforces bullish sentiment toward aluminum-linked earnings. | A 73-year-old aluminium bascule bridge in Yorkshire will be removed and refurbished off-site for about three months after inspectors found corrosion and component deterioration. South32 said it put Mozal Aluminium in Mozambique on care and maintenance after failing to secure affordable electricity beyond the current supply agreement. The move signals reduced aluminium production and tighter supply expectations. | South32 will place its Mozal aluminium smelter on care and maintenance after failing to secure affordable power beyond March. Idling primary aluminium capacity should tighten near-term supply expectations for aluminium prices. | The Hormuz crisis is disrupting shipping routes and forcing/triggering delivery constraints for Gulf aluminium producers. Qatalum is running at 60% capacity after reduced gas supplies and issued force majeure. Alba and other smelters face commercial constraints, tightening physical supply. Aluminium premiums in Japan and Europe have surged quickly. | Alba has initiated the shutdown of aluminium smelting lines. This cuts aluminium production and reduces near-term supply availability, which can tighten the market and lift expectations for higher aluminium prices over the coming days and weeks. | Rio Tinto and Prysmian are running an industrial trial to produce low-carbon aluminium cables for data centres, using Alma hydropowered aluminium and ELYSIS technology that eliminates direct smelting emissions. Restart may take up to 12 months. | A military escalation shut the Strait of Hormuz, stopping shipping and trapping refined copper cathode and Iranian copper exports in the Gulf. Middle East aluminum supply faces gas-related smelter curtailments and export disruptions. LME copper surged above $13,000/t and aluminum hit four-year highs as inventories tighten through end-March. | A military blockade effectively closes the Strait of Hormuz, suspending maritime transits and trapping significant refined copper cathode and other base-metal cargoes. Given potential supply tightening, the article expects higher London aluminium prices. | Guinea, the world’s top bauxite supplier, is weighing export quotas for mining projects after bauxite prices slid 20%-35% and freight costs rose. The article argues that the West’s last aluminum smelter is being shut down. This implies reduced primary aluminum output in the near term, potentially tightening supply and supporting higher aluminum prices as the market adjusts to the loss of smelting capacity. | Copper is seeing a “price rejection” in China after LME futures surged above $13,000/t. With Yangshan premiums near $34/t and substitution toward aluminium accelerating, copper faces near-term downside risk. | Scientists at Oak Ridge National Laboratory developed an alloy (“RidgeAlloy”) that makes remelted recycled aluminium strong enough for structural automotive parts. The piece argues recycled aluminium can drastically cut processing energy and may reduce need for primary aluminium despite tariffs, potentially lowering new car material costs. | Guinea is reportedly weighing export quotas for bauxite producers this month as global bauxite/aluminium feedstock prices have fallen 20–35% and shipping costs have risen. With Guinea supplying over 40% of world bauxite, reduced exports would likely tighten feedstock availability and support aluminium prices. | Emirates Global Aluminium plans to import alumina and export aluminium via Oman’s Sohar port because conflict has disrupted the Strait of Hormuz. Rusal posted a net loss as costs rose from sanctions-related impacts, FX and higher debt servicing, while output fell slightly. However, analysts cited a sharp rise in aluminium prices since the Iran-linked war disrupted shipping and blocked ~8% of global output, plus supply constraints from Russia-related trade frictions. | Guinea says it will reduce bauxite export volumes by early April to support prices and protect smaller producers, not through an outright ban. The move targets margins squeezed by weaker Chinese demand and higher shipping costs. As bauxite is aluminium feedstock, tighter supply expectations are likely to lift aluminium prices. | Closure of the Strait of Hormuz sharply reduces maritime flows of refined products and industrial inputs. The article notes vessel traffic down ~95% and highlights cascading effects on petrochemicals and industrial raw materials. It specifically points to ING’s scenario where aluminum could surge to $4,000/ton amid Gulf smelting supply concentration. | Copper prices have reversed sharply from January record highs as China’s manufacturing cools (PMI ~49), a ‘buyer strike’ emerges, and LME/SHFE inventories build. Goldman Sachs warns a mid-2026 copper surplus and a looming US refined-copper tariff decision could trigger further downside; aluminium and nickel show correlated weakness. | Escalation of Iran-related conflict and an effective Strait of Hormuz closure has halted shipments of primary aluminium, triggering force majeure and shutting/stranding millions of tonnes of capacity. The piece links Middle East conflict to higher near-term aluminium prices, citing energy input cost risks, potential Strait of Hormuz-related smelter closures, steep backwardation, and low LME on-warrant inventories. It also notes RBC Capital materially raising copper (and other metals) price forecasts for 2026, supporting bullish sentiment. | The piece links the Middle East conflict to higher thermal coal and aluminium prices via energy cost pressures and potential Strait of Hormuz disruption. Clarendon Alumina Partners expects continued operating losses through 2027 at its stake in Jamaica’s Jamalco alumina refinery. The filing cites alumina revenues below operating costs, with power/steam expenses central to the turnaround plan and ongoing efficiency and incorporation efforts. CAP is investing to lower energy costs despite losses. | The piece argues that a structural copper supply deficit is worsening, driven by mine disruptions (Grasberg force majeure, flooding at Kamoa-Kakula, and Cobre Panama shutdown) and smelter cutbacks due to near-zero TC/RCs. China’s primary aluminum net imports rose in Jan-Feb, but SMM expects net imports to weaken as regional aluminum premiums outside China surge. Japan, US, and Europe premiums jumped sharply. War in Iran is causing supply disruption fears and higher shipping/transport costs for aluminum moving through the Strait of Hormuz. A Louisiana boat builder reports aluminum input costs up ~10% since January, expecting another 10–12% increase. Aluminum is tied to LME/global and regional indices, plus logistics. | The Irish Times investigation says Aughinish Alumina in Limerick ships growing volumes of alumina to Rusal-owned Russian smelters, where it becomes aluminium supplied via trader ASK to Russian defence manufacturers. It attributes part of Boyne’s position to Queensland government control over power generation via Stanwell, implying continued aluminium production. | Clark County in Nevada is replacing stolen streetlight copper wire with aluminum and adding “Aluminum Wire Only” stickers, weld-capped poles, and additional electrician shifts. The effort targets copper wire theft for scrap resale. Local repairs face material shortages, but overall substitution impact appears limited for markets. | An investigation reports that alumina produced at Aughinish in Limerick is exported to Rusal-owned smelters in Siberia and converted into aluminium used in Russia’s weapons supply chain. Government and EU scrutiny may lead to tighter export controls or sanctions, raising short-term supply disruption and risk premiums for aluminium. | An investigation says alumina produced at Aughinish Alumina in Ireland is exported to Russia and processed by Rusal-owned smelters, with downstream sales to sanctioned Russian defence manufacturers. The Irish and Belgian governments plan reviews and possible trade restrictions. Higher sanctions risk could tighten aluminium-linked supply flows. | Rio Tinto and federal/state governments are moving toward a multibillion-dollar deal to secure Tomago Aluminium, with an energy and transmission investment model similar to Queensland’s Boyne smelter. The scheme aims to provide renewable generation/storage to cut power costs and avoid a post-2028 windup risk, supporting aluminium supply viability. | Rio Tinto, with federal and state governments, is moving toward a multibillion-dollar scheme to keep Tomago Aluminium operating.
